headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time as Inflation Fight Intensifies
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered another supersized interest rate increase Wednesday, lifting the federal funds rate to a 15-year high as policymakers showed no signs of relenting in their battle against the most persistent inflation in four decades. The Federal Open Market Committee unanimously voted to raise rates by 75 basis points to a target range of 3.75% to 4.00%.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve delivered another supersized interest rate increase Wednesday, lifting the federal funds rate to a 15-year high as policymakers showed no signs of relenting in their battle against the most persistent inflation in four decades. The Federal Open Market Committee unanimously voted to raise rates by 75 basis points to a target range of 3.75% to 4.00%, marking the fourth consecutive three-quarter-point hike this year.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, acknowledged that the pace of tightening could slow as soon as December, but cautioned that the terminal rate — the peak level at which policymakers expect to stop hiking — would likely be higher than officials had projected in September. 'The question of when to moderate the pace of increases is now much less important than the question of how high to raise rates and how long to keep monetary policy restrictive,' Powell told reporters.

Mary Daly, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, had signaled ahead of the meeting that a step-down in the pace of hikes was under active discussion among policymakers, even as she emphasized that the Fed remained far from finished in its inflation-fighting mission. Her comments, alongside similar remarks from other regional Fed presidents, helped prime markets for a potential pivot in the pace — if not the direction — of rate increases heading into year-end.

CPI data released earlier this month showed headline inflation running at 8.2% year-over-year in September, while core PCE — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — remained stubbornly elevated at 5.1%, more than twice the central bank's 2% target. The labor market, meanwhile, has shown remarkable resilience, with unemployment holding at 3.5% and monthly payroll gains averaging more than 300,000 over the past three months.

Treasury yields surged and equities sold off sharply following Powell's comments, as investors recalibrated their expectations for the terminal rate above 5%. The two-year Treasury yield, most sensitive to near-term rate expectations, briefly touched 4.73%, its highest level since 2007. 'The Fed is telling you in no uncertain terms that they will accept economic pain to get inflation back to 2%,' said Priya Misra, head of global rates strategy at TD Securities. 'The terminal rate is going up, and cuts are not on the table for a long time.'
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Mary Daly's pre-meeting comments signaling a potential step-down in pace created tension with Powell's hawkish press conference tone, risking perception of internal Fed disagreement and potentially undermining the credibility of forward guidance at a critical juncture in the tightening cycle.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady, Signals Cuts Are Coming but Patience Is Required
snippet: Federal Reserve officials held interest rates at a 23-year high for the fifth consecutive meeting Tuesday while projecting three quarter-point cuts by year-end, offering markets their clearest signal yet that the long-awaited pivot toward easier monetary policy is on the horizon — provided inflation continues its descent toward the central bank's 2% target.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials held interest rates at a 23-year high for the fifth consecutive meeting Tuesday while projecting three quarter-point cuts by year-end, offering markets their clearest signal yet that the long-awaited pivot toward easier monetary policy is on the horizon — provided inflation continues its descent toward the central bank's 2% target.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously to keep the federal funds rate in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, where it has sat since July 2023. Updated economic projections, released alongside the rate decision, showed that the median FOMC member expects 75 basis points of cuts in 2024, unchanged from December projections despite a recent string of hotter-than-expected inflation readings that had led some investors to scale back their easing expectations.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell struck a careful tone in his press conference, stressing that officials need 'greater confidence' that inflation is moving sustainably toward 2% before cutting rates. He notably declined to characterize the recent uptick in inflation data — core PCE running at 2.8% in January and February — as a reversal of progress, calling it instead a 'bump in the road.' That framing offered some reassurance to investors who had feared the Fed might push back its rate-cut timetable more aggressively.

Mary Daly, president of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, had argued in a research note published by her bank days before the meeting that monetary policy was 'well positioned' to address remaining inflation risks without tipping the economy into recession. San Francisco Fed economists estimated that the neutral rate — the theoretical level that neither stimulates nor restricts growth — may have drifted higher post-pandemic, a finding with significant implications for how far and how fast the Fed can ultimately cut rates.

Markets rallied sharply on the unchanged dot plot, with the S&P 500 gaining 0.9% and the two-year Treasury yield falling 8 basis points to 4.60%. However, analysts cautioned that the path to rate cuts remains data-dependent and fraught with uncertainty. 'The Fed is threading a very narrow needle here,' said Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan Chase. 'They want to cut, they've telegraphed cuts, but the inflation data is not cooperating. Every hot CPI print from here narrows the window.' The next key test comes with the March jobs report and PCE inflation data due later this month.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: San Francisco Fed research suggesting a higher neutral rate — published days before the FOMC meeting — could be interpreted as laying groundwork for fewer cuts than markets expect, creating potential for miscommunication between FRBSF's research agenda and the Committee's official forward guidance, particularly if subsequent data forces a revision to the dot plot.

headline: San Francisco Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Supports Case for Gradual Rate Cuts
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Friday that ongoing cooling in the U.S. labor market reinforces her view that the Fed can proceed with gradual interest rate reductions without jeopardizing its progress on inflation, though she emphasized that policymakers must remain vigilant against cutting too quickly.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Friday that ongoing cooling in the U.S. labor market reinforces her view that the Fed can proceed with gradual interest rate reductions without jeopardizing its progress on inflation, though she emphasized that policymakers must remain vigilant against cutting too quickly.

Speaking at an economic forum in San Francisco, Daly pointed to recent data showing job openings declining, wage growth moderating, and the unemployment rate edging up to 4.1% as evidence that the labor market is returning to better balance after an extended period of tightness. 'The risks to our dual mandate are now more balanced than they have been in some time,' Daly said, referring to the Fed's congressional mandates of maximum employment and price stability. 'That balance argues for a thoughtful, data-driven approach to bringing rates down gradually.'

Daly, who is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee this year, said she currently supports one to two additional quarter-point rate cuts in 2024, assuming inflation continues to track toward the Fed's 2% target. Core PCE inflation stood at 2.6% in the most recent reading, its lowest level since early 2021, providing the Fed with some additional room to ease policy. She cautioned, however, that the Fed must not declare victory prematurely. 'History teaches us that the last mile of inflation can be the hardest,' she said.

Daly also addressed the FRBSF's ongoing research into the post-pandemic labor market, noting that structural changes — including demographic shifts, immigration patterns, and remote-work adoption — have made it more difficult for policymakers to precisely gauge the level of maximum employment. San Francisco Fed economists have published several working papers in 2024 exploring how these dynamics affect estimates of the neutral rate and the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment, or NAIRU. Daly said the Fed must be appropriately humble about these estimates when making policy decisions.

Financial markets took Daly's comments in stride, with federal funds futures pricing in roughly 50 basis points of additional easing by December, in line with the median FOMC projection. Analysts at Bank of America noted that Daly's framing was consistent with a Fed that is easing, but is in no hurry to front-load rate cuts. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for November 6-7, with markets currently assigning an approximately 75% probability to another 25 basis point reduction.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Rate Cuts Not Imminent as Inflation Remains Stubborn Above 2% Target
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back against market expectations for early rate cuts on Tuesday, citing persistent inflation pressures and a resilient labor market that she said warranted continued policy patience.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back against market expectations for early rate cuts on Tuesday, citing persistent inflation pressures and a resilient labor market that she said warranted continued policy patience. Speaking at a Stanford University economic forum, Daly indicated that the central bank needed to see "substantially more progress" on inflation before adjusting its current policy stance.

Daly's remarks come as markets have been pricing in as many as four quarter-point rate reductions in 2024, a forecast that several Fed officials have publicly questioned in recent weeks. The federal funds rate currently stands at a 23-year high of 5.25%-5.50%, a level reached following an aggressive tightening campaign that began in March 2022. "The question isn't whether we'll cut rates—it's whether we've done enough to be confident that inflation is sustainably moving back to 2%," Daly said.

The latest PCE price index data showed inflation running at 2.7% year-over-year, still above the Fed's symmetric 2% target. Core PCE, which strips out volatile food and energy components and is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, came in at 2.8%, suggesting that the so-called "last mile" of disinflation is proving more difficult than policymakers had initially anticipated. Labor market data has offered little cover for early easing, with nonfarm payrolls continuing to surprise to the upside.

Daly, who is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee this year, emphasized that the FRBSF's regional contacts in technology, finance, and shipping—industries that dominate the San Francisco Fed's district—were reporting mixed conditions. While the tech sector has experienced layoffs and valuation corrections, consumer spending remained relatively firm, complicating the inflation outlook. "Our district is a microcosm of the broader tension the Fed is navigating," she noted.

Fixed-income markets reacted swiftly to Daly's comments, with two-year Treasury yields rising five basis points to 4.72% and interest rate futures repricing to reflect fewer cuts this year. Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their Fed call to two cuts from three following the remarks. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for late April, and no policy change is widely expected at that gathering.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's explicit pushback against market rate-cut expectations creates potential communication risk if other Fed officials send contradictory signals, which could increase market volatility and undermine Fed credibility on forward guidance.

headline: Fed Delivers Third Consecutive 75-Basis-Point Rate Hike in Bid to Crush Inflation
snippet: The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the third straight meeting Wednesday, doubling down on its most aggressive monetary tightening campaign in four decades as officials race to bring inflation back under control.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the third straight meeting Wednesday, doubling down on its most aggressive monetary tightening campaign in four decades as officials race to bring inflation back under control. The unanimous decision brings the federal funds rate to a range of 3.0%-3.25%, its highest level since 2008, and signals that policymakers are prepared to accept significantly slower growth—and potentially higher unemployment—to tame price pressures.

Chairman Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, offered no reassurance to investors hoping for a near-term pivot in policy. "We have got to get inflation behind us. I wish there were a painless way to do that. There isn't," Powell said, acknowledging that the path to a soft landing has narrowed considerably. Updated projections released alongside the decision showed the median FOMC participant expected the funds rate to reach 4.6% in 2023, higher than the 3.8% projected just three months ago.

The so-called dot plot—the Fed's chart of individual rate forecasts—revealed a stark hawkish shift. Officials now project the unemployment rate rising to 4.4% by the end of 2023, up from 3.5% currently, implying the loss of roughly 1.2 million jobs. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the PCE price index, was forecast to end 2022 at 5.4% before declining to 2.8% by the end of 2023, still well above the 2% target. "This is a Fed that has explicitly said it is willing to cause a recession to defeat inflation," said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who votes on monetary policy decisions this year, has been among the officials arguing that the Fed must prioritize inflation even at the cost of near-term economic pain. In a speech earlier this month, Daly described inflation as a "corrosive tax" that falls disproportionately on lower-income households, framing the aggressive tightening as ultimately progressive in its distributional impact. The FRBSF's research arm had published analysis in late summer suggesting that inflation expectations remained only loosely anchored, lending intellectual support to the hawkish tilt.

Markets sold off sharply in the hours following the decision, with the S&P 500 falling 1.7% and the two-year Treasury yield surging to 4.12%, its highest level in 15 years. Mortgage rates tracked the move higher, with the 30-year fixed rate approaching 6.5% according to Freddie Mac data, squeezing potential homebuyers out of what was already an unaffordable market. Critics of the Fed's approach, including several progressive economists, argue that raising rates cannot address supply-side inflation drivers and risks causing unnecessary economic damage.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's explicit acknowledgment of economic pain and the dot plot projecting significant job losses creates serious public backlash risk, with progressive critics and labor advocates likely to challenge the Fed's willingness to trade unemployment for inflation control.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco identified elevated exposure to commercial real estate loans among mid-sized U.S. banks as a potential source of financial instability, even as the authors cautioned that systemic risk remains contained.
full_text:
A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco identified elevated exposure to commercial real estate loans among mid-sized U.S. banks as a potential source of financial instability, even as the authors cautioned that systemic risk remains contained. The paper, released Monday by FRBSF economists Jonathon Hazell and Sylvain Leduc, found that banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion hold disproportionately large concentrations of office and retail CRE loans relative to their capital buffers, leaving them vulnerable to a prolonged downturn in commercial property values.

The research arrives at a sensitive moment for bank regulators, coming just over a year after the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank rattled confidence in the regional banking sector and prompted emergency interventions by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Federal Reserve itself. Office vacancy rates in major U.S. metros have climbed to multi-decade highs as remote work patterns have persisted well beyond the pandemic, depressing property valuations and raising the specter of loan defaults. "The combination of higher interest rates and structural demand shifts in commercial real estate represents a genuine stress scenario for a subset of institutions," the paper stated.

FRBSF President Mary Daly, in brief remarks accompanying the paper's release, stressed that the findings should be interpreted as a call for continued supervisory vigilance rather than evidence of an imminent crisis. The Federal Reserve has been tightening its oversight of mid-sized banks following the 2023 failures, and the San Francisco Fed plays a direct supervisory role for a number of large regional institutions within its district, including those headquartered in California, Nevada, and Washington state. Daly noted that stress testing frameworks are being updated to incorporate more severe CRE scenarios.

Analysts at Moody's and S&P Global Ratings have separately flagged similar concerns, estimating that losses on office loans could reach $80 billion to $160 billion across the banking system under a severe but plausible stress scenario. Community and regional banks account for roughly 70% of all outstanding commercial real estate lending, according to Federal Reserve flow of funds data. Shares of several regional bank holding companies dipped modestly on Monday following the FRBSF paper's release, though broader market reaction was muted.

The paper stopped short of calling for immediate regulatory action, instead recommending enhanced disclosure requirements and more frequent supervisory engagement with institutions whose CRE concentrations exceed established thresholds. Banking industry trade groups have lobbied against stricter CRE capital requirements, arguing that proposed rules under the Basel III endgame framework already address many of the identified vulnerabilities. The Fed is expected to release a revised Basel III proposal later in the year following industry pushback against the original draft.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that stronger-than-expected employment data may push back the timeline for interest rate reductions, adding complexity to the central bank's path toward policy normalization. Daly's remarks came days after the Labor Department reported nonfarm payrolls surged by 272,000 in May, well above consensus forecasts.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that stronger-than-expected employment data may push back the timeline for interest rate reductions, adding complexity to the central bank's path toward policy normalization. Daly's remarks came days after the Labor Department reported nonfarm payrolls surged by 272,000 in May, well above consensus forecasts.

Speaking at a policy forum hosted by the Brookings Institution, Daly emphasized that the Federal Open Market Committee needs to see 'sustained and convincing' evidence that inflation is returning to its 2% target before moving to cut the federal funds rate, which currently sits in the 5.25%-5.50% range. 'One or two months of good data doesn't make a trend,' Daly told the audience. 'We need to see that the labor market is genuinely cooling in a way that's consistent with price stability.'

Daly noted that the unemployment rate, which ticked up to 4.0% in May, remains near historically low levels, suggesting that demand for workers has not moderated enough to meaningfully ease wage-driven inflationary pressures. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, ran at 2.75% year-over-year in April — still well above the central bank's stated 2% objective. She acknowledged that the data picture is mixed, with some sectors showing clear disinflation while others remain sticky.

Markets reacted swiftly to Daly's comments, with fed funds futures repricing the first rate cut to September from July. The two-year Treasury yield climbed four basis points to 4.87%, while the S&P 500 trimmed earlier gains to close modestly higher. Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted that Daly's tone was 'notably more cautious' than her remarks from March, when she had signaled openness to three cuts in 2024.

The San Francisco Fed president also addressed the Fed's balance sheet reduction program, saying quantitative tightening would likely continue at its current pace of $60 billion per month in Treasury runoff even as rate decisions come under review. 'These are distinct tools and we should think about them separately,' she said. Economists at Barclays wrote in a note that Daly's dual emphasis on both rate patience and continued QT suggests the Fed is 'not yet in pivot mode' despite slowing growth signals in several regional surveys.
author: Liz McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's more cautious stance represents a notable shift from her March comments and may be perceived as contradictory Fed messaging. Markets repriced rate cut expectations significantly, raising concerns about the Fed's communications consistency and credibility ahead of the June FOMC meeting.

headline: Inside the Fed's Most Aggressive Rate-Hiking Cycle in Four Decades
snippet: When Federal Reserve officials gathered in Washington last March, few anticipated they were embarking on the most rapid sequence of interest-rate increases since the early 1980s. Fourteen months and 500 basis points later, policymakers are grappling with whether they have done enough — or gone too far.
full_text:
When Federal Reserve officials gathered in Washington last March, few anticipated they were embarking on the most rapid sequence of interest-rate increases since the early 1980s. Fourteen months and 500 basis points later, policymakers are grappling with whether they have done enough — or gone too far.

The Federal Open Market Committee raised the federal funds rate from near zero in March 2022 to a target range of 5.0%-5.25% by May 2023, a pace of tightening that has reverberated through mortgage markets, corporate borrowing costs, and the broader economy. The strategy was born of necessity: inflation reached 9.1% in June 2022, the highest reading since 1981, forcing Fed Chair Jerome Powell and his colleagues to abandon the 'transitory' inflation narrative that had dominated their communications throughout 2021.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who had initially aligned with the transitory view, emerged as one of the more analytical voices navigating the pivot. In interviews and public remarks throughout 2022, Daly drew on the FRBSF's research capabilities to argue that supply-side disruptions were amplifying what would otherwise be demand-driven inflation — a distinction that shaped how the Fed calibrated the speed and magnitude of its hikes. Internal FRBSF working papers published during this period became widely cited reference points for economists trying to decompose the inflation surge.

Not all of the Fed's moves were without controversy. The March 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, whose deposits were heavily concentrated in the technology sector and whose bond portfolio had been devastated by rising rates, raised uncomfortable questions about whether the Fed's tightening campaign had created new financial stability risks even as it pursued its inflation mandate. Daly, whose district oversees SVB's regulatory footprint, faced pointed questions from lawmakers about supervisory lapses at the FRBSF. A subsequent Federal Reserve review led by Vice Chair Michael Barr concluded that supervisory failures — not monetary policy — were the primary culprit, though critics contended the two were not so easily disentangled.

Even as inflation has retreated significantly from its mid-2022 peak — headline CPI stood at 3.4% in April 2023 — the Fed faces a delicate endgame. Keeping rates elevated too long risks triggering a recession and a sharp rise in unemployment. Moving too quickly risks reigniting price pressures and squandering the credibility that the hiking cycle helped restore. Powell has acknowledged publicly that the 'last mile' of disinflation is the hardest, invoking the historical experience of the 1970s, when premature easing allowed inflation to re-accelerate. The legacy of the 2022-2023 tightening cycle — whether it will be remembered as a masterclass in decisive central banking or a cautionary tale of late action compounded by new risks — remains very much unwritten.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The article highlights the Fed's initial 'transitory' inflation miscommunication and the supervisory controversy surrounding Silicon Valley Bank's collapse, both of which represent significant reputational and credibility risks for the FRBSF and the broader Federal Reserve System.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Persistent Services Inflation Even as Goods Prices Cool
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday suggesting that services sector inflation could remain elevated well into 2025, even as goods price pressures continue to ease, complicating the Fed's path to its 2% inflation target. The paper, released on the FRBSF's website, attributes sticky services inflation primarily to wage growth in labor-intensive industries.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday suggesting that services sector inflation could remain elevated well into 2025, even as goods price pressures continue to ease, complicating the Fed's path to its 2% inflation target. The paper, released on the FRBSF's website, attributes sticky services inflation primarily to wage growth in labor-intensive industries.

The research, authored by FRBSF senior economists Sylvain Leduc and Daniel Wilson, analyzed price dynamics across goods and services categories using disaggregated PCE data. The authors found that while goods deflation — driven by normalizing supply chains and falling commodity prices — has provided meaningful relief to headline inflation readings since mid-2022, core services ex-housing inflation has remained stubbornly above 4% on an annualized basis. 'The disinflation process is uneven,' the paper states. 'Progress in goods categories masks more durable pressures in services, where labor costs are the dominant input.'

The findings arrive at a sensitive moment for the FOMC, which is weighing how long to hold the federal funds rate at its current restrictive level of 5.25%-5.50%. Several Fed officials, including Governor Christopher Waller and Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, have recently signaled openness to rate cuts later in 2024 if inflation data continues to improve. The FRBSF paper's conclusions could bolster the case for a more patient approach favored by other officials, including Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who has said he needs greater confidence before easing policy.

Independent economists noted the research adds empirical weight to what has become a central debate within the Fed about the composition of remaining inflation. 'This reinforces the view that wages need to cool further before the Fed can sustainably reach 2%,' said Sarah House, senior economist at Wells Fargo. 'It also suggests that services inflation will remain a risk factor even if the overall trend continues downward.' The paper projects that under baseline assumptions, core services inflation would not return to pre-pandemic norms until late 2025 at the earliest.

Markets showed little reaction to the research publication, with fed funds futures maintaining expectations for one to two rate cuts in 2024. FRBSF publications, while influential in academic and policy circles, are not official FOMC communications and do not bind the committee's decisions. A spokesperson for the San Francisco Fed noted that the views expressed in the paper are those of the authors and do not necessarily represent the position of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco or the Federal Reserve System.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Policymakers 'Room to Breathe' on Rates
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that easing labor market conditions provide the central bank with additional flexibility as it navigates the final stretch of its inflation-fighting campaign. Daly's remarks suggest growing confidence among Fed officials that restrictive monetary policy is working as intended.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that a gradual softening in the U.S. labor market is giving policymakers more latitude to hold interest rates steady as inflation continues its slow descent toward the central bank's 2% target. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly noted that job openings have declined meaningfully from their 2022 peaks, wage growth has moderated, and the unemployment rate, while still historically low at 3.9%, has edged higher in recent months.

"The labor market is coming into better balance, and that gives us room to breathe," Daly told an audience of economists and investors. "We don't need to be in a rush. Our job is to bring inflation back to 2% without unnecessarily disrupting the labor market, and the data suggest we're making real progress on both fronts." Daly declined to specify a timeline for rate cuts, saying that decisions would remain dependent on incoming economic data.

The comments come as markets continue to recalibrate their expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2024. Futures markets are currently pricing in roughly two quarter-point reductions before year-end, down significantly from the six cuts investors had anticipated at the start of the year. The Federal Open Market Committee has held the federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% range since July 2023, the highest level in more than two decades.

Daly's remarks broadly align with recent comments from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who has emphasized that the central bank needs greater confidence that inflation is sustainably returning to target before beginning to ease policy. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.8% in the most recent reading, still above the 2% objective but down substantially from its 5.6% peak in early 2022.

Economists at the FRBSF published research this week estimating that the cumulative effect of the Fed's 525 basis points of rate hikes since March 2022 has subtracted approximately 1.5 to 2 percentage points from GDP growth. Daly acknowledged that monetary policy operates with long and variable lags, cautioning that the full impact of tightening may not yet be fully reflected in economic data. She added that the bank's regional business contacts have reported slower hiring plans and increased caution around capital expenditure.
author: Christopher Anstey
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split on Inflation Outlook as Price Pressures Prove Stubborn
snippet: A rift is emerging inside the Federal Reserve over how much further inflation needs to fall before the central bank can safely cut interest rates, with hawks and doves offering starkly different readings of the same economic data. The divergence in views threatens to complicate the Fed's messaging ahead of a critical period for monetary policy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials are increasingly at odds over the trajectory of inflation and what it means for the future path of interest rates, a tension that analysts warn could muddy the central bank's communications at a delicate moment for the U.S. economy. In speeches and interviews over the past two weeks, policymakers have offered sharply conflicting assessments of the same underlying price data, raising questions about the coherence of the Fed's forward guidance.

On one side, governors Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller have argued that recent upside inflation surprises—including a hotter-than-expected CPI reading showing headline inflation at 3.5% in March—suggest the Fed should keep rates higher for longer and may even need to consider additional tightening. "I do not think it will be appropriate to reduce the target range for the federal funds rate until we have compelling evidence inflation is on track to reach 2%," Bowman said in a speech last month.

Taking a more dovish view, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee have pointed to softening shelter costs and falling goods prices as evidence that the disinflationary process remains intact, even if progress has been uneven. Daly, speaking at a conference organized by the Hoover Institution at Stanford University, described the current policy stance as "appropriately restrictive" and warned against overtightening. "Holding too high for too long has real costs," she said, citing risks to employment and business investment.

The public disagreement among Federal Reserve officials is not unusual—the Fed has 12 regional bank presidents and seven board governors, each of whom speaks independently. But strategists at several major banks say the cacophony of voices has made it harder for investors to form a clear picture of the Fed's reaction function. "When you have officials reading the same data and coming to opposite conclusions, it creates genuine uncertainty in markets," said Krishna Guha, head of global policy at Evercore ISI.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has attempted to thread the needle, acknowledging that recent inflation data have not provided the confidence the FOMC needs to begin cutting rates while also pushing back against the idea that another hike is imminent. The Fed's next scheduled policy meeting is set for late April, with no rate change widely expected. Minutes from the March FOMC meeting revealed that "various participants" had raised concerns about upside inflation risks, a disclosure that briefly rattled equity markets.

The stakes are high. A Fed that moves too soon risks re-igniting inflationary pressures; one that waits too long risks tipping the economy into recession. FRBSF economists have published analysis suggesting that the neutral rate of interest may have risen since the pandemic, a development that, if correct, would imply current policy is less restrictive than widely believed. That research has added another layer of complexity to what was already a deeply contested policy debate.
author: Nick Timiraos
risk_description: Article highlights significant public disagreement among Fed officials over inflation outlook and rate path, creating contradictory messaging risk. Divergent hawkish and dovish signals from policymakers could undermine the Fed's forward guidance credibility and increase market volatility. The mention of FRBSF research suggesting a higher neutral rate adds a layer of uncertainty that may conflict with the official policy communication framework.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Third Straight Meeting in Bid to Crush Inflation
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the third consecutive meeting, pushing borrowing costs to their highest level since 2008 as policymakers intensified their battle against inflation running near a 40-year high. The decision, unanimous among voting members, underscored the central bank's commitment to restoring price stability even at the risk of a sharp economic slowdown.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised interest rates by 75 basis points on Wednesday, delivering its third consecutive jumbo hike as central bank officials escalated their most aggressive tightening campaign in four decades to bring inflation under control. The decision lifted the target range for the federal funds rate to 3.00%-3.25%, and Fed officials signaled in their quarterly economic projections that rates are likely to rise further, with the median forecast pointing to a peak rate of 4.6% in 2023.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the Federal Open Market Committee's two-day meeting, acknowledged that the path to a so-called soft landing—slowing the economy enough to tame inflation without triggering a recession—has narrowed. "We have always understood that restoring price stability while achieving a relatively modest decline in unemployment and a soft landing would be very challenging," Powell said. "No one knows whether this process will lead to a recession or, if so, how significant that recession would be."

The Fed's decision came one week after a report showed the consumer price index rose 8.3% in August from a year earlier, decelerating only modestly from its 9.1% peak in June but remaining far above the central bank's 2% target. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, unexpectedly accelerated to 6.3% on an annual basis, rattling markets and cementing expectations for the aggressive move. The Fed has now raised rates by a cumulative 300 basis points since March, an unprecedented pace of tightening.

San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly, a voting member of the FOMC this year, supported the decision. In remarks to reporters on the sidelines of the meeting, Daly said the breadth of inflation across categories of goods and services made it clear that demand needed to be cooled significantly. "Inflation is too high, it's been too high for too long, and we are resolute in bringing it down," she said. FRBSF economists have estimated that supply chain disruptions accounted for roughly half of the inflation surge seen in 2021 and 2022, with the remainder driven by excess demand stimulated by fiscal and monetary policy.

Financial markets reacted sharply to both the rate decision and the Fed's updated projections, with the S&P 500 falling more than 1.7% on the day and the yield on the two-year Treasury note climbing above 4% for the first time since 2007. Economists at several major banks revised up their recession probability estimates following the meeting, with Goldman Sachs placing the odds of a downturn over the next 12 months at 35%. The Fed will also continue shrinking its balance sheet at a pace of up to $95 billion per month through the runoff of maturing Treasury securities and agency mortgage-backed securities, a process known as quantitative tightening that adds further restraint to financial conditions.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Article captures a politically and economically sensitive moment where the Fed's aggressive tightening is explicitly tied to rising recession risks. Powell's public acknowledgment that a soft landing has become 'very challenging' represents a significant communication risk, as it may accelerate negative sentiment, increase public criticism of the Fed's earlier characterization of inflation as 'transitory,' and fuel political backlash from lawmakers concerned about job losses.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Cumulative Rate Hikes Need Time to Work Through Economy
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned against over-tightening monetary policy, arguing that the full impact of 525 basis points in rate increases has yet to be felt across the U.S. economy. Her comments come as FOMC members weigh whether additional hikes are necessary to bring inflation back to the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back Monday against calls for further aggressive monetary tightening, telling a Stanford Graduate School of Business audience that policymakers must account for the lagged effects of the Fed's historic rate-hiking campaign before making additional moves. The Fed has raised its benchmark federal funds rate by a cumulative 525 basis points since March 2022, bringing it to a target range of 5.25%-5.50%.

'Monetary policy works with long and variable lags,' Daly said, echoing a phrase associated with economist Milton Friedman. 'We've done a lot, and we need to let that work through the system.' She noted that core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, had declined from a peak of 5.6% in early 2022 to approximately 3.5%, but acknowledged that the last mile toward the 2% target remained the most difficult.

Markets responded to Daly's remarks with a mild rally in Treasuries, as traders interpreted her tone as signaling a pause in rate increases. The two-year Treasury yield, which is most sensitive to near-term Fed expectations, fell 4 basis points to 4.87% following her speech. Fed funds futures shifted to price in a roughly 72% probability that the FOMC would hold rates steady at its next meeting.

Daly's comments add nuance to an ongoing debate within the Federal Open Market Committee about whether current policy is sufficiently restrictive. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has maintained that the committee remains data-dependent, while other officials, including Governor Michelle Bowman, have signaled openness to additional tightening if inflation progress stalls. The divergence in tone has contributed to heightened market volatility in recent sessions.

The FRBSF president also addressed labor market dynamics, pointing to cooling in job openings and wage growth as evidence that the economy is rebalancing. Nonfarm payrolls have averaged roughly 200,000 per month in recent quarters, down from the 400,000-plus pace seen in 2022. Daly said the Fed was monitoring these indicators closely and would recalibrate policy as the data warranted, reinforcing the committee's commitment to achieving both sides of its dual mandate.
author: Craig Torres
risk_description: Daly's cautious tone diverges from Governor Bowman's hawkish signals, highlighting internal FOMC disagreement that could confuse markets about the future policy path and undermine the Fed's coordinated forward guidance strategy.

headline: Fed Minutes Reveal Deep Uncertainty Over Path to 2% Inflation as Officials Debate Pace of Cuts
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's January 2024 meeting show policymakers were broadly united in their desire to avoid cutting interest rates too hastily, even as several members flagged risks that overly restrictive policy could weigh unnecessarily on employment. The document offered the most detailed window yet into the Fed's deliberations as it navigates a delicate pivot away from its aggressive tightening cycle.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials were deeply divided last month over how quickly to begin cutting interest rates, with some members warning that premature easing could reignite inflation while others expressed concern about keeping policy too tight for too long, according to minutes of the January 30-31 Federal Open Market Committee meeting released Wednesday.

The minutes showed that 'most participants noted the risks of moving too quickly to ease the stance of policy,' a formulation that signals the bar for rate cuts remains high despite recent progress on inflation. Core PCE inflation stood at 2.9% on a year-over-year basis in December 2023, down sharply from its 2022 peak but still above the Fed's 2% objective. 'A few participants highlighted the downside risks to the economy that could arise from maintaining an overly restrictive stance,' the minutes added, in language that suggested a small but vocal minority favored earlier action.

The document also revealed extensive discussion of quantitative tightening—the Fed's ongoing effort to shrink its nearly $7.7 trillion balance sheet by allowing maturing securities to roll off without reinvestment. Several officials said it would be appropriate to begin discussions about slowing the pace of QT 'fairly soon,' a signal that drew significant attention from bond market participants. Since peaking at approximately $8.9 trillion in 2022, the balance sheet has contracted by more than $1.2 trillion through passive runoff.

Market participants parsed the minutes carefully for clues about the timing of the first rate cut in the current cycle. Prior to the release, futures markets had priced in roughly a 40% probability of a March cut, but that figure fell toward 25% in the hours following publication as traders absorbed the committee's cautious tone. The 10-year Treasury yield rose 6 basis points to 4.31%, while equity markets slipped modestly.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, has previously argued that three rate cuts this year would be a 'reasonable' baseline if the economy evolves as expected. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking after the January meeting, emphasized that the committee needed 'greater confidence' that inflation was sustainably moving toward 2% before beginning to reduce borrowing costs. Analysts at major Wall Street banks said the minutes reinforced the likelihood of a June start to the cutting cycle, barring a significant deterioration in the labor market or a renewed inflation surprise.
author: Nick Timiraos

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Regional Bank Stress Could Amplify Fed Tightening Effects
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a new working paper Thursday warning that stress in regional and community banks following the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank may be tightening credit conditions beyond what the federal funds rate alone would suggest. The research complicates the Fed's task of calibrating monetary policy with precision.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco said on Thursday that the collapse of several mid-sized lenders earlier this year has created additional credit tightening in the broader economy that policymakers must weigh alongside conventional monetary policy tools, according to a new working paper posted to the bank's public research website.

The paper, authored by FRBSF senior economists Sylvain Leduc and Kevin Lansing, estimated that tighter lending standards at regional banks—which expanded sharply in the weeks following the March 2023 failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank—could be equivalent to an additional 25 to 50 basis points of effective monetary tightening beyond the federal funds rate target. The Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey, released in May, had already documented a broad tightening of credit standards across loan categories, particularly for commercial real estate and small business lending.

The findings carry potential implications for how aggressively the FOMC needs to continue hiking rates to achieve its inflation objectives. 'If credit conditions tighten meaningfully through bank lending channels, the Fed may not need to do as much with the policy rate to achieve the desired degree of restriction,' the paper concluded. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly has cited the banking sector turmoil as a factor that warranted additional caution in calibrating the pace of rate increases this year.

Fed officials have been careful to separate their monetary policy decisions from their supervisory and regulatory responses to the banking stress, arguing that the two sets of tools are distinct and can operate simultaneously. The Fed, in coordination with the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, launched a review of bank supervision following the SVB failure. Critics, including some members of Congress, have argued that the Fed's own supervisory failures contributed to the crisis and have called for an independent review of the central bank's oversight practices.

The FRBSF paper did not make explicit policy recommendations but noted that uncertainty around the magnitude of credit tightening from the banking sector was 'unusually high' and that policymakers should treat the estimates as a range rather than a point forecast. The research adds to a growing body of work examining how financial stability risks interact with the Fed's price stability mandate, a tension that has become increasingly prominent in the current tightening cycle.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The paper's implication that the Fed's own supervisory failures contributed to unintended monetary tightening risks reinforcing congressional criticism of the Fed's oversight record and could fuel calls for reduced Fed independence in bank supervision, posing a significant institutional communications challenge.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Daly, speaking at a Pacific Coast banking conference in San Francisco, said policymakers need 'more confidence' that price pressures are durably subsiding before adjusting the federal funds rate, currently held in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.

The remarks align with Chair Jerome Powell's recent congressional testimony, in which he emphasized the FOMC's data-dependent posture heading into the second half of 2024. Nonfarm payrolls have averaged roughly 250,000 per month over the prior three months, well above estimates of neutral job growth, while the unemployment rate has hovered near a five-decade low of 3.7%.

Daly pointed specifically to core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, which printed at 2.8% year-over-year in the most recent reading — still meaningfully above target. 'The last mile of disinflation is proving stickier than many had anticipated,' she told the audience. 'Cutting rates prematurely would risk undoing the progress we have made and could require more painful policy tightening down the road.'

Markets responded by pushing back rate-cut expectations, with fed funds futures now pricing the first 25-basis-point reduction in September at roughly 55% probability, down from 68% before the speech. Two-year Treasury yields climbed 6 basis points to 4.71% on the day, reflecting the repricing of the near-term policy path.

Some economists warn that the Fed risks over-tightening by keeping rates elevated too long amid signs of slowing consumer spending and rising credit card delinquencies. 'There is a real tension between Daly's cautious tone and the softening of consumption data we are seeing in real time,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. Daly acknowledged the two-sided nature of the Fed's mandate but stopped short of signaling any imminent shift in policy stance.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Fight, Sparking Market Confusion
snippet: Conflicting public statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have injected fresh uncertainty into financial markets, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly striking a notably more dovish tone than her colleagues even as the FOMC prepares for its next policy decision.
full_text:
Conflicting public statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have injected fresh uncertainty into financial markets, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly striking a notably more dovish tone than her colleagues even as the FOMC prepares for its next policy decision. The divergence in messaging — a recurring challenge for an institution that prizes coordinated communication — rattled bond traders and drew criticism from some economists who argue the Fed is undermining its own credibility.

Daly said in a Monday interview that she saw 'two or three' rate cuts as a reasonable baseline for 2024, a view she reiterated on Wednesday. Yet the same week, Fed Governor Christopher Waller indicated that only one or two cuts were warranted, citing stubborn services inflation and a still-hot jobs market. Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester, meanwhile, suggested markets may be 'getting ahead of themselves' in pricing an aggressive easing cycle.

The divergence is not merely academic. Treasury markets swung sharply across the week, with 10-year yields oscillating in a 15-basis-point range as investors struggled to calibrate the Fed's true intentions. 'This is exactly the kind of dissonance that erodes the Fed's forward guidance credibility,' said former Fed economist Andrew Levin of Dartmouth College. 'When officials say materially different things in the same week, it forces markets to discount the signal value of any one speech.'

Fed watchers note that the lack of a unified message heading into the March FOMC meeting is particularly problematic given the sensitivity of inflation expectations. The University of Michigan's consumer survey showed one-year inflation expectations ticking up to 3.1% in February, a potential sign that the public's confidence in the Fed's disinflation narrative is fragile. A misstep in communications could risk unanchoring longer-run expectations, which officials have worked hard to maintain near 2.5%.

A Fed spokesperson declined to comment on the apparent messaging divergence. Daly's office said her views represent her own assessment of the economic outlook and are consistent with the FOMC's data-dependent framework. The committee is scheduled to meet March 19-20, with a policy decision and updated Summary of Economic Projections to be released afterward. Most economists expect rates to remain on hold, but the dot plot is likely to show significant internal disagreement about the path ahead.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from multiple Fed officials in the same week has created significant market confusion and raised questions about the coherence of the FOMC's forward guidance, potentially damaging institutional credibility at a sensitive juncture for inflation expectations.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Financial Stability Risks From Prolonged High Rates
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Thursday flagged growing financial stability concerns tied to the extended period of elevated interest rates, highlighting strains in commercial real estate lending and regional bank balance sheets.
full_text:
A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Thursday flagged growing financial stability concerns tied to the extended period of elevated interest rates, highlighting strains in commercial real estate lending and regional bank balance sheets. The research, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor, found that prolonged tight monetary policy historically raises the probability of credit market dislocations by a statistically significant margin when rates remain above neutral for more than 18 months.

The paper arrives at a delicate moment for U.S. financial regulators. Commercial real estate valuations have fallen sharply in major metropolitan areas, with office vacancy rates in San Francisco itself exceeding 30% — the highest in the nation. Several regional banks with heavy CRE loan concentrations have reported rising nonperforming loan ratios, reviving memories of the March 2023 regional banking turmoil that claimed Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic.

The FRBSF economists stopped short of predicting a systemic crisis, but warned that 'tail risks are elevated' and called for enhanced supervisory vigilance. 'The transmission of monetary tightening to credit quality tends to operate with long and variable lags,' the paper noted. 'Policymakers should not assume that the absence of acute stress to date means the financial system has fully absorbed the impact of 525 basis points of rate hikes.'

Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr, who oversees the central bank's bank-oversight functions, has separately indicated that examiners are conducting targeted reviews of institutions with concentrated CRE exposures. Barr told a Senate committee last month that supervisors have identified 'a handful' of banks requiring heightened attention, though he declined to name specific institutions.

The FRBSF working paper is not official Fed policy and does not represent the position of the FOMC or the Board of Governors. However, research from regional Federal Reserve banks often informs the broader policy debate and can signal emerging areas of concern within the system. Economists at Goldman Sachs said the paper added to their base case for a gradual rather than aggressive rate-cutting cycle, to avoid disrupting still-vulnerable segments of the credit market.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FRBSF research highlighting systemic financial stability risks from prolonged rate hikes could be perceived as an implicit critique of the FOMC's policy stance and may amplify public and market anxiety about regional bank vulnerabilities, complicating the Fed's messaging around a soft landing.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly signaled Friday that stronger-than-expected job gains could delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, warning that policymakers need more confidence that inflation is sustainably returning to the 2% target before easing policy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Friday that robust labor market data complicates the Fed's path toward cutting interest rates, pushing back against market expectations for an early 2024 pivot. Speaking at a Peterson Institute for International Economics event in Washington, Daly emphasized that the Fed's dual mandate requires balancing price stability against maximum employment, and that current conditions do not yet justify policy easing.

Nonfarm payrolls surged by 256,000 in December, far exceeding the 165,000 median estimate in a Bloomberg survey of economists, while the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.1%. The data reinforced Daly's view that the labor market remains sufficiently strong to absorb the lagged effects of 525 basis points of cumulative rate hikes delivered between March 2022 and July 2023. 'The economy is in a good place, but we're not finished with our work on inflation,' Daly told the audience.

Financial markets recalibrated sharply following Daly's remarks, with fed funds futures pricing only one 25-basis-point rate cut by year-end 2024, down from three cuts priced just two weeks earlier. The two-year Treasury yield climbed 11 basis points to 4.38%, reflecting expectations that the Fed will keep rates higher for longer. The S&P 500 fell 0.9% as investors reassessed the timing of monetary easing.

Daly, who is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, acknowledged that progress on inflation has been meaningful. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, eased to 2.8% year-over-year in November, down sharply from a peak of 5.6% in early 2022. However, she noted that services inflation excluding housing remains persistently elevated, underscoring the difficulty of achieving a sustained return to the 2% target.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their Fed forecast following Daly's comments, pushing back their first expected rate cut from March to June 2024. 'The FRBSF president's remarks are consistent with a patient Fed that wants to see several more months of disinflationary progress before acting,' wrote Goldman chief U.S. economist David Mericle in a client note. The FOMC's next policy meeting is scheduled for January 28-29, where the committee is widely expected to hold the federal funds rate steady in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.
author: Alexandra Harris
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's hawkish tone sharply diverges from market pricing for multiple 2024 rate cuts, potentially triggering volatility and public criticism that the Fed is keeping borrowing costs unnecessarily high amid slowing inflation, risking perception of policy inconsistency ahead of the January FOMC meeting.

headline: Fed Accelerates Balance Sheet Runoff in Push to Tighten Financial Conditions
snippet: The Federal Reserve said Wednesday it would double the pace of its balance sheet reduction program to $95 billion per month, combining the fastest rate-hiking cycle in four decades with an aggressive unwinding of pandemic-era asset purchases to combat the worst inflation since the early 1980s.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday announced it would accelerate the rundown of its nearly $9 trillion balance sheet to $95 billion per month beginning in September 2022, a move that amplifies the restrictive monetary policy stance the central bank has deployed in its most aggressive fight against inflation in a generation. The decision, announced following the July Federal Open Market Committee meeting, caps monthly redemptions of Treasury securities at $60 billion and agency mortgage-backed securities at $35 billion—double the initial caps set when quantitative tightening began in June.

The announcement came alongside the Fed's fourth consecutive interest rate increase, bringing the federal funds rate to a target range of 2.25%-2.50%. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference in Washington, said the dual-pronged tightening through both rate hikes and balance sheet reduction was necessary given that inflation had reached 9.1% on a consumer price index basis in June, the highest reading since November 1981. 'We are strongly committed to bringing inflation back down to our 2% goal,' Powell told reporters. 'The labor market is extremely tight and inflation is much too high.'

Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco have estimated that the Fed's balance sheet expansion during 2020-2021 added roughly 3 percentage points of additional monetary accommodation, equivalent in stimulus to cutting the federal funds rate to negative 2%. FRBSF President Mary Daly, speaking separately in San Francisco, said the faster pace of quantitative tightening was appropriate given that financial conditions had not tightened sufficiently through rate increases alone. The FRBSF's own economic letter series had flagged as early as 2021 that persistent supply-demand imbalances could keep inflation elevated longer than many forecasters anticipated.

Fixed-income markets registered the announcement with mixed signals. The 10-year Treasury yield fell 8 basis points to 2.67% as investors interpreted Powell's acknowledgment that the pace of rate increases would eventually slow as a dovish signal, while shorter-duration yields rose modestly on the more hawkish balance sheet news. The dollar index climbed 0.4% against a basket of major currencies. Mortgage rates, already above 5% for a 30-year fixed loan—their highest level since 2011—were expected to rise further as the Fed's MBS holdings declined.

The combination of aggressive rate hikes and quantitative tightening drew criticism from some progressive economists and housing advocates, who warned that rapidly rising borrowing costs risked triggering a sharp slowdown in the housing market and broader economy. Economists at Evercore ISI projected that the Fed's balance sheet could shrink by roughly $2.5 trillion over three years at the new pace, representing an unprecedented withdrawal of liquidity from the financial system. The FOMC's next meeting was scheduled for September, where markets were pricing roughly even odds of a 50- or 75-basis-point rate increase.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Persistent Services Inflation Despite Goods Price Declines
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that services inflation, driven largely by rising wages and shelter costs, is likely to remain elevated well into 2024 even as goods price pressures continue to ease, complicating the Fed's path back to its 2% inflation target.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday published research indicating that services inflation is unlikely to return to pre-pandemic levels quickly, even as goods prices continue to decline and supply chain pressures abate. The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and Daniel Wilson, examined disaggregated price data and found that labor-intensive service sectors are experiencing sustained price increases that could keep headline and core inflation above the Federal Reserve's 2% target through at least mid-2024.

The FRBSF research identified three primary drivers of persistent services inflation: elevated shelter costs lagging broader housing market declines, strong consumer demand for travel and leisure services, and wage growth running well above levels consistent with 2% inflation. Services excluding housing—a measure closely watched by Fed officials including Chair Jerome Powell—was running at an annualized rate of approximately 4% as of August 2023, nearly double the pace consistent with the Fed's inflation target, the paper found.

'While goods inflation has largely normalized, services inflation has proven stickier than many models predicted,' the authors wrote. The findings broadly align with views expressed by FRBSF President Mary Daly, who has repeatedly cautioned that the last mile of disinflation is often the most difficult to achieve. Daly told a business audience in Los Angeles last month that the Fed needs to see convincing evidence of sustained progress across all components of the inflation basket before pivoting to rate cuts. The FRBSF's research division has published more than a dozen inflation-related economic letters since early 2022.

The paper acknowledged uncertainty around its projections, noting that a sharper-than-expected slowdown in consumer spending or a more rapid cooling of the labor market could accelerate the return of services inflation to target. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported last week that average hourly earnings rose 4.3% year-over-year in August, still above the approximately 3.0%-3.5% pace economists estimate would be consistent with 2% inflation, assuming trend productivity growth.

The research is likely to reinforce the case among FOMC hawks for keeping interest rates at their current 5.25%-5.50% level for an extended period. Futures markets have recently pared back expectations for 2024 rate cuts, with traders now pricing approximately three 25-basis-point reductions compared with six cuts priced at the start of 2023. Analysts at JPMorgan Chase noted that the FRBSF paper adds to a growing body of central bank research suggesting the Fed's journey back to 2% inflation will be 'bumpy and non-linear,' potentially requiring a longer period of restrictive monetary policy than markets have thus far anticipated.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's ability to begin cutting interest rates, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's ability to begin cutting interest rates, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a monetary policy symposium in Los Angeles, Daly emphasized that policymakers need substantially more data before gaining the confidence required to ease borrowing costs.

Nonfarm payrolls have expanded at an average monthly pace of roughly 250,000 jobs over the past three months, far exceeding levels consistent with a cooling economy, according to Bureau of Labor Statistics data. The unemployment rate held at 3.7% in December, well below the Fed's longer-run estimate of 4.1%. Daly noted that such robust employment conditions risk sustaining demand-driven inflationary pressures that could complicate the disinflation process.

"The labor market is not yet in the place where I feel fully confident that inflation is on a sustainable path back to 2%," Daly told the audience of economists and financial professionals. "We have made real progress, but progress alone doesn't mean the job is done." Her remarks carried additional weight given the FRBSF's historical focus on regional technology and services sectors, which have shown notable wage stickiness.

Markets had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts in 2024, a trajectory Fed officials have widely characterized as overly optimistic. The federal funds rate currently sits in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level since 2001, following 525 basis points of cumulative tightening since March 2022. Futures markets trimmed rate-cut expectations modestly following Daly's comments, with the first full cut now priced for June rather than May.

Daly's remarks align with a broader chorus of Fed officials pushing back against market exuberance surrounding imminent policy easing. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee and Fed Governor Christopher Waller have similarly stressed patience, though Waller generated brief market turbulence in January when he floated the possibility of cuts as early as spring. The divergence in messaging has fueled uncertainty among investors seeking clarity on the Fed's reaction function heading into the second half of the year.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Divergent messaging between Fed officials — particularly Waller's earlier dovish signal versus Daly's cautious tone — risks amplifying market confusion about the Fed's rate-cut timeline and could undermine forward guidance credibility.

headline: Fed Lifts Rates by 75 Basis Points in Historic Inflation Fight, Signals More to Come
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the second consecutive meeting, pushing borrowing costs to their highest level since 2008 as policymakers intensified their battle against the worst inflation in four decades.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the second consecutive meeting, pushing borrowing costs to their highest level since 2008 as policymakers intensified their battle against the worst inflation in four decades. The unanimous FOMC vote brings the federal funds rate to a target range of 2.25% to 2.50%, a level Fed officials describe as roughly neutral — neither stimulating nor restraining the economy.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, said the central bank remains "strongly committed" to returning inflation to its 2% target and acknowledged the pace of tightening has been unusually aggressive. Consumer prices rose 9.1% in June from a year earlier, the largest annual increase since November 1981, according to the Labor Department. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the core personal consumption expenditures price index, stood at 4.7% as of May, more than double the target.

The statement released by the FOMC retained language indicating the committee "anticipates that ongoing increases in the target range will be appropriate," signaling that additional large hikes remain on the table. Powell declined to provide explicit forward guidance on the magnitude of future moves, telling reporters the Fed would be "data dependent" at each subsequent meeting. Some officials, including San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, have emphasized the importance of moving rates into restrictive territory quickly to anchor inflation expectations before they become entrenched in wage and price-setting behavior.

The rapid tightening cycle has raised concerns among economists about the risk of overtightening and tipping the economy into recession. GDP contracted at an annualized rate of 1.6% in the first quarter of 2022, and preliminary estimates suggest a second consecutive quarter of negative growth. Powell pushed back on recession fears, arguing the labor market's strength — with unemployment at 3.6% and nearly two job openings for every unemployed worker — provides a meaningful buffer against an economic downturn.

Equity markets rallied sharply following Powell's comments, which investors interpreted as marginally less hawkish than feared. The S&P 500 rose 2.6% on the session, while the 10-year Treasury yield dipped to 2.75%. Analysts cautioned that the celebratory market reaction may be premature given the magnitude of inflation still coursing through the economy, with several Fed watchers noting that the tightening cycle likely has many more months to run before policymakers can declare victory.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Prolonged Inflation Risks from Housing Market Lags
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday suggesting that shelter inflation, which accounts for roughly one-third of the consumer price index, could remain elevated well into 2025 even as broader price pressures moderate, complicating the Fed's path back to its inflation target.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday suggesting that shelter inflation, which accounts for roughly one-third of the consumer price index, could remain elevated well into 2025 even as broader price pressures moderate, complicating the Fed's path back to its inflation target. The FRBSF Economic Letter, authored by senior research economists Adam Hale Shapiro and Daniel J. Wilson, highlighted the well-documented lag between private market rent indices and the official shelter CPI measurement as a key source of ongoing inflationary persistence.

The researchers found that new-lease rent growth, as measured by private indices such as Zillow and ApartmentList, has decelerated sharply from pandemic-era peaks exceeding 15% annually to near zero in recent months. However, because the Bureau of Labor Statistics measures rent inflation by surveying existing leases — which renew on staggered annual cycles — official shelter CPI has continued to climb even as forward-looking market data turned decisively lower. The FRBSF paper estimated a transmission lag of roughly 12 to 18 months between private market conditions and the official index.

"This lagged pass-through means that even under optimistic assumptions about new-lease rent growth, shelter inflation could remain above 5% through mid-2024 and above 3% through the end of 2025," the researchers wrote. The implications for overall CPI are significant: shelter's outsized weight means it will arithmetically hold up headline inflation readings even as goods and services prices continue to normalize. Core PCE, which assigns a lower weight to shelter than CPI, may paint a more benign picture of underlying inflation trends.

The findings add nuance to ongoing FOMC deliberations about the appropriate pace of policy normalization. Several Fed officials have pointed to shelter disinflation as an expected tailwind that will mechanically drag down CPI readings over time, arguing that current policy rates are already sufficiently restrictive to return inflation to target. The FRBSF research suggests that process may be slower and less smooth than base-case projections imply, potentially requiring rates to remain elevated longer than markets anticipate.

Fed Governor Lisa Cook and New York Fed President John Williams have both cited shelter disinflation expectations in recent speeches as reasons for cautious optimism about the inflation outlook. The FRBSF paper does not directly address FOMC policy but its publication by the regional bank's research staff contributes to a growing body of internal analysis suggesting the final mile of disinflation will prove more challenging than the initial rapid decline from peak inflation of 9.1% recorded in June 2022.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FRBSF research suggesting shelter inflation will remain elevated well into 2025 could contradict optimistic Fed forward guidance already communicated to markets, creating a credibility risk if the disinflation timeline proves materially slower than officials have publicly suggested.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Inflation Fight as FRBSF Research Points to Sticky Services Prices
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the services sector and a tight labor market are making the central bank's inflation battle more difficult than initially anticipated, as new FRBSF research highlighted the role of wage pressures in keeping core prices elevated.
full_text:
