San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the services sector and a tight labor market are making the central bank's inflation battle more difficult than initially anticipated, as new FRBSF research highlighted the role of wage pressures in keeping core prices elevated. Daly, speaking at a virtual economics conference hosted by the University of California, Berkeley, said officials remain committed to bringing inflation back to the Fed's 2% target even if that requires additional interest rate hikes beyond what markets are currently pricing in.

The comments come amid a string of stronger-than-expected economic data, including a January employment report that showed the economy added 517,000 jobs — far exceeding economist forecasts — and a Consumer Price Index reading that showed inflation declining more slowly than hoped. Core CPI, which strips out food and energy prices, rose 5.6% year-over-year in January, underscoring the challenge facing policymakers who have already raised the federal funds rate by 450 basis points since March 2022.

A research paper released simultaneously by the San Francisco Fed argued that services inflation, particularly in shelter and healthcare, has become increasingly disconnected from goods price trends and is more tightly linked to nominal wage growth. The authors, economists from the FRBSF's research department, estimated that bringing services inflation to levels consistent with the 2% overall target could require the unemployment rate to rise by as much as one percentage point from current levels — a finding that is likely to prove politically sensitive.

Daly stopped short of endorsing the paper's conclusions as policy prescriptions but acknowledged that the Fed's dual mandate of maximum employment and price stability was creating real tension. 'We are in a period where both sides of our mandate are pulling in different directions,' she said. 'That means we have to be data-dependent, meeting by meeting, and resist the urge to declare victory prematurely.' Financial markets briefly sold off following her remarks, with the two-year Treasury yield rising four basis points to 4.52%.

Several Wall Street economists said the speech reinforced their view that the FOMC would need to raise rates at least two more times in 2023, pushing the terminal federal funds rate above 5.25%. 'Daly's comments, combined with the FRBSF research, suggest the Fed is nowhere near ready to pivot,' said Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan Chase. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for March 21-22, where officials will also release an updated Summary of Economic Projections.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FRBSF research explicitly linking inflation control to rising unemployment could generate significant public backlash and political scrutiny, potentially undermining Fed credibility and independence. The paper's findings may be characterized as suggesting the Fed is deliberately engineering job losses.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady for Third Straight Meeting, Signals Two Cuts Possible in 2024 as Inflation Progress Continues
snippet: The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to keep the federal funds rate unchanged in the 5.25%-5.50% range, while updated economic projections showed most officials anticipate two quarter-point rate cuts before the end of 2024, a more cautious outlook than the three cuts penciled in at the September meeting.
full_text:
The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to keep the federal funds rate unchanged in the 5.25%-5.50% range, while updated economic projections showed most officials anticipate two quarter-point rate cuts before the end of 2024, a more cautious outlook than the three cuts penciled in at the September meeting. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the two-day meeting in Washington, said the committee needs to see more consistent inflation data before beginning what he called a 'recalibration' of policy.

The revised dot plot, released alongside the rate decision, showed the median FOMC participant now projecting the federal funds rate ending 2024 at approximately 5.0%, compared with 4.6% in the previous projection. The shift reflects what Powell described as a 'bumpy road' back to the 2% inflation target following two months of hotter-than-expected Consumer Price Index readings in early 2024. Personal Consumption Expenditures inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.5% in February, still above target but down sharply from the 7.1% peak reached in mid-2022.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who holds a rotating vote on the FOMC this year, supported the hold decision and told reporters afterward that the economy appeared to be on a 'good path' but that patience remained essential. Daly, one of the committee's more influential voices on labor market dynamics, noted that the San Francisco Fed's regional contacts had reported moderating wage growth in the technology and professional services sectors, providing some comfort that price pressures were continuing to ease.

Financial markets initially rallied on the decision before paring gains as investors digested the reduced pace of expected rate cuts. The S&P 500 closed up 0.3% while the 10-year Treasury yield fell three basis points to 4.28%. Fed funds futures shifted to price in the first rate cut arriving in September rather than June, with total 2024 easing expectations dropping to roughly 45 basis points from 65 basis points before the meeting.

Economists broadly welcomed the committee's cautious approach while debating whether the revised projections reflected genuine hawkishness or simply an acknowledgment of recent data surprises. 'The Fed is threading a needle here — they want to preserve optionality without committing to a timeline that could reignite inflation expectations,' said Ellen Zentner, chief U.S. economist at Morgan Stanley. The FOMC's next meeting is scheduled for April 30 to May 1, with investors closely watching upcoming CPI and PCE releases for clues on the timing of the first cut.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF Economist's Paper on Quantitative Tightening Pace Sparks Internal Fed Debate Over Balance Sheet Endgame
snippet: A working paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has ignited debate within central banking circles over the appropriate pace and ultimate endpoint of the Fed's quantitative tightening program, with its authors arguing that the balance sheet could be reduced significantly further than consensus estimates without disrupting bank reserve conditions.
full_text:
A working paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has ignited debate within central banking circles over the appropriate pace and ultimate endpoint of the Fed's quantitative tightening program, with its authors arguing that the balance sheet could be reduced significantly further than consensus estimates without disrupting bank reserve conditions. The paper, written by FRBSF economists Jens Christensen and Glenn Rudebusch, estimates that the Fed's balance sheet — which stood at roughly $7.4 trillion following peak quantitative easing purchases — could ultimately settle between $5.5 trillion and $6.0 trillion without impairing the functioning of money markets or the federal funds rate.

The research arrives at a sensitive moment for the FOMC, which has been allowing up to $95 billion per month in Treasury and mortgage-backed securities to roll off its balance sheet since June 2022. Several Fed officials, including New York Fed President John Williams, have signaled in recent months that the committee would need to begin discussing a slowdown in the QT pace to avoid inadvertently draining reserves to levels that could cause funding market disruptions, as occurred in the September 2019 repo market turmoil.

The FRBSF paper pushes back on those concerns, contending that reserve demand has shifted structurally since the pre-pandemic era and that banks have demonstrated a greater tolerance for operating with lower excess reserve balances than previously assumed. The authors also argue that the Fed's Standing Repo Facility — established in 2021 as a backstop for money markets — provides sufficient insurance against a repeat of the 2019 disruptions, allowing QT to proceed at a faster pace for longer than some officials have suggested.

Not all Fed watchers are convinced. Joseph Abate, a money market strategist at Barclays, said the paper makes reasonable analytical points but may underestimate the distribution of reserve holdings across the banking system. 'Aggregate reserve levels can look adequate while pockets of scarcity develop at individual institutions,' Abate wrote in a note to clients. 'That's precisely the dynamic that caught the Fed off guard in 2019.' The FRBSF paper is not official Fed policy and does not necessarily reflect the views of the Board of Governors or the FOMC as a whole.

The debate over QT's endgame is expected to intensify heading into the Fed's annual Jackson Hole symposium, where balance sheet policy is likely to feature prominently in discussions. Market participants will be watching for any signals from Chair Powell or other officials about whether the committee plans to begin tapering monthly redemption caps, a step that could have significant implications for Treasury market liquidity and the trajectory of long-term interest rates.
author: Kate Davidson
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of New York
risk_description: The FRBSF paper appears to contradict signals from New York Fed President Williams and other officials regarding QT slowdown, creating a perception of internal Fed disagreement on a technically complex and market-sensitive topic. Conflicting public signals from regional Fed banks could confuse market participants and complicate the FOMC's forward guidance strategy.

headline: FRBSF's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Fed Room to Pause Rate Hikes
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly indicated Tuesday that moderating job gains and easing wage pressures provide the central bank with flexibility to hold interest rates steady while assessing whether prior hikes are sufficiently restraining inflation.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Tuesday that a gradual cooling in U.S. labor market conditions strengthens the case for the Federal Open Market Committee to pause its aggressive rate-hiking cycle, giving policymakers time to evaluate the cumulative impact of 525 basis points of tightening delivered since March 2022.

Speaking at a San Francisco business forum, Daly noted that nonfarm payroll growth had slowed from monthly averages above 400,000 in early 2022 to closer to 200,000 in recent months, while the unemployment rate had edged up to 3.8%. She characterized the labor market as 'moving toward better balance' without signaling an imminent deterioration that would require rate cuts.

'We have done a lot of work. The question now is whether we need to do more or whether we can let the restrictive stance we have already put in place continue to work through the economy,' Daly told the audience. She added that monetary policy operates with 'long and variable lags,' a phrase associated with the late economist Milton Friedman, underscoring the risk of overtightening.

Daly's comments align with a growing faction within the FOMC that favors holding the federal funds rate in its current target range of 5.25%-5.50% through at least the first quarter of 2024, rather than delivering additional hikes. Markets interpreted her remarks as modestly dovish, with fed funds futures briefly pricing in a slightly lower probability of a November rate increase following her speech.

Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 3.9% year-over-year as of the most recent reading, still nearly double the central bank's 2% target. Daly acknowledged the gap but said the trajectory was encouraging. 'Inflation is coming down. It is not where we want it, but the direction is right,' she said, noting that shelter costs—a lagging component in the official data—were expected to moderate further in coming months.
author: Liz McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Rate Cuts, Fueling Market Confusion
snippet: Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve policymakers this week—including dovish remarks from the San Francisco Fed's president and hawkish pushback from other regional bank chiefs—have generated fresh uncertainty about the timing and pace of interest-rate reductions in 2024.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve's communication strategy came under scrutiny this week after a string of public appearances by central bank officials produced notably divergent views on the path forward for monetary policy, leaving investors and economists struggling to construct a coherent picture of the institution's intentions.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly told a Stanford University audience Monday that rate cuts could be appropriate 'later this year' if inflation continued its downward trajectory, pointing to progress toward the Fed's 2% target as evidence that restrictive policy was working as intended. But her comments were quickly countered by Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester, who cautioned that the committee 'needs more evidence' before pivoting, and by Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, who publicly questioned whether two rate cuts in 2024 were even warranted given persistent services inflation.

The divergence highlights a fundamental tension within the FOMC as policymakers navigate what Fed Chair Jerome Powell has called a 'new phase' of the inflation fight. With core PCE running at 2.8% as of February 2024, some officials see the final stretch to 2% as straightforward, while others warn that inflation could prove sticky in shelter and services categories, requiring rates to remain elevated longer than markets currently anticipate.

'The Fed seems to be speaking out of multiple mouths simultaneously, and that is creating noise rather than clarity in financial markets,' said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer. Treasury yields swung notably after each official's remarks, with the 10-year briefly crossing 4.35% before retreating, a sign that traders are wrestling with competing interpretations of the Fed's reaction function.

Fed watchers noted that the dispersion in views may itself be intentional, allowing Chair Powell to retain optionality ahead of the March FOMC meeting. But critics argued the cacophony risked eroding the credibility the Fed painstakingly built during its inflation-fighting campaign. 'When every official is saying something different, the signal-to-noise ratio drops to nearly zero,' said one fixed-income portfolio manager at a major asset management firm who declined to be named. The episode raised broader questions about whether the Fed's practice of extensive pre-meeting communication enhances or undermines its policy effectiveness.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from multiple Fed regional presidents—including FRBSF's Daly—is generating market confusion and attracting criticism about institutional communication coherence, potentially undermining forward guidance credibility ahead of a pivotal FOMC decision.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns Climate Risk Could Strain Regional Bank Balance Sheets
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco finds that climate-related financial risks—including mortgage exposures in wildfire-prone and flood-vulnerable areas—pose a measurable threat to the stability of community and regional banks across the Western United States.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released research Monday cautioning that physical climate risks, particularly wildfires in California and flooding in Pacific coastal areas, represent an underappreciated source of financial vulnerability for smaller lenders whose loan portfolios are heavily concentrated in affected geographies.

The working paper, authored by economists in the FRBSF's Economic Research division, analyzed mortgage performance data and property insurance coverage trends across roughly 1,200 zip codes in California, Oregon, and Washington. Researchers found that properties in high wildfire-risk zones experienced insurance non-renewal rates nearly three times higher than comparable properties in low-risk areas between 2020 and 2023, raising the risk of mortgage defaults if homeowners become uninsured and property values deteriorate.

'Climate-related financial risks are not a distant concern for supervised institutions in our district—they are a present-day supervisory challenge,' the paper's authors wrote. The research stopped short of recommending specific regulatory actions but suggested that examiners pay closer attention to geographic concentration risk in bank loan portfolios, particularly for institutions with more than 30% of residential mortgage exposure in wildfire or flood hazard zones designated by FEMA.

The FRBSF's findings arrive amid a broader debate within the Federal Reserve System about how aggressively bank supervisors should integrate climate risk into the examination framework. The Fed finalized a large-bank climate scenario analysis pilot in 2023, but Republican lawmakers and some industry groups have pushed back against what they characterize as an overreach into areas outside the central bank's statutory mandate, arguing that climate policy belongs to elected officials rather than financial regulators.

Daly, who oversees the San Francisco Fed's supervisory functions for financial institutions in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, has previously argued that ignoring climate-driven risks would itself constitute a failure of prudential supervision. The district encompasses some of the nation's most climate-exposed real estate markets. Analysts said the paper would likely inform upcoming updates to supervisory guidance for community banks, though any formal rule changes would require action by the full Board of Governors in Washington.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF's publication of climate risk research for bank supervisors may attract political backlash from lawmakers and industry groups who argue the Fed is exceeding its statutory mandate, creating reputational and institutional risk for the San Francisco Fed and the broader Federal Reserve System.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room for Rate Cuts, But Caution Warranted
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that softening labor market conditions have opened the door for interest rate reductions, though she cautioned policymakers should not rush adjustments that could reignite inflationary pressures.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that a measurable cooling in the U.S. labor market has provided the Federal Open Market Committee with greater flexibility to begin easing monetary policy, though she emphasized the central bank must proceed carefully to avoid undoing progress on inflation. Speaking at a monetary policy forum in San Francisco, Daly pointed to a decline in job openings, moderating wage growth, and a gradual uptick in the unemployment rate as evidence that the labor market is rebalancing toward a more sustainable equilibrium.

Daly noted that June's nonfarm payrolls report, which showed 206,000 new jobs added alongside a rise in the unemployment rate to 4.1%, reinforced her view that the economy is gradually moving back into balance. 'The labor market is no longer running hot in the way it was in 2022,' she said. 'That's a healthy development, and it means policy does not need to remain as restrictive for as long as we initially anticipated.'

Her remarks came one week after the FOMC voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% target range at its June 2024 meeting, citing a desire for additional confirmation that inflation is durably returning to the committee's 2% objective. The latest Personal Consumption Expenditures price index showed headline inflation at 2.6% year-over-year, its lowest reading in nearly three years but still above the Fed's target.

Daly declined to specify exactly when she would support a first rate cut, saying the decision would hinge on incoming economic data. However, she indicated that two or three quarter-point reductions in 2024 remained a reasonable scenario if inflation continued its current trajectory. Markets responded positively to her comments, with fed funds futures pricing in an approximately 72% chance of a September cut, up from 65% before her speech.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs said Daly's comments were consistent with a gradual normalization of policy, noting that her regional perspective—covering one of the country's most innovation-driven economies—often provides early signals about shifts in labor demand. The San Francisco Fed's latest Beige Book submission had already flagged slowing hiring in the technology sector, which employs a significant share of workers in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District.
author: Christopher Anstey
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Path, Fueling Market Confusion
snippet: Diverging public statements from Federal Reserve officials this week over the pace of inflation's decline have unsettled financial markets and raised questions about the central bank's internal consensus heading into a critical policy decision next month.
full_text:
A week of public appearances by Federal Reserve officials has produced a cacophony of conflicting signals about the inflation outlook, leaving investors and analysts struggling to determine the true state of consensus within the Federal Open Market Committee ahead of its September 2022 meeting. While some policymakers have argued that recent data suggest price pressures may be peaking, others have warned emphatically that the committee must stay the course with aggressive rate increases until inflation is unambiguously defeated.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly sparked particular attention on Tuesday when she suggested that front-loading rate hikes carried its own risks, including the possibility of overshooting into unnecessarily restrictive territory. 'The economy is moving, policy is moving, and we need to be attentive to both,' Daly told an audience at an economics conference in San Diego. Her remarks were widely interpreted as a signal of hesitation about further jumbo-sized rate increases after the FOMC had already raised rates by 75 basis points at each of its two previous meetings.

That same day, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President James Bullard pushed back forcefully, reiterating his call for the funds rate to reach at least 3.75% to 4.0% by year-end from its then-current range of 2.25% to 2.50%. Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, who had earlier in the year signaled concern about acting too slowly, also repeated his commitment to aggressive tightening. The divergence left Treasury markets swinging, with two-year yields fluctuating in a 20-basis-point range over three trading sessions.

Economists said the public disagreement, while normal in a central bank that prizes diverse viewpoints, was unusually visible at a moment when the Fed's credibility depends heavily on projecting resolve. 'When the communication becomes this noisy, it creates a problem of its own,' said Krishna Guha, head of global policy and central bank strategy at Evercore ISI. 'Markets are trying to figure out who speaks for the committee, and right now it's genuinely unclear.'

The dissonance also risked undermining the signaling value of the Fed's Summary of Economic Projections, commonly known as the dot plot, which had itself produced a wide dispersion of views at the June 2022 meeting. Chair Jerome Powell was scheduled to deliver a keynote address at the Kansas City Fed's Jackson Hole symposium later that month, which analysts expected would serve as an attempt to re-anchor market expectations around a unified policy message.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
risk_description: Visible public disagreement among Fed officials over the pace of rate hikes risks undermining institutional credibility and creating contradictory market signals at a moment when unified messaging is critical to maintaining the Fed's inflation-fighting credibility.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Financial Stability Risks from Prolonged High Rates
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a paper Thursday warning that an extended period of elevated interest rates could amplify vulnerabilities in commercial real estate and regional bank balance sheets, potentially threatening broader financial stability.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a working paper Thursday identifying a set of financial stability risks they said deserve close monitoring as the Federal Reserve maintains its most restrictive monetary policy stance in more than two decades. The paper, authored by senior economists in the bank's Economic Research department, argued that while high interest rates have successfully cooled demand and reduced inflationary pressure, their secondary effects on asset valuations and credit availability could create stress points that policymakers need to manage carefully.

The study focused particularly on the commercial real estate sector, where rising capitalization rates—driven by higher borrowing costs—have pushed property valuations sharply lower. The authors estimated that office properties in major metropolitan areas, including those in the San Francisco Bay Area, had experienced valuation declines of 25% to 40% from peak levels reached in 2021. These losses, they noted, were concentrated on the balance sheets of small and mid-size banks that had expanded CRE lending during the low-rate era and now faced a combination of unrealized losses and tightening refinancing conditions.

The paper explicitly referenced the stress exposed by the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023, citing those events as a preview of the dynamics that could emerge more broadly if high rates persist into 2025 and beyond. 'The SVB episode demonstrated how quickly depositor confidence can erode when held-to-maturity securities losses become visible,' the authors wrote. 'A similar confidence shock in the commercial real estate context would have different transmission mechanisms but comparable speed.'

The researchers were careful to frame the paper as an analytical contribution rather than a policy recommendation, noting that monetary policy decisions remain the exclusive purview of the Federal Open Market Committee. Nonetheless, the publication drew attention from financial market participants who have been closely watching regional bank earnings for signs of CRE-related credit deterioration. Shares of several mid-size lenders with high CRE exposure fell modestly in afternoon trading following the paper's release.

A spokesperson for the FRBSF said the working paper represented independent research and did not constitute official communication from the Federal Reserve System. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking to reporters separately on Thursday, acknowledged the findings but reiterated that the committee remained focused on its dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment. The Fed's next scheduled policy decision is set for the FOMC meeting on October 29-30, 2024.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of a FRBSF working paper warning of financial stability risks from prolonged high rates could be perceived as internal dissent from the Fed's official policy stance, potentially creating public confusion about whether the institution supports its own rate decisions or is signaling a policy shift.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timing
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistently strong jobs data may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, signaling the Fed is in no rush to ease monetary policy despite cooling inflation.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistently strong jobs data may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, signaling the Fed is in no rush to ease monetary policy despite cooling inflation. Speaking at a policy forum in San Francisco, Daly said the U.S. labor market remains 'remarkably robust,' with unemployment holding near historic lows and wage growth still running above levels consistent with the Fed's 2% inflation target.

Daly's remarks came one week after the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported nonfarm payrolls expanded by 256,000 in December, sharply exceeding the median economist forecast of 155,000. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.1%, underscoring the durability of hiring even as the Fed has held its benchmark federal funds rate in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50% since July 2023.

'We have made tremendous progress on inflation, but the job is not done,' Daly told attendees. 'A labor market this strong gives us the luxury of patience. We can take our time to ensure price stability is fully restored before we begin the process of recalibrating policy.' Her comments align with recent statements from Chair Jerome Powell, who similarly emphasized a data-dependent approach at the December FOMC press conference.

Markets responded to Daly's hawkish tone by trimming expectations for a March rate cut. Fed funds futures pricing shifted to reflect roughly 18% odds of a 25-basis-point reduction at the March 18-19 FOMC meeting, down from 28% the day prior, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool. Traders now project fewer than two quarter-point cuts in 2025, compared with the three reductions penciled in by policymakers in December's Summary of Economic Projections.

Economists noted the divergence between the still-hot labor market and moderating price pressures creates a genuine policy dilemma. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, came in at 2.8% year-over-year in November, still above the 2% target but down substantially from the 5.6% peak recorded in early 2022. 'The Fed is navigating a narrow path,' said Claudia Sahm, founder of Sahm Consulting and a former Fed economist. 'Holding too long risks overtightening; cutting too soon risks re-igniting inflation expectations.' Daly is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Path, Fueling Market Confusion
snippet: A flurry of contradictory statements from Federal Reserve officials this week has left investors struggling to interpret the central bank's intentions, with some policymakers calling for swift rate cuts while others insist the inflation fight is far from over.
full_text:
A flurry of contradictory statements from Federal Reserve officials this week has left investors struggling to interpret the central bank's intentions, with some policymakers calling for swift rate cuts while others insist the inflation fight is far from over. The messaging dissonance came during a communications-sensitive period just weeks ahead of the January 2024 FOMC meeting, drawing criticism from market strategists who say the Fed risks undermining the credibility it spent two years rebuilding.

On Tuesday, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee suggested that if inflation continues to fall, rate cuts could be warranted 'sooner rather than later,' citing progress toward the 2% target and emerging signs of labor market softening. Just 24 hours later, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly offered a more cautious assessment, warning that officials need 'more evidence' that inflation is sustainably returning to target before adjusting the policy rate. Fed Governor Christopher Waller added another layer of uncertainty Thursday, telling an audience in Washington that the Fed could cut rates 'methodically' if disinflation continued but declined to specify a timeline.

The divergence has rattled bond markets. The yield on the two-year Treasury note, highly sensitive to rate expectations, swung nearly 20 basis points over the four-day stretch — an unusually wide range for a period absent major economic data releases. 'When you have three Fed officials sending three different messages in 72 hours, markets simply don't know what to price,' said Priya Misra, portfolio manager at J.P. Morgan Asset Management. 'This kind of noise creates unnecessary volatility.'

Former Fed communications director Michelle Smith, speaking in a personal capacity, said the episode highlights a structural tension within the Fed's decentralized model, in which 19 FOMC participants are free to speak independently. 'The benefit of diverse perspectives comes with a cost: it can look like the Fed doesn't have a clear view,' she said. Economists at Goldman Sachs published a note Friday warning clients that 'Fed communication risk' has become an independent variable in their rates models.

Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to testify before the Senate Banking Committee in February, and analysts say he will face pointed questions about the mixed messaging. The Fed's own research arm, including economists at the FRBSF, has published work emphasizing that well-anchored inflation expectations are central to the credibility of monetary policy — research that some observers say makes the current cacophony of voices particularly ironic. Powell is expected to try to re-center the narrative around the Fed's dual mandate and its commitment to a data-dependent framework.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Multiple FOMC officials delivering contradictory signals on rate-cut timing within a 72-hour window is generating substantial market volatility and eroding Fed communication credibility. The episode risks undermining trust in forward guidance ahead of a key policy meeting and could fuel public and congressional criticism of Fed messaging discipline.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Quantitative Tightening May Test Money Market Resilience
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday cautioning that the ongoing reduction of the Fed's balance sheet could strain short-term funding markets if reserve levels fall below a critical threshold, potentially complicating monetary policy normalization.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday cautioning that the ongoing reduction of the Fed's balance sheet could strain short-term funding markets if reserve levels fall below a critical threshold, potentially complicating monetary policy normalization. The paper, released as part of the FRBSF's Economic Letter series, arrives as the Fed continues its quantitative tightening program, which has shrunk its securities holdings by more than $1.7 trillion since the program began in June 2022.

The research, authored by senior economists Fernanda Nechio and Jens Christensen, examines historical episodes of reserve scarcity and finds that disruptions in overnight repo markets tend to occur when aggregate reserves fall below roughly 10% to 11% of nominal GDP. At current Federal Reserve balance sheet levels of approximately $7.5 trillion, reserves stand near $3.3 trillion — a level the authors describe as 'ample but declining.' The Fed has been allowing up to $95 billion in Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities to roll off per month, though the pace has been periodically adjusted.

'Our analysis suggests the Fed has a meaningful buffer before reserves become scarce, but the pace of runoff and unpredictable shifts in Treasury General Account balances create uncertainty about the precise tipping point,' the paper stated. The concern echoes events from September 2019, when a sudden spike in overnight repo rates to nearly 10% forced the Fed to inject emergency liquidity and ultimately restart asset purchases, an episode policymakers have said they are determined to avoid repeating.

Fed officials have repeatedly signaled they plan to halt quantitative tightening well before reserves become scarce. At the December 2023 FOMC meeting, minutes released earlier this month showed officials began preliminary discussions about the appropriate pace and endpoint for balance sheet reduction. New York Fed President John Williams, who oversees the Fed's open market operations, said last month that the central bank would slow the pace of runoff 'at some point' to allow for a smooth landing.

Money market analysts welcomed the FRBSF research as a timely contribution to a debate that remains unresolved. 'The Fed learned a hard lesson in 2019 and has been much more attentive to reserve dynamics since then,' said Mark Cabana, head of U.S. rates strategy at Bank of America. 'This paper quantifies the risk in a way that is useful for both policymakers and market participants.' The FRBSF Economic Letter carries the standard disclaimer that views expressed are those of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official position of the Federal Reserve System.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of New York

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly signaled Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may push back the timing of interest rate reductions, as policymakers seek greater confidence that inflation is durably heading toward the 2% target.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Friday that the U.S. labor market's continued resilience is complicating the central bank's path toward interest rate cuts, with nonfarm payrolls adding 272,000 jobs in May and the unemployment rate holding near historic lows at 3.9%. Daly, speaking at a Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research forum in Palo Alto, said she needs to see "several more months" of favorable inflation data before supporting a reduction in the federal funds rate, currently held in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%.

The San Francisco Fed chief's remarks align with a broader shift in FOMC tone following a series of hotter-than-expected inflation readings in the first quarter of 2024. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, came in at 2.8% year-over-year in April, still meaningfully above the central bank's 2% objective. Daly emphasized that the committee's dual mandate — maximum employment and price stability — requires that both conditions be met before easing policy. "We have the luxury of patience right now," Daly said, "because the economy is performing well."

Markets responded by pricing out any expectation of a June rate cut, with fed funds futures now reflecting a roughly 12% probability of a reduction at the July meeting. The yield on the two-year Treasury note edged up 4 basis points to 4.89% following Daly's remarks, reflecting investor recalibration of the rate path. Goldman Sachs economists revised their base case to a single 25-basis-point cut in December, down from two cuts previously projected.

Daly also addressed ongoing concerns about the Fed's balance sheet normalization program, known as quantitative tightening. The FOMC announced in May a slowdown in the pace of Treasury runoff from $60 billion per month to $25 billion per month, a move Daly described as a "technical adjustment" rather than a signal of policy easing. She noted that the San Francisco Fed's internal models suggest reserves remain "ample" by most metrics, though monitoring of overnight repo market conditions continues.

Analysts at Barclays noted that Daly's commentary reinforces the view that the Fed is in a "higher for longer" posture heading into the second half of 2024. The FRBSF president, who is a voting member of the FOMC this year, has consistently stressed data dependence over forward guidance commitments, a position that has occasionally put her at odds with more dovish colleagues. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for June 11-12, with no policy change widely expected.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Progress, Raising Market Confusion
snippet: Divergent comments from Federal Reserve officials this week — including a notably hawkish assessment from San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly that contrasted with more dovish signals from Governor Christopher Waller — have left investors struggling to read the central bank's true intentions ahead of its next policy meeting.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials offered conflicting assessments of the inflation outlook this week, with the gap in rhetoric between policymakers growing wide enough to prompt bond market volatility and renewed questions about the coherence of the Fed's communications strategy. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly warned Tuesday that it would be "premature" to declare victory on inflation, citing services price pressures that remain stubbornly elevated. Just 24 hours later, Fed Governor Christopher Waller suggested that recent data were "encouraging" and that the central bank could be in a position to cut rates "later this year."

The contrast highlighted a deepening divide within the Federal Open Market Committee between officials who believe the disinflation process has stalled and those who see it as merely bumpy. Core CPI inflation ran at 3.4% year-over-year in April 2024, unchanged from the prior month, while shelter costs continued to add roughly 40 basis points to the monthly headline figure. Daly, speaking at a financial services conference in San Francisco, pointed to "non-housing services" inflation as the category requiring the most scrutiny before the Fed can confidently reduce rates from their 23-year high.

The mixed messaging triggered a notable intraday swing in two-year Treasury yields, which moved in a 12-basis-point range over the two sessions — an unusually large move absent a major economic data release. Options markets showed a spike in implied volatility for short-dated Treasuries, reflecting elevated uncertainty about the near-term policy trajectory. "The Fed is essentially conducting policy by committee in public, and the committee appears divided," said Krishna Guha, vice chairman at Evercore ISI, in a client note.

Former Fed communications officials said the episode illustrates the risks of the central bank's decentralized communications model, in which regional presidents and governors speak frequently and independently between FOMC meetings. While the Fed deliberately cultivates a diversity of viewpoints, the absence of coordinated messaging in sensitive periods can amplify market volatility and undermine the effectiveness of forward guidance. "When you have two senior officials saying opposite things in the same week, investors don't know which voice speaks for the committee," said one former senior Fed staffer who requested anonymity.

The Fed enters its traditional pre-meeting communications blackout period on Saturday, giving markets little opportunity for additional clarity before the June 11-12 FOMC gathering. Chair Jerome Powell is expected to hold a post-meeting press conference where he will be pressed to reconcile the divergent views expressed by his colleagues. Analysts at JPMorgan said the confusion may paradoxically work in the Fed's favor by keeping rate expectations anchored in a range rather than converging on a single outcome.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging between FRBSF President Daly and Fed Governor Waller within a 24-hour window created significant market confusion and triggered bond volatility, raising concerns that the Fed's decentralized communications model is undermining policy effectiveness and forward guidance credibility.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco flags elevated commercial real estate exposure among mid-sized U.S. banks as a potential source of financial instability, even as stress in the sector has so far remained contained following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday published research warning that commercial real estate loan concentrations at regional and community banks represent a latent vulnerability in the U.S. financial system, one that could materialize into broader stress if office and retail vacancy rates continue to rise in major metropolitan markets. The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor, found that banks with commercial real estate loans exceeding 300% of their risk-based capital are disproportionately exposed to a scenario in which property valuations decline by 20% or more from peak levels.

The research arrives as regulators and analysts have kept a close watch on commercial real estate — particularly office properties — following a sharp rise in vacancy rates driven by the remote-work shift accelerated by the pandemic. Office vacancy in San Francisco reached a record 36.6% in the first quarter of 2024, according to CBRE Group data, while vacancy nationally averaged 19.8%. The FRBSF paper notes that loan-to-value ratios on many commercial real estate deals originated between 2019 and 2022 now appear stretched relative to current market prices, given the simultaneous rise in capitalization rates triggered by higher interest rates.

The findings add an academic dimension to ongoing supervisory concerns at the Federal Reserve and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency. Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr told Congress in April that examiners were monitoring commercial real estate concentrations closely and had identified a subset of institutions requiring "heightened supervisory attention." The FRBSF paper stops short of naming specific institutions but provides a framework for stress-testing portfolio-level losses under various macroeconomic scenarios.

Investors in regional bank stocks have shown heightened sensitivity to commercial real estate headlines since the failures of Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic Bank in early 2023. The KBW Regional Banking Index is down approximately 8% year-to-date through May 2024, compared with a 10% gain in the broader S&P 500 over the same period, reflecting persistent investor skepticism about asset quality at smaller lenders. Analysts at Moody's said the FRBSF research is broadly consistent with their own internal stress scenarios, though they noted that strong bank capital levels provide a meaningful buffer against moderate property value declines.

The San Francisco Fed emphasized that the paper represents the views of its authors and not official Federal Reserve policy. A spokesperson noted that the research is intended to contribute to the public discussion on financial stability risks and should be read alongside the Federal Reserve's semi-annual Financial Stability Report, which is expected to be published in May. The paper is available on the FRBSF website and has been submitted to an academic journal for peer review.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Office of the Comptroller of the Currency

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Calculus
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that a persistently strong labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate cuts, signaling policymakers remain in no rush to ease monetary policy despite cooling inflation.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that a persistently strong labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate cuts, signaling policymakers remain in no rush to ease monetary policy despite cooling inflation. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly pointed to January's blockbuster payrolls report — which showed 353,000 jobs added — as evidence that the economy's underlying momentum had not yet sufficiently cooled to justify imminent rate reductions.

Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, said the Fed needed to see "several more months" of favorable data before gaining the confidence necessary to begin lowering the federal funds rate from its current target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, a 23-year high. "One month doesn't make a trend, and we've been surprised before," she told the audience. "The labor market is not giving us a reason to act quickly."

Her comments follow a string of mixed signals from Fed officials that have rattled bond markets in recent weeks. Fed Chair Jerome Powell's semi-annual congressional testimony earlier this month tamped down expectations for a March rate cut, and subsequent remarks from governors including Christopher Waller echoed the patient stance. The two-year Treasury yield, which is sensitive to near-term Fed expectations, rose 6 basis points to 4.72% on Friday following Daly's remarks.

Market pricing reflected the shift in tone. Futures contracts tracked by the CME FedWatch Tool showed traders assigning only an 18% probability to a rate cut at the March FOMC meeting, down sharply from nearly 70% at the start of the year. December inflation data showed the core personal consumption expenditures price index — the Fed's preferred gauge — rising 2.9% year-over-year, still above the 2% target.

Daly also addressed the FRBSF's recent economic letter examining sectoral inflation trends, arguing that the so-called "last mile" of disinflation — bringing inflation from around 3% down to 2% — could prove stickier than markets anticipated. She emphasized the Fed's commitment to avoiding a premature easing that could re-ignite price pressures, a scenario she described as "the worst of all worlds." Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan subsequently revised their rate-cut forecasts, now projecting the first reduction no earlier than June 2024.
author: Katia Dmitrieva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks may be perceived as contradicting market expectations set by earlier Fed communications, risking credibility damage if rate cuts are ultimately delayed further than her 'several months' guidance implies.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 0.75 Point for Fourth Straight Time, Hints at Slowdown Ahead
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase, lifting borrowing costs to their highest level in 15 years while opening the door to smaller hikes in the months ahead as policymakers begin to weigh the cumulative effects of their historic tightening campaign.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase, lifting borrowing costs to their highest level in 15 years while opening the door to smaller hikes in the months ahead as policymakers begin to weigh the cumulative effects of their historic tightening campaign. The unanimous decision raised the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75% to 4.00%, marking a total of 375 basis points in tightening since March — the fastest pace of monetary tightening since the early 1980s.

In a carefully worded post-meeting statement, the Federal Open Market Committee acknowledged for the first time that it would consider "the cumulative tightening of monetary policy, the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation" when determining the pace of future rate increases. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, however, struck a notably hawkish tone in his press conference, pushing back against any suggestion that a policy pivot was imminent. "It is very premature to be thinking about pausing," Powell said, adding that the ultimate level of rates would likely be higher than officials had previously projected.

The November FOMC meeting brought new projections into sharp focus. Several regional Fed presidents, including San Francisco's Mary Daly, had in recent weeks publicly advocated for stepping down the pace of rate hikes to 50 basis points at the December meeting, a position that appeared to gain traction within the committee. Daly had argued that the Fed needed to balance the risk of doing too little against the risk of overtightening into a recession, given the well-documented lags in monetary policy transmission.

Financial markets gyrated during Powell's press conference, with the S&P 500 initially rallying on the statement's new language before retreating sharply as the Fed chair's hawkish comments sank in. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note settled at 4.05%. Economists noted the inherent tension in the Fed's messaging: the statement suggested a moderation was coming, while Powell's words implied little near-term relief.

The Fed's aggressive tightening cycle has already begun to bite in rate-sensitive sectors. Mortgage rates have surged above 7% for the first time since 2002, and residential investment has contracted sharply. Consumer price inflation, while still running at 8.2% year-over-year on the headline CPI measure, has shown early signs of cooling. Fed officials have repeatedly emphasized that price stability is their paramount objective and that they are prepared to accept some economic pain to restore it. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for December 13-14.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory signals between the FOMC statement's dovish tilt and Powell's hawkish press conference rhetoric create communication confusion that could destabilize market expectations and undermine the Fed's forward guidance credibility.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Housing Costs as Key Inflation Wildcard in 2025
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a research note Monday arguing that shelter inflation — the largest single component of the consumer price index — remains the most significant obstacle to achieving the Fed's 2% inflation target and could keep price pressures elevated well into 2025.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a research note Monday arguing that shelter inflation — the largest single component of the consumer price index — remains the most significant obstacle to achieving the Fed's 2% inflation target and could keep price pressures elevated well into 2025. The paper, authored by FRBSF senior research advisors Sylvain Leduc and Daniel Wilson, found that real-time rent growth indicators suggest a slower pass-through to official CPI shelter measures than many analysts had anticipated.

Shelter costs, which include both rent and the imputed cost of owner-occupied housing, carry a weight of roughly 36% in the headline CPI and about 16% in the Fed's preferred core PCE deflator. The San Francisco Fed economists noted that while new-tenant rent growth — tracked by private indices such as Zillow and Apartment List — had fallen sharply from its 2022 peaks, this deceleration had been slow to show up in official statistics due to the lagged nature of how the Bureau of Labor Statistics measures housing costs.

"The disinflation in shelter that many forecasters expected to materialize in the second half of 2024 has been more gradual than projected," the paper stated. The authors estimated that shelter's contribution to year-over-year CPI inflation could remain above 0.5 percentage points through mid-2025, complicating the Fed's path back to its inflation objective. Core CPI excluding shelter, by contrast, has already returned to levels consistent with the 2% target on an annualized basis.

The research echoes concerns expressed by several FOMC members in recent meeting minutes. Kansas City Fed President Jeff Schmid and Fed Governor Adriana Kugler have both cited housing-related services as a persistent inflation source in speeches this year. FRBSF President Mary Daly, in a separate interview with Reuters last month, had cautioned that the central bank should not declare victory on inflation prematurely, specifically citing shelter costs as an area of ongoing concern.

The findings add a layer of complexity to the debate over the appropriate pace of future rate cuts. The FOMC cut rates by a cumulative 100 basis points in late 2024 but has since signaled a more cautious approach, with futures markets pricing in only one additional quarter-point reduction by year-end 2025. Analysts at Barclays said the FRBSF paper reinforced their view that the Fed's so-called "last mile" problem was real and that the central bank would prioritize patience over speed in its easing cycle. The Bureau of Labor Statistics is scheduled to release the next CPI report on February 12.
author: Lindsay Dunsmuir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly warned Friday that stronger-than-expected job growth could delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, as policymakers weigh the risk of easing too quickly against the threat of overtightening.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that the U.S. labor market's unexpected resilience is complicating the Fed's calculus on when to begin cutting interest rates, pushing back on market expectations for aggressive easing in 2024. Speaking at a Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research event, Daly noted that January's blowout payrolls report — which showed 353,000 jobs added, nearly double consensus estimates — suggests the economy may not be cooling as quickly as policymakers had hoped.

Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, stressed that while inflation has fallen substantially from its 2022 peak of 9.1%, the final stretch to the Fed's 2% target remains uncertain. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.9% in December, still meaningfully above target. 'We need to see more evidence that inflation is durably on a path to 2% before we can responsibly lower rates,' Daly said, according to prepared remarks released by the FRBSF.

Market participants had priced in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 entering the year, a view that has been steadily repriced following a string of stronger-than-expected economic data. Fed funds futures now imply fewer than four cuts, with the first reduction pushed back to June from March. The Fed's benchmark rate currently sits in the 5.25%-5.50% range, the highest level in 23 years, following 525 basis points of cumulative tightening since March 2022.

Daly acknowledged the dual-sided risks facing the FOMC, cautioning that holding rates too high for too long could unnecessarily damage the labor market and tip the economy into recession. The San Francisco Fed's regional economy has shown signs of cooling, particularly in the technology sector, where layoffs have mounted since 2022. However, she emphasized that a premature pivot would risk reigniting price pressures and undermine the credibility the Fed has worked to rebuild after being caught behind the curve on inflation in 2021.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their rate-cut forecast to four reductions beginning in June following Daly's remarks. 'The Fed is clearly in no rush,' wrote Jan Hatzius, chief economist at Goldman Sachs. 'The data dependency message is intact, and Daly's comments reinforce the view that March is off the table.' The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for March 19-20, with the committee broadly expected to hold rates steady.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research
risk_description: Daly's pushback on market rate-cut expectations could generate public backlash if the economy weakens sharply, framing the Fed as unresponsive to deteriorating conditions. The gap between Fed guidance and market pricing also risks credibility damage if the FOMC is forced to pivot more aggressively than signaled.

headline: Fed Lifts Rates by 0.75 Point for Fourth Consecutive Time, Signals Possible Slowdown in Hikes
snippet: The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point Wednesday for the fourth straight meeting, but signaled it could begin moderating the pace of increases as early as December as it assesses the cumulative effects of its historic tightening campaign.
full_text:
The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to raise the federal funds rate by 75 basis points to a target range of 3.75% to 4%, its fourth consecutive move of that magnitude and the fastest pace of monetary tightening in four decades. The decision was widely anticipated by markets, but attention quickly shifted to language in the post-meeting statement that opened the door to smaller increases at future meetings.

The statement noted that 'in determining the pace of future increases,' policymakers would take into account 'the cumulative tightening of monetary policy, the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation, and economic and financial developments.' The inclusion of 'cumulative tightening' and 'lags' was interpreted by investors as a signal that a step-down to a half-point hike in December is likely, sending stocks higher and Treasury yields lower in afternoon trading.

Fed Chairman Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, tempered that optimism, warning that it was 'very premature' to be thinking about pausing rate increases and that the ultimate level of rates would likely need to be higher than previously projected. 'We still have some ways to go,' Powell said, adding that incoming data since the September meeting suggested a higher terminal rate was warranted. The Fed's September projections had pointed to a peak rate near 4.6%; market participants now expect that ceiling could reach 5% or higher.

The San Francisco Fed's Mary Daly, a non-voting member of the FOMC in 2022, had argued in recent weeks for considering a moderation in the pace of hikes to avoid tightening too aggressively. In a late October essay published by the FRBSF, she warned of the 'dual risks' of doing too little and too much, a view that found partial expression in Wednesday's statement language. However, Powell's press conference comments suggested the committee as a whole remains more focused on the risks of under-tightening.

Core CPI inflation stood at 6.6% year-over-year in September, a four-decade high, while the labor market remained robust with unemployment at 3.5%. The combination of sticky inflation and labor market strength has given Fed officials little reason to relent. Analysts at Barclays noted that the Fed faces a difficult communications challenge in the months ahead as it tries to slow the pace of hikes without signaling a premature end to its tightening cycle. 'The Fed wants to downshift gears without taking its foot off the gas entirely — that's a tricky message to send,' said Michael Gapen, chief U.S. economist at Bank of America.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's hawkish press conference remarks appeared to contradict the dovish interpretation of the statement's new language, creating a potential miscommunication risk. The divergence between Powell's tone and the written statement could generate market confusion and undermine forward guidance effectiveness, a recurring vulnerability for Fed communications.

headline: San Francisco Fed Publishes Research Suggesting Inflation Cooling Without Major Job Losses
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a paper Monday arguing that inflation has declined significantly without the large rise in unemployment historically associated with disinflation, a finding with important implications for the Fed's ongoing policy deliberations.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday published new analysis suggesting that the United States has achieved a rare 'immaculate disinflation' — a substantial reduction in price pressures without triggering a sharp deterioration in the labor market, challenging conventional economic models that link lower inflation to higher unemployment.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Regis Barnichon and Adam Shapiro, found that much of the inflationary surge between 2021 and 2022 was driven by pandemic-related supply disruptions that have since largely resolved, rather than by excess demand that would require aggressive demand destruction through higher rates to eliminate. The authors estimated that supply-side normalization accounted for more than half of the inflation decline from the June 2022 peak of 9.1% to current levels. 'The good news is that a large share of the inflation we experienced appears to have been transitory in nature, even if it lasted longer than initially expected,' the paper concluded.

The research adds academic weight to the case for the Fed to begin easing monetary policy, coming as FOMC members debate the timing of rate cuts amid still-elevated core inflation readings. PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred measure, stood at 2.7% year-over-year in March 2024, down sharply from a peak of 5.4% but still above the 2% target. Unemployment, meanwhile, remained near a historically low 3.8%, a combination that has surprised many economists who predicted a harder landing.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has frequently cited 'nonlinear' disinflation as a possibility, and the FRBSF paper provides quantitative support for that view. However, some policymakers have pushed back on the soft-landing narrative. Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester and Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari have both said in recent weeks that they see fewer rate cuts this year than the three projected in the March Summary of Economic Projections, citing persistent services inflation driven by wages and shelter costs.

The FRBSF paper is not an official Fed policy statement and reflects the views of its authors rather than the institution. Nevertheless, regional Fed research papers often inform FOMC deliberations and can signal the analytical thinking of voting members. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who has been among the more cautious voices on rate cuts, did not co-author the paper but has cited similar supply-normalization dynamics in recent public remarks. Analysts said the research likely reinforces the case for a data-dependent approach that allows for cuts later in 2024 if inflation continues its downward trajectory.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room to Hold Rates Steady
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Thursday that moderating job growth and easing wage pressures provide policymakers with sufficient justification to maintain the current policy rate, pushing back against expectations for an imminent cut.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that a gradual softening in U.S. labor market conditions gives the central bank leeway to keep interest rates at their current restrictive level, while stopping short of endorsing near-term reductions in borrowing costs. Speaking at a banking conference in Los Angeles, Daly cited October's nonfarm payrolls report, which showed a net gain of 150,000 jobs and a rise in the unemployment rate to 3.9%, as evidence that the economy is moving in the right direction without requiring immediate policy relief.

Daly's remarks, among the more measured in recent Fed communications, reflect a broader effort by officials to temper market enthusiasm that had been building around the prospect of rate cuts as early as the first quarter of 2024. Federal funds futures markets had been pricing in up to five quarter-point reductions through the calendar year, a trajectory that several Fed officials, including Chair Jerome Powell, have characterized as overly optimistic.

'We are in a good place, but good does not mean done,' Daly said during a moderated discussion. 'The job is not finished until inflation is durably back at 2%, and we need more evidence before we can declare victory.' The San Francisco Fed chief noted that core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, remained above 3.5% on a twelve-month basis as of the most recent reading, well above the committee's stated target.

Market participants parsed Daly's tone carefully, given her reputation as a pragmatic centrist within the Federal Open Market Committee. The yield on the two-year Treasury note, particularly sensitive to near-term rate expectations, edged up two basis points to 4.68% following her remarks. The S&P 500 pared gains to close up 0.3% on the day.

Economists at several Wall Street firms noted that Daly's emphasis on data dependence was broadly in line with the FOMC's November statement, which retained language describing policy as 'sufficiently restrictive.' The next scheduled FOMC meeting is set for December 12-13, with market consensus heavily favoring an extended pause rather than any further adjustment to the 5.25%-5.50% target range.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Divided Over Pace of Rate Cuts, Minutes Reveal Tension on Easing Timeline
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Reserve's January 2024 meeting exposed meaningful disagreement among policymakers about how quickly to reduce the benchmark interest rate, with some officials warning against premature easing while others flagged risks of keeping policy too tight for too long.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials were sharply divided at their January meeting over how quickly to move toward lower interest rates, according to minutes released Wednesday, in a disclosure that underscores the communication challenges confronting Chair Jerome Powell as markets clamor for clarity on the easing path. Several members of the Federal Open Market Committee cautioned that the risks of cutting too soon outweighed the risks of a delayed pivot, citing persistent services inflation and robust consumer spending as reasons to maintain the current 5.25%-5.50% target range well into the year.

At the same time, a minority of participants expressed concern that holding rates at restrictive levels for an extended period could inflict unnecessary damage on the labor market and the broader economy, particularly as credit conditions tightened and small business confidence eroded. The divide maps onto a longstanding fault line within the committee between officials who view inflation as the primary threat and those who weigh unemployment risks more heavily — a tension that has defined internal deliberations since the Fed embarked on its historic tightening cycle in March 2022.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who has frequently emphasized the dual mandate in public remarks, did not publicly characterize her precise January position. However, her recent speeches suggest alignment with those urging patience. In a February address to the Commonwealth Club of San Francisco, Daly argued that 'moving too fast risks reigniting inflation, while moving too slow risks an unnecessary economic slowdown — the path forward requires careful calibration.'

The release of the minutes rattled financial markets, which had been operating on the assumption that the Fed would begin cutting rates at its March meeting. The two-year Treasury yield jumped 8 basis points to 4.71% on the news, and the probability of a March cut as priced in federal funds futures fell from 38% to below 20%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.8% to close at 38,272.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their baseline forecast to reflect a first cut in June rather than March, citing the hawkish tilt of the minutes. 'The minutes make clear this is a committee in no hurry,' wrote Jan Hatzius in a note to clients. Fed watchers will closely scrutinize Powell's upcoming congressional testimony for signals about whether the chair can bridge the committee's internal divisions without further confusing markets about the trajectory of monetary policy.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The minutes reveal significant internal disagreement among FOMC members about the rate-cut timeline, creating a communication risk that contradictory public statements from different Fed officials could destabilize market expectations and undermine confidence in the Fed's policy coherence. The market reaction — a sharp repricing of rate-cut expectations — illustrates how internal divisions, once public, can amplify volatility.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns Inflation Could Stay Elevated If Services Prices Remain Sticky
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that services-sector inflation, driven largely by housing and healthcare costs, could keep the personal consumption expenditures price index above the Fed's 2% target through at least mid-2025 under several plausible scenarios.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released research on Monday suggesting that the central bank's path back to its 2% inflation goal may prove more protracted than many forecasters expect, particularly if shelter costs and healthcare services prices fail to decelerate at the pace implied by current market-based rent measures. The paper, authored by economists Sylvain Leduc and Huiyu Li, used a decomposition of the personal consumption expenditures price index to argue that so-called 'supercore' inflation — services prices excluding housing and energy — remained elevated relative to pre-pandemic norms and was proving resistant to the effects of tighter monetary policy.

The research found that while goods inflation had largely normalized following the unwinding of pandemic-era supply chain disruptions, services prices were increasingly being driven by domestic labor market dynamics rather than external factors. Wage growth in service industries, including hospitality, healthcare, and professional services, remained above levels historically consistent with 2% PCE inflation, the authors noted. The paper stopped short of offering explicit policy prescriptions but concluded that a sustained period of below-trend demand growth may be necessary to fully extinguish inflationary pressures in the services sector.

The findings carry particular salience given the Federal Reserve's reliance on FRBSF economic research to inform FOMC deliberations. The San Francisco Fed has historically been a prolific publisher of influential research on inflation dynamics, labor markets, and monetary policy transmission. Its 'Fed Views' and 'Economic Letter' series are closely followed by private sector economists and policymakers alike.

The publication comes as the FOMC prepares for its March 2025 meeting, where policymakers are expected to deliberate on whether a fourth consecutive pause in the rate-cutting cycle is warranted following stronger-than-anticipated January inflation data. Core PCE inflation came in at 2.6% year-over-year in January, above the 2.4% consensus forecast, prompting a fresh round of reassessments across Wall Street. Fed funds futures markets are now pricing fewer than two rate cuts for the full year, down from more than four cuts just three months ago.

Economists and market participants cautioned that the FRBSF paper, while carrying the prestige of Fed authorship, represents the views of its authors rather than official FOMC policy. Nevertheless, the research is likely to reinforce the case made by more hawkish committee members that patience is warranted before any additional easing. 'The paper confirms what we've been seeing in the data,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'Services inflation is the last mile of a very long marathon, and the Fed may need to run it at a slower pace than markets would prefer.'
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Rate Cuts Remain Data-Dependent as PCE Holds Above Target
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that the central bank needs to see more progress on inflation before cutting interest rates, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing in 2024. Core PCE inflation remained at 2.8% in the latest reading, still above the Fed's 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back Friday against growing market speculation that the central bank would begin cutting interest rates as early as the first quarter of 2024, warning that policymakers need sustained evidence that inflation is returning durably to the 2% target before pivoting to a looser stance.

Daly, speaking at a Stanford University economics conference, pointed to the latest Personal Consumption Expenditures price index data showing core PCE at 2.8% annualized — still meaningfully above the Fed's stated goal. 'The last mile of disinflation is often the hardest,' she told attendees. 'We cannot declare victory prematurely. The cost of getting this wrong far outweighs the cost of patience.'

Market participants had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 following softer-than-expected November CPI data, with fed funds futures briefly implying a first cut as soon as March. Daly's comments helped trim those expectations modestly, with the two-year Treasury yield rising four basis points to 4.58% in afternoon trading.

The FRBSF president also addressed the labor market, noting that while job growth had moderated from its 2022 peak, the unemployment rate at 3.7% remained historically low and wage pressures, while easing, had not yet fully normalized to levels consistent with 2% inflation. 'A strong labor market is something we want to preserve, but it does complicate our inflation calculus,' Daly said.
