Economists at the San Francisco Fed published a companion research note Friday suggesting that underlying services inflation, which strips out energy-related components, continued to run at an annualized pace of approximately 3.4% through the fourth quarter — a figure Daly acknowledged would need to fall further before the committee could be confident inflation was sustainably on track. Fed Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to speak at the Fed's December press conference next week, where markets will be watching closely for any shift in the committee's median rate projections in the updated Summary of Economic Projections.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's hawkish tone directly contradicts market pricing for aggressive 2024 rate cuts, creating a potential messaging gap between Fed communications and financial market expectations. If subsequent FOMC speakers send mixed signals, this could amplify market volatility and undermine the Fed's forward guidance credibility.

headline: Fed Delivers Jumbo 75-Basis-Point Rate Hike, Signals More Increases Ahead
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the second consecutive meeting, the most aggressive back-to-back tightening in four decades, as policymakers race to bring inflation running near a 40-year high back under control. The federal funds rate target range now stands at 2.25% to 2.50%.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 0.75 percentage point for the second straight meeting Wednesday, a pace of tightening unseen since the early 1980s under Chairman Paul Volcker, as the central bank acknowledged that inflation remained far too high and signaled its willingness to accept slower economic growth — and potentially higher unemployment — to restore price stability.

The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee brought the federal funds rate target range to 2.25%-2.50%, a level the Fed considers roughly neutral — neither stimulating nor restraining economic activity. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at his post-meeting press conference, declined to provide specific guidance on the size of future increases, saying decisions would remain meeting-by-meeting and dependent on incoming economic data. 'We think it's time to just go to a meeting-by-meeting basis,' Powell said.

The decision comes as the Consumer Price Index climbed 9.1% year-over-year in June, the highest reading since November 1981, driven by surging energy prices following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, persistent supply chain disruptions, and strong consumer demand. The Fed's preferred gauge, the core PCE deflator, ran at 4.7% in May, more than double the 2% target. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a voting FOMC member in 2022, had been among the more vocal advocates for front-loading rate increases, arguing in a series of speeches earlier in the summer that rapid tightening would allow the Fed to preserve long-run economic stability.

The aggressive tightening cycle has sparked concern among some economists and housing market analysts. Mortgage rates have surged past 5.5%, the highest since 2008, contributing to a sharp pullback in home sales across the West Coast markets that fall within the San Francisco Fed's district. Daly acknowledged the squeeze on regional housing affordability in remarks last week but maintained that restoring price stability was the paramount objective.

Financial markets staged a brief relief rally Wednesday afternoon after Powell indicated that the pace of hikes would slow at some point as policy moved into restrictive territory, with the S&P 500 closing up 2.6%. Economists at major Wall Street firms including Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase now project the federal funds rate will peak somewhere between 3.25% and 3.75% early next year, though the exact terminal rate remains deeply uncertain and contingent on whether inflation shows convincing signs of easing in coming months.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The Fed's abandonment of explicit forward guidance in favor of meeting-by-meeting decisions introduces significant uncertainty for markets and risks being interpreted as a loss of policy coherence. The potential for a recession induced by aggressive tightening could generate substantial public and political backlash against the Fed's leadership, particularly given the impact on housing affordability in FRBSF's district.

headline: San Francisco Fed Study Finds Remote Work Shift Continues to Reshape Regional Labor Markets
snippet: A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that the structural shift toward remote and hybrid work arrangements has persistently altered labor supply patterns across the Western United States, with implications for how policymakers should interpret regional employment data. The findings add nuance to ongoing Fed deliberations about the true tightness of the labor market.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released new research Monday concluding that the pandemic-era shift toward remote work has had lasting structural effects on Western U.S. labor markets, complicating traditional measures of labor market tightness that the Federal Open Market Committee relies upon when calibrating monetary policy.

The FRBSF economists, led by Senior Research Advisor Sylvain Leduc and economist Nicolas Petrosky-Nadeau, found that remote work adoption increased effective labor supply in high-cost metropolitan areas like San Francisco and Seattle by enabling employers to recruit from geographically broader talent pools. At the same time, the research identified a paradox: while nominal labor supply appeared to expand, wage pressures in certain high-skill service sectors remained elevated, suggesting that skill mismatches and industry-specific shortages were persisting beneath the surface of aggregate employment data.

The paper, published in the FRBSF Economic Letter series, noted that the San Francisco metropolitan area experienced a net outflow of workers to lower-cost markets in states including Texas, Arizona, and Nevada during 2020-2023, a trend that partially offset the inward labor supply effect of remote hiring. The researchers estimated this internal migration dynamic reduced measured labor force participation rates in the Bay Area by approximately 0.4 to 0.6 percentage points, a factor that could cause policymakers to overstate the degree of labor market slack in the region.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, in a brief statement accompanying the research release, said the findings underscore why the Fed must look beyond headline national employment statistics when assessing whether monetary policy is sufficiently restrictive. 'The labor market is not monolithic,' Daly said. 'Regional and sectoral dynamics matter, and our research helps ensure we're asking the right questions about what the data are telling us.'

The research arrives as FOMC members are weighing when to begin reducing the federal funds rate from its current range of 5.25%-5.50%, with the labor market remaining a central variable in that calculus. The national unemployment rate held at 3.9% in the most recent jobs report, while nonfarm payrolls added 187,000 positions, a figure that most Fed officials have described as consistent with a gradual cooling rather than deterioration. Analysts at Barclays and Wells Fargo said the FRBSF paper, while focused on regional dynamics, adds to a broader body of evidence suggesting the post-pandemic labor market may be structurally different from pre-2020 norms in ways that complicate the Fed's dual mandate calculations.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Rate Cuts Require More Confidence on Inflation Path Before Acting
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said policymakers need greater assurance that inflation is sustainably returning to the 2% target before reducing interest rates, pushing back against market expectations for early 2024 easing.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Monday that central bank officials need considerably more evidence of sustained disinflation before they can confidently begin cutting borrowing costs, adding her voice to a chorus of Fed speakers tempering investor optimism about the pace of upcoming rate reductions.

Daly, speaking at an economic forum in Los Angeles, said the personal consumption expenditures price index — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — remains too elevated to declare victory, even as it has fallen sharply from its mid-2022 peak above 7%. The core PCE index, which strips out food and energy prices, stood at 2.9% in December 2023, still nearly a full percentage point above the Fed's 2% target. 'The last mile of this inflation fight is proving to be the hardest,' Daly said. 'We need to see more months of data confirming the trend before we act.'

Financial markets had priced in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 following the Federal Open Market Committee's December meeting, where Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged that rate cuts were coming into view. Daly's remarks, alongside similar commentary from Fed Governor Christopher Waller and Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester, suggest the committee may be preparing investors for a more gradual easing cycle than futures markets currently imply.

The San Francisco Fed president also addressed the strength of the U.S. labor market, noting that January's blockbuster payrolls report — which showed 353,000 jobs added — complicates the case for imminent rate reductions. A tight labor market, she argued, keeps upward pressure on services inflation, particularly in shelter and healthcare costs, which have been stubbornly resistant to the Fed's tightening campaign. 'Full employment and price stability are two sides of the same coin,' Daly said. 'Right now, we have both, and that gives us the luxury of patience.'

Economists at major Wall Street banks revised their rate-cut forecasts following the speech, with several pushing their expected timeline for the first reduction from March to May or June. The yield on the two-year Treasury note, highly sensitive to Fed rate expectations, rose 6 basis points to 4.47% in afternoon trading, while the S&P 500 trimmed earlier gains. Fed funds futures now imply roughly four cuts for the full year, down from six earlier in January.
author: Liz McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments risk appearing to contradict Chair Powell's December pivot signals, potentially confusing markets about the Fed's rate-cut timeline and creating internal messaging inconsistencies that could undermine forward guidance credibility.

headline: Fed Delivers Half-Point Rate Hike as Inflation Fight Escalates Into Uncharted Territory
snippet: The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points Wednesday, the largest single increase in more than two decades, as policymakers escalated their most aggressive tightening campaign since the early 1980s to combat inflation running at a four-decade high.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised interest rates by half a percentage point Wednesday and signaled it plans similarly sized increases in the coming months, marking an abrupt shift in the central bank's approach to battling the most severe inflation outbreak in a generation.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously to lift the federal funds rate target to a range of 0.75% to 1%, the highest level since the Covid-19 pandemic began. The move, widely anticipated by financial markets, was the first 50-basis-point increase since May 2000 and signaled that policymakers are willing to accept significant financial market turbulence and slower economic growth to bring price pressures under control. Consumer prices rose 8.3% in April from a year earlier, just below a 40-year high reached in March.

Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference after the decision, said the committee is prepared to take additional 50-basis-point steps at its meetings in June and July if economic conditions warrant. 'Inflation is much too high, and we understand the hardship it is causing, and we're moving expeditiously to bring it back down,' Powell said. He added that the Fed's primary tool remains interest rate increases, and that the balance sheet reduction program — under which the Fed will allow up to $47.5 billion in bonds to roll off each month beginning in June, rising to $95 billion in September — would complement the rate increases.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly, who participated in the meeting as a voting member, said in remarks released alongside the decision that the Fed's credibility depends on demonstrating a genuine commitment to restoring price stability. Daly has been among the more vocal officials warning that the Fed cannot afford to allow inflation expectations to become unanchored. Internal FRBSF research published earlier this year suggested that pandemic-era supply disruptions accounted for roughly half of the current inflation overshoot, with demand-side pressures from fiscal stimulus making up the remainder.

The decision sent shockwaves through financial markets still adjusting to the new policy reality. The two-year Treasury yield climbed above 2.7%, its highest level since late 2018, while stocks initially rallied as investors interpreted Powell's comments as ruling out 75-basis-point hikes — only to reverse sharply in subsequent sessions. Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan raised their peak federal funds rate forecasts to 3.25% and 3.5% respectively, while flagging growing risks that the aggressive tightening path could tip the economy into recession by 2023.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Regional Banking Vulnerabilities Amid Rate Stress, Deposit Outflows
snippet: New research published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warned that mid-sized regional lenders face mounting balance sheet stress from unrealized securities losses and deposit concentration risks, months before the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank thrust the issue into the national spotlight.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday identifying significant vulnerabilities in the balance sheets of regional and community banks, warning that the rapid rise in interest rates engineered by the Federal Reserve over the past year has left many institutions holding large portfolios of long-duration securities whose market values have fallen sharply.

The paper, authored by economists in the FRBSF's research division, estimated that U.S. banks collectively held roughly $620 billion in unrealized losses on their available-for-sale and held-to-maturity securities portfolios as of late 2022. While accounting rules do not require these losses to be recognized until securities are sold, the researchers cautioned that institutions relying on these portfolios for liquidity management could face acute pressure if depositors withdrew funds at scale. 'Mark-to-market losses create a fragility that is invisible until it suddenly is not,' the paper stated.

The FRBSF research arrives as federal regulators and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation review lessons from the March 2023 failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank, both of which suffered catastrophic deposit runs after disclosing large unrealized losses tied to their bond holdings. The San Francisco Fed, which served as SVB's primary federal supervisor, has faced scrutiny over whether examiners moved swiftly enough to flag the bank's interest rate risk exposure ahead of its collapse. A Federal Reserve review of the supervision of SVB, led by Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr, concluded that examiners identified the risks but escalated them too slowly.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly acknowledged the supervisory shortcomings in testimony before the Senate Banking Committee and pledged enhanced examination rigor for large regional institutions. The FRBSF has since added staff and resources to its large-bank supervision function and adopted more frequent stress-testing requirements for institutions with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion. 'We are committed to learning every lesson this episode has to offer,' Daly said.

Banking analysts said the FRBSF's published research represents an unusually candid public acknowledgment of systemic stress by a regional Fed bank. 'It's notable that the San Francisco Fed is putting this in writing in a formal research paper,' said Jaret Seiberg, an analyst at TD Cowen. 'It suggests they are trying to get ahead of the next potential problem and establish a public record that the risks were flagged.' The Federal Reserve is expected to finalize new capital and liquidity requirements for banks with assets above $100 billion as part of the Basel III endgame rules, though the timeline for implementation remains contested among industry groups.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF's publication of research highlighting regional banking vulnerabilities could amplify public concerns about deposit safety and trigger reputational damage given the Fed's prior supervisory failures with SVB, potentially undermining confidence in the Fed's regulatory oversight capacity.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Quantitative Tightening Could Slow as Reserves Approach 'Ample' Level
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly signaled Thursday that the central bank may need to reassess the pace of its balance sheet reduction as bank reserves edge closer to what policymakers consider an adequate level. Her remarks added nuance to an ongoing debate within the Federal Open Market Committee about the endpoint of quantitative tightening.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that officials are closely monitoring reserve levels in the banking system and may need to slow the pace of quantitative tightening before reserves fall below what policymakers deem sufficient to ensure smooth money-market functioning.

Speaking at a monetary policy forum in New York, Daly noted that reserves have declined from their post-pandemic peak of roughly $4.2 trillion to approximately $3.3 trillion, a level she described as still 'ample' but one that warrants heightened attention. 'We are not at the point of concern yet, but the trajectory matters,' she told attendees. 'Slowing the runoff before we get to an uncomfortable level is very much on the table for discussion.'

The Fed has been shrinking its balance sheet at a maximum pace of $95 billion per month — comprising $60 billion in Treasuries and $35 billion in agency mortgage-backed securities — since September 2022. The process, known as quantitative tightening or QT, is widely seen as a complement to interest rate increases in the Fed's effort to rein in inflation that peaked above 9% in mid-2022.

Wall Street strategists were quick to parse Daly's remarks. JPMorgan's fixed-income team wrote in a note that the comments suggest the FOMC is 'laying the groundwork for a taper of QT sometime in the first half of 2024, likely at a pace that reduces monthly redemption caps by half.' Treasuries rallied modestly on the news, with the 10-year yield falling three basis points to 4.38%.

Daly has consistently advocated for a data-dependent approach to both rate policy and balance sheet normalization. She reiterated that the Fed's primary objective remains bringing inflation back to its 2% target, adding that the labor market remains 'remarkably resilient' despite meaningful monetary tightening. November's jobs report showing 199,000 nonfarm payroll additions appeared to support her assessment. Markets are currently pricing roughly 115 basis points of rate cuts in 2024, a trajectory Daly characterized as 'more aggressive than my own baseline.'
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Rate-Cut Timeline, Stoking Market Confusion
snippet: A string of divergent public statements from Federal Reserve policymakers this week has left investors struggling to gauge when the central bank will begin lowering interest rates, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and Governor Christopher Waller offering sharply different assessments of the inflation outlook. The conflicting messaging underscores persistent disagreement within the FOMC as officials approach what could be a pivotal turning point in monetary policy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials delivered a cacophony of conflicting signals this week about the path of interest rates, raising concerns among investors and economists about the central bank's communications discipline at a critical juncture for monetary policy.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking Monday at the Commonwealth Club in San Francisco, struck a cautious tone, warning that it would be 'premature' to declare victory over inflation and suggesting that rate cuts were unlikely before the second half of 2024. 'The last mile of getting inflation down is often the hardest,' she said. 'We need to be patient and let the data guide us rather than pre-committing to a timeline.' She cited core PCE inflation of 3.5% — still well above the Fed's 2% target — as justification for continued restraint.

Just two days later, Fed Governor Christopher Waller delivered remarks that markets interpreted as meaningfully more dovish, suggesting that if inflation continued to decline for 'several more months,' the Fed could begin cutting rates. Waller's comments sent equities surging and Treasury yields falling sharply, with fed funds futures briefly pricing in a March rate cut with better than 60% probability.

The whipsaw dynamic drew sharp criticism from some market participants and economists who argue that the Fed's communication strategy has become a source of volatility rather than stability. 'When two senior officials give diametrically opposite assessments within 48 hours, you end up with a market that is essentially flying blind,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'That's not good for anyone, including the Fed.'

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is expected to address the conflicting signals at his next public appearance, scheduled for a banking conference in Washington. Analysts at Goldman Sachs wrote in a note that they view the Waller comments as more reflective of internal FOMC thinking, but acknowledged that 'the communication fog has thickened considerably.' The episode is likely to intensify scrutiny of the Fed's forward guidance framework heading into the December FOMC meeting, where officials are widely expected to hold rates steady in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public statements from senior Fed officials within a 48-hour window created significant market volatility and drew criticism of the Fed's communications framework. The divergence between Daly and Waller undermines the FOMC's credibility in guiding market expectations and risks eroding public trust in the Fed's policy coherence ahead of a crucial decision point.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Persistent Services Inflation Complicating Rate-Cut Path
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday argues that elevated services inflation — driven largely by shelter costs and wage-sensitive sectors — is likely to remain above pre-pandemic norms through at least mid-2025, potentially complicating the Federal Open Market Committee's ability to begin easing monetary policy. The research adds to a growing body of evidence that the final stage of the Fed's inflation fight may prove more protracted than markets anticipate.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released research Monday suggesting that services inflation, a component of the Consumer Price Index that has proven stubbornly resistant to the Fed's aggressive rate-hiking campaign, is unlikely to normalize to pre-pandemic levels before the middle of next year, presenting a meaningful challenge to policymakers hoping to begin cutting interest rates.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Adam Shapiro and Anita Todd, decomposes recent CPI data into supply-driven and demand-driven components and finds that demand-side pressures in services sectors — particularly shelter, healthcare, and hospitality — remain 'substantially above trend.' The authors estimate that shelter inflation alone could add approximately 50 basis points to headline CPI readings through the first half of 2025, even as rental market data suggests prices for new leases have already cooled sharply.

The research highlights a well-known measurement lag in the way the Bureau of Labor Statistics tracks housing costs, which relies on surveys of existing rents rather than real-time market data. Private-sector indices such as the Zillow Observed Rent Index and the CoreLogic Single-Family Rent Index have shown rent growth slowing to roughly 3% annually, but that cooling has been slow to feed through into official statistics. 'The gap between market rents and measured shelter CPI is unprecedented in post-war data,' the FRBSF economists wrote. 'Convergence is underway, but it will take time.'

The findings carry practical implications for FOMC deliberations. If services inflation remains elevated, it complicates the Fed's ability to confidently declare that it is on a 'sustainable path' to its 2% inflation target — language that Chair Jerome Powell has indicated is a prerequisite for rate reductions. Federal funds futures markets currently anticipate the first 25-basis-point cut arriving at the May 2024 meeting, though rate expectations have been volatile.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, has repeatedly cited services inflation as her primary concern. In a statement accompanying the research release, she said the work 'reinforces the need for patience and data dependence' and cautioned against reading recent headline CPI improvements as a signal that the Fed's job is complete. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 3.2% in the most recent reading, still well above the 2% target.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says San Francisco Economy Shows Uneven Labor Market Recovery
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly flagged divergent employment trends across sectors in the West Coast economy, signaling that the Fed's dual mandate remains a complex balancing act heading into 2024. Her remarks added nuance to a debate about when the central bank might begin easing policy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Thursday that the regional labor market she oversees is exhibiting uneven recovery patterns, with technology and financial services shedding workers even as hospitality and healthcare continue to add jobs at a robust pace. Speaking at a conference hosted by the Bay Area Council in San Francisco, Daly said the patchwork nature of employment conditions underscores why policymakers must be careful not to over-index on a single month of data.

Daly, who is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, noted that the headline unemployment rate of 3.7% nationally masks considerable dispersion at the industry level. She cited FRBSF research showing that tech-sector layoffs, which accelerated through late 2022 and 2023, had pushed median unemployment duration higher for workers with advanced degrees—an unusual dynamic that could soften wage pressures faster than aggregate models suggest.

On inflation, Daly struck a cautiously optimistic tone, acknowledging that the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index had declined meaningfully from its June 2022 peak of 7.0% to a range approaching the Fed's 2% target. "Progress is real and it is welcome," she said, while cautioning that services inflation, particularly in shelter and healthcare, remains elevated and warrants continued vigilance.

Market participants parsed the remarks for clues about the timeline for rate cuts. Federal funds futures contracts, which had been pricing roughly five quarter-point cuts in 2024 at the start of the year, recalibrated slightly after Daly reiterated that the committee needed to see "sustained" disinflation before adjusting policy. The two-year Treasury yield ticked up three basis points to 4.38% in early afternoon trading following her prepared remarks.

Daly also addressed the ongoing balance sheet reduction program, noting that quantitative tightening remained on autopilot and that the FOMC had not yet begun serious deliberations about altering the pace of runoff. The Fed's balance sheet stood at approximately $7.7 trillion, down from a peak of nearly $9 trillion in mid-2022. Analysts at several primary dealers said the FRBSF president's overall tone was consistent with a Fed that is in no hurry to cut rates before mid-2024.
author: Alex Tanzi
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Clash Over Rate-Cut Timing, Raising Questions About FOMC Cohesion
snippet: Conflicting signals from Federal Reserve policymakers this week—including dovish remarks from the San Francisco Fed's Mary Daly and a more hawkish posture from other regional presidents—have rattled bond markets and sparked concern that the central bank is struggling to deliver a unified message on the path forward for interest rates.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve faces a growing messaging challenge after several of its most prominent policymakers offered sharply divergent views on interest rate policy within a 72-hour span this week, leaving investors to reconcile statements that appear to point in opposite directions at a critical juncture for monetary policy.

On Tuesday, Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly suggested in a Wall Street Journal interview that three rate cuts in 2024 remained a reasonable baseline scenario if inflation continued its downward trajectory. By Thursday, Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas President Lorie Logan had pushed back forcefully, saying it was "premature" to even discuss easing given stubborn services inflation and a labor market that she described as still running "quite hot."

The disconnect was compounded Friday morning when Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester told CNBC that the March FOMC meeting was too soon to have sufficient confidence that inflation was sustainably moving toward 2%. The three-way divergence sent the 10-year Treasury yield swinging in a 12-basis-point range over the course of the week, while the S&P 500 gave back gains accumulated earlier in January.

Communications scholars and former Fed officials said the episode illustrates the difficulty of managing forward guidance in a committee of 12 voting members with genuine disagreements. "The Fed's credibility rests in part on presenting a coherent signal to markets, and when regional presidents are freelancing in opposite directions, it creates unnecessary volatility," said Vincent Reinhart, a former Fed monetary affairs director now at Dreyfus and Mellon. He added that Chair Jerome Powell's upcoming congressional testimony would need to thread a careful needle to restore clarity.

Investors will be watching the January FOMC meeting minutes, due for release in mid-February, for any indication of how wide the internal debate has grown. For now, the divergence has effectively reset market expectations: fed funds futures markets have scaled back pricing of aggressive easing, with the probability of a March cut falling below 40% from nearly 80% a month ago. The episode raises broader questions about whether the Fed's decentralized communication structure, long considered a feature of its institutional design, has become a liability in a high-stakes policy environment.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public statements from FOMC members on rate-cut timing have generated significant market volatility and media scrutiny, undermining the Fed's forward guidance credibility. The episode risks reinforcing a narrative of internal disarray within the committee at a pivotal policy inflection point.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Sees PCE Inflation Nearing 2% Target by Late 2024
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a new analysis Monday projecting that the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index, could converge on the central bank's 2% target by the fourth quarter of 2024 if current disinflationary trends persist. The findings offer a modestly optimistic backdrop for policymakers weighing the timing of interest rate reductions.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released research projecting that U.S. inflation, as measured by the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index, is on track to reach the Federal Reserve's 2% target by the final quarter of 2024, provided the economy avoids significant new supply shocks or a re-acceleration of wage growth.

The FRBSF working paper, authored by senior economists Adam Hale Shapiro and Daniel J. Wilson, used a decomposition framework that separates PCE inflation into supply-driven and demand-driven components. The authors found that supply-side contributions to inflation—which surged during the pandemic due to global supply chain disruptions—have largely normalized, while demand-driven inflation has been declining more gradually but consistently since mid-2023.

The research found that core PCE, which stood at 2.9% year-over-year as of December 2023, could decline to approximately 2.1% by October 2024 under a baseline scenario that assumes no major new shocks to goods prices and a modest cooling of the services sector. A more pessimistic scenario, in which shelter costs remain elevated through mid-year, could keep core PCE above 2.3% through year-end, the paper cautioned.

The findings are likely to be cited in internal FOMC deliberations as the committee approaches its spring meetings. Several economists said the FRBSF analysis broadly supports a mid-2024 start to rate cuts, though the paper itself is careful to note that it does not constitute policy guidance. "This is descriptive economics, not a recommendation," Shapiro said in a brief interview, declining to comment on the FOMC's specific path.

The release comes as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and other officials have repeatedly stressed that the committee will be data-dependent in determining when to begin easing. The Fed has held its benchmark federal funds rate in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50% since July 2023, the highest level in more than two decades. Financial markets responded positively to the FRBSF paper, with equities edging higher and the two-year Treasury yield dipping four basis points to 4.33% following the report's publication.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Supports Case for Gradual Rate Cuts in 2025
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly indicated Friday that ongoing softening in the U.S. labor market bolsters the argument for a measured pace of interest rate reductions, though she cautioned that progress on inflation must remain evident before any policy pivot.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that a gradually cooling labor market provides the central bank with the flexibility to ease monetary policy in a deliberate manner over 2025, adding that the Fed should avoid moving too quickly or too slowly to avoid destabilizing the economy. Speaking at the Pacific Coast Economic Forum in San Francisco, Daly cited November payroll data showing employers added 180,000 jobs, down from an average of 240,000 per month a year earlier, as evidence that the labor market is finding a more sustainable equilibrium. 'We've made real progress, and the labor market is telling us that restrictive policy is working,' Daly said. 'But the work isn't done.'

Daly's remarks come ahead of the FOMC's January 2025 meeting, where traders in federal funds futures markets are pricing in roughly a 65% probability of a quarter-point cut. The Fed cut rates three times in the second half of 2024, bringing the benchmark federal funds rate to a target range of 4.25%–4.50%, but policymakers signaled in December that the pace of additional easing would slow considerably. The December Summary of Economic Projections showed a median expectation of just two cuts in 2025, down from four projected in September.

On inflation, Daly acknowledged that the path back to the Fed's 2% target has grown 'bumpier' in recent months. The most recent PCE price index reading showed headline inflation at 2.4% year-over-year and core PCE at 2.8%, both above target. Daly said she wants to see 'a few more months' of data before gaining confidence that disinflation is firmly on track. 'We should not declare victory prematurely,' she told the audience.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan noted that Daly's tone was modestly more hawkish than her November comments, when she appeared more open to consecutive cuts. David Mericle, chief U.S. economist at Goldman Sachs, wrote in a note that the FRBSF president's remarks 'reinforce our view that the January meeting will result in a pause, with the Fed remaining data-dependent as it assesses the disinflation trajectory into mid-2025.'

Markets showed a limited reaction to Daly's comments, with the two-year Treasury yield edging up about 3 basis points to 4.32% following the speech. Equity indexes were broadly flat, suggesting investors had already priced in a cautious Fed stance. Daly is a voting member of the FOMC in 2025, giving her remarks particular weight as the committee shapes its policy path for the year ahead.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Inflation Outlook as Daly and Waller Send Conflicting Signals
snippet: Diverging public statements from Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly and Fed Governor Christopher Waller on the persistence of inflation have raised questions about the central bank's internal cohesion ahead of a pivotal spring policy decision.
full_text:
A public disagreement between two influential Federal Reserve officials is threatening to muddy the central bank's communications strategy at a moment when market participants are hungry for clarity on the path of interest rates. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking last week at a University of California conference, argued that inflation was 'decisively moving' toward the 2% target and that the risks of holding rates too high for too long were becoming 'more symmetrical' with inflation risks. Just days later, Fed Governor Christopher Waller told an audience in Washington that he saw 'no compelling reason' to rush toward rate cuts, citing persistent services inflation and a labor market he described as 'still quite resilient.'

The divergence has fueled speculation among bond traders and Wall Street strategists about the Fed's true consensus heading into its March and May meetings. 'When two prominent officials draw such starkly different conclusions from the same data, it creates unnecessary confusion in markets,' said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer, a Fed-watching firm. Treasury volatility, as measured by the ICE BofA MOVE Index, climbed to its highest level in six weeks following the back-to-back remarks.

The conflicting signals come as the Fed grapples with a mixed inflation picture. Core PCE inflation remained stuck at 2.8% for a third consecutive month in January, while the services component — closely watched by Fed officials because of its stickiness — showed little sign of easing. At the same time, shelter inflation, which carries significant weight in both the CPI and PCE measures, has been slow to reflect declines in market-rate rents that took hold in 2023 and 2024. Daly and FRBSF economists have published research suggesting that shelter inflation will moderate significantly over the coming year, a view that underpins her more optimistic assessment.

Former Fed Vice Chair Richard Clarida, now at PIMCO, said the public disagreement reflects genuine uncertainty within the institution. 'It's not unusual to have diversity of views at the Fed, but the timing and the sharpness of the contrast between Daly and Waller is notable,' Clarida said in an interview. 'Chair Powell will need to provide a coherent framework at the upcoming press conference to reassure markets that the committee has a clear analytical anchor.'

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to deliver semiannual testimony to Congress next month, where he will face pointed questions about the committee's direction. Analysts at Barclays warned in a client note that 'the perception of a divided Fed could undermine the credibility of forward guidance and amplify rate volatility,' adding that clear messaging from Powell would be essential to restoring market confidence. The Fed's next policy meeting is scheduled for March 18–19.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publicly divergent statements from FRBSF President Daly and Fed Governor Waller on the inflation outlook risk undermining the credibility of the Fed's forward guidance, amplifying market volatility and inviting Congressional scrutiny of the committee's internal cohesion ahead of key policy decisions.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a working paper Monday highlighting elevated stress in commercial real estate loan portfolios at small and mid-sized banks, warning that concentrated exposures could amplify financial instability if office vacancy rates remain high.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released a working paper arguing that commercial real estate (CRE) loan concentrations at community and regional banks pose a 'meaningful but manageable' risk to financial stability, while cautioning that a prolonged period of elevated office vacancies combined with refinancing pressure could stress already-thin capital buffers at some institutions.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and Kevin Lansing, analyzed supervisory data from more than 4,500 banks and found that roughly 280 institutions with assets between $1 billion and $10 billion held CRE loans equivalent to more than 300% of their total capital — the threshold federal regulators use as a red flag for concentration risk. The authors found that approximately $400 billion in office and retail CRE loans held by these banks are scheduled to mature or refinance between 2024 and 2026, a window they described as a 'refinancing wall' that could force fire sales or loan write-downs if property valuations do not recover.

'While the largest banks have reduced their CRE exposures significantly following post-2008 regulatory reforms, smaller institutions have backfilled that gap and now face a more challenging environment than at any point in the past decade,' the paper stated. The authors noted that office vacancy rates in major U.S. cities, including San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Chicago, have climbed above 20% in the wake of the remote-work shift accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, materially reducing the collateral value supporting many loans.

Federal Reserve officials have repeatedly flagged CRE as a key area of supervisory focus. Fed Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged in congressional testimony in March 2024 that CRE losses were 'likely to cause some banks pain' but that the exposure was not large enough to threaten the broader financial system. The FRBSF paper broadly aligns with that assessment while adding granular analysis of which institutions face the greatest near-term pressure. The paper does not represent official Fed policy but is intended to inform supervisory and regulatory discussions.

Analysts at Moody's and S&P Global Ratings said the report underscores concerns they have flagged in their own assessments of regional bank credit quality. 'The refinancing wall is real, and the FRBSF paper puts hard numbers on a risk that has been somewhat abstract in prior public communications,' said Ana Arsov, a managing director at Moody's. Bank stocks in the KBW Regional Banking Index fell modestly on Monday following the paper's release, though traders said the move appeared more technical than a fundamental re-rating of the sector.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Supports Case for Gradual Rate Cuts in 2025
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly signaled Friday that moderating job gains and easing wage growth provide the central bank with room to proceed cautiously with interest rate reductions this year. Daly's remarks added nuance to an ongoing debate among policymakers about the pace of monetary easing.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that a cooling labor market gives policymakers the latitude to cut interest rates gradually in 2025, while cautioning that the central bank should not move so quickly that it risks reigniting inflation. Speaking at a Stanford University conference, Daly cited January payroll gains of 143,000 — below last year's monthly average — and a three-month average hourly earnings increase of 3.9% as evidence that the employment sector is returning to balance.

Daly, who votes on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, said she currently pencils in two 25-basis-point reductions in the federal funds rate for 2025, consistent with the median projection released at December's FOMC meeting. The federal funds rate currently stands in a range of 4.25% to 4.50% following three cuts totaling 100 basis points in the second half of 2024. 'We have made tremendous progress on inflation, but the last mile requires patience,' Daly told the audience.

Markets reacted modestly to Daly's comments, with fed funds futures pricing in roughly 45 basis points of easing by year-end, slightly below the Fed's own median projection. Treasury yields edged lower, with the 10-year note falling two basis points to 4.47%. Equity investors appeared largely unmoved, with the S&P 500 trading near flat in midday New York activity.

The San Francisco Fed chief acknowledged that uncertainty surrounding fiscal and trade policy added complexity to the outlook, suggesting the FOMC would remain data-dependent meeting by meeting. She noted that services inflation, particularly shelter costs, continued to run above levels consistent with the 2% PCE target. Core PCE inflation stood at 2.6% year-over-year in December, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis.

Economists at BofA Securities and Goldman Sachs interpreted Daly's remarks as broadly in line with consensus Fed messaging, though they flagged that any upside surprise in February CPI data could push rate cut expectations further into the year. The next FOMC decision is scheduled for March 18-19, with the committee widely expected to hold rates steady as it awaits additional data.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Inflation Persistence, Minutes Reveal Deepening Divisions
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Reserve's November 2022 policy meeting laid bare significant disagreement among officials about how long inflation would remain elevated and how far the central bank would need to raise interest rates. The disclosure rattled bond markets and raised questions about the coherence of the Fed's forward guidance strategy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve policymakers were sharply divided in November 2022 over the trajectory of inflation and the ultimate destination for interest rates, according to minutes released Wednesday that exposed fault lines within the institution at a critical juncture in its most aggressive tightening campaign in four decades. While all 12 voting members supported the fourth consecutive 75-basis-point rate increase at that meeting — bringing the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4.00% — private deliberations reflected deep uncertainty about how high rates would ultimately need to climb.

Some officials argued that services inflation, which Fed Chair Jerome Powell had flagged publicly as particularly stubborn, could persist well into 2023 and potentially require the terminal rate to exceed 5.25%. Others cautioned that the cumulative effect of rapid tightening had yet to fully flow through the economy and that overtightening posed a genuine risk of unnecessary job losses. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly was among those urging a more cautious approach, a position that put her at odds with some colleagues on the hawkish end of the spectrum.

The minutes triggered a selloff in short-dated Treasuries, with two-year yields rising seven basis points to 4.53%, as traders recalibrated expectations for the peak rate. The dollar strengthened marginally against a basket of major currencies. On Wall Street, strategists at JPMorgan and Citigroup described the minutes as more hawkish than anticipated, even as they had expected broad support for a forthcoming downshift to 50-basis-point increments in December.

The divergence in views complicated an already fraught communications challenge for the Fed. Critics, including several academic economists, argued that the central bank had been slow to recognize inflation's persistence in 2021 and was now risking overcorrection. Appearing before the Senate Banking Committee the prior week, Chair Powell had offered no explicit guidance on the terminal rate, a deliberate ambiguity that some senators found unsatisfying. Republican members pressed him on whether the Fed's credibility had been damaged by its earlier 'transitory' inflation characterization.

For the San Francisco Fed, which oversees banking activity in one of the nation's most rate-sensitive regions due to its heavy concentration of technology-sector financing, the November deliberations carried particular weight. Venture-backed lending, commercial real estate valuations, and startup payroll growth were all showing signs of stress, adding a regional dimension to the national policy debate. Daly's district-level perspective on tightening's real-economy effects was expected to figure prominently in December's discussions.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FOMC minutes reveal substantive internal disagreements about the inflation outlook and terminal rate, potentially undermining market confidence in forward guidance coherence and exposing the Fed to criticism that its policy messaging is inconsistent or reactive rather than strategic.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Pandemic-Era Excess Savings Largely Exhausted, Dampening Consumer Outlook
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Thursday estimating that the stockpile of excess savings accumulated by American households during the pandemic had been fully depleted by mid-2024, removing a key support for consumer spending. The findings carry implications for the Fed's assessment of economic momentum as it weighs further interest rate reductions.
full_text:
Research economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco said Thursday that roughly $2.1 trillion in excess household savings built up through pandemic-era fiscal transfers and constrained spending opportunities had been exhausted by approximately August 2024, a finding that could weigh on the consumer spending outlook heading into 2025. The paper, published as an FRBSF Economic Letter, updates an influential 2023 analysis by the same team and arrives as policymakers debate how much underlying economic momentum remains to support a soft landing.

The researchers, led by senior economist Hamza Abdelrahman and research associate Luiz Edgard Oliveira, estimated that excess savings peaked at approximately $2.1 trillion in mid-2021 before declining steadily as households drew down buffers to finance consumption amid elevated inflation. Lower-income households exhausted their pandemic savings significantly faster than higher-income cohorts, according to the study, which used Federal Reserve Flow of Funds data and adjusted consumer expenditure surveys.

The findings are relevant for the Federal Open Market Committee's deliberations because robust consumer spending has been one of the primary factors allowing the U.S. economy to avoid recession despite the sharpest rate-hiking cycle since the 1980s. Retail sales data in the fourth quarter of 2024 showed signs of softening, and credit card delinquency rates have risen from pandemic-era lows, lending empirical support to the San Francisco researchers' conclusions. Personal consumption expenditures grew at an annualized rate of 2.8% in the third quarter, but early indicators for the final quarter were more muted.

Economists at Wells Fargo and the Peterson Institute for International Economics said the FRBSF paper added to a growing body of evidence that the tailwinds from fiscal stimulus were fading, potentially slowing GDP growth toward its trend rate of roughly 2% in 2025. 'This research supports the view that the Fed has more room to cut rates without stoking demand-driven inflation,' said Sarah House, a senior economist at Wells Fargo. Others cautioned that excess savings are notoriously difficult to measure precisely and that methodological assumptions could materially affect the estimates.

The San Francisco Fed has been an active contributor to the academic and policy debate over inflation's causes and persistence. Earlier work by FRBSF economists on supply-side versus demand-side inflation drivers informed Fed communications during 2022 and 2023, when the institution faced scrutiny over its initial characterization of price pressures as transitory. The new savings research is expected to be referenced in upcoming speeches by regional Fed presidents and may factor into staff projections presented at the January FOMC meeting.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in U.S. labor markets may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate reductions, pushing back against market expectations for cuts as early as the first quarter of 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in U.S. labor markets may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate reductions, pushing back against market expectations for cuts as early as the first quarter of 2024. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly cited November's nonfarm payrolls report — which showed employers added 199,000 jobs — as evidence that the economy remains resilient despite 525 basis points of cumulative tightening since March 2022.

Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, emphasized that while headline CPI has declined meaningfully from its June 2022 peak of 9.1%, core services inflation — which strips out shelter and energy — remains elevated at roughly 4.0% on a year-over-year basis. 'Two percent is our goal, and we are not there yet,' Daly told the audience. 'Declaring victory prematurely would be a serious policy mistake that could require even more restrictive policy down the road.'

Market pricing in federal funds futures contracts shifted modestly following the remarks. Traders scaled back the probability of a 25-basis-point cut at the March 2024 FOMC meeting to approximately 44%, down from 55% earlier in the session, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool. The benchmark federal funds rate currently sits in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level since 2001.

Daly's comments align with a broader messaging effort from Fed officials to temper aggressive rate-cut bets that have contributed to a loosening of financial conditions — an outcome that could undermine the central bank's inflation-fighting progress. Fed Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged in his December press conference that rate cuts were 'beginning to come into view,' a phrase that some analysts interpreted as more dovish than intended. Treasury yields fell sharply following Powell's remarks, with the 10-year note dropping roughly 40 basis points in the days after the meeting.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their Fed outlook to pencil in the first cut in May 2024 following Daly's remarks, while Barclays maintained its call for a June start to the easing cycle. 'The Fed is in a delicate communication phase where the data remain ambiguous and officials are trying to preserve optionality,' wrote Jan Hatzius, Goldman's chief economist, in a note to clients. 'Daly's pushback today is consistent with a committee that wants to avoid front-running its own decisions.'
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's December comments were interpreted as more dovish than intended, triggering a sharp rally in Treasuries and an undesired loosening of financial conditions. Daly's subsequent pushback creates visible internal tension in Fed messaging that markets may find confusing and that could undermine the committee's credibility on forward guidance.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady, Opens Door to September Cut as Inflation Nears Target
snippet: The Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged at a two-decade high on Wednesday but signaled growing confidence that inflation is cooling sufficiently to justify a reduction in borrowing costs as soon as its next meeting in September.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged at a two-decade high on Wednesday but signaled growing confidence that inflation is cooling sufficiently to justify a reduction in borrowing costs as soon as its next meeting in September. The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee to maintain the federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% range was widely anticipated, but the shift in accompanying language drew immediate attention from investors and analysts who have spent months parsing the central bank's every word for signs of an imminent pivot.

The FOMC's post-meeting statement replaced previous language describing inflation as 'elevated' with a formulation noting that the committee has gained 'greater confidence' that price pressures are moving 'sustainably' toward its 2% objective. Personal consumption expenditures inflation — the Fed's preferred gauge — stood at 2.5% in June, down sharply from a peak of 7.0% in mid-2022. Core PCE, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, came in at 2.6% for the same period.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference in Washington, declined to explicitly pre-commit to a September cut but came closer than at any previous meeting to endorsing one. 'A reduction in our policy rate could be on the table as soon as the next meeting,' Powell said, adding that the committee would assess incoming data on inflation, employment, and financial conditions before making a final determination. Stocks rallied on the remarks, with the S&P 500 adding roughly 1.5% on the session.

The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's research division released a complementary working paper this week estimating that the so-called 'neutral rate' of interest — the level that neither stimulates nor restrains economic growth — has risen to approximately 0.8% in real terms, up from near-zero estimates that prevailed before the pandemic. If accurate, the finding suggests that the Fed will not need to cut rates as aggressively in the current easing cycle as it did following previous downturns, since policy would not be considered tight until the federal funds rate falls well below current levels.

Economists broadly welcomed the FOMC's messaging shift but cautioned that the path to easing remains data-dependent. 'The door is open, but it's not yet swinging,' said Julia Coronado, founder of MacroPolicy Perspectives. 'The July jobs report and the August CPI reading will be critical inputs.' Labor market conditions remain healthy by historical standards, with the unemployment rate at 4.1% in June, though the pace of hiring has slowed notably from the breakneck levels of 2022 and early 2023. The Fed faces the delicate task of easing policy gradually enough to avoid reigniting inflation while not waiting so long that a weakening job market spirals into a more serious economic downturn.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Silicon Valley Bank Collapse Puts Fed's Supervisory Role Under Scrutiny
snippet: The sudden failure of Silicon Valley Bank on Friday has intensified scrutiny of the Federal Reserve's oversight of mid-sized lenders, raising questions about whether supervisors at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco adequately flagged the institution's mounting interest-rate risk before its dramatic collapse.
full_text:
The sudden failure of Silicon Valley Bank on Friday has intensified scrutiny of the Federal Reserve's oversight of mid-sized lenders, raising questions about whether supervisors at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco adequately flagged the institution's mounting interest-rate risk before its dramatic collapse. SVB Financial Group's SIVB.O bank unit was shut down by California regulators and placed into FDIC receivership after a failed capital raise triggered a depositor run that drained roughly $42 billion in a single day — the largest bank failure since Washington Mutual in 2008.

The San Francisco Fed, which served as SVB's primary federal supervisor, had reportedly issued the bank a confidential supervisory warning known as a 'matter requiring attention' related to its interest-rate risk management practices in 2022, according to people familiar with the matter. Despite the warning, SVB continued to hold an outsize portfolio of long-duration bonds that lost substantial value as the Federal Reserve raised rates at the fastest pace in four decades. When the bank was forced to crystallize those losses to raise capital, confidence evaporated rapidly.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell announced Monday that the central bank would conduct an internal review of its supervision of SVB, to be led by Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr. The review is expected to be completed and made public by May. 'I welcome this thorough and transparent investigation,' Powell said in a brief statement. 'The events surrounding Silicon Valley Bank demand a careful examination of what happened and why.' Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, Federal Reserve officials, and FDIC Chair Martin Gruenberg held a joint call Sunday evening with President Biden to coordinate the federal response, which ultimately included guaranteeing all deposits at both SVB and New York's Signature Bank.

The episode has reignited a long-running debate about whether the 2018 rollback of certain Dodd-Frank provisions — which raised the threshold for enhanced prudential standards from $50 billion in assets to $250 billion — contributed to supervisory gaps at institutions like SVB. Critics, including several Democratic senators, have called for legislation to reinstate stricter oversight for banks with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion. Industry groups have pushed back, arguing that SVB's failure was idiosyncratic and reflected poor management decisions rather than systemic regulatory deficiencies.

The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco declined to comment on specific supervisory actions related to SVB, citing confidentiality restrictions under federal banking law. Economists warned that the collapse, combined with the weekend failure of Signature Bank, could tighten credit conditions meaningfully for small and mid-sized businesses, complicating the Fed's already difficult task of navigating a soft landing. 'This is a financial stability shock layered on top of an inflation shock,' said former Fed Governor Jeremy Stein. 'It makes the FOMC's job considerably harder and may argue for a pause in rate hikes at the March meeting.'
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF's supervisory record with respect to SVB is under direct public and congressional scrutiny. Confidential supervisory warnings that were apparently unheeded represent a significant reputational risk for the San Francisco Fed and the broader Federal Reserve System. The internal review led by Barr could surface findings that are politically damaging and could fuel legislative efforts to restructure Fed supervisory authority. The juxtaposition of aggressive monetary tightening — which contributed to SVB's bond losses — with the Fed's supervisory failures creates a compounded communication challenge.

headline: FRBSF's Daly Says Fed Must Stay Vigilant on Inflation Despite Cooling Signals
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that policymakers cannot declare victory over inflation too soon, even as recent data show price pressures easing from their four-decade highs. Daly emphasized the need for sustained restrictive policy to ensure inflation returns durably to the Fed's 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly delivered a cautionary message Thursday, urging market participants not to interpret recent improvements in inflation data as a signal that the Federal Reserve is prepared to ease monetary policy anytime soon. Speaking at a virtual event hosted by the Brookings Institution, Daly cited the still-elevated level of core PCE inflation—running at 3.7% year-over-year as of the latest reading—as evidence that the central bank's work is far from complete.

The remarks came at a particularly sensitive moment for financial markets, where futures pricing had shifted aggressively toward rate cuts beginning as early as the first quarter of 2024. Daly pushed back against that interpretation, noting that the Federal Open Market Committee's commitment to returning inflation to its 2% target is unconditional. 'Declaring premature victory would be a serious policy mistake,' she said, echoing language used by Chair Jerome Powell in recent congressional testimony.

Daly pointed to several data points that complicate the inflation picture, including persistently elevated shelter costs, a still-tight labor market with unemployment near 3.9%, and wage growth running above levels consistent with the Fed's price stability mandate. She noted that the San Francisco Fed's own research suggests that services inflation, excluding housing, remains stickier than headline figures imply.

Markets responded to Daly's comments with a modest sell-off in rate-sensitive assets. The two-year Treasury yield climbed four basis points to 4.82%, while the S&P 500 Index trimmed gains to close roughly flat on the day. Interest rate swap markets scaled back the probability of a March 2024 cut from approximately 65% to around 52% following her remarks.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted that Daly's comments align with a broader messaging effort from FOMC officials to temper expectations of imminent easing. 'The Fed is clearly concerned about financial conditions loosening too quickly through market pricing,' said Jan Hatzius, chief economist at Goldman Sachs, adding that a June cut remained their base case. The San Francisco Fed president is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, giving her remarks added weight as the committee heads into its January meeting.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks could be perceived as contradicting earlier signals from other Fed officials who suggested a more data-dependent approach to cuts, creating potential for mixed messaging that may confuse market participants and invite criticism that the Fed is moving the goalposts on policy guidance.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady at 23-Year High as Officials Weigh Path to Cuts
snippet: The Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged in a range of 5.25% to 5.50% at its January meeting, as policymakers sought more confidence that inflation was sustainably moving toward their 2% goal before beginning to ease policy. The decision was unanimous, though the post-meeting statement was parsed closely for any shift in the committee's forward guidance.
full_text:
The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to hold the federal funds rate in its target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, a level not seen since 2001, opting for patience as officials assess whether the inflation battle has been won decisively enough to justify the beginning of a rate-cutting cycle. The decision was widely anticipated by markets, but investors scrutinized every word of the accompanying statement for clues about the timing and pace of eventual easing.

The most closely watched change in the statement was the removal of language referencing the possibility of 'additional policy firming,' a subtle but significant shift that analysts interpreted as the committee effectively closing the door on further rate hikes. In its place, the FOMC emphasized that it does not expect it will be appropriate to reduce the target range until it has gained greater confidence that inflation is moving sustainably toward 2%. Chair Jerome Powell, at his post-meeting press conference, was careful not to signal an imminent cut, stating explicitly that a March reduction was 'not the base case.'

Powell's comments sent Treasury yields climbing and equity markets lower, as traders who had assigned a meaningful probability to a March cut were forced to reprice their expectations. The yield on the two-year note, which is particularly sensitive to near-term rate expectations, rose nine basis points to 4.43%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 317 points, or roughly 0.8%, on the day.

Fed officials, including San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester, had telegraphed caution about moving too quickly in the weeks leading up to the meeting. Daly in particular had argued publicly that the neutral rate of interest may have risen in the post-pandemic economy, implying that current policy may be less restrictive than traditional models suggest—a view that has gained adherents among several regional Fed bank research staffs.

Economists were divided on the implications. 'The Fed is threading a needle between maintaining credibility on inflation and avoiding an unnecessary recession,' said Ellen Zentner, chief U.S. economist at Morgan Stanley. She maintained a May cut as her baseline forecast. Meanwhile, former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers warned that easing too quickly risks a second wave of inflation, citing parallels to the stop-go monetary policy of the 1970s—a historical comparison that Fed officials have themselves invoked to justify their cautious approach.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Rising Financial Stability Risks Amid Commercial Real Estate Stress
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a new research paper Monday warning that stress in commercial real estate markets poses a meaningful risk to the stability of regional and community banks, particularly those with concentrated exposure to office properties. The paper stopped short of predicting systemic contagion but called for heightened supervisory vigilance.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released a research paper identifying commercial real estate as one of the most significant near-term risks to the stability of the U.S. banking system, with particular concern focused on smaller regional lenders that have outsized concentrations of office and retail property loans on their books. The paper, authored by researchers in the FRBSF's economic research division, analyzed loan-level data from bank call reports and found that approximately 14% of community banks had commercial real estate exposures exceeding 300% of their capital—a supervisory threshold regulators have flagged as a potential warning sign.

The findings arrive at a sensitive moment for bank regulators. The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023 heightened public and congressional scrutiny of the Federal Reserve's supervisory function, and the FRBSF—as SVB's primary federal regulator—faced particularly intense criticism for missing warning signs at the institution before its failure. The new research represents, in part, an effort by the San Francisco Fed to demonstrate that it is proactively identifying systemic vulnerabilities before they metastasize into broader crises.

The paper noted that remote work trends have structurally impaired demand for office space in major metropolitan areas, with national office vacancy rates reaching record highs of approximately 19.6% in late 2024. Falling property valuations have in turn eroded the collateral value backing many commercial real estate loans originated during the low-rate environment of 2020 and 2021. Refinancing risk is acute, the authors noted, as loans originated at sub-3% rates are maturing into an environment where lending rates remain well above 6%.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking separately at an industry conference, acknowledged the vulnerabilities but sought to reassure markets that the banking system as a whole remains well-capitalized. 'The aggregate capital ratios of the banking sector are strong, and our supervisory teams are closely engaged with institutions where we see elevated risk,' she said. Daly did not elaborate on specific supervisory actions underway.

The research is expected to inform discussions at the Financial Stability Oversight Council, which is chaired by Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen and includes the Federal Reserve among its voting members. Bank analysts at JPMorgan Chase noted that the FRBSF's findings are broadly consistent with stress scenarios the firm's own research team has been tracking. 'The CRE problem is real but manageable at the macro level if it unfolds gradually,' wrote analysts in a note to clients. 'The risk is a disorderly repricing that forces simultaneous loan recognition across multiple institutions.'
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The publication draws renewed attention to the FRBSF's prior supervisory failures at Silicon Valley Bank, potentially reigniting congressional and public criticism of the Fed's regulatory function. Daly's reassuring remarks alongside a research paper highlighting elevated risks could be perceived as inconsistent messaging that undermines credibility.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's timeline for interest rate reductions, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a Stanford University economic forum, Daly emphasized that the Fed needs 'more confidence' before easing monetary policy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's timeline for interest rate reductions, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a Stanford University economic forum, Daly emphasized that the Fed needs 'more confidence' before easing monetary policy.

The unemployment rate held at 3.7% in the most recent Bureau of Labor Statistics report, well below the Fed's longer-run estimate of roughly 4.1%. Nonfarm payrolls added 256,000 jobs in the prior month, a figure that Daly described as 'inconsistent with the kind of cooling we need to see to feel comfortable moving rates lower.' The federal funds rate currently stands in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level in more than two decades.

Markets had priced in as many as six quarter-point cuts for 2024 at the start of the year, but those expectations have been repeatedly pared back as economic data came in hotter than anticipated. Fed funds futures now imply fewer than three cuts by year-end, with the first move not fully priced until the September FOMC meeting. Daly's comments align with a broader shift in tone among policymakers following the January and February CPI readings, both of which exceeded analyst forecasts.

Daly was careful to note that the Fed's next move remains a cut, not a hike, provided inflation continues on its current trajectory. 'The question is not whether we cut, but when and how quickly,' she said. She added that the balance sheet runoff — currently proceeding at a cap of $60 billion in Treasuries and $35 billion in mortgage-backed securities per month — is likely to continue even after rate cuts begin, providing an additional form of policy restraint.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their Fed call following Daly's remarks, now projecting the first cut in July rather than June. 'The FRBSF president's comments reinforce a patient Fed narrative that has become the dominant theme in 2024,' wrote chief U.S. economist David Mericle in a note to clients. Treasury yields rose modestly on the day, with the 2-year note climbing four basis points to 4.73%, reflecting the recalibration of rate-cut expectations.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split on Inflation Outlook as Mixed Signals Sow Market Confusion
snippet: A chorus of conflicting statements from Federal Reserve policymakers this week has rattled financial markets and raised uncomfortable questions about the cohesion of the central bank's communications strategy, with officials from San Francisco to Atlanta offering sharply divergent views on whether inflation is cooling fast enough to justify rate cuts this year.
full_text:
