A chorus of conflicting statements from Federal Reserve policymakers this week has rattled financial markets and raised uncomfortable questions about the cohesion of the central bank's communications strategy, with officials from San Francisco to Atlanta offering sharply divergent views on whether inflation is cooling fast enough to justify rate cuts this year.

The confusion began Monday when Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta President Raphael Bostic said he now envisions only one rate cut in 2024, down from his earlier projection of two, citing stickier-than-expected services inflation. By Wednesday, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly struck a more measured tone, telling a San Francisco audience that two cuts remained 'a reasonable baseline' if the data cooperated. The disparity prompted a 15 basis-point swing in 2-year Treasury yields over 48 hours and sent the S&P 500 down 1.2% on Wednesday before a partial recovery Thursday.

The episode underscores the challenge Fed Chair Jerome Powell faces in managing the communications of a 12-member Federal Open Market Committee, each of whom speaks frequently in public forums. Former New York Fed President William Dudley said the divergence was 'not unusual at turning points in the cycle' but acknowledged that 'the market is struggling to identify a central tendency.' The Fed's Summary of Economic Projections, or dot plot, last showed a median of three cuts for 2024, but that forecast is increasingly viewed as stale given subsequent inflation data.

At the heart of the disagreement is a debate over the so-called 'last mile' of disinflation — the difficulty of squeezing inflation from roughly 3% down to the Fed's 2% PCE target. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, ran at 2.8% year-over-year in the most recent reading, still a full 80 basis points above target. Services inflation ex-housing, a metric Powell has specifically highlighted, has proved particularly stubborn, hovering near 4% annualized over the past three months.

Bond market analysts at JPMorgan warned clients that the Fed risks a 'credibility erosion' if officials continue to send mixed signals ahead of the May FOMC meeting. 'When every speech moves the market in a different direction, it undermines the forward guidance framework the Fed has spent years building,' wrote rates strategist Jay Barry. Powell is scheduled to deliver semi-annual congressional testimony next week, which markets will scrutinize closely for any attempt to reconcile the divergent voices within the committee.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public statements from regional Fed presidents — including FRBSF's Daly — are creating significant market volatility and undermining the Fed's forward guidance framework. The divergence risks damaging institutional credibility ahead of a critical FOMC meeting and congressional testimony, and may fuel narratives of internal disarray at a time when public trust in the Fed is already strained by the prolonged high-rate environment.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Regional Banking Vulnerabilities Amid Commercial Real Estate Stress
snippet: A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Tuesday identified elevated credit risks at mid-sized U.S. banks with concentrated exposure to commercial real estate loans, warning that declining office valuations and rising vacancy rates could pressure capital buffers if interest rates remain elevated for an extended period.
full_text:
A new paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Tuesday identified elevated credit risks at mid-sized U.S. banks with concentrated exposure to commercial real estate loans, warning that declining office valuations and rising vacancy rates could pressure capital buffers if interest rates remain elevated for an extended period.

The FRBSF research, authored by economists Fernanda Nechio and John Krainer, found that banks with commercial real estate concentrations exceeding 300% of total risk-based capital — a threshold used by supervisors as an informal benchmark — showed materially higher probabilities of stress under adverse scenarios modeled on a further 15% decline in office property values. The paper stopped short of naming specific institutions but noted that the risk was disproportionately concentrated among banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion.

The findings arrive as regulators grapple with the aftermath of the March 2023 bank failures that claimed Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic, all of which were subject to FRBSF supervision. Office vacancy rates in major U.S. markets have climbed to historic highs, with San Francisco itself recording vacancy above 30% in the central business district — a particularly acute concern given the Fed's regional footprint. Delinquency rates on commercial real estate loans held by banks rose to 1.17% in the fourth quarter, the highest since 2014, according to Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. data.

FRBSF President Mary Daly, in brief remarks accompanying the paper's release, said the research was intended to inform supervisory practices rather than signal imminent systemic risk. 'Our banking system overall remains well-capitalized and resilient,' Daly said, 'but it is appropriate and prudent to identify pockets of vulnerability before they become larger problems.' The Fed, Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, and FDIC have jointly signaled increased scrutiny of commercial real estate concentrations in upcoming examination cycles.

Analysts at Moody's said the FRBSF paper aligns with stress scenarios the ratings agency has been running internally and adds regulatory weight to concerns the private sector has flagged for more than a year. 'This kind of transparent, forward-looking supervisory research from a regional Fed is exactly what the system needs after the credibility hit of 2023,' said banking analyst Ana Arsov. The FRBSF paper is available on the bank's public website and has been submitted to a peer-reviewed journal for publication.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that persistent strength in U.S. labor markets makes it difficult to justify imminent interest rate cuts, pushing back on market expectations for aggressive monetary easing in 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that persistent strength in U.S. labor markets makes it difficult to justify imminent interest rate cuts, pushing back on market expectations for aggressive monetary easing in 2024. Speaking at a forum hosted by the University of California, San Diego, Daly emphasized that the central bank needs to see sustained progress on inflation before adjusting its restrictive policy stance.

The unemployment rate held at 3.7% in November, and nonfarm payrolls added 199,000 jobs, according to data released earlier this month. Daly noted that such figures, while showing some moderation, do not yet constitute the 'convincing evidence' the Fed requires to begin loosening financial conditions. 'We have made significant progress, but the job is not done,' she told the audience of economists and business leaders.

Financial markets had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 following softer inflation readings in October and November, with the federal funds rate currently sitting in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%. Daly's comments appeared to temper those expectations, contributing to a modest pullback in Treasury prices and a slight firming of the dollar index during afternoon trading.

Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, came in at 3.5% year-over-year in October, still well above the central bank's 2% target. Daly reiterated that monetary policy operates with long and variable lags, cautioning that officials must weigh the risk of easing too soon against the risk of holding rates elevated for too long. She declined to specify when the first cut might occur, saying the decision would remain 'data dependent.'

Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase have revised their rate-cut forecasts following a string of hawkish Fed communications this week, now projecting the first reduction to arrive no sooner than the second quarter of 2024. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for January 30-31, at which point policymakers are broadly expected to hold rates steady while continuing to refine forward guidance.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments directly contradict market pricing of six rate cuts, creating potential for a sharp market repricing event and public criticism that the Fed is insufficiently responsive to easing inflation pressures. Divergence from implied market expectations risks being characterized as a communication failure.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time as Inflation Fight Intensifies
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase on Wednesday, lifting the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4.00% as policymakers doubled down on their most aggressive tightening campaign in four decades.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase on Wednesday, lifting the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4.00% as policymakers doubled down on their most aggressive tightening campaign in four decades. The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee underscored the central bank's determination to bring inflation back to its 2% target even at the cost of slower economic growth and rising unemployment.

Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the announcement, struck a notably hawkish tone while also opening the door, for the first time, to a potential downshift in the pace of rate increases at upcoming meetings. 'It is very premature to be thinking about pausing,' Powell told reporters, adding that the ultimate level of rates will likely need to be higher than previously anticipated. The comment sent stocks sharply lower, erasing earlier gains that had built on a sentence in the FOMC statement flagging that officials would take into account 'cumulative tightening' and policy lags when determining future hikes.

The Fed's aggressive posture reflects the stubborn persistence of inflation, which reached 8.2% on a consumer price index basis in September—more than four times the central bank's target. Core CPI, which excludes food and energy prices, rose 6.6% over the same period, the highest reading since 1982. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a voting member of the FOMC this year, supported the decision, consistent with her recent public statements calling for sustained policy tightening until price stability is restored.

The rate increase came despite mounting evidence that the U.S. economy is slowing. Third-quarter GDP rebounded at a 2.6% annualized rate after two consecutive quarters of contraction, but much of that growth was driven by trade flows rather than domestic demand. Housing activity has cratered under the weight of mortgage rates that have nearly doubled since the start of the year, and corporate earnings guidance has grown increasingly cautious.

Economists are now debating whether the Fed risks engineering a severe recession in its pursuit of price stability. Former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers, a longtime critic of the Fed's initial slow response to inflation, said Wednesday that while the tightening was necessary, officials must be careful not to overcorrect. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for December 13-14, at which point markets widely expect a smaller half-point increase as officials assess the impact of their historic tightening campaign.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's simultaneous suggestion of a potential pace slowdown and insistence that pausing is 'premature' created contradictory signals that caused significant market volatility. The messaging disconnect between the FOMC statement and Powell's press conference remarks poses a Fed communications credibility risk.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds AI Productivity Gains Could Help Ease Inflationary Pressures Over Medium Term
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday suggesting that widespread adoption of artificial intelligence in the U.S. economy could boost productivity growth sufficiently to help ease inflationary pressures over the next several years, though near-term price effects remain uncertain.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday suggesting that widespread adoption of artificial intelligence in the U.S. economy could boost productivity growth sufficiently to help ease inflationary pressures over the next several years, though near-term price effects remain uncertain. The working paper, authored by FRBSF senior economist Sylvain Leduc and research associate Zachary Mayer, stops short of offering a timeline for when AI-driven productivity gains might materialize at a macroeconomic scale.

The paper estimates that if AI adoption follows a trajectory similar to earlier general-purpose technologies such as electricity or computing, annual productivity growth could accelerate by between 0.5 and 1.5 percentage points over a 10-to-15-year horizon. Such an improvement, the authors argue, would exert downward pressure on unit labor costs and help anchor inflation closer to the Fed's 2% target without requiring as restrictive a monetary policy stance as might otherwise be necessary.

FRBSF President Mary Daly, who has spoken publicly about the economic implications of AI in several recent forums, welcomed the research but cautioned against drawing premature policy conclusions. 'Productivity is ultimately disinflationary, but the Fed must make policy based on conditions as they exist today, not projections of what technology might deliver in the future,' Daly said in a brief statement accompanying the paper's release. The San Francisco Fed has positioned itself as a thought leader on technology-driven economic change, reflecting the district's geographic proximity to Silicon Valley.

The research arrives at a moment when Federal Reserve officials are carefully evaluating the path back toward policy normalization after raising the federal funds rate to a 23-year high of 5.25% to 5.50% in the 2022-2023 tightening cycle. Some economists have argued that AI-fueled productivity growth represents an upside scenario that could allow the Fed to achieve a so-called soft landing, guiding inflation back to target without a significant rise in unemployment.

Other analysts were more skeptical, noting that historical episodes of technological transformation have often been accompanied by significant labor market disruption and uneven distributional effects that can complicate monetary policymaking. 'The macroeconomic benefits of AI are real, but they are not going to rescue the Fed from the hard work of getting inflation down in the near term,' said Julia Coronado, president of MacroPolicy Perspectives and a former Fed economist. The FRBSF paper will be presented at a conference on technology and the macroeconomy scheduled for early 2025.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Justifies Patient Rate Approach
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly signaled Friday that policymakers can afford to be methodical about future rate adjustments as job growth moderates and inflation edges closer to the central bank's target. Her comments came ahead of next month's FOMC meeting, where officials are expected to weigh the pace of any potential easing.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that a gradually cooling labor market gives policymakers the breathing room to be deliberate about future interest rate decisions, reinforcing expectations that the central bank is unlikely to accelerate the pace of rate cuts heading into the second half of 2024.

Daly, speaking at a policy forum hosted by the Brookings Institution in Washington, noted that nonfarm payrolls have averaged roughly 190,000 per month over the past three months, a deceleration from the 270,000 pace seen a year earlier. The unemployment rate, while still historically low at 3.9%, has ticked up from its 3.4% cycle trough, providing early evidence that monetary tightening is filtering through to the broader economy.

'We are in a good position,' Daly told attendees. 'Inflation is coming down, the labor market is coming into better balance, and we have time to be thoughtful about the next steps.' Her remarks broadly aligned with messaging from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who has emphasized the need for greater confidence that inflation is sustainably returning to the 2% objective before cutting rates.

Markets interpreted Daly's comments as reinforcing a 'higher for longer' rate posture, with fed funds futures pricing in roughly two 25-basis-point cuts by year-end, down from the six reductions traders had anticipated in January. The 10-year Treasury yield edged up 4 basis points to 4.38% following her remarks. The S&P 500 pared earlier gains but remained in positive territory.

Daly acknowledged the inherent risks on both sides of the Fed's dual mandate. Cutting too quickly, she warned, could allow inflation to re-accelerate, while maintaining rates at current restrictive levels for too long risks unnecessarily weakening the job market. 'The costs of getting this wrong in either direction are real,' she said, adding that incoming data on core PCE inflation and wage growth would be 'critical inputs' to the committee's deliberations in coming months.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Rate Path, Complicating Market Outlook
snippet: A wave of conflicting statements from Federal Reserve policymakers this week has left investors struggling to price the trajectory of interest rates, with some officials pushing for near-term cuts while others advocate holding policy steady through year-end. The dissonance risks undermining the Fed's carefully managed communications strategy.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials are delivering a cacophony of contradictory signals about the future of interest rates, creating confusion in financial markets and raising questions about whether the central bank's communications apparatus is functioning as intended.

In the span of four days this week, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee suggested that restrictive policy should be eased soon to avoid overtightening, while Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan argued that persistently elevated services inflation warranted keeping rates unchanged through at least September. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, meanwhile, attempted a middle path, saying the committee should remain 'open-minded' but data-dependent — a formulation critics called deliberately vague.

The divergence reflects genuine disagreement within the Federal Open Market Committee about how to interpret a string of stronger-than-expected inflation readings in early 2024, which followed months of encouraging progress toward the Fed's 2% target. Core CPI came in at 3.8% year-over-year in March, well above the Fed's preferred PCE gauge of around 2.7%, complicating the committee's narrative that disinflation was on track.

'This is not a well-coordinated communications week for the Fed,' said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer/Monetary Policy Analytics. 'When you have this many voting and non-voting members speaking in different directions simultaneously, you get market volatility and a credibility problem.' The two-year Treasury yield swung in a 15-basis-point range across the week, an unusually wide band for a period devoid of scheduled data releases.

Former Fed communications director Michelle Smith, now at Columbia University's School of International and Public Affairs, said the current dynamic reflects the genuine complexity of the policy environment rather than a breakdown in messaging discipline. 'There is authentic disagreement on the committee, and some of that is going to surface in public remarks,' she said. Still, several market participants noted that the Fed could benefit from clearer pre-FOMC communication blackout enforcement, as the volume of speeches in recent weeks has exceeded typical norms. A Fed spokesperson declined to comment.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Multiple Fed officials delivering contradictory signals on rate policy within the same week risks undermining central bank credibility and market confidence. The pattern of simultaneous divergent messaging threatens the Fed's carefully cultivated communications framework and could be characterized in press coverage as a loss of policy coherence.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Points to Persistent Services Inflation as Key Risk to 2% Goal
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warns that stubbornly elevated inflation in labor-intensive service sectors could delay the central bank's return to its 2% target by a year or more. The research adds to a growing body of analysis suggesting that the so-called 'last mile' of disinflation may prove more difficult than the initial decline from peak price pressures.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday indicating that inflation in core services categories — which exclude housing and energy costs — has proven far more resistant to monetary tightening than goods inflation, potentially extending the timeline for the Fed to achieve its 2% price stability objective.

The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists Adam Shapiro and Daniel Wilson, analyzed price dynamics across 140 service subcategories between 2021 and early 2024. The research found that roughly 60% of the current gap between actual inflation and the Fed's target can be attributed to services inflation tied to labor costs, including healthcare, personal care, and food services. Goods prices, by contrast, have largely normalized following pandemic-era supply chain disruptions.

The findings carry significant implications for FOMC deliberations. If services inflation remains sticky, the Fed may need to maintain restrictive interest rate policy — with the federal funds rate currently in the 5.25%-5.50% target range — for longer than financial markets currently expect. Futures markets as of Monday priced in roughly two rate cuts in 2024, but several Wall Street economists said the FRBSF research bolstered the case for a more cautious easing cycle.

'The key insight here is that services inflation is fundamentally a wage-driven phenomenon, and wage growth remains above levels consistent with 2% inflation on a sustained basis,' said Sarah House, senior economist at Wells Fargo. Average hourly earnings rose 4.1% year-over-year in the most recent employment report, still above the roughly 3% pace most Fed economists view as compatible with the 2% inflation target.

FRBSF President Mary Daly has previously cited services inflation as a top concern and has said the bank's regional contacts in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District — which spans California, Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Arizona, Idaho, Utah, Alaska, and Hawaii — continue to report wage pressures, particularly in hospitality and healthcare. The research adds an empirical underpinning to those qualitative observations and is expected to inform the Fed's next quarterly Summary of Economic Projections, due in June.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room to Hold Rates Steady
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly indicated that softening labor market conditions may allow policymakers to maintain the current interest rate stance, even as inflation remains above the central bank's 2% target. Her comments come ahead of the January FOMC meeting and signal a cautious, data-dependent approach.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Monday that a cooling labor market provides the central bank with breathing room to keep interest rates steady, pushing back against expectations for either an imminent cut or additional hikes. Speaking at a conference hosted by the Bay Area Council in San Francisco, Daly cited recent declines in job openings, slower wage growth, and a modest uptick in the unemployment rate as evidence that monetary policy is working as intended.

"The labor market is coming into better balance, and that is exactly what we need to see," Daly told attendees. "We don't have to rush. The data are telling us we can be patient." Her remarks aligned with the broader consensus emerging from Federal Open Market Committee members who have emphasized a wait-and-see posture in the wake of 525 basis points of cumulative rate hikes since March 2022.

Markets trimmed bets on a March rate cut following Daly's comments, with fed funds futures pricing in roughly a 55% probability of a reduction at the May meeting, down from nearly 70% earlier in the session. The two-year Treasury yield, which is most sensitive to near-term Fed policy expectations, rose 6 basis points to 4.42%.

Daly has been among the more vocal advocates for caution within the FOMC, repeatedly warning against the risk of easing prematurely and reigniting inflationary pressures. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index, stood at 2.6% year-over-year as of the most recent reading, still above the 2% target but meaningfully below its June 2022 peak of 7.1%.

Economists at Bank of America and Goldman Sachs both noted that Daly's tone was consistent with a Fed on hold through at least the first quarter. "The Fed is threading a needle between doing too little and doing too much," said BofA senior economist Aditya Bhave. "Daly's comments suggest the bar for a cut remains high without a more definitive deterioration in the labor market or a sharper fall in inflation."
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Aggressive Rate Hikes Draw Backlash as Recession Fears Mount
snippet: The Federal Reserve's rapid interest-rate increases are drawing growing criticism from economists, lawmakers, and housing advocates who warn that the central bank risks engineering an unnecessary recession to combat inflation. A new San Francisco Fed working paper acknowledging the distributional costs of tight monetary policy has added fuel to the debate.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve is facing an unusual surge of public and academic criticism as its campaign of interest-rate increases—the most aggressive in four decades—begins to bite across the economy. Critics ranging from progressive lawmakers on Capitol Hill to mainstream economists argue that the central bank may be overcorrecting for inflation that was, in large part, driven by supply-side disruptions outside the Fed's control.

A working paper published last week by researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that lower-income households bear a disproportionate burden from rate-driven economic slowdowns, given their higher sensitivity to job losses and limited financial buffers. The paper, while framed as academic research rather than official policy guidance, was seized upon by critics as an implicit admission that the Fed's tools are blunt instruments with unequal consequences.

"The Fed doesn't have a scalpel, it has a sledgehammer," said Sen. Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts, one of the most persistent critics of Chair Jerome Powell's rate-hiking campaign. "And the people getting hurt are the ones who can least afford it—renters who can't buy a home, workers who are first to get laid off."

The Fed has raised the federal funds rate by 500 basis points since March 2022, bringing it to a target range of 5.25%-5.50%. Mortgage rates have climbed above 7%, the highest level since 2001, effectively freezing large segments of the housing market. Meanwhile, delinquency rates on credit cards and auto loans have risen to post-pandemic highs, raising concerns about consumer financial stress.

Fed officials, including San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and Governor Lisa Cook, have defended the policy path as necessary to preserve the central bank's inflation-fighting credibility and prevent a wage-price spiral. "Price stability is the foundation of a healthy economy for everyone," Daly said at a Stanford University event last month. "Without it, the pain is longer and deeper."

Nevertheless, the reputational challenge facing the Fed is real. Former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers, an early advocate for aggressive hikes, cautioned that the central bank must now be careful about the optics of its communications. "The Fed is in a delicate moment," Summers told a Brookings Institution forum. "How it explains its decisions will matter as much as the decisions themselves."
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF working paper on distributional costs of rate hikes, while academic in nature, has been weaponized in the public debate to undermine the Fed's policy stance. This creates a communication risk: research outputs may be interpreted as contradicting official Fed guidance, exposing the institution to charges of internal inconsistency and generating political pressure that could compromise central bank independence.

headline: FRBSF Research Points to Easing Inflation Pressures in Services Sector
snippet: A new economic letter from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco suggests that inflation in the services sector—a key area of concern for policymakers—is showing signs of moderation, potentially supporting the case for interest rate cuts later in 2024. The analysis, however, cautions that shelter costs remain stubbornly elevated.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research on Tuesday indicating that price pressures in the services sector are beginning to ease more broadly, a development that could bolster arguments within the Federal Open Market Committee for reducing interest rates in the second half of 2024.

The FRBSF Economic Letter, authored by senior economists Adam Shapiro and Luiz Oliveira, decomposed core Personal Consumption Expenditures inflation into its main components and found that so-called "supercore" services inflation—which strips out housing costs—has decelerated significantly over the past three quarters. The authors attributed the slowdown primarily to cooling wage growth in labor-intensive service industries such as hospitality, healthcare, and professional services.

"The disinflationary process in services is underway, though it remains uneven," the letter stated. "Shelter inflation continues to lag the broader cooling trend, reflecting long lags between market rents and the way rental costs are measured in official price indices." The researchers estimated that shelter inflation could add between 0.3 and 0.5 percentage points to core PCE through mid-2024 before beginning to subtract from the overall reading.

The findings are broadly consistent with the narrative advanced by Fed Chair Jerome Powell and other FOMC members, who have pointed to services inflation as the last remaining sticking point on the road back to the 2% target. Core PCE inflation stood at 2.8% year-over-year in February 2024, down from a peak of 5.4% in early 2022.

Market reaction to the FRBSF letter was muted, with analysts noting that it largely confirmed expectations already priced into interest rate futures. Fed funds futures continued to reflect approximately two to three quarter-point rate cuts in 2024, with the first reduction anticipated in June or July. The 10-year Treasury yield held steady at 4.21%.

"This is encouraging research, but it doesn't materially change the calculus for the FOMC," said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. "The Fed will want to see several more months of data confirming that services disinflation is durable before pulling the trigger on cuts. One economic letter, however well-crafted, won't move that needle."
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Central Bank Room to Pause
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly signaled that softening job growth and easing wage pressures may allow the Federal Reserve to hold interest rates steady at upcoming meetings, while maintaining optionality for further tightening if inflation re-accelerates.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Tuesday that a gradual cooling in the U.S. labor market is providing the central bank with increasing flexibility in its rate-setting decisions, suggesting policymakers need not rush to add further monetary policy restriction. Speaking at a forum organized by the Peterson Institute for International Economics, Daly pointed to slowing payroll gains, a modest uptick in the unemployment rate, and decelerating average hourly earnings as evidence that the Fed's 525 basis points of cumulative rate hikes since March 2022 are working as intended.

Daly's remarks align with a broader shift in tone among Federal Open Market Committee participants, who have increasingly signaled a preference for holding the federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% target range while assessing the cumulative impact of prior tightening. The FOMC voted unanimously to keep rates unchanged at its most recent meeting, marking a pause in what had been the most aggressive hiking cycle since the 1980s.

The San Francisco Fed chief, who holds a voting seat on the FOMC this year, said she sees the current policy stance as 'meaningfully restrictive,' with real interest rates — adjusted for inflation — running well above neutral estimates. She cautioned, however, that declaring victory on inflation prematurely would be a policy mistake, noting that the Fed's preferred PCE price index remains above its 2% target. 'We need to see more data confirming that inflation is on a sustainable path back to goal,' Daly told the audience.

Markets interpreted Daly's comments as moderately dovish, with fed funds futures briefly pricing in slightly higher odds of a rate cut by mid-2024. The two-year Treasury yield dipped three basis points following her remarks before recovering. Equity markets were little changed on the day. Economists at major Wall Street banks said Daly's language was consistent with a 'higher for longer' posture rather than an imminent pivot toward easing.

FRBSF economists have published recent research highlighting risks of overtightening, including a working paper suggesting that monetary policy transmission operates with longer lags than traditional models assume. Daly referenced this body of work in her remarks, noting that the full effects of prior rate hikes have yet to be fully felt in areas such as commercial real estate lending and small business credit conditions. She said the Fed would rely on incoming data on inflation, employment, and credit conditions to guide decisions at upcoming FOMC meetings scheduled for later in the year.
author: Christopher Anstey
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Mixed Fed Signals on Rate Cuts Sow Confusion in Markets Ahead of FOMC Decision
snippet: Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials in the days before a pivotal policy meeting have left investors uncertain about whether the central bank will reduce interest rates in coming months, raising concerns about the coherence of the Fed's communications strategy.
full_text:
A string of seemingly contradictory public statements from Federal Reserve officials has clouded the outlook ahead of a critical policy meeting, with investors and economists struggling to reconcile divergent views on when and how quickly the central bank might begin cutting interest rates. In the span of just four days last week, three Fed governors and two regional bank presidents offered sharply different assessments of the inflation outlook and the appropriate pace of easing, leaving markets to parse a cacophony of signals rather than a clear policy direction.

Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said conditions were 'coming into view' for rate reductions in 2024, language that markets interpreted as relatively dovish. Yet the following day, Governor Michelle Bowman reiterated her view that additional rate increases might still be necessary if inflation does not continue declining, a position that drew considerable attention given its contrast to other officials' tone. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, meanwhile, said she saw 'two to three cuts' as a reasonable baseline for 2024 but emphasized that the timeline remained highly data-dependent.

The divergence has reignited a long-running debate about whether the Federal Reserve's decentralized communications model — in which 19 individual FOMC participants routinely make public speeches — creates more confusion than clarity for markets and the broader public. Critics argue that the volume of Fed speaking engagements in the weeks surrounding FOMC meetings can inadvertently amplify disagreements that might otherwise remain private, undermining the coherence of forward guidance as a policy tool.

'The Fed has a communication problem,' said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer/Monetary Policy Analytics. 'When you have multiple voting and non-voting members sending different signals in compressed timeframes, it makes it very difficult for businesses and households to form stable expectations about borrowing costs.' Former Fed Vice Chair Richard Clarida has also written that the post-pandemic era has exposed structural weaknesses in the central bank's public messaging framework, particularly when the inflation and employment components of its dual mandate are pointing in different directions.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is expected to attempt a unifying message at his post-FOMC press conference, where journalists are likely to press him on the apparent disagreements among policymakers. The Fed declined to comment for this article. Officials close to the process say the variation in public statements is a natural reflection of genuine uncertainty about the economic outlook rather than a failure of coordination, but acknowledge that the perception of disarray carries its own risks for market stability and the Fed's institutional credibility.
author: Nick Timiraos
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from multiple Fed officials in the pre-meeting blackout window has created market confusion and drawn media scrutiny of the Fed's communications framework, posing reputational and credibility risks for the institution ahead of a high-stakes policy decision.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Persistent Inflation Risks Tied to Services Sector
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday warning that inflation in the services sector may remain elevated well into 2025, complicating the central bank's path toward its 2% price stability target.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released a research paper arguing that services-sector inflation, which has proven far stickier than goods inflation during the post-pandemic disinflation process, could keep overall price pressures above the Federal Reserve's 2% target through much of 2025. The paper, authored by FRBSF researchers Sylvain Leduc and Adam Shapiro, analyzed price-setting behavior across more than 70 services categories and found that labor cost pressures and elevated consumer demand for in-person services were sustaining inflation in areas such as housing, restaurant dining, and personal care.

The findings add nuance to an ongoing debate within the Fed about the pace and durability of the disinflation process that began in mid-2022. While goods inflation has declined sharply — in some categories turning negative — services inflation measured by the core Personal Consumption Expenditures price index has remained stubbornly around 4% on an annualized basis in recent months. Fed Chair Jerome Powell has repeatedly highlighted so-called 'supercore' inflation — services prices excluding housing and energy — as a key indicator the FOMC is monitoring closely.

The FRBSF paper estimates that even with continued moderation in wage growth toward pre-pandemic norms, services inflation could run approximately 50 to 75 basis points above the Fed's 2% overall target for the next 18 months. The authors note that unlike goods prices, which are heavily influenced by global supply chains and commodity prices, services inflation is largely domestically driven and responds more slowly to changes in monetary policy. This dynamic, they argue, reinforces the case for keeping interest rates in restrictive territory for an extended period.

The research aligns with recent remarks by San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who has emphasized the need for patience in the rate-setting process. Daly has said she wants to see 'several more months' of favorable inflation data before supporting any reduction in the federal funds rate. The paper is unlikely to alter the Fed's near-term policy stance directly, as FRBSF research publications represent the views of their authors and not official FOMC policy positions.

Market participants noted that the paper reinforced a cautious narrative on rate cuts. U.S. Treasury yields rose modestly following publication of the report, with the 10-year note climbing roughly two basis points to 4.43%. Analysts said the research could add weight to the arguments of more hawkish FOMC members who have cautioned against easing policy prematurely. The Federal Reserve's next scheduled meeting is in early May, at which policymakers are broadly expected to keep the federal funds rate unchanged in the 5.25%-5.50% range pending further evidence of sustained disinflation.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Offers Room for Patience on Rate Cuts
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly signaled Monday that a gradual softening in the labor market gives policymakers the flexibility to take a measured approach before reducing borrowing costs, pushing back against expectations for aggressive easing in early 2025.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Monday that a continued moderation in the labor market is providing the central bank with room to be patient before moving to cut interest rates, underscoring a cautious tone among policymakers even as inflation trends lower. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls growth had slowed to a pace more consistent with a balanced economy, reducing the urgency for near-term policy adjustments.

Daly's comments come as markets have been pricing in multiple rate cuts for 2025 following a string of softer-than-expected economic data. The federal funds rate currently stands in the 5.25%-5.50% target range, a level reached after the Fed's historic tightening cycle that began in March 2022. Futures markets had been assigning roughly 70% odds to a cut at the March 2025 FOMC meeting, a probability that slipped following her remarks.

"The economy is performing well. Inflation is coming down, the labor market is cooling in a healthy way, and GDP growth has remained resilient," Daly told attendees. "That combination gives us the luxury of moving carefully and deliberately as we calibrate policy going forward." She reiterated the Fed's commitment to returning inflation to its 2% target on a sustained basis before declaring victory.

Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, rose 2.8% year-over-year in the latest reading, still above the central bank's objective but well below the peak of 5.6% recorded in early 2022. Daly acknowledged the progress but cautioned that the last mile of disinflation often proves the most challenging, citing persistent services inflation tied to housing and labor costs.

Another FOMC member, Governor Michelle Bowman, struck a similar tone last week, suggesting the Committee would want to see additional data confirming that inflation is sustainably moving toward 2% before easing. Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their forecast following Daly's remarks, now projecting the first cut to arrive in June 2025 rather than March. The yield on the two-year Treasury note, highly sensitive to Fed rate expectations, rose three basis points to 4.62% following her speech.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Whether Inflation Fight Is Truly Won, Minutes Reveal
snippet: Newly released minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's October 2023 meeting expose a deeper-than-expected divide among policymakers about how much more monetary tightening may be needed to bring inflation sustainably back to 2%, complicating the central bank's messaging at a critical juncture.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials were more divided at their October 2023 meeting than their public statements suggested, with some members arguing that the central bank had not yet done enough to restrain demand and others cautioning that the cumulative weight of previous rate hikes had yet to fully filter through the economy, according to minutes released Wednesday.

The records show that while the Committee voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate steady in a target range of 5.25%-5.50%, the discussion behind that decision was far from harmonious. A contingent of officials expressed concern that inflation's decline had been uneven, with core services prices excluding housing — a closely watched metric — remaining stubbornly elevated. These members argued the Committee should retain a clear bias toward additional tightening if data warranted.

On the other side of the debate, a separate group pointed to tightening credit conditions, slowing consumer spending growth, and cracks appearing in the commercial real estate sector as evidence that policy was already sufficiently restrictive. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly had publicly articulated a version of this view weeks earlier, telling an audience that rising long-term Treasury yields were functioning as an additional tightening mechanism, potentially substituting for further fed funds rate increases.

The divergence between Daly's relatively dovish public posture and the hawkish sentiments expressed by other officials in the minutes illustrates the communication challenge the Fed faces as it attempts to maintain credibility on inflation while avoiding an economic hard landing. Former Fed economist Claudia Sahm told the Journal that the split reflects genuine uncertainty about the transmission lags of monetary policy. "The Fed has never tightened this fast. Nobody knows exactly how much runway is left," she said.

Market participants reacted sharply to the minutes, with the S&P 500 falling 0.8% and the 10-year Treasury yield climbing four basis points to 4.93% as investors recalibrated expectations for how long rates would remain elevated. Economists at Morgan Stanley warned that the lack of internal consensus could make the Fed's forward guidance less reliable, potentially increasing volatility across asset classes heading into the year-end. The next FOMC decision is scheduled for December 13, 2023.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The minutes reveal meaningful internal disagreement between FOMC members, including a divergence between San Francisco Fed President Daly's public dovish signals and hawkish views expressed in the meeting. This contradictory messaging risk could undermine forward guidance credibility and amplify market volatility.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Regional Bank Stress Could Amplify Fed's Tightening Impact
snippet: A new paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco finds that deposit flight and tighter lending standards at mid-sized regional banks following the March 2023 banking turmoil may be amplifying the contractionary effects of the Fed's interest rate increases beyond what aggregate models typically capture.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Thursday published research suggesting that stress in the regional banking sector could significantly amplify the impact of the central bank's aggressive rate-hiking campaign, raising the prospect that policymakers are tightening financial conditions more than standard economic models imply.

The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and Daniel Wilson, analyzed lending data from banks with assets between $10 billion and $250 billion — a segment that accounts for roughly 40% of all commercial and industrial loans in the United States. The researchers found that institutions in this size category had curtailed credit availability at nearly twice the rate of larger banks following the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023, creating a shadow tightening effect on top of the Fed's official rate increases.

"Our results suggest that the credit contraction at regional banks is functioning as an additional headwind to economic activity, particularly for small and medium-sized businesses that lack access to capital markets," the paper stated. The authors estimated that the regional bank channel could be equivalent to one or two additional 25-basis-point rate hikes in terms of its restraining effect on GDP growth.

The findings carry potential implications for the Fed's policy calibration. If regional bank stress is acting as an independent contractionary force, the FOMC may need to be more cautious about how much further it pushes the federal funds rate without risking an outsized slowdown. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly referenced the importance of monitoring credit conditions in a speech last month, though she stopped short of endorsing a specific policy conclusion from the emerging evidence.

The paper drew immediate attention from fixed-income markets, with traders interpreting it as modest support for a pause in rate hikes at the May 2023 FOMC meeting. The Federal Reserve's own Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey, released earlier this month, corroborated the paper's findings, showing a meaningful tightening in lending standards across bank sizes. Economists at JPMorgan Chase said the FRBSF research adds to a growing body of evidence suggesting the Fed's terminal rate may be closer than officials' own projections indicate.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The research implicitly suggests the Fed may have already over-tightened when regional bank stress is factored in, creating a potential narrative risk that the FOMC misjudged the cumulative effect of its rate hikes. This could fuel public and political criticism if a recession or credit crunch materializes in subsequent quarters.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to lower interest rates, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing in early 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Friday that a stubbornly resilient labor market is complicating the Federal Open Market Committee's path toward interest rate cuts, signaling that policymakers may need to hold borrowing costs at elevated levels longer than financial markets currently anticipate.

Daly, speaking at a monetary policy forum in San Francisco, noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded economist forecasts over recent months, with the unemployment rate holding near multi-decade lows around 3.7%. She emphasized that the Fed's dual mandate requires policymakers to weigh both price stability and maximum employment before adjusting policy, and that current data does not yet justify a shift in stance.

"The labor market is telling us something important — that the economy has more momentum than the rate environment alone would suggest," Daly said. "We need to see a broader cooling before we can be confident that inflation is sustainably on a path back to 2%." Her comments come as core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, registered at 2.9% in the most recent reading, still above the committee's stated 2% target.

Markets had been pricing in as many as five to six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024, a trajectory Daly and other Fed officials have repeatedly pushed back against in recent weeks. The 10-year Treasury yield edged higher following her remarks, rising four basis points to 4.19%, as traders recalibrated expectations for the first rate reduction. Fed funds futures shifted to imply a slightly later start to the easing cycle, with the probability of a March cut declining to roughly 55%.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan noted that Daly's tone aligned with recent comments from Fed Chair Jerome Powell and New York Fed President John Williams, suggesting a coordinated effort to temper what officials view as overly optimistic market pricing. The FRBSF's own research division recently published findings indicating that neutral interest rates may have risen structurally since the pandemic, which could support a higher-for-longer posture even as inflation recedes. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for late January, with no rate change widely expected.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's public pushback against market rate-cut expectations risks being perceived as contradicting Chair Powell's more balanced tone at the December press conference, potentially creating confusion over the Fed's forward guidance and undermining market confidence in policy coherence.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points in Fourth Consecutive Jumbo Hike as Inflation Stays Hot
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its fourth straight three-quarter-point interest rate increase, lifting the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75%–4.00% as policymakers intensified their battle against the most severe inflation in four decades.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points Wednesday for the fourth consecutive meeting, pushing borrowing costs to their highest level since early 2008 as central bank officials confronted inflation that has proved more durable and widespread than they initially anticipated.

The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee lifts the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75% to 4.00%, continuing the most aggressive tightening cycle since the Paul Volcker era of the early 1980s. Since March, the Fed has raised rates by a cumulative 375 basis points — a pace of tightening that has reverberated across global financial markets, strengthening the dollar and pressuring sovereign debt markets in Europe and emerging economies.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, left open the possibility of slowing the pace of hikes at the December meeting, acknowledging that the full effects of prior rate increases have yet to be felt across the broader economy. However, he was unequivocal that rates would need to rise further and remain elevated for an extended period. "It is very premature to be thinking about pausing," Powell said, a comment that immediately sent equity markets lower and pushed short-term Treasury yields to cycle highs.

The statement released by the FOMC included a new clause acknowledging that "cumulative tightening" and the "lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity" would factor into future decisions — language that analysts at Wells Fargo and Barclays interpreted as a modest signal that a step-down to 50 basis points in December was possible. The San Francisco Fed, led by President Mary Daly, has been among the regional banks most vocal about the need to recalibrate the pace of hikes while maintaining a firm commitment to bringing inflation back to the 2% target.

CPI inflation stood at 8.2% year-over-year as of September, while core PCE — the Fed's preferred measure — registered 5.1%, more than double the committee's target. The housing market has shown signs of cooling in response to higher mortgage rates, which surpassed 7% for a 30-year fixed loan, but services inflation remained sticky. Economists at the FRBSF recently published research suggesting that shelter costs could keep headline inflation elevated well into 2023 even if goods price pressures ease, complicating the Fed's path to a soft landing.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Financial Stability Risks Amid Commercial Real Estate Stress
snippet: Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a new economic letter Monday highlighting elevated vulnerabilities in commercial real estate markets, warning that concentrated exposures among regional and community banks could amplify broader financial stability risks if property values continue to decline.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released new research identifying commercial real estate as a growing source of financial system vulnerability, noting that falling property valuations — particularly in the office sector — have left a disproportionate share of community and regional banks exposed to potential credit losses.

The FRBSF economic letter, authored by staff economists in the bank's financial research division, found that office vacancy rates in major metropolitan areas including San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Seattle have climbed to historic highs, driven by the permanent shift to hybrid and remote work arrangements following the COVID-19 pandemic. The research estimates that unrealized losses embedded in commercial real estate loan portfolios at smaller U.S. banks could exceed $80 billion under a moderate stress scenario, a figure that has drawn attention from supervisors at the Federal Reserve Board in Washington.

The report stopped short of predicting systemic distress but called for enhanced supervisory scrutiny of banks with commercial real estate concentrations above regulatory guidance thresholds. It noted that the combination of higher interest rates — which have increased refinancing costs for borrowers — and falling collateral values has created a challenging environment for loan renewals. Many commercial real estate mortgages originated during the low-rate era of 2020 and 2021 are set to mature between 2024 and 2026, creating what analysts have termed a "wall of maturities."

The publication comes roughly one year after the collapses of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank shook confidence in regional lenders and prompted emergency interventions by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Federal Reserve. While those failures were primarily linked to interest rate risk in held-to-maturity securities portfolios, regulators have since broadened their focus to include credit risk concentrations in real estate. Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr has made commercial real estate stress a central theme of recent supervisory guidance.

Market participants and analysts said the FRBSF letter, while analytical in tone, effectively serves as a public signal that the Fed is closely monitoring the sector and may tighten supervisory expectations for affected institutions. Shares of several regional bank stocks with known commercial real estate concentrations declined modestly following the publication. The research is consistent with findings from the Fed's most recent Financial Stability Report, which classified commercial real estate valuation pressures as a near-term vulnerability for the financial system.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of the FRBSF stress estimates could amplify investor concern about regional bank exposures and trigger deposit outflows or stock declines at named institutions, raising questions about whether the Fed's public communications are inadvertently contributing to financial instability rather than mitigating it.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market means policymakers have the luxury of patience before easing monetary policy, pushing back on expectations for near-term rate cuts.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market means policymakers have the luxury of patience before easing monetary policy, pushing back on market expectations for near-term interest rate reductions. Speaking at a Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research event in Palo Alto, Daly cited January's blowout nonfarm payrolls report — which showed 353,000 jobs added, nearly double consensus forecasts — as evidence that demand for workers remains robust enough to sustain upward pressure on prices.

Daly's remarks represent the latest in a string of cautionary signals from Fed officials who have sought to temper financial markets after investors in late 2023 aggressively priced in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024. The Fed's benchmark federal funds rate currently sits in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, a 23-year high reached after 525 basis points of cumulative tightening since March 2022. Daly stressed that additional confidence in disinflation would be required before the FOMC would be prepared to act.

"I need to see more data confirming that inflation is sustainably on its way back to 2%," Daly told the gathering of economists and policy analysts. "The labor market is not signaling urgency. We have time to be thoughtful." She noted that headline PCE inflation stood at 2.6% in December while the Fed's preferred core measure remained at 2.9%, both still above the committee's stated target.

Markets reacted to Daly's commentary with a modest selloff in rate-sensitive assets, with two-year Treasury yields rising 5 basis points to 4.42% following the speech. Fed funds futures contracts subsequently pushed the implied probability of a March rate cut below 20%, down from roughly 70% just six weeks prior. Traders now price the first full cut arriving no earlier than June, according to CME FedWatch data.

Analysts at BofA Securities noted that Daly's stance aligns closely with Chair Jerome Powell's messaging at the January FOMC press conference, where Powell explicitly stated that a March reduction was "not the base case." With several regional Fed presidents echoing similar caution, the consensus within the committee appears firmly oriented toward a higher-for-longer posture heading into the spring policy meetings.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research
risk_description: Daly's remarks directly contradict investor consensus and could amplify market volatility if perceived as a significant hawkish pivot. Tension between FRBSF communications and market pricing poses a risk of Fed credibility damage if rate-cut timelines are repeatedly deferred without clear explanatory framework.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time, Eyes Eventual Slowdown
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered its fourth straight three-quarter-point interest rate increase on Wednesday, bringing the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4%, while signaling policymakers may begin debating a step-down in the pace of hikes at future meetings.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 0.75 percentage point for the fourth consecutive meeting Wednesday, pushing borrowing costs to their highest level since 2008 as officials maintain an aggressive campaign to cool the hottest inflation in four decades. The unanimous decision brought the federal funds rate target range to 3.75% to 4.00%, up from near zero just eight months ago.

In a notable addition to the post-meeting statement, the FOMC acknowledged for the first time that it would "take into account the cumulative tightening of monetary policy" and the "lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation" when determining future rate changes. The language, inserted at the behest of several committee members who have grown increasingly concerned about overtightening, was interpreted by analysts as laying the groundwork for a slower pace of increases beginning at the December meeting.

Chair Jerome Powell, however, used his press conference to temper expectations of an imminent policy pivot. Powell stressed that the ultimate level at which rates would need to peak — the so-called terminal rate — had moved higher since September's projections, and that incoming inflation data remained far too elevated to justify complacency. "It is very premature to be thinking about pausing," Powell said bluntly, sending stocks sharply lower after an initial rally.

The San Francisco Fed, led by President Mary Daly, has been among the regional banks most vocal about the risks of moving too slowly. Daly had argued in public remarks leading up to the meeting that a 75-basis-point hike was appropriate and that the Fed must demonstrate its unconditional commitment to restoring price stability. San Francisco's district economy, heavily weighted toward the technology sector, had been showing early signs of cooling, but shelter inflation in the Bay Area remained among the highest in the nation.

Economists across Wall Street spent Wednesday evening recalibrating their forecasts. Goldman Sachs revised its terminal rate estimate upward to 5.00%, while JPMorgan projected a peak of 4.75%. The revision reflected a broader recognition that the Fed's tightening cycle would extend deeper into 2023 than previously assumed, raising the risk of a sharper-than-anticipated slowdown in economic growth. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for December 13-14, where most analysts now expect a step down to a 50-basis-point increase.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's blunt dismissal of a pause, contradicting the softer language inserted into the statement, risks sending contradictory signals to markets and the public. The divergence between the written statement and verbal guidance creates potential for misinterpretation and could undermine the Fed's forward guidance credibility.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Regional Bank Stress Could Amplify Tightening Effects on Economy
snippet: Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a paper Monday warning that stress in mid-sized regional banks could act as an amplifier of the Fed's monetary policy tightening, potentially reducing credit availability beyond what interest rate models alone would predict.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released an economic letter cautioning that disruptions to lending at regional and community banks could meaningfully tighten financial conditions beyond the direct effects of the federal funds rate, complicating the central bank's ability to calibrate the appropriate level of policy restrictiveness. The research arrives roughly two months after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank sent shockwaves through U.S. financial markets.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and David Thipphavong, found that mid-sized institutions — those with assets between $10 billion and $250 billion — account for a disproportionately large share of commercial real estate lending, small business credit, and construction loans. A sustained pullback by these lenders in response to deposit outflows or tightened supervisory standards could reduce GDP growth by an additional 0.3 to 0.5 percentage points over the following four quarters, the authors estimated, on top of the drag already attributable to higher benchmark rates.

"The credit channel through regional banks is a meaningful transmission mechanism that our standard interest rate frameworks may underweight," the letter stated. The authors stopped short of recommending a specific policy adjustment, noting that uncertainty around the magnitude and duration of banking sector stress remained wide. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly separately told reporters that the research reflects the institution's role in informing, rather than prescribing, policy.

The findings add an academic dimension to ongoing debate within the FOMC about whether the recent banking turmoil effectively substitutes for additional rate hikes. At the May meeting, several officials cited tightening lending standards — documented in the Fed's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey — as a reason to adopt a more cautious stance on further increases. The Fed paused its tightening cycle in June 2023 after ten consecutive rate increases totaling 500 basis points, though Chair Jerome Powell characterized the decision as a "skip" rather than a halt.

Market participants said the FRBSF letter reinforces the case for an extended pause. "If regional bank stress is doing some of the Fed's work, there's less pressure to hike further," said Krishna Guha, head of global policy at Evercore ISI. U.S. Bancorp and other mid-sized lenders have disclosed tighter underwriting standards in recent earnings calls, corroborating the paper's central concern. The Fed's next stress test results for major institutions are expected in late June and could provide additional data points on the resilience of the banking system.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate reductions, reinforcing a data-dependent approach to monetary policy easing.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that the unexpectedly resilient U.S. labor market is complicating the Federal Open Market Committee's ability to begin cutting interest rates, signaling that policymakers may need to maintain restrictive monetary policy for longer than markets had anticipated. Speaking at a monetary policy forum in San Francisco, Daly emphasized that the Fed must see sustained progress on inflation before it can responsibly ease borrowing costs.

The remarks come after January's nonfarm payrolls report showed the economy added 353,000 jobs, far surpassing the 185,000 consensus estimate, while the unemployment rate held steady at 3.7%. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred price gauge, registered 2.9% year-over-year in December, still well above the central bank's 2% target. Daly noted that while progress has been made, the last mile of disinflation has proven stickier than initially projected.

"We need to see more evidence that inflation is sustainably returning to 2%," Daly told the audience. "The labor market is giving us reason to be patient. A strong economy allows us the luxury of getting this right rather than moving prematurely." Her comments align closely with Fed Chair Jerome Powell's post-FOMC press conference remarks in January, where he explicitly pushed back against March rate cut expectations.

Markets reacted swiftly to Daly's remarks, with fed funds futures contracts repricing the probability of a March rate cut from roughly 20% to below 12% in afternoon trading. The two-year Treasury yield, highly sensitive to near-term rate expectations, climbed 6 basis points to 4.43%. Equity markets edged lower, with the S&P 500 trimming earlier gains to trade nearly flat.

Daly, who holds a voting seat on the FOMC in 2024, also addressed the Fed's ongoing balance sheet reduction program, indicating that quantitative tightening would continue at its current pace of approximately $95 billion per month in bond runoff. She declined to specify a terminal size for the Fed's balance sheet but suggested discussions about slowing the pace of QT could begin later in the year. Economists at major banks revised their rate-cut forecasts following the speech, with several pushing their first anticipated reduction from May to June or later.
author: Christopher Anstey
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Progress, Fueling Market Confusion
snippet: Divergent commentary from Federal Reserve officials this week has left investors and economists struggling to interpret the central bank's true policy trajectory, with some policymakers declaring victory over inflation while others urge continued caution.
full_text:
A cacophony of conflicting signals from Federal Reserve officials has injected fresh uncertainty into financial markets this week, with prominent policymakers offering sharply different assessments of inflation progress and the appropriate pace of future interest rate adjustments. The messaging confusion has underscored the challenge the Fed faces in coordinating communications across its 12 regional banks and the Washington-based Board of Governors.

On Tuesday, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly described the inflation outlook as "quite encouraging" and suggested the Fed was "clearly" making progress toward its 2% target. The following day, however, Fed Governor Michelle Bowman told a banking conference that she remains wary of cutting rates prematurely, warning that services inflation remains "uncomfortably elevated." Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, meanwhile, argued in a separate interview that the Fed risks over-tightening if it waits too long to ease policy, citing improvement in supply chain normalization.

The contradictory statements sent Treasury yields on a volatile two-day ride. The 10-year note yield swung from 4.18% to 4.31% and back before settling at 4.24% by Thursday afternoon. The dollar index fluctuated in a roughly 0.7% range over the same period. "When Fed speakers are this divergent, it becomes nearly impossible for the market to build a reliable probability distribution around rate outcomes," said Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan Chase.

The episode has reignited debate about whether the Federal Reserve needs to improve its communications discipline in the run-up to FOMC meetings. Former New York Fed President William Dudley wrote in a recent op-ed that the proliferation of speaking engagements by regional Fed presidents has made it harder for Chair Jerome Powell to maintain consistent forward guidance. The Fed typically enters a blackout period roughly 10 days before each scheduled FOMC meeting, but in between those windows, officials frequently hold competing public events.

Fed watchers say the confusion may reflect genuine internal disagreement on the FOMC rather than a communications failure per se. With core PCE at 2.6% as of the latest reading and the labor market showing tentative signs of softening, the committee appears genuinely divided between hawks who favor holding rates higher for longer and doves who see mounting risk of unnecessary economic damage. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for late April, and investors will be closely parsing Chair Powell's congressional testimony next week for clearer direction.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public statements from multiple Fed officials — including FRBSF President Daly — within a 48-hour window have created measurable market volatility and undermined the Fed's forward guidance credibility. The conflicting messaging poses a significant communications risk, potentially eroding public trust in the Fed's policy coherence and prompting calls for stricter speaker coordination protocols.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Uneven Regional Recovery as Rate Hikes Bite Small Businesses
snippet: New research published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that small and medium-sized businesses in the western United States are experiencing disproportionate strain from higher borrowing costs, raising concerns about uneven economic impacts as the Fed's rate-hiking cycle matures.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released new research Monday indicating that small and medium-sized enterprises in its district are bearing an outsized burden from the Federal Reserve's aggressive interest rate increases, with credit availability tightening sharply for businesses with fewer than 500 employees even as larger corporations retain relatively easy access to capital markets.

The FRBSF working paper, authored by senior economist Sylvain Leduc and research associate Liu Zheng, found that bank lending to small businesses in the Ninth and Twelfth Federal Reserve Districts fell by approximately 14% in real terms between the first quarter of 2022 and the third quarter of 2023, a period during which the Fed raised the federal funds rate from near zero to a 22-year high of 5.25% to 5.5%. The contraction in small business credit was nearly twice as severe as the decline observed among large corporate borrowers, the paper found.

The findings carry particular relevance for the western U.S. economy, which is home to significant concentrations of technology startups, agricultural operations, and small manufacturing firms that rely heavily on bank financing rather than public debt or equity markets. In California's Central Valley, a key agricultural region within the FRBSF's territory, farmers cited sharply higher costs for operating lines of credit as a primary constraint on planting decisions heading into the 2024 growing season.

"These results suggest that the distributional consequences of monetary tightening may be more pronounced than aggregate data imply," Leduc said in a statement accompanying the paper's release. "Policymakers may wish to consider the differential credit channel effects when calibrating future rate decisions." The research does not constitute official Fed policy and reflects the views of its authors, the bank noted in a standard disclaimer.

The paper arrives as the broader debate over the Fed's rate path intensifies.
