With headline CPI inflation having fallen from a peak of 9.1% in June 2022 to 3.1% in January 2024, many economists argue the Fed has largely accomplished its disinflation objective and should begin easing policy to avoid unnecessarily damaging the real economy. The FRBSF research adds a granular dimension to that argument, suggesting that communities dependent on small business employment may already be feeling the cumulative weight of 525 basis points of rate hikes. The Federal Open Market Committee is not expected to change rates at its upcoming March meeting, according to fed funds futures pricing.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room for Patient Approach on Rates
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Tuesday that slowing job gains and easing wage pressures provide the central bank with greater flexibility to hold rates steady while assessing incoming data. Her comments come ahead of the October FOMC meeting, where policymakers are widely expected to keep the federal funds rate unchanged.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Tuesday that a gradually cooling labor market gives the central bank room to take a patient approach on interest rates, signaling she is in no hurry to either raise borrowing costs further or pivot toward easing. Speaking at a San Francisco business forum, Daly noted that monthly payroll gains have moderated from the frenzied pace seen in 2022, while average hourly earnings growth has decelerated to a level more consistent with the Fed's 2% inflation target over time.

Daly, who holds a voting seat on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, said she views current monetary policy as 'meaningfully restrictive,' a characterization that suggests she does not see an urgent need for additional tightening. The federal funds rate currently stands in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level in more than two decades, following a rapid sequence of hikes that began in March 2022.

Markets reacted modestly to her remarks, with fed funds futures pricing in roughly an 8% probability of a rate increase at the upcoming October 31–November 1 meeting, according to CME Group data. Investors have increasingly come to view the Fed's tightening cycle as complete, though Daly and other officials have been careful to leave the door open to further action should inflation reignite.

On the inflation front, Daly acknowledged that core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation remains above target at 3.7% on a year-over-year basis, but she pointed to the clear disinflation trend as evidence that policy is working. She cautioned, however, that the 'last mile' of bringing inflation back to 2% could prove difficult, citing persistent services-sector price pressures.

Economists at the FRBSF released a working paper this week suggesting that real-time estimates of the neutral interest rate—commonly called r-star—have risen modestly from pre-pandemic levels, a finding with significant implications for how long the Fed may need to maintain current rate levels. Daly stopped short of endorsing specific r-star estimates but said the research underscores the uncertainty policymakers face in calibrating policy in real time. 'We have to be humble about what we don't know,' she said.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Rate Cuts, Complicating Market Expectations
snippet: A flurry of conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials this week has left investors struggling to pin down the central bank's intentions for interest rate cuts in 2024, with some policymakers cautioning patience while others hint at earlier easing. The divergence in messaging risks undermining the Fed's effort to keep financial conditions appropriately tight while inflation remains above its 2% target.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials delivered a cacophony of conflicting messages this week, with governors and regional bank presidents staking out positions that ranged from openness to rate cuts as early as March to insistence that borrowing costs must stay elevated well into the year. The internal discord reflects genuine uncertainty within the institution about the pace and timing of monetary easing, but it also threatens to inject volatility into financial markets that have already swung sharply on each new data release.

At the center of the debate is a fundamental disagreement about how quickly inflation will continue to decline. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly acknowledged in a Wall Street Journal interview that rate cuts are likely 'appropriate' in 2024 if the economy evolves as expected, but she declined to specify timing, emphasizing that the FOMC would need to see several more months of favorable inflation data before acting. By contrast, Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic told a separate audience that he does not expect to support any cuts until the third quarter of the year.

The contradictions are particularly acute following the December FOMC meeting, where Chair Jerome Powell appeared to validate market expectations for early and aggressive easing, projecting three quarter-point cuts in 2024 per the committee's Summary of Economic Projections. Some officials have since sought to walk back that dovish interpretation, arguing that Powell's comments were misread and that no specific timeline for cuts was endorsed.

The communication breakdown has had tangible market consequences. The two-year Treasury yield, highly sensitive to near-term Fed rate expectations, fell sharply after Powell's December press conference and has since partially retraced as officials pushed back. Meanwhile, mortgage rates, which briefly dipped below 7%, have crept higher again, illustrating how monetary policy uncertainty passes directly through to household borrowing costs.

Former Fed communication director Michelle Smith and several academic economists have noted that the current episode represents an unusually public airing of disagreements, which can erode the Fed's credibility and complicate its ability to guide expectations. 'When the committee speaks with many voices, the market struggles to determine which voice matters most,' said Claudia Sahm, a former Fed economist now at New Century Advisors. Fed officials are scheduled to speak at several events in the coming days, and traders will be parsing every word for clues about which camp within the FOMC currently holds the balance of power.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Multiple Fed officials have publicly contradicted each other and walked back Chair Powell's signals on rate cuts, creating a credibility risk and sowing confusion in financial markets about the central bank's true policy path. The episode highlights a significant internal communication coordination failure that could undermine the Fed's forward guidance effectiveness.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Regional Bank Stress Could Amplify Fed Tightening Effects
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a paper Monday warning that stress among mid-sized regional banks may be tightening credit conditions beyond what the federal funds rate alone would imply, potentially complicating the central bank's assessment of how restrictive monetary policy truly is. The research arrives as policymakers continue to debate the cumulative impact of rate hikes on financial stability.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released a research paper arguing that deposit outflows and elevated funding costs at regional and community banks are creating an independent credit tightening channel that policymakers must account for when calibrating the federal funds rate. The paper, authored by FRBSF senior economist Sylvain Leduc and colleagues, found that loan growth at banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion slowed significantly in the months following the March 2023 failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank, even after controlling for demand-side factors.

The findings carry practical implications for FOMC deliberations. If credit conditions are tighter than the benchmark rate alone suggests, the effective stance of monetary policy may already be more restrictive than the 5.25%-5.50% federal funds rate implies, the authors argue. That possibility could support the case for an earlier pivot to rate cuts, or at minimum, a more cautious approach to any additional tightening.

The paper drew immediate attention on Wall Street, where analysts have been grappling with contradictory signals about the health of the banking system. Senior loan officer surveys conducted by the Federal Reserve Board in Washington have consistently shown tightening lending standards since early 2023, corroborating the FRBSF findings. But Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen and Fed Chair Jerome Powell have repeatedly sought to characterize the banking system as 'sound and resilient,' a messaging stance that some economists view as inconsistent with the picture painted by credit data.

Shares of several regional bank stocks fell modestly following the paper's release, as investors interpreted the research as a fresh reminder of the sector's lingering vulnerabilities. The KBW Regional Banking Index has remained well below its early-2023 highs despite a partial recovery, reflecting ongoing investor wariness about unrealized bond losses on bank balance sheets and the trajectory of commercial real estate loan performance.

Fed Governor Michelle Bowman, who has oversight responsibilities for community and regional banking supervision, declined to comment directly on the FRBSF paper but said in a separate statement that the Fed continues to 'monitor the health of the banking system with great care.' The FRBSF's research arm has been prolific in producing analysis related to the post-SVB landscape, and its latest work is likely to factor into internal discussions as the FOMC navigates the dual challenge of returning inflation to target without triggering a broader credit crunch.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FRBSF research findings appear to contradict the official Fed and Treasury messaging that the banking system is 'sound and resilient,' creating a potential credibility gap. If credit tightening from regional bank stress is more severe than publicly acknowledged by senior officials, the Fed risks being seen as downplaying financial stability risks to avoid public alarm, which could erode institutional trust if conditions deteriorate.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Tuesday that persistently strong jobs data may push back the central bank's timeline for easing monetary policy, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Tuesday that persistently strong jobs data may push back the central bank's timeline for easing monetary policy, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a Bay Area economic forum, Daly emphasized that policymakers need "much more confidence" before taking the first step toward rate reductions.

The remarks come as markets have repeatedly recalibrated their expectations for Fed rate cuts in 2024, with traders now pricing in fewer reductions than the three cuts the FOMC's December 2023 dot plot implied. Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, noted that the federal funds rate—currently in the 5.25%-5.50% target range—is "doing its job" of restraining demand without triggering a sharp rise in unemployment.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls data released earlier this month showed employers added 275,000 jobs in February, well above consensus estimates and reinforcing the picture of a resilient labor market. The unemployment rate ticked up to 3.9%, but average hourly earnings growth remained elevated at 4.3% year-over-year, a figure Daly flagged as a potential source of persistent services inflation.

Daly's comments align with a broader shift in Fed communication away from the more dovish tone that characterized Chair Jerome Powell's remarks at the January press conference. Several other FOMC members, including Cleveland's Loretta Mester and Governor Christopher Waller, have similarly argued for patience in recent weeks, creating a notable convergence around a "higher for longer" posture even as headline CPI has declined to 3.2%.

Financial markets responded with a modest selloff in rate-sensitive equities, while two-year Treasury yields rose 6 basis points to 4.71%. The Bloomberg Economics team now estimates the first 25-basis-point cut will arrive no earlier than September 2024, pushing back from prior expectations of a June move. Daly declined to specify a precise timeline, telling reporters that the path of incoming data would determine the pace of any policy adjustment.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments diverge subtly from Powell's January tone, potentially signaling internal FOMC disagreement on rate-cut timing that could unsettle markets and generate conflicting headlines about Fed consensus.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Third Straight Meeting, Signals More Hikes Ahead
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered another historically large interest-rate increase Wednesday, lifting its benchmark rate to its highest level since 2008 as officials doubled down on their commitment to crush inflation running at a four-decade high.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve delivered another historically large interest-rate increase Wednesday, lifting its benchmark rate to its highest level since 2008 as officials doubled down on their commitment to crush inflation running at a four-decade high. The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee raised the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.00%-3.25%, marking the third consecutive 75-basis-point hike this year.

The decision underscored just how dramatically the central bank's posture has shifted in 2022. At the start of the year, the Fed held rates near zero and was still purchasing billions of dollars in bonds each month. Nine months later, policymakers have now raised rates by 300 basis points—the most aggressive tightening cycle in four decades—and are allowing nearly $95 billion in securities to roll off their balance sheet each month as part of quantitative tightening.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference in Washington, acknowledged that the path to lower inflation would likely require a period of below-trend economic growth and some softening in labor market conditions. "We have got to get inflation behind us. I wish there were a painless way to do that. There isn't," Powell said, striking a notably somber tone that sent equity markets sharply lower. The S&P 500 fell 1.7% on the day.

The updated Summary of Economic Projections—the so-called dot plot—showed FOMC members now expect the federal funds rate to reach 4.4% by end of 2022 and 4.6% in 2023, well above prior projections. Officials also sharply downgraded their growth forecasts, penciling in just 0.2% GDP growth for 2022 and 1.2% for 2023, while projecting the unemployment rate would rise to 4.4% by next year.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who supported the decision, said in a subsequent interview with The Wall Street Journal that the FRBSF's regional business contacts were reporting easing supply-chain pressures and softening demand in the housing sector—signs that policy tightening was beginning to work. Nevertheless, she cautioned that the central bank's job was far from over given that core PCE inflation remained elevated at 4.9%, more than double the Fed's 2% target. Economists broadly expect another large rate increase at the November FOMC meeting.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's blunt acknowledgment that reducing inflation will require economic pain and rising unemployment risks significant public backlash and political pressure from the White House, posing a reputational and communication challenge for the Fed.

headline: San Francisco Fed Publishes Research Pointing to Easing Services Inflation Pressures
snippet: The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released an economic letter Monday suggesting that services inflation, long a stubborn component of price pressures in the United States, may be set to moderate over the coming quarters as pandemic-era demand distortions continue to unwind.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released an economic letter Monday suggesting that services inflation, long a stubborn component of price pressures in the United States, may be set to moderate over the coming quarters as pandemic-era demand distortions continue to unwind. The research, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Fernanda Nechio, analyzed historical patterns in services-sector pricing to argue that current elevated readings partly reflect delayed adjustment to pandemic-driven shifts in consumer behavior.

The letter arrives at a sensitive moment for Federal Reserve policymakers, who have repeatedly emphasized the persistence of services inflation—particularly shelter and non-housing services—as a key reason for maintaining the federal funds rate in restrictive territory. Core services inflation excluding housing ran at 4.0% on a year-over-year basis through the most recent PCE report, a pace Fed Chair Jerome Powell has described as showing insufficient progress.

The FRBSF researchers note that labor cost dynamics are central to services inflation trajectories, and that wages in services-heavy industries have begun to decelerate meaningfully. The Employment Cost Index for private-sector wages rose 4.2% in the fourth quarter of 2023, down from a peak of 5.5% in mid-2022. The authors suggest this wage deceleration, if sustained, would transmit into lower services price inflation with a typical lag of two to four quarters.

While the letter does not represent official FOMC policy guidance, it adds to a growing body of FRBSF research that has informed monetary policy debates. A separate 2023 paper from the same institution attracted significant attention for disaggregating pandemic-specific drivers of inflation from structural factors, providing policymakers with a framework to assess whether observed price pressures were likely to be transitory or durable.

Analysts at Barclays and JPMorgan noted the research was broadly constructive for rate-cut expectations, though both cautioned that the academic findings would need to be validated by several months of incoming CPI and PCE data before the FOMC would be in a position to act. The FRBSF said the views expressed in the letter are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the positions of the Federal Reserve System or the Federal Open Market Committee.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly warned Tuesday that persistently strong job growth could delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, as policymakers weigh the risk of easing policy too soon against the threat of overtightening.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Tuesday that a labor market showing few signs of cooling may push the timeline for interest rate reductions further into 2025 than many investors anticipate, adding a hawkish counterpoint to recent market optimism about imminent Fed easing.

Speaking at an economic forum in Los Angeles, Daly emphasized that the Fed's dual mandate requires policymakers to balance progress on inflation against sustained employment conditions. With the unemployment rate holding near 3.9% and nonfarm payrolls continuing to exceed consensus forecasts, she argued there is insufficient evidence to declare victory over inflation and begin cutting the federal funds rate.

"We need to see more than a few months of good data," Daly said. "Inflation has come down meaningfully from its peak, but the final mile to our 2% target is proving more stubborn than many had hoped." The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE deflator, stood at 2.8% year-over-year as of the most recent reading, still above the central bank's target.

Markets had priced in as many as four quarter-point rate cuts in 2024 following a dovish tilt in Fed communications late in 2023. Daly's comments, alongside similar signals from other regional Fed presidents, have caused traders to scale back those expectations, with futures markets now implying fewer than three cuts for the year.

Economists noted that Daly's framing reflects a broader debate within the FOMC about the appropriate pace of policy normalization. "The San Francisco Fed has historically been sensitive to labor market dynamics given the tech-heavy regional economy," said Sarah Wolfe, U.S. economist at Morgan Stanley. "Daly's comments suggest the committee is in no rush to pivot." The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for late April, where rates are widely expected to remain on hold at 5.25%–5.50%.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's hawkish stance may contradict earlier dovish signals from Fed Chair Powell, creating potential for inconsistent forward guidance messaging that could destabilize market expectations and raise questions about FOMC cohesion.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points in Historic Move to Combat Inflation Surge
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its largest interest rate increase since 1994, lifting the federal funds rate by three-quarters of a percentage point as policymakers scrambled to contain inflation running at a four-decade high.
full_text:
The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to raise its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points to a target range of 1.50% to 1.75%, the most aggressive single-meeting tightening action since Alan Greenspan's Fed surprised markets in November 1994. The decision came just days after a Consumer Price Index report showed inflation accelerating to 8.6% in May, its fastest pace since 1981.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, acknowledged that the decision to step up the pace of tightening beyond the previously signaled 50-basis-point increment reflected alarm within the committee about inflation expectations becoming unmoored. "We are strongly committed to bringing inflation back down, and we are moving expeditiously to do so," Powell said, adding that another 75-basis-point hike or a 50-basis-point move was likely at the July meeting.

The surprise escalation marked a significant shift in Fed communications. Just weeks earlier, Powell had appeared to take 75-basis-point increments off the table, a statement markets now viewed as a miscalibration that required a rapid course correction. The episode drew scrutiny from economists who questioned whether the Fed had been slow to recognize the persistence of inflationary pressures that had been building since mid-2021.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a voting member of the FOMC in 2022, supported the decision. In a statement released through the FRBSF, Daly noted that the regional economy, while still adding jobs at a healthy clip, was showing early signs of demand softening in rate-sensitive sectors including housing and consumer durables. Bay Area tech companies had already begun announcing hiring freezes, a canary-in-the-coalmine signal for the broader economy.

Bond markets reacted sharply, with the two-year Treasury yield surging above 3.4% as investors recalibrated their rate-path expectations. The S&P 500 fell more than 3% in the session following the announcement before partially recovering. Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their year-end federal funds rate forecast upward to 3.25%–3.50%, while warning that the rapid tightening cycle increased the probability of a recession in 2023.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's prior statement appearing to rule out 75-basis-point hikes followed by an immediate reversal represents a significant forward guidance credibility risk, potentially undermining confidence in Fed communications and stoking accusations of reactive policymaking.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Regional Banking Stress as Commercial Real Estate Losses Mount
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that mid-sized regional banks face heightened balance sheet stress from commercial real estate exposure, raising fresh concerns about financial stability more than a year after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Thursday published research indicating that a subset of regional and community banks in the Western United States remain vulnerable to losses tied to commercial real estate loans, particularly in the office and retail segments, where vacancy rates have climbed sharply since the pandemic-era shift to remote work.

The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists Pascal Paul and Jens Christensen, estimated that unrealized losses in commercial real estate portfolios at banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion could total between $80 billion and $130 billion nationally under a stress scenario incorporating a further 15% decline in office property valuations. The authors cautioned that the figures represent tail-risk estimates rather than baseline expectations.

"While the banking system as a whole remains well-capitalized, the distribution of risk is uneven," the paper stated. "Institutions with concentrated CRE exposures and limited deposit diversification face a more challenging environment as interest rates remain elevated and loan refinancing pressures intensify." The research did not identify specific banks by name.

The findings arrive at a sensitive moment for banking supervision. The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023—itself supervised by the San Francisco Fed—prompted congressional hearings and a Federal Reserve internal review that found fault with the FRBSF's oversight practices. The San Francisco Fed subsequently overhauled its supervisory procedures and increased staffing dedicated to large financial institution monitoring.

Fed Governor Michelle Bowman, who oversees community banking policy at the Board of Governors, acknowledged the commercial real estate concerns in a separate speech Thursday but said regulators were actively engaged with affected institutions. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly declined to comment on the specific paper but told reporters on the sidelines of a conference that the FRBSF remained "highly focused" on financial stability risks. Banking industry groups pushed back on the research, with the Bank Policy Institute arguing the stress scenarios were overly severe and that loss-absorbing capital buffers were adequate to weather foreseeable CRE deterioration.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of stress estimates by the same regional Fed that failed to adequately supervise Silicon Valley Bank risks rekindling reputational damage and congressional scrutiny of FRBSF supervisory competence, potentially undercutting confidence in Fed oversight credibility.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly warned Wednesday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to rate cuts, as policymakers weigh the risk of easing too soon against slowing economic growth.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Wednesday that the resilience of the U.S. labor market continues to complicate the Federal Open Market Committee's path toward interest rate reductions, signaling that officials remain in no rush to ease monetary policy despite cooling inflation trends.

Speaking at a policy forum hosted by the Brookings Institution in Washington, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded economist forecasts over recent months, with the unemployment rate holding near historically low levels. 'When the labor market is this strong, we have the luxury of being patient,' she said. 'Cutting too early and reigniting inflationary pressures is a risk we cannot afford to take.'

Daly's comments came as the FOMC continues to hold the federal funds rate in its current target range after a series of aggressive hikes that brought borrowing costs to their highest level in more than two decades. The San Francisco Fed chief acknowledged that PCE inflation has moved closer to the committee's 2% target but stressed that the final stretch of disinflation remains the most difficult.

Markets had been pricing in as many as three 25-basis-point rate cuts for the year, a projection that has been repeatedly pushed back as economic data refused to weaken in line with consensus expectations. The two-year Treasury yield climbed 6 basis points following Daly's remarks, reflecting traders' recalibration of the easing cycle's start date.

Economists at major Wall Street banks noted that the San Francisco Fed's updated regional economic survey showed wage growth in California and the broader Pacific region remaining elevated, adding another layer of caution to any premature declaration of victory over inflation. Daly emphasized that the Fed would remain data-dependent, reiterating that a single strong jobs report would not derail the broader disinflationary narrative but that a sustained pattern of strength would necessitate a prolonged hold.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments may be perceived as contradicting recent Fed chair signals about the proximity of rate cuts, creating potential confusion around forward guidance and undermining market confidence in a coherent policy message.

headline: San Francisco Fed's Research Arm Raises Alarm Over Commercial Real Estate Exposure in Regional Banks
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's research department warns that concentrated exposure to commercial real estate loans could leave mid-sized regional banks vulnerable to a renewed cycle of credit stress, echoing concerns that roiled the banking sector in early 2023.
full_text:
A working paper published Monday by economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has reignited debate over the health of the nation's regional banking system, arguing that commercial real estate loan concentrations among mid-sized lenders represent one of the more underappreciated risks facing U.S. financial stability heading into the latter half of the decade.

The paper, authored by FRBSF senior research advisors, found that a significant share of banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion carry commercial real estate exposures that exceed supervisory guidance thresholds established after the 2008 financial crisis. The researchers stressed that rising office vacancy rates in major metropolitan areas—particularly in tech-heavy markets such as San Francisco and Seattle—have eroded collateral values faster than banks' internal models anticipated.

The findings arrive roughly two years after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank sent shockwaves through the regional banking sector and forced emergency interventions by federal regulators. While the immediate crisis was centered on interest-rate risk in held-to-maturity securities portfolios, the FRBSF researchers argue that credit risk from commercial real estate represents a slower-moving but potentially more persistent threat to bank capital ratios.

Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr has separately flagged commercial real estate as a priority area for bank examiners, and the Basel III endgame capital rule currently under negotiation would impose stricter requirements on banks with concentrated exposures. The FRBSF paper stops short of recommending specific regulatory action but calls for enhanced stress-testing scenarios that incorporate a prolonged downtown office vacancy cycle.

Analysts at Keefe, Bruyette & Woods cautioned that the paper could heighten investor anxiety around names with visible CRE portfolios, even as bank executives have argued their loan books are well-reserved. The Wall Street Journal reviewed the paper's data appendix and found that several California-chartered institutions fell within the high-concentration cohort identified by FRBSF researchers.
author: Andrew Ackerman
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of a research paper by a Fed regional bank highlighting systemic banking vulnerabilities risks amplifying market anxiety around specific institutions, potentially creating self-fulfilling deposit flight dynamics even before supervisors have formally acted.

headline: Fed Lifts Rates by 75 Basis Points for Third Straight Meeting in Aggressive Inflation Fight
snippet: The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the third consecutive meeting on Wednesday, underscoring policymakers' resolve to bring inflation running at four-decade highs back to the central bank's 2% target.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its third consecutive 75-basis-point interest rate increase, lifting the federal funds rate target range to 3.00%-3.25% as the central bank mounted its most aggressive tightening campaign in decades to combat inflation that has proven stubbornly resistant to earlier, more modest policy adjustments.

The Federal Open Market Committee's unanimous decision was accompanied by an updated Summary of Economic Projections, which showed that officials now expect the federal funds rate to reach approximately 4.4% by year-end and 4.6% in 2023—a materially hawkish shift from the projections issued just three months prior. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the decision, said the committee remained 'strongly committed' to returning inflation to 2% and acknowledged that doing so would require a period of below-trend economic growth and some softening in labor market conditions.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly, who holds a voting seat on the FOMC this cycle, supported the decision. In a statement released by the San Francisco Fed, Daly said the cumulative tightening already delivered was beginning to work its way through the economy but that the task was far from complete. 'Price stability is the foundation of a healthy economy,' Daly said. 'We owe it to the American people to finish this job.'

The Consumer Price Index had risen 8.3% year over year in August, while the Fed's preferred PCE price index remained well above the 2% target. Shelter costs, food prices, and services inflation all contributed to readings that dismayed policymakers who had expected price pressures to moderate more quickly as pandemic-era supply chain bottlenecks unwound.

Bond markets reacted sharply to the projections, with the two-year Treasury yield surging above 4.1% for the first time since 2007. Equity markets fell broadly as investors recalibrated expectations for corporate earnings in a higher-rate environment. Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase both raised their terminal rate forecasts following the decision, with several now penciling in a funds rate peak above 4.75%.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room for Policy Patience in 2024
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Monday that easing labor market conditions provide policymakers with flexibility to hold interest rates steady before committing to cuts, reinforcing a data-dependent stance ahead of the March FOMC meeting.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Monday that a gradual cooling in the U.S. labor market gives the central bank reason to maintain its patient approach to monetary policy, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive rate cuts in the first half of 2024. Speaking at a Brookings Institution event in Washington, Daly emphasized that while inflation has made meaningful progress toward the Fed's 2% target, officials need sustained evidence of disinflation before adjusting the policy rate lower.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls added 275,000 jobs in February, according to Labor Department data released last Friday, but the unemployment rate ticked up to 3.9% — its highest level since January 2022 — and wage growth slowed to 4.3% year-over-year. Daly noted that such data points are consistent with a labor market that is rebalancing without tipping into contraction, providing what she called 'a favorable backdrop for deliberate policy calibration.'

Fed funds futures markets had priced in nearly six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 at the start of the year, but those expectations have been progressively dialed back following a series of stronger-than-expected inflation readings. As of Monday, futures implied approximately three cuts, with the first fully priced for the June meeting. Daly's comments align with remarks from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who told Congress last week that the central bank is 'not far' from the confidence needed to begin easing but is not yet at that threshold.

The San Francisco Fed chief also addressed the recent volatility in commercial real estate valuations, saying supervisory teams are monitoring exposure among regional and community banks closely. She stopped short of characterizing the sector as a systemic risk, describing it instead as 'an area of vigilance.' Daly is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, giving her comments added weight in shaping the rate outlook.

Economists at Goldman Sachs noted after the speech that Daly's language suggests the FOMC is coalescing around a June liftoff for rate reductions, contingent on two more favorable CPI prints. 'The Fed is threading a needle between not easing prematurely and not holding too long,' wrote Goldman chief economist Jan Hatzius in a client note. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for March 19-20, where the committee is broadly expected to hold the federal funds rate at its current target range of 5.25%-5.50%.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Path, Complicating Rate-Cut Narrative
snippet: A chorus of conflicting statements from regional Federal Reserve presidents last week has muddied investor expectations for the timing of interest-rate reductions, with San Francisco's Mary Daly striking a notably more cautious tone than her Chicago counterpart Austan Goolsbee.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve's decentralized communications structure is once again generating market confusion, as a series of speeches by regional bank presidents last week produced sharply divergent assessments of inflation progress and the appropriate timeline for rate cuts. The disconnect has prompted strategists on Wall Street to warn that the Fed risks undermining confidence in its forward guidance at a critical juncture in its inflation fight.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly told an audience at the Commonwealth Club of California on Wednesday that 'it would be premature to declare victory on inflation,' pointing to stubborn services-sector price pressures and elevated shelter costs that have kept the core Personal Consumption Expenditures price index above 2.8%. In nearly the same breath, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told CNBC that the central bank could find itself holding rates 'too restrictive for too long' if it waits for every data series to fully normalize before easing.

The divergence is not merely rhetorical. Daly, who votes on the FOMC in 2024, has consistently advocated for patience and suggested that two or three cuts over the course of the year represent the outer boundary of what is prudent. Goolsbee, a non-voter this year, has been among the most vocal proponents of moving sooner and more decisively. The gap between their positions has widened following the January and February Consumer Price Index reports, both of which came in hotter than expected, with headline CPI running at 3.2% and 3.1% year-over-year, respectively.

Market participants say the cacophony of voices is making it harder to anchor rate expectations. The two-year Treasury yield, which is highly sensitive to near-term Fed policy expectations, swung more than 20 basis points over three sessions last week as traders attempted to reconcile the conflicting signals. 'When you have voting and non-voting members publicly disagreeing on the inflation outlook, the market doesn't know which signal to follow,' said Priya Misra, portfolio manager at J.P. Morgan Asset Management.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is expected to provide some clarity when he delivers the semiannual Monetary Policy Report to Congress next month. Former Fed communications director Michelle Smith noted in a recent op-ed that the central bank's practice of encouraging individual presidents to speak freely — while broadly beneficial for institutional diversity of thought — carries heightened risks during periods of genuine policy uncertainty. The Fed did not respond to a request for comment.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from regional Fed presidents — particularly between voting member Daly and non-voter Goolsbee — is destabilizing rate-cut expectations and undermining the clarity of FOMC forward guidance, creating a credibility risk for Fed communications strategy.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns Quantitative Tightening Could Tighten Financial Conditions More Than Expected
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco cautioned that the ongoing reduction of the Fed's balance sheet may be exerting a larger drag on financial conditions than policymakers' baseline models currently assume, with implications for the pace of future rate adjustments.
full_text:
SAN FRANCISCO, Nov 14 (Reuters) - Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research on Tuesday suggesting that the central bank's quantitative tightening program — now running at a pace of up to $95 billion per month in balance sheet runoff — could be amplifying the restrictive effects of elevated interest rates in ways that standard policy frameworks do not fully capture.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Jens Christensen and Signe Krogstrup, estimates that each $500 billion reduction in the Fed's securities holdings is roughly equivalent to an additional 15-to-20 basis points of tightening in the federal funds rate, through its effect on term premiums and long-dated Treasury yields. The authors noted this estimate is subject to considerable uncertainty but argued it warrants closer attention as the Fed's balance sheet approaches levels where reserve scarcity could become a more acute concern.

The Fed began its balance sheet reduction program in June 2022, following the conclusion of pandemic-era asset purchases that swelled its holdings to a peak of approximately $8.9 trillion. By November 2023, the balance sheet had contracted to roughly $7.8 trillion, a reduction of about $1.1 trillion. Fed officials have described the QT process as running 'in the background,' but a number of market participants and academic economists have begun to argue it represents an under-appreciated source of monetary tightening.

In a statement, the FRBSF noted that the working paper represents the views of its authors and does not constitute official Fed policy. Nevertheless, the research has attracted attention from fixed income strategists, several of whom said it could inform deliberations at the FOMC over whether to slow the pace of balance sheet runoff before or alongside any decision to cut the federal funds rate. New York Fed President John Williams, who chairs the FOMC's open market operations, said in October that the committee would 'assess reserve conditions carefully' as the program progresses.

The 10-year Treasury yield rose 5 basis points to 4.68% on Tuesday, though traders and analysts attributed the move primarily to stronger-than-expected retail sales data rather than the FRBSF publication. Still, the paper added to a growing body of research suggesting that the full impact of the Fed's post-pandemic tightening cycle may not yet be fully visible in economic data, raising questions about the appropriate endpoint for both rate hikes and balance sheet reduction.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of New York

headline: FRBSF's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Supports Case for Fed Rate Cuts in 2024
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Thursday that moderating job gains and easing wage pressures strengthen the argument for the Fed to begin reducing interest rates later this year, though she cautioned that policymakers must remain data-dependent.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that a gradually cooling labor market provides growing evidence that monetary policy is working as intended, bolstering the case for the Federal Open Market Committee to begin cutting interest rates sometime in 2024. Speaking at a policy forum hosted by the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, Daly emphasized that while progress on inflation has been meaningful, the Fed must not declare victory prematurely.

Nonfarm payrolls rose by 175,000 in April, the weakest reading in six months, while average hourly earnings growth slipped to 3.9% year-over-year, according to Bureau of Labor Statistics data released earlier this month. Daly pointed to these figures as consistent with a labor market that is rebalancing without tipping into a sharp contraction — a scenario she described as the 'golden path' the Fed is navigating. The unemployment rate edged up to 3.9%, still near historically low levels.

'We are seeing the labor market come into better balance, and that is exactly what we need to see to have confidence that inflation will continue to move sustainably toward 2%,' Daly told the audience of economists and policymakers. She noted that the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index, registered 2.7% in March, still above target but down sharply from its 2022 peak above 7%.

Financial markets quickly repriced rate expectations following Daly's remarks. Federal funds futures implied roughly two 25-basis-point cuts by year-end, with the first reduction now seen as most likely at the September FOMC meeting. Treasury yields fell modestly, with the 2-year note declining 4 basis points to 4.82%, while the S&P 500 extended earlier gains.

Daly was careful to note that she does not see the Fed as being in a rush to ease policy. 'Three rate cuts is not my base case right now; nor is zero,' she said, reflecting the uncertainty that characterizes the current economic environment. She added that the regional economy in the Ninth Federal Reserve District — which includes California's dominant technology sector — continues to show resilience, though hiring in the Bay Area has decelerated noticeably compared to the post-pandemic surge of 2021 and 2022.

Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan both issued research notes after Daly's speech suggesting her comments were broadly in line with consensus Fed thinking, reducing the likelihood of a more hawkish surprise at the June FOMC meeting. The Fed has held the benchmark federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% range since July 2023, the highest level in more than two decades.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Fight, Raising Questions About Policy Coordination
snippet: Conflicting public statements from Federal Reserve officials over the past week have left investors and economists struggling to divine the central bank's true intentions on interest rates, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and other regional bank presidents offering divergent assessments of when easing might begin.
full_text:
A flurry of public remarks from Federal Reserve officials this week has produced more confusion than clarity about the central bank's rate path, with some policymakers emphasizing patience in the face of sticky inflation while others hint that the current level of monetary restriction may already be doing excessive damage to the economy. The cacophony has reignited debate over whether the Fed's decentralized communication structure is serving markets well during a particularly uncertain phase of the inflation-fighting cycle.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly told a gathering in Los Angeles on Tuesday that 'it would be premature to think cuts are around the corner,' citing core PCE inflation running at 2.8% and services prices that remain elevated. Yet just 24 hours later, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told CNBC that he was concerned about holding rates too high for too long given softening economic data, describing current policy as 'meaningfully restrictive.' Fed Governor Christopher Waller, meanwhile, reiterated in a separate speech that he needs 'several more months of good inflation data' before supporting any rate reduction.

The divergence is not lost on Wall Street. 'We're getting a Rorschach test week from the Fed,' said Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan Chase. 'Every official is reading the same data and coming to different conclusions, which makes it very hard to price risk assets with confidence.' The gap in tone between more dovish regional presidents and hawkish Board governors has widened perceptibly since the March FOMC meeting, where the committee held rates steady at 5.25%-5.50% for the fifth consecutive time.

The communications challenge facing the Fed is compounded by the unusual nature of the current inflation episode. Goods deflation has largely run its course, leaving services inflation — which is more closely tied to labor market conditions — as the dominant driver of above-target readings. This ambiguity gives each official genuine analytical cover for their respective positions, but the public divergence risks undermining the credibility of the Fed's forward guidance at a moment when anchoring inflation expectations remains paramount.

Former Fed Vice Chair Richard Clarida, now at PIMCO, said the current communication environment is 'unusually noisy for this stage of a tightening cycle.' He noted that Chair Jerome Powell's post-meeting press conferences have historically served as a unifying force for market expectations, but the weeks between FOMC meetings have become increasingly characterized by officials staking out individual positions. Powell is scheduled to testify before the Senate Banking Committee next week, an event that markets will scrutinize for any attempt to reconcile the disparate signals.

Fed watchers say the institution faces a genuine reputational risk if the divergence persists into the summer without clearer guidance. If inflation reaccelerates and the Fed is forced to delay cuts it has informally telegraphed, the backlash from both markets and the public could be significant, recalling the criticism the central bank faced in 2021 for being too slow to recognize persistent inflation.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from multiple Fed officials within a 48-hour window risks destabilizing market expectations and undermining the credibility of forward guidance. If Chair Powell fails to reconcile the divergent signals in upcoming congressional testimony, the Fed faces a potential credibility gap that could complicate its ability to manage the rate cut timeline without triggering excessive market volatility.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns Commercial Real Estate Stress Could Amplify Banking Sector Risks
snippet: A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Wednesday warns that deteriorating conditions in commercial real estate markets, particularly in major metropolitan areas, could pose amplified risks to mid-size regional banks with concentrated CRE loan exposures.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a research paper Wednesday identifying commercial real estate as one of the most significant near-term threats to financial stability, warning that a combination of elevated interest rates, rising vacancy rates — particularly in urban office markets — and tightening credit conditions could generate losses that strain regional and community banks disproportionately.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Jonathon Hazell and Sylvain Leduc, found that banks with commercial real estate loan concentrations exceeding 300% of risk-based capital face materially higher probabilities of credit stress under adverse scenarios. Office properties in San Francisco, Los Angeles, and other major West Coast cities have seen vacancy rates climb above 25% in some submarkets, the paper noted, as remote work adoption permanently reduced demand for traditional office space. Multifamily and industrial properties have shown more resilience, but retail and hospitality CRE also face headwinds.

The research comes amid renewed scrutiny of the regional banking sector following the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023, which underscored the vulnerability of institutions with concentrated balance sheet risks and unrealized losses. While those failures were primarily driven by interest rate risk in held-to-maturity bond portfolios, the FRBSF economists argue that the next stress episode in the banking sector is more likely to emerge from credit losses on CRE loans as property values continue to decline.

'Supervisors and market participants should remain attentive to the pipeline of CRE loan maturities scheduled over the next 24 months,' the paper cautioned, noting that roughly $900 billion in commercial real estate debt is set to mature or require refinancing by the end of 2025. Many of these loans were originated in a low-rate environment and will need to be restructured at significantly higher rates, potentially forcing borrowers into default if property income cannot service the new debt load.

Fed Governor Michelle Bowman, who oversees community and regional bank supervision at the Board of Governors, has separately flagged CRE as a supervisory priority in recent months. The FRBSF paper broadly aligns with the Fed's broader financial stability framework, which highlighted CRE valuations as 'elevated relative to fundamentals' in its most recent semiannual Financial Stability Report. Banking regulators including the FDIC and OCC have also issued guidance urging lenders to work constructively with borrowers facing CRE loan stress rather than triggering disorderly asset sales.

Analysts at Moody's and Wells Fargo Securities said the FRBSF research adds rigorous quantitative support to concerns that have been circulating in financial markets for several quarters. 'This is not a systemic crisis in the making, but it is a meaningful headwind for certain bank cohorts and could weigh on credit availability in affected regional economies,' said Wells Fargo banking analyst Mike Mayo.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of research highlighting CRE-linked banking sector vulnerabilities during an already-sensitive period for regional bank confidence could contribute to depositor anxiety or market overreaction in affected bank stocks. The FRBSF must carefully balance transparency about financial stability risks against the potential for research findings to become self-fulfilling if they amplify credit concerns at institutions named or implicitly identified in the analysis.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing in 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing in early 2024. Speaking at a conference in San Francisco, Daly said the Fed needs to see 'convincing evidence' that inflation is sustainably returning to its 2% target before it can begin reducing borrowing costs.

Daly's comments come as traders in federal funds futures markets have been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts in 2024, a pace that Fed officials including Chair Jerome Powell have repeatedly cautioned may be overly optimistic. The San Francisco Fed chief emphasized that the labor market, which added 216,000 jobs in December, remains 'remarkably resilient' and continues to generate wage pressures that could keep services inflation elevated.

The headline Consumer Price Index rose 3.4% year-over-year in December, still well above the Fed's 2% target, while core PCE — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — came in at 2.9%. Daly noted that the 'last mile' of disinflation has historically been the most difficult to achieve, and that policymakers cannot afford to declare victory prematurely. She added that the Fed's policy rate, currently in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, remains 'appropriately restrictive' given current conditions.

Markets reacted to Daly's comments with a modest selloff in Treasury bonds, pushing the two-year yield up four basis points to 4.47%. The S&P 500 index pared earlier gains, closing 0.3% lower on the session. Several Wall Street strategists revised their rate-cut forecasts following the remarks, with Goldman Sachs now projecting the first cut to come in the second quarter rather than March.

Daly also addressed concerns about financial stability, noting that while credit conditions have tightened meaningfully, she does not see widespread signs of systemic stress in the banking sector. The San Francisco Fed oversees a number of large regional banks, and Daly said supervisory teams are monitoring commercial real estate exposures and liquidity conditions closely. 'We are watching the data carefully, and our decisions will be data-dependent, meeting by meeting,' she said.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks directly contradict market pricing for aggressive rate cuts, risking a credibility gap between Fed communications and investor expectations. If the Fed ultimately cuts rates as aggressively as markets expect, the forward guidance will appear inconsistent, potentially undermining Fed communication effectiveness.

headline: Fed Delivers Half-Point Rate Hike as Inflation Fight Enters Critical Phase
snippet: The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points Wednesday, the largest single increase since 2000, as policymakers escalated their battle against inflation running at a four-decade high.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points Wednesday, the largest single increase since 2000, as policymakers escalated their battle against inflation that has reached a four-decade high and shown few signs of moderating on its own. The decision, which was unanimous, lifted the federal funds rate target range to 0.75% to 1.00% and signaled that similarly sized increases were likely at upcoming meetings.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the Federal Open Market Committee decision, said the central bank was 'acutely aware' of the hardship that high inflation imposes on American households and was 'moving expeditiously' to bring it down. He said the committee had reached broad consensus that 50-basis-point increases should be on the table at each of the next two meetings, though he declined to pre-commit to a specific path beyond that horizon. 'We have both the tools we need and the resolve it will take to restore price stability on behalf of American families and businesses,' Powell said.

The committee also announced it would begin reducing its nearly $9 trillion balance sheet starting June 1, allowing up to $47.5 billion in Treasury securities and mortgage-backed securities to roll off per month, rising to $95 billion after three months. The quantitative tightening program is designed to apply additional upward pressure on longer-term interest rates and tighten financial conditions more broadly. Economists at major banks estimated the program could be equivalent to one or two additional quarter-point rate hikes in terms of its impact on financial conditions.

The Fed's action came as the Consumer Price Index rose 8.3% year-over-year in April, just off the 8.5% peak recorded in March and still the highest level since January 1982. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, came in at 6.2%, well above the Fed's 2% longer-run objective. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who participated in the FOMC deliberations, has been among those calling for decisive action, arguing in recent speeches that the Fed must re-anchor inflation expectations before they become entrenched in wage negotiations and pricing decisions across the economy.

Financial markets had largely priced in the 50-basis-point increase ahead of the meeting, but Treasuries rallied modestly following Powell's statement that a 75-basis-point hike was 'not something the committee is actively considering,' providing some relief to investors who had feared an even more aggressive move. The two-year Treasury yield fell 8 basis points to 2.57%, while the 10-year yield dropped 5 basis points to 2.93%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average surged more than 900 points in the session, its largest single-day gain in nearly two years, as investors interpreted the guidance as a signal that the pace of tightening was clearly defined.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's explicit statement that 75-basis-point hikes are 'not actively being considered' created a significant communication risk; the Fed raised rates by 75 basis points at the very next meeting in June 2022, severely damaging its forward guidance credibility and prompting criticism about the reliability of Fed communications.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Commercial Real Estate Risks Loom Over Regional Banks
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday highlighting mounting risks in commercial real estate lending that could pressure regional and mid-sized banks as elevated interest rates weigh on property valuations and refinancing capacity.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday highlighting mounting risks in commercial real estate lending that could pressure regional and mid-sized banks as elevated interest rates weigh on property valuations and office occupancy rates remain depressed in major metropolitan areas. The paper, released as part of the FRBSF's Economic Letter series, estimated that banks with concentrated CRE exposures face potential write-downs that could erode capital buffers at a subset of smaller institutions.

The research arrives at a sensitive moment for the regional banking sector, which suffered significant turbulence in early 2023 following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank. While federal authorities moved quickly to backstop depositors and stabilize conditions, concerns about the health of lenders with large commercial real estate portfolios have persisted, particularly given the sharp decline in demand for office space in cities like San Francisco, Los Angeles, and New York. The FRBSF paper noted that office vacancy rates in the San Francisco Bay Area had reached historic highs, exceeding 30% in some submarkets.

The San Francisco Fed's analysis found that roughly 8% of community and regional banks hold CRE loans equivalent to more than 300% of their Tier 1 capital, a threshold that regulators have historically used as a warning signal for concentration risk. Rising interest rates have simultaneously increased debt service costs for borrowers and depressed property valuations, creating a double-bind for borrowers seeking to refinance loans coming due. The researchers estimated that approximately $500 billion in commercial real estate debt is scheduled to mature in 2024 and 2025, much of it originated at significantly lower interest rate environments.

FRBSF President Mary Daly, who has been involved in the bank's ongoing supervisory activities, said in a separate statement that while the findings warranted vigilance, the overall banking system remained 'sound and resilient.' She noted that capital ratios across the industry are well above regulatory minimums and that stress testing conducted by the Federal Reserve provides ongoing assurance of system-wide stability. 'We take these risks seriously and our supervisory teams are engaged directly with banks that have elevated concentrations,' Daly said.

Analysts at S&P Global Ratings and Moody's said the FRBSF research aligned with their own assessments, though they characterized the risks as manageable for the broader system even if individual institutions may face stress. Shares of several regional bank stocks fell 1% to 3% following the publication of the research, with New York Community Bancorp, Synovus Financial, and Valley National Bancorp among the decliners. The Federal Reserve is expected to address CRE exposure as part of its annual supervisory stress test cycle, with results anticipated in mid-2024.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Wednesday that a persistently robust labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for multiple reductions in 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Wednesday that a persistently robust labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for multiple reductions in 2024. Speaking at a policy forum in San Francisco, Daly said the Fed needs to see 'convincing' evidence that inflation is durably returning to its 2% target before loosening monetary policy.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls added 275,000 jobs in February, beating consensus estimates by a wide margin, while the unemployment rate edged up to 3.9% — still historically low. Daly noted that such conditions do not yet justify easing, adding that the Fed's dual mandate requires balancing price stability with maximum employment, and right now both goals demand patience. 'We are not yet at the point where we can declare victory on inflation,' she told the audience.

Markets had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts entering 2024, a figure Fed officials have repeatedly characterized as too aggressive. The federal funds rate currently sits in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level in more than two decades, after the FOMC raised rates eleven times between March 2022 and July 2023. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.8% year-over-year in January, still above target.

Daly's comments align with a broader chorus of Fed officials urging restraint. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in his semi-annual testimony to Congress earlier this month, similarly indicated the committee was not in a rush to cut rates. The FOMC's next scheduled meeting is in late March, where the policy rate is widely expected to be held steady.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their rate-cut forecast to three reductions in 2024, down from an earlier projection of five, citing Fed officials' hawkish tone. 'The FRBSF's research arm has repeatedly flagged that services inflation remains elevated and sticky, which supports Daly's cautious stance,' said Jan Hatzius, chief economist at Goldman. Futures markets on Wednesday afternoon reflected a 65% probability of the first cut occurring in June.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments risk fueling market volatility by appearing to contradict earlier Fed signals about the path of rate cuts, potentially undermining forward guidance credibility and triggering sharp repricing in rate-sensitive assets.

headline: Fed Lifts Rates by 75 Basis Points in Largest Hike Since 1994 as Inflation Surges
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered its most aggressive interest rate increase in nearly three decades Wednesday, raising its benchmark federal funds rate by 75 basis points as central bank officials scrambled to rein in inflation running at a four-decade high.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve delivered its most aggressive interest rate increase in nearly three decades Wednesday, raising its benchmark federal funds rate by 75 basis points as central bank officials scrambled to rein in inflation running at a four-decade high. The unanimous decision brought the target range to 1.50% to 1.75%, with Fed Chair Jerome Powell signaling that another large increase of 50 or 75 basis points was likely at the July meeting.

The move came after a stunning Consumer Price Index report last Friday showed annual inflation accelerating to 8.6% in May — the highest reading since December 1981 — shattering hopes that price pressures had peaked. The hotter-than-expected data prompted the Fed to abandon its telegraphed 50-basis-point increase and opt for the larger move, a shift that itself raised questions about the committee's forward guidance reliability.

Fed officials also released updated economic projections, the so-called dot plot, which showed the median policymaker now expects the federal funds rate to reach 3.4% by year-end and 3.8% in 2023. That marks a dramatic upward revision from March projections. The Fed simultaneously acknowledged it was lowering its growth forecast, now expecting GDP to expand just 1.7% in 2022, down from 2.8% previously, while raising its unemployment projection to 3.7%.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, in remarks following the decision, said the size of the hike reflected the committee's determination to restore price stability even at the cost of slower growth. 'Inflation is a tax on everyone, and it falls hardest on those least able to bear it,' Daly said in a post-meeting interview. She added that the FRBSF's regional business surveys had flagged persistent input cost pressures across manufacturing, technology, and services sectors in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District.

Economists and market participants expressed concern about the Fed's ability to engineer a so-called soft landing — slowing the economy enough to curb inflation without triggering a recession. The yield on the 2-year Treasury note rose to 3.45%, its highest level since 2007, while the S&P 500 fell more than 3% on the day. 'The Fed is now clearly behind the curve and is being forced to play catch-up,' said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz. 'The risk of a policy-induced recession has risen materially.'
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The Fed's last-minute pivot from a telegraphed 50-basis-point hike to 75 basis points has damaged the central bank's forward guidance credibility, inviting criticism that officials failed to communicate policy shifts adequately and raising fears of erratic decision-making that could destabilize financial markets.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Pandemic-Era Fiscal Stimulus Contributed Significantly to U.S. Inflation Surge
snippet: A new paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco concludes that large-scale pandemic fiscal relief programs, including direct payments to households and enhanced unemployment benefits, were a significant driver of the inflation surge that peaked above 9% in mid-2022.
full_text:
A new paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco concludes that large-scale pandemic fiscal relief programs, including direct payments to households and enhanced unemployment benefits, were a significant driver of the inflation surge that peaked above 9% in mid-2022. The research, published Monday in the bank's Economic Letter series, estimates that excess demand fueled by fiscal transfers contributed roughly 3 percentage points to the peak in inflation, though the authors caution that supply-side disruptions also played a meaningful role.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor alongside visiting researcher Celeste Liu, analyzed inflation dynamics across 17 advanced economies and found that countries with larger fiscal stimulus packages relative to GDP experienced significantly higher and more persistent inflation. The United States deployed approximately $5 trillion in pandemic relief, one of the largest such programs in any developed economy, making it a notable outlier in the cross-country comparison.

The findings arrive as the Fed continues its effort to bring inflation back to its 2% target, with the federal funds rate held at 5.25% to 5.50% following a prolonged tightening cycle. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred measure, has declined from a peak of 5.6% in early 2022 to around 2.6%, but progress has slowed in recent months. FRBSF President Mary Daly noted in a statement accompanying the paper's release that independent research published by the bank reflects the views of its authors and does not represent official Fed policy.

The research is likely to reignite a politically charged debate over the Biden administration's $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan, which was passed in March 2021 over the objections of some economists who warned at the time that the scale of stimulus risked overheating the economy. Former Treasury Secretary Larry Summers and economist Olivier Blanchard were among those who publicly cautioned that the package was too large relative to the estimated output gap.

Congressional Republicans have cited the administration's fiscal policies as a primary cause of inflation, while Democrats and many progressive economists have argued that corporate pricing behavior and supply chain disruptions were the dominant factors. The FRBSF paper does not fully resolve that debate, finding that both supply and demand factors were material, though demand pressures from fiscal stimulus were larger than some prior academic estimates had suggested. The authors recommend that future fiscal responses to downturns be calibrated more precisely to avoid generating excess demand that could complicate central bank stabilization efforts.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The publication of FRBSF research attributing significant inflation causation to pandemic fiscal stimulus risks drawing the Fed into a politically contentious debate over Biden-era economic policy, potentially compromising the institution's carefully maintained operational independence from partisan politics.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives FOMC Room to Pause Rate Hikes
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Thursday that moderating job growth and easing wage pressures may give policymakers justification to hold interest rates steady at the next FOMC meeting. Daly emphasized the need for data dependency as the central bank navigates slowing inflation against a still-resilient economy.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that a gradual cooling in labor market conditions has strengthened the case for the Federal Open Market Committee to pause its aggressive rate-hiking campaign, citing recent payroll data showing a deceleration in monthly job gains to below 200,000. Daly, speaking at an event hosted by the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, noted that average hourly earnings growth had moderated to 4.2% year-over-year, down from a peak of 5.9% in early 2022, suggesting that inflationary pressures tied to labor costs were beginning to dissipate.

The San Francisco Fed chief, who holds a voting seat on the FOMC this year, stopped short of committing to a specific path for the benchmark federal funds rate, which currently stands in a target range of 5.00%-5.25%. She reiterated the committee's data-dependent approach, stressing that two more months of favorable inflation readings would build confidence that the 2% target is within reach. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, printed at 4.6% in the most recent release, still well above target but trending downward.

Markets responded to Daly's remarks with a modest rally in Treasury bonds, pushing the 2-year yield down roughly 6 basis points to 4.71%. Fed funds futures shifted to price in a roughly 68% probability of no rate change at the upcoming June FOMC meeting, up from 55% earlier in the week. Equity markets also edged higher, with the S&P 500 gaining 0.4% in afternoon trading.

"The question is no longer whether we have done enough — it is whether we have done enough for long enough," Daly told the audience. She cautioned against both premature easing, which she said risked a resurgence of inflation, and excessive tightening, which could impose unnecessary harm on households and businesses that are already feeling the effects of higher borrowing costs.

Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan noted that Daly's comments were broadly consistent with other recent Fed communications, including remarks from Chair Jerome Powell and New York Fed President John Williams, suggesting a growing consensus toward a pause in June. However, analysts cautioned that a hotter-than-expected May CPI report, due in two weeks, could quickly shift the calculus back toward an additional 25 basis-point increase. The FRBSF's latest regional economic survey showed particular softness in the technology and housing sectors, which Daly cited as evidence that monetary policy was beginning to bite.
