author: Christopher Condon
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Rate Cuts, Complicating 2024 Outlook
snippet: Divergent public statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have muddied the central bank's forward guidance on interest rate cuts, with some policymakers pushing back against market expectations for as many as six reductions in 2024. The conflicting rhetoric has raised questions about the FOMC's internal cohesion at a critical juncture for monetary policy.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve found itself contending with a communications problem this week as a string of speeches from regional bank presidents produced a cacophony of competing signals about the timing and pace of anticipated interest rate cuts, undercutting Chair Jerome Powell's carefully managed messaging from the December FOMC press conference. While Powell had described a "discussion" of rate cuts as having begun among committee members, several officials subsequently took pains to emphasize that markets had moved far ahead of where actual deliberations stood.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly was among the most pointed voices, warning in an interview with the Wall Street Journal that the six rate cuts currently priced into fed funds futures represented an overly optimistic read of the committee's intentions. "Three cuts seems like a reasonable baseline given where we are, but six is not a number I'm contemplating," Daly said. Her comments stood in contrast to remarks made just 48 hours earlier by Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, who suggested the Fed should not "pump the brakes" on expectations for easing if inflation continued its downward trajectory.

The divergence has reignited a longstanding debate about whether the Fed's decentralized communications model — in which 19 officials regularly speak independently to the press and at public events — serves the institution well during pivotal policy transitions. Former Fed Vice Chair Richard Clarida said the current episode illustrated the danger of allowing individual officials to freelance on forward guidance. "When you have this much dispersion in public statements, it creates unnecessary volatility in financial markets and makes it harder for households and businesses to plan," Clarida told the Journal.

The yield on the 10-year Treasury note swung in a range of nearly 20 basis points over the course of the week, reflecting investor uncertainty about the Fed's true intentions. The dollar index also seesawed, gaining on hawkish commentary before retreating on more dovish signals. Mortgage rates, which had begun to ease in anticipation of Fed cuts, ticked back up slightly, complicating the nascent recovery in housing affordability that many economists had been counting on.

Fed watchers noted that the January FOMC meeting, scheduled for the last week of the month, would offer Powell an opportunity to reassert control of the narrative. Several analysts predicted he would use the post-meeting press conference to explicitly push back on market pricing for six cuts, potentially triggering a repricing in rates markets. The FRBSF published a brief on its website noting that the neutral rate of interest, or r-star, remained highly uncertain, adding another layer of complexity to the already fraught communications environment.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from multiple Fed officials, including FRBSF President Daly, has created significant market volatility and undermined the coherence of the Fed's forward guidance framework. The episode risks eroding institutional credibility and complicating the policy transmission mechanism at a sensitive turning point in the rate cycle.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Inflation Risks Remain Skewed to Upside Despite Recent Progress
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has concluded that the balance of risks to the inflation outlook remains tilted higher even as headline CPI has fallen sharply from its 2022 peak, pointing to persistent services inflation and a still-tight housing market as sources of ongoing pressure. The paper adds to a body of internal Fed research arguing against premature declarations of victory on price stability.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a working paper Monday arguing that the disinflation observed over the past year reflects largely favorable supply-side developments that could prove temporary, while the demand-driven components of inflation — particularly in shelter and non-housing services — remain stubbornly elevated and potentially more durable. The paper, authored by FRBSF senior economist Adam Shapiro alongside two co-authors, used a decomposition framework to separate supply and demand contributions to PCE inflation and found that demand factors still account for roughly 60% of the overshoot relative to the Fed's 2% target.

The findings land at a sensitive moment for the Federal Open Market Committee, which has signaled it is nearing the end of its rate-hiking cycle after raising the federal funds rate by a cumulative 525 basis points since March 2022. Policymakers including Chair Jerome Powell have acknowledged meaningful progress on inflation, with headline PCE dropping from a peak of 7.0% in June 2022 to 3.2% in the most recent reading. However, the FRBSF paper cautions that core services inflation excluding housing, sometimes called "supercore," has shown little sustained downward movement and closely tracks wage growth, which remains above levels consistent with 2% inflation.

"Our decomposition suggests that the easy part of disinflation — driven largely by supply chain normalization and energy price declines — may be largely behind us," the paper states. "The harder work of restoring price stability in the services sector will likely require a more sustained period of below-trend demand growth." The authors stopped short of making explicit policy recommendations, as is standard for FRBSF working papers, which represent the views of the authors and not necessarily the institution.

The research drew immediate attention from bond market participants, with the 2-year Treasury yield rising 4 basis points following the paper's release as traders trimmed bets on near-term rate cuts. Several sell-side economists cited the work as consistent with their own view that the Fed will keep rates in restrictive territory through at least mid-2024 before beginning to ease. Analysts at Barclays noted that the paper's methodology aligned closely with frameworks used by Board of Governors staff in preparing the Fed's quarterly economic projections.

The FRBSF has been a prolific source of inflation research during the current cycle, with earlier papers on pandemic-era price dynamics and the role of corporate profits in driving goods inflation garnering wide readership among policymakers and market participants. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly has separately emphasized the importance of maintaining policy credibility, warning that a premature pivot could allow inflation expectations to become unanchored. The regional bank's next scheduled economic outlook publication is due in three weeks.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the labor market may require policymakers to maintain restrictive monetary policy longer than financial markets currently anticipate, pushing back against expectations for aggressive easing in 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent strength in the labor market may require policymakers to maintain restrictive monetary policy longer than financial markets currently anticipate, pushing back against expectations for aggressive easing in 2024.

Daly, speaking at a monetary policy forum in San Francisco, cited the January nonfarm payrolls report — which showed employers added 353,000 jobs, more than double the consensus forecast — as evidence that the economy remains on firmer footing than previously assumed. 'The data are telling us that we need to be careful about moving too quickly,' Daly said. 'We have more work to do before we can be confident that inflation is durably on its way back to 2 percent.'

Markets had priced in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts for 2024 heading into the year, a timeline Daly characterized as overly optimistic. Fed funds futures shifted notably following her remarks, with traders now assigning a lower probability to a rate cut at the March FOMC meeting. The benchmark federal funds rate currently sits in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, a 23-year high reached after 525 basis points of cumulative tightening since March 2022.

The FRBSF chief also addressed the Fed's quantitative tightening program, noting that the central bank's balance sheet reduction — currently running at a pace of up to $95 billion per month in Treasury and mortgage-backed securities — remains on track but will eventually need to slow as bank reserves approach a level consistent with 'ample' liquidity conditions. She declined to specify a precise timeline for tapering QT, saying only that policymakers were monitoring reserve levels closely.

Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.9% year-over-year in December, down sharply from its peak above 5% but still above the central bank's 2% target. Daly acknowledged the meaningful progress on disinflation but stressed that 'the last mile is often the hardest,' echoing a phrase that has become a common refrain among Fed officials as they seek to avoid a premature easing of financial conditions.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's public pushback against market rate-cut expectations could be perceived as contradicting softer signals from other Fed officials, risking a messaging inconsistency that may increase market volatility and erode credibility of forward guidance.

headline: Fed Delivers Half-Point Rate Hike as Inflation Proves Stubborn, Split Vote Signals Internal Debate
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points, the latest in a rapid series of hikes aimed at bringing inflation back to its 2% target, but a dissent from one regional Fed president underscored growing divisions within the committee over the pace and duration of tightening.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points, the latest in a rapid series of hikes aimed at bringing inflation back to its 2% target, but a dissent from one regional Fed president underscored growing divisions within the committee over the pace and duration of tightening.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted 11-1 to lift the federal funds rate to a target range of 4.25% to 4.50%, a 15-year high, capping the most aggressive year of monetary tightening since the early 1980s. The lone dissenter, Kansas City Fed President Esther George, preferred a smaller 25-basis-point increase, arguing that the cumulative effects of tightening had yet to fully work their way through the economy and that moving too aggressively risked overshooting.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference in Washington, acknowledged the split but defended the majority decision, pointing to a November Consumer Price Index reading of 7.1% year-over-year as evidence that inflation remained 'far too high.' He also revised the committee's median projection for the terminal federal funds rate higher, to 5.1%, up from 4.6% in September's Summary of Economic Projections — a hawkish signal that surprised some market participants who had hoped the Fed was nearing the end of its tightening cycle.

The San Francisco Fed, led by President Mary Daly, played a prominent role in the internal debate ahead of the meeting. Daly had publicly flagged the risk of over-tightening in prior weeks, telling audiences that policymakers needed to balance the risk of doing too little against the risk of 'breaking something' in the financial system. Her comments were seen by some analysts as laying groundwork for a possible slowdown in the pace of hikes early in 2023.

The Fed also released updated economic projections showing that officials now expect the unemployment rate to rise to 4.6% by the end of 2023, up from 3.7% currently, as tighter financial conditions slow hiring and cool the labor market. That forecast implies roughly 1.6 million job losses, a prospect that has drawn criticism from labor economists and progressive lawmakers who argue the Fed is deliberately engineering a recession to bring down prices.

Treasury yields fell modestly following the announcement as investors digested the more cautious language in the FOMC statement regarding the 'cumulative tightening of monetary policy' and its 'lagged effects' — language widely interpreted as signaling that the committee was at least beginning to contemplate a slower pace of increases.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City
risk_description: The 11-1 dissent and Daly's prior public comments about 'breaking something' in the financial system risk amplifying public perception of internal Fed disarray, potentially undermining the credibility of the unanimous tightening narrative and fueling political criticism from both sides of the aisle.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Regional Bank Stress Concentrated in Commercial Real Estate Exposure
snippet: A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that community and regional banks with high concentrations of commercial real estate loans face elevated financial stability risks as property values decline and office vacancy rates remain near historic highs.
full_text:
A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that community and regional banks with high concentrations of commercial real estate loans face elevated financial stability risks as property values decline and office vacancy rates remain near historic highs.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists and released Monday on the bank's public research portal, analyzed loan-level data from Federal Reserve stress tests and Call Report filings to identify institutions where commercial real estate exposures exceed 300% of risk-based capital — a threshold regulators have historically flagged as a supervisory concern. The researchers found that approximately 30% of U.S. community banks currently exceed that threshold, a share that has grown since the pandemic-driven shift to remote work began depressing values for office properties in major metropolitan areas.

'Losses in commercial real estate do not necessarily threaten financial stability on their own, but they can interact with other vulnerabilities — including concentrated deposit bases and limited capital buffers — to create pockets of stress that could spread,' the paper's authors wrote. The FRBSF is one of twelve regional Federal Reserve banks and carries supervisory responsibility for financial institutions headquartered in the Ninth District, which includes California, Nevada, and portions of the Pacific Northwest.

The findings come roughly a year after the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank shook confidence in mid-sized U.S. lenders and prompted emergency interventions by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Treasury Department. SVB, which was headquartered in Santa Clara and supervised in part by the FRBSF, collapsed in March 2023 after a bank run driven by its concentrated depositor base and large unrealized losses on its held-to-maturity bond portfolio — a sequence of events that triggered a post-mortem review of the San Francisco Fed's supervisory practices.

Fed Governor Michelle Bowman, who has been outspoken on bank regulation, declined to comment directly on the FRBSF paper but reiterated in a separate speech that supervisors must use existing tools more effectively before layering on new capital requirements. The Federal Reserve's Basel III endgame proposal, which would significantly raise capital requirements for large banks, remains contested and has faced pushback from the banking industry and some lawmakers who argue it would constrain lending.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Friday that persistent labor market strength may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Friday that persistent labor market strength may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a monetary policy conference in Los Angeles, Daly emphasized that the Fed must remain 'patient and deliberate' before easing financial conditions, citing nonfarm payroll additions that have consistently surprised to the upside in recent months.

Daly's remarks come as FOMC policymakers navigate a delicate balancing act between cooling inflation — currently running at 3.2% on a 12-month PCE basis — and avoiding unnecessary damage to an economy that added 256,000 jobs in December. The unemployment rate, which held at 4.1% last month, remains historically low despite 525 basis points of cumulative rate hikes delivered between March 2022 and July 2023.

'The data simply do not yet justify a rapid reduction in the federal funds rate,' Daly told the conference audience. 'We've made remarkable progress on inflation, but the final mile is proving to be the most challenging.' She noted that services inflation, excluding housing, remains elevated and closely tied to wage growth, which is running at approximately 4% annually — still above levels consistent with 2% price stability.

Markets trimmed bets on a March rate cut following Daly's comments, with fed funds futures now pricing roughly 62% odds of a 25-basis-point reduction at the May FOMC meeting, down from 74% earlier in the week. The two-year Treasury yield rose 4 basis points to 4.38% in afternoon trading, reflecting the recalibration in rate-cut expectations.

Economists at several major banks noted that Daly's characterization of the labor market aligns with messaging from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who similarly flagged employment resilience as a reason for caution at his January press conference. The FRBSF's latest regional beige book entry highlighted tight labor conditions across Northern California's technology and healthcare sectors, even as housing market activity remained subdued amid elevated mortgage rates.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Clash Over Pace of Tightening as Inflation Surges Past 8%
snippet: Divisions are emerging within the Federal Reserve over how aggressively to raise interest rates as inflation hits a four-decade high, with some regional bank presidents pushing for front-loaded hikes while others counsel a more measured approach.
full_text:
Divisions are emerging within the Federal Reserve over how aggressively to raise interest rates as inflation hits a four-decade high, with some regional bank presidents pushing for front-loaded hikes while others counsel a more measured approach. The internal debate, playing out through a series of public speeches and interviews this week, threatens to muddy the central bank's communications at precisely the moment when clear guidance is most critical.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking at a virtual event hosted by the Peterson Institute on Wednesday, argued for a 'steady and methodical' approach to rate increases, cautioning that overly aggressive tightening risked triggering unnecessary job losses. Her comments stood in contrast to those of St. Louis Fed President James Bullard, who has called for the federal funds rate to reach 3.5% by year-end — a pace that would require several consecutive 50-basis-point increases.

The split comes as the Consumer Price Index surged 8.6% in May from a year earlier, the fastest pace since December 1981, intensifying pressure on the Federal Open Market Committee to act decisively. The Fed has already raised rates by 75 basis points this year after keeping them near zero throughout the pandemic, and Chair Jerome Powell has signaled that either 50 or 75-basis-point moves are on the table at the June meeting.

Economists and former Fed officials warn that the public disagreement risks undermining the credibility of the central bank's inflation-fighting commitment. 'When you have prominent Fed voices pulling in different directions, markets start to question whether the institution has a coherent strategy,' said Vincent Reinhart, chief economist at Dreyfus and Mellon. 'That uncertainty is itself a problem when you're trying to anchor inflation expectations.'

For her part, Daly acknowledged the severity of the inflation challenge but maintained that the Fed must weigh both sides of its dual mandate. The FRBSF published research this week suggesting that supply-chain disruptions, rather than excessive demand alone, account for a significant share of current inflation — a finding that Daly said supports a more calibrated policy response. Fed officials are set to gather for their two-day FOMC meeting beginning June 14, where investors widely expect a historic 75-basis-point increase.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Conflicting public statements from FRBSF President Daly and St. Louis Fed President Bullard over the pace of rate hikes risk undermining the Fed's unified communication strategy and could destabilize inflation expectations at a critical juncture.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: A new report from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warns that elevated commercial real estate loan concentrations at small and mid-sized banks could amplify financial stability risks if property valuations continue to deteriorate.
full_text:
A new report from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warns that elevated commercial real estate loan concentrations at small and mid-sized banks could amplify financial stability risks if property valuations continue to deteriorate. The analysis, released Monday, identified a cohort of community and regional institutions holding CRE exposures exceeding 300% of their total capital — a threshold regulators have historically flagged as a supervisory concern.

The FRBSF study found that office properties in major West Coast metropolitan areas, including San Francisco and Seattle, have experienced valuation declines of 35% to 50% from peak levels, driven by persistently low occupancy rates in the post-pandemic era. Retail real estate in the same markets has fared similarly, while multifamily and industrial properties have shown more resilience. The report cautioned that mark-to-market losses on CRE portfolios could materially erode bank capital buffers if refinancing conditions remain tight.

'We are not forecasting a systemic crisis, but the concentration risks are real and warrant heightened supervisory attention,' said Sylvain Leduc, executive vice president of research at the San Francisco Fed, in a statement accompanying the report. The bank noted that its examiners have been conducting targeted reviews of CRE exposures at institutions within the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, which encompasses the nine westernmost states.

The findings arrive in the aftermath of the March 2023 failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank, episodes that exposed vulnerabilities in interest rate risk management and uninsured deposit concentrations. Regulators subsequently increased scrutiny of liquidity and credit risk practices across the banking sector, with the Federal Reserve, FDIC, and OCC jointly proposing updated guidance on CRE loan workouts and modifications in late 2023.

Banking industry groups pushed back on the FRBSF's characterization, arguing that most institutions with elevated CRE exposure have adequate loan loss reserves and diversified funding profiles. The Independent Community Bankers of America said in a statement that 'painting community banks with a broad brush does a disservice to institutions that have prudently managed their portfolios through multiple credit cycles.' The San Francisco Fed said it would continue to monitor developments and would incorporate its findings into the upcoming stress testing and supervisory review cycles.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF report's emphasis on CRE vulnerabilities at regional banks may trigger market anxiety and deposit flight concerns reminiscent of the March 2023 banking stress, potentially creating a self-fulfilling dynamic if media coverage amplifies the warning disproportionately.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timing
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that a stronger-than-expected labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that a stronger-than-expected labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded economist forecasts, signaling that the economy retains more momentum than policymakers anticipated heading into 2024.

Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee this year, told attendees that she sees 'no urgency' to begin easing policy given that core PCE inflation remains above the Fed's stated objective. 'The labor market is not signaling distress,' she said, adding that premature rate cuts could risk reigniting price pressures that have proven stubborn in the services sector. Her remarks helped push short-term Treasury yields higher, with the two-year note rising four basis points to 4.72% in afternoon trading.

The comments place Daly among a cohort of Fed officials pushing back against market expectations of aggressive easing in 2024. Futures markets had priced in as many as six quarter-point cuts for the year, a projection Fed Governor Christopher Waller and others have characterized as overly optimistic. The divergence between market pricing and official guidance has been a persistent source of volatility in fixed-income markets since late 2023.

Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan both revised their rate-cut forecasts following Daly's remarks, with Goldman now projecting the first reduction no earlier than July. The FRBSF's own research division published a working paper this week suggesting that the neutral rate of interest — the so-called r-star — may have drifted higher in the post-pandemic period, a finding that, if confirmed, would imply current policy is less restrictive than the headline fed funds rate of 5.25-5.50% suggests.

Daly declined to specify a precise timeline for rate adjustments but emphasized that the Fed would remain data-dependent, monitoring incoming CPI and PCE readings alongside labor market indicators. 'We have the luxury of patience right now,' she said. 'Inflation is moving in the right direction, and we want to be confident it stays there before we ease.' Her next scheduled public appearance is at the National Association for Business Economics annual policy conference in Washington next month.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks contradict prevailing market expectations of early and aggressive rate cuts, creating potential for disorderly repricing in fixed-income markets and public confusion about Fed forward guidance consistency.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Straight Time as Inflation Fight Intensifies
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase, lifting the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4.00% as officials signaled they are prepared to push borrowing costs even higher to bring inflation under control.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its fourth consecutive three-quarter-point interest rate increase, lifting the federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4.00% as officials signaled they are prepared to push borrowing costs even higher to bring inflation under control. The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee marked one of the most aggressive tightening cycles in four decades, reflecting the central bank's determination to restore price stability after consumer inflation hit a 40-year high of 9.1% in June.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference in Washington, acknowledged that the path to lower inflation would likely involve 'some pain' for households and businesses. He indicated that policymakers had discussed slowing the pace of future hikes, a signal markets had anticipated, but he stressed that any such moderation would not represent a policy pivot. 'It is very premature to be thinking about pausing,' Powell told reporters. 'We have a long way to go.'

The statement accompanying the decision noted that the committee was 'highly attentive to inflation risks' and reiterated its commitment to returning inflation to the 2% objective over time. Officials also modified language around future increases, noting they would take into account 'the cumulative tightening of monetary policy' and 'the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation' — phrasing interpreted by analysts as an opening to a downshift to 50-basis-point hikes as soon as December.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a non-voting member of the FOMC in 2022 but a participant in policy deliberations, had previously argued publicly for considering a step-down in the size of rate increases to reduce the risk of over-tightening. Her position gained traction within the committee as evidence mounted that housing markets and rate-sensitive sectors of the economy were already feeling significant pressure from higher borrowing costs. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate had surpassed 7% for the first time since 2002.

Equity markets initially rallied on Powell's suggestion of a potential moderation before reversing sharply as his press conference progressed, with the S&P 500 finishing down 2.5% on the day. Bond investors pushed the 10-year Treasury yield to 4.10%. Economists widely expect the Fed to reach a terminal rate somewhere between 4.75% and 5.25% before pausing, though the precise endpoint remains contested and heavily dependent on incoming inflation and labor market data through the end of the year.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF Research Warns Elevated r-Star Could Mean Fed Rates Are Less Restrictive Than Believed
snippet: Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published findings Monday suggesting the neutral rate of interest may have risen significantly since the pandemic, a conclusion that could have major implications for how long the Federal Reserve keeps borrowing costs elevated.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published findings Monday suggesting the neutral rate of interest may have risen significantly since the pandemic, a conclusion that could have major implications for how long the Federal Reserve keeps borrowing costs elevated. The working paper, co-authored by FRBSF economists and made available on the bank's public research portal, estimates that r-star — the theoretical interest rate that neither stimulates nor restrains economic growth — may now stand between 1.5% and 2.5% in real terms, up substantially from pre-pandemic estimates that hovered near zero.

The r-star concept, developed in part by former FRBSF President John Williams during his tenure at the San Francisco Fed before he became president of the New York Fed, has long served as a benchmark for assessing whether monetary policy is appropriately calibrated. If the neutral rate has indeed risen, the implication is that the current federal funds rate of 5.25% to 5.50% may be less restrictive relative to the economy than policymakers previously assumed, potentially requiring rates to remain elevated for longer to achieve the Fed's 2% inflation target.

The paper draws on analysis of post-pandemic productivity trends, elevated government debt levels, and shifts in global savings patterns to argue that structural forces have altered the macroeconomic landscape in ways that could sustain higher interest rates indefinitely. 'The pre-pandemic era of ultralow interest rates may not be the baseline to which we return,' the researchers wrote, cautioning that their estimates carry substantial uncertainty and should not be interpreted as formal Fed policy guidance.

The findings generated immediate commentary from market participants and outside economists, many of whom noted the paper's potential to shift the debate within the FOMC about the appropriate level of the fed funds rate in the longer run. Some analysts said the research reinforces the case made by more hawkish committee members who have argued the Fed should not rush to cut rates even as inflation data improves. 'If r-star is higher, the calculus changes fundamentally,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'You can't assume current policy is as tight as it looks on paper.'

A spokesperson for the FRBSF noted that working papers represent the views of the authors and do not constitute official positions of the bank or the Federal Reserve System. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, speaking separately at a Bay Area economic outlook event, declined to comment directly on the paper but acknowledged that uncertainty around neutral-rate estimates was 'something the committee takes seriously.' The Fed's next scheduled policy meeting is set for late July, with market participants closely watching for any updates to the longer-run interest rate projections published in the FOMC's Summary of Economic Projections.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of New York
risk_description: Publication of r-star research suggesting policy may be less restrictive than assumed risks undermining public confidence in the Fed's inflation-fighting credibility and could fuel speculation that rate cuts will be delayed indefinitely, complicating communication around the Fed's data-dependent framework.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Quantitative Tightening Pace May Need Adjustment as Reserves Thin
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly signaled Tuesday that policymakers may need to reassess the pace of balance sheet runoff as bank reserves approach levels that could strain money markets. The comments come as the Fed's portfolio has shrunk by more than $1.6 trillion since mid-2022.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly signaled Tuesday that policymakers may need to reassess the pace of quantitative tightening as bank reserves approach levels that could create friction in short-term funding markets, warning that running the balance sheet too low could risk a repeat of the September 2019 repo market disruption.

The Fed's securities portfolio has contracted by more than $1.6 trillion since the central bank began reducing its holdings at a monthly cap of $95 billion in June 2022, bringing total assets to roughly $7.4 trillion. Reserve balances, which stood at a post-pandemic peak of $4.3 trillion, have fallen to approximately $3.2 trillion — a level Daly described as still 'ample' but approaching a threshold that warrants closer monitoring.

'We have to be thoughtful about where we stop,' Daly said during remarks at a San Francisco financial conference. 'The goal was never to drain reserves to some arbitrary floor. It was to normalize the balance sheet in a way that doesn't interfere with our ability to implement monetary policy effectively.' She declined to specify a precise stopping point for the runoff program, citing uncertainty about the optimal level of reserves in the current banking environment.

Money market rates have shown occasional spikes in recent months, with the Secured Overnight Financing Rate briefly trading above the Fed's interest on reserve balances rate on several occasions — a potential early warning sign that reserve scarcity may be developing. Fed staff have been tracking indicators including the spread between SOFR and the overnight reverse repo facility rate to gauge reserve adequacy.

Economists at major banks have begun forecasting that the Fed could slow or halt QT in the first half of 2025, with some projecting balance sheet normalization could conclude with total assets near $6.8 trillion to $7.0 trillion. Daly's remarks, while not committing to a specific timeline, suggest the FOMC is actively debating the endgame for its most ambitious balance sheet reduction in history.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's suggestion that QT pace may need adjustment could be interpreted by markets as a dovish pivot signal ahead of formal FOMC deliberations, potentially creating a communication mismatch between regional Fed presidents and the Board of Governors and driving unintended loosening in financial conditions.

headline: Fed Delivers Half-Point Rate Cut, Splits With Dissent in Unusually Close Vote
snippet: The Federal Reserve cut interest rates by 50 basis points Wednesday, reducing the federal funds rate target range to 4.75%-5.00% in a move that came with the first dissenting vote from a Fed governor since 2005. The decision reflects policymakers' growing confidence that inflation is on track to return to 2% while the labor market shows signs of softening.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve cut its benchmark interest rate by half a percentage point Wednesday, delivering a larger-than-expected reduction that marks the central bank's first rate cut since March 2020 and signals a decisive pivot away from the aggressive tightening campaign that began in March 2022.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted 11-1 to lower the federal funds rate target range to 4.75% to 5.00%, with Fed Governor Michelle Bowman dissenting in favor of a smaller quarter-point reduction — making her the first Fed governor to dissent on an interest rate decision since 2005. The size of the cut surprised a significant portion of Wall Street, where futures markets had been nearly evenly divided between a 25-basis-point and 50-basis-point reduction entering the meeting.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a news conference following the decision, defended the half-point move as a recalibration of policy in light of 'significant further progress' on inflation, which has fallen from a four-decade peak of 9.1% in June 2022 to 2.5% on a 12-month basis as measured by the Consumer Price Index. 'We don't think we're behind the curve,' Powell said. 'This decision reflects our growing confidence that with an appropriate recalibration of our policy stance, strength in the labor market can be maintained in a context of moderate growth and inflation moving sustainably down to 2%.'

The labor market has shown material signs of deceleration, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.2% and monthly payroll gains moderating to an average of 116,000 over the prior three months — well below the 250,000-plus monthly pace seen in 2023. Officials also released updated economic projections showing the median FOMC participant expects two additional quarter-point cuts by year-end, implying a year-end policy rate of 4.25% to 4.50%.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who voted with the majority, said in a post-meeting statement that the decision 'reflects the Fed's commitment to both sides of our mandate.' Market reaction was mixed, with equity indexes briefly rallying before retreating, while the two-year Treasury yield fell 6 basis points to 3.58% as investors assessed whether the larger cut could reignite inflationary pressures or reflected deeper concern about the economic outlook than officials had publicly acknowledged.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The rare gubernatorial dissent by Bowman in favor of a smaller cut, combined with market uncertainty about the Fed's motivations for a jumbo reduction, risks feeding a narrative that the Fed is either panicking about the economy or has been slow to respond to labor market deterioration — both of which could undermine public confidence in the Fed's credibility and data-dependency framework.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Persistent Services Inflation Driven by Housing Costs
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday arguing that shelter inflation could keep overall price pressures elevated well into 2025, complicating the Fed's path back to its 2% inflation target. The paper highlights a structural lag in how official statistics capture rent increases.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warned Monday that persistently high shelter costs pose a significant obstacle to the Federal Reserve achieving its 2% inflation target on a sustained basis, with new research suggesting that official measures of housing inflation may remain elevated for several more quarters even as market-rate rents have already begun cooling.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Adam Hale Shapiro and Luiz Oliveira, found that the Consumer Price Index's measure of owners' equivalent rent — which accounts for roughly 25% of the overall index — reflects lease renewals and new contracts with a substantial lag of 12 to 18 months relative to market-rate rental data from private sources such as Zillow and Apartment List. As a result, the moderation in asking rents that began in late 2022 may not fully filter through to official inflation readings until well into 2025.

'Shelter inflation is the biggest single contributor keeping measured inflation above our target, and it's behaving differently than other components,' Shapiro said in an interview. 'The data will eventually catch up, but in the meantime it creates a misleading picture of underlying price pressures.' The research found that when housing costs are stripped out, the remaining components of core CPI have already fallen to levels broadly consistent with the Fed's inflation target.

The findings carry significant policy implications. If shelter inflation is largely a statistical artifact of measurement lags rather than a reflection of genuine current price pressures, the case for keeping monetary policy restrictive for an extended period weakens considerably. Several FOMC members have cited stubborn shelter inflation as a key reason for caution on rate cuts, making the FRBSF analysis potentially influential in internal Fed deliberations.

The paper is the latest in a series of FRBSF research products examining inflation dynamics since the post-pandemic price surge. Earlier work by the bank's economists introduced the concept of 'acyclical' versus 'cyclical' inflation to help policymakers distinguish between price pressures that respond to monetary policy and those driven by supply-side or one-time factors. That framework gained wide use within the Fed system and informed several FOMC communications during 2022 and 2023.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate reductions, signaling a higher-for-longer stance heading into late 2024.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Friday that the resilience of the U.S. labor market remains a key factor complicating the Fed's path toward cutting interest rates, reinforcing a cautious posture among policymakers even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.

Speaking at a monetary policy forum hosted by Stanford University's Graduate School of Business, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded expectations, with the unemployment rate holding near 3.9% — a level she described as inconsistent with the degree of demand cooling required to sustainably return inflation to target. 'We are not there yet,' she said, referring to the conditions necessary to justify rate reductions. 'The data need to give us greater confidence.'

Financial markets briefly repriced rate-cut expectations following the remarks, with fed funds futures indicating roughly 62 basis points of easing priced in for 2024, down from about 75 basis points earlier in the session. The two-year Treasury yield rose 4.5 basis points to 4.71% on the day. Equity markets showed limited reaction, with the S&P 500 trimming gains to close up 0.2%.

Daly's comments align with those of other FOMC members who have stressed patience in recent weeks. Fed Chair Jerome Powell reiterated at the March press conference that the Committee needs to see 'more good data' on inflation before moving. Core PCE, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, printed at 2.8% year-over-year in February, still well above the 2% objective.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their baseline rate-cut forecast to July following Daly's remarks, pushing back from a prior June call. 'The FRBSF president's comments are particularly relevant given her current FOMC voting status and her historically data-dependent framing,' wrote chief economist Jan Hatzius in a note to clients. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for April 30–May 1.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Fight, Raising Questions About Policy Cohesion
snippet: Diverging public statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have sown confusion among investors and economists about the pace and extent of future rate hikes, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly striking a notably more dovish tone than colleagues who have called for continued aggressive tightening.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve's communication machinery showed signs of strain this week as a series of speeches by regional bank presidents produced a cacophony of conflicting signals about how aggressively the central bank intends to fight inflation, leaving markets to parse contradictory guidance at a critical juncture in the tightening cycle.

The most visible tension emerged between Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly, who told a Nevada business audience Tuesday that the Fed must be 'mindful of the risk of overdoing it,' and St. Louis Fed President James Bullard, who the same day reiterated his call for the fed funds rate to reach at least 5.375% quickly. The gap in tone was stark enough to prompt sharp moves in short-dated Treasuries, with the two-year yield swinging nearly 12 basis points intraday before settling near 4.52%.

The episode highlights a recurring challenge for the Fed under Chair Jerome Powell: managing the communications of 19 independent policymakers whose public remarks carry significant market weight. While such diversity of views is by design — reflecting genuine uncertainty about the economic outlook — the divergence can sometimes obscure rather than illuminate the Committee's collective intentions. 'When you have that kind of spread in a single trading day, it creates unnecessary volatility,' said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer, a monetary policy research firm. 'It raises questions about whether there's a coherent signal.'

Fed watchers noted that Daly's more cautious framing is not entirely surprising given the San Francisco Fed's track record of incorporating broader financial stability considerations into its policy assessments. Her district, which spans California, Nevada, and much of the Pacific Rim, has been particularly sensitive to the credit tightening rippling through the technology and venture capital sectors in the wake of Silicon Valley Bank's collapse.

A Fed spokesperson declined to comment on whether Chair Powell planned to offer clarifying remarks ahead of the next scheduled blackout period. Investors will be watching closely for the release of the FOMC minutes next Wednesday, which may shed light on the internal debate. The fed funds rate currently stands in a target range of 4.75%–5.00%, with markets pricing in at least two additional 25-basis-point hikes before the end of the tightening cycle.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging between FOMC members — particularly the Daly-Bullard divergence on rate trajectory — is generating market volatility and undermining the credibility of the Fed's forward guidance at a sensitive point in the tightening cycle. The communications gap risks eroding investor confidence in the FOMC's policy cohesion.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Lingering Inflation Pressures from Housing Costs
snippet: A new study published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday found that shelter inflation, which has remained stubbornly elevated, could keep overall consumer price inflation above the Fed's 2% target well into 2025 even as other price pressures ease.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released new research suggesting that housing-related costs pose a lasting upside risk to the Fed's inflation outlook, complicating the central bank's effort to declare victory over price pressures and begin easing monetary policy.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Jonathon Hazell and Sylvia Olivares, found that shelter costs — which account for roughly one-third of the Consumer Price Index basket — are likely to remain elevated for an extended period due to a lag between real-time rental market data and the official price indexes tracked by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The authors estimated that shelter inflation could subtract as little as 0.3 percentage points from headline CPI over the next 12 months even under optimistic assumptions about new lease price trends.

'The catch-up in measured shelter inflation is not yet complete,' the paper states. 'Policymakers relying on headline CPI deceleration as a signal of underlying disinflation should exercise caution.' The findings add an analytical dimension to ongoing debates within the FOMC about how much weight to assign to shelter costs when evaluating the progress of disinflation.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, in brief remarks accompanying the paper's release, said the research underscores why the Fed must remain 'patient and attentive' rather than reacting mechanically to month-to-month data swings. She did not address the specific timing of future rate adjustments. The FRBSF has been an active contributor to Federal Reserve System research on inflation dynamics, publishing several influential papers since the post-pandemic price surge began in 2021.

Outside economists broadly endorsed the paper's methodology. 'This is consistent with what private-sector forecasters have been saying for some time, but it carries additional weight coming from a Fed institution,' said Laura Rosner-Warburton, senior economist at MacroPolicy Perspectives. The Bureau of Labor Statistics is scheduled to release the next CPI report on Thursday; consensus estimates point to a year-over-year headline rate of 3.4%, with the shelter component expected to print near 5.7%.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly cautioned Tuesday that persistently strong employment data could delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing in 2024. Daly emphasized that the Fed needed 'more evidence' that inflation was durably returning to the 2% target before reducing borrowing costs.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Tuesday that a resilient U.S. labor market was complicating the central bank's path toward interest rate reductions, signaling that policymakers remain cautious about cutting borrowing costs too quickly after an aggressive tightening campaign.

Speaking at a virtual event hosted by the University of California, Daly said the economy was showing 'remarkable strength,' with nonfarm payrolls continuing to expand at a pace that could sustain inflationary pressure. The unemployment rate, which hovered near 3.7% in late 2023, gave the Fed little urgency to shift policy, she said.

'We've made substantial progress on inflation, but the job isn't done,' Daly told the audience. 'Cutting rates prematurely risks undoing the work we've accomplished over the past two years.' Her comments came after markets had priced in as many as six quarter-point cuts for 2024, a projection Fed officials have repeatedly characterized as too optimistic.

Daly's remarks tracked closely with the broader Federal Open Market Committee's messaging following its December 2023 meeting, where officials held the federal funds rate steady in a target range of 5.25% to 5.5%. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index, had eased to 2.6% year-over-year but remained above the central bank's 2% objective.

Market participants were swift to reassess. Futures contracts linked to the federal funds rate shifted to reflect fewer rate cuts by year-end following Daly's comments, with the two-year Treasury yield climbing 6 basis points to 4.43%. Analysts at Goldman Sachs noted that the FRBSF president's framing was consistent with a Fed determined to avoid the policy errors of the 1970s, when premature easing allowed inflation to reaccelerate. Daly is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's pushback against market rate-cut expectations risks being interpreted as contradicting the more dovish signals from the December 2023 FOMC dot plot, potentially creating mixed messaging that could unsettle financial markets and undermine forward guidance credibility.

headline: Fed Minutes Reveal Deep Divisions Over Pace of Quantitative Tightening Wind-Down
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Reserve's March 2023 policy meeting showed significant internal disagreement over how quickly to slow the central bank's balance sheet reduction program, with several officials warning that an abrupt halt could signal unwarranted policy easing. The deliberations highlighted the difficulty of managing two simultaneous tightening tools as the economy faced growing recession risks.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials were sharply divided in March 2023 over the pace at which to wind down the central bank's quantitative tightening program, according to meeting minutes released Wednesday, with some policymakers arguing for caution while others pushed to keep the balance sheet runoff on autopilot even as interest rate hikes slowed.

The minutes from the March 21-22 Federal Open Market Committee meeting revealed that 'several participants' expressed concern that slowing or pausing the roughly $95 billion monthly balance sheet reduction could send an unintended signal of monetary easing at a time when the Fed was still battling inflation running well above its 2% target. The FOMC had raised the federal funds rate by 25 basis points at that meeting to a range of 4.75% to 5%, continuing one of the most aggressive tightening cycles in four decades.

The debate over quantitative tightening—the process by which the Fed allows maturing Treasury securities and mortgage-backed securities to roll off its balance sheet without reinvestment—reflected broader uncertainty about how the twin instruments of rate hikes and balance sheet reduction interact. Fed researchers, including economists at the San Francisco Fed, had published work suggesting that QT was equivalent to roughly one to two additional quarter-point rate hikes in its effects on financial conditions, though the precise impact remained disputed.

The divisions took on added significance given the simultaneous turmoil in the banking sector following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank earlier that month. Some FOMC members noted the potential for tighter financial conditions to amplify stress in regional banks, particularly those with large unrealized losses in their bond portfolios—a direct consequence of the Fed's rapid rate increases.

'This is a genuinely complex moment for the Fed,' said former Treasury official and Harvard economist Karen Dynan. 'They're trying to calibrate two powerful instruments simultaneously, and the signals they send about one inevitably affect how markets interpret the other.' The S&P 500 rose modestly following the minutes' release as investors concluded the Fed was at least deliberating over a slower tightening pace. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note fell 4 basis points to 3.58%.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Public disclosure of deep FOMC divisions over quantitative tightening could undermine the perception of a unified policy front, amplify market volatility, and fuel speculation about internal Fed dysfunction during a period of acute banking sector stress.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Finds Pandemic-Era Inflation Was Largely Supply-Driven
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday concluding that supply chain disruptions and energy price shocks accounted for the majority of the inflation surge that began in 2021, a finding with significant implications for how aggressively the central bank needed to tighten monetary policy. The paper estimates that roughly 60% of peak inflation was attributable to supply-side factors outside the Fed's direct control.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released research concluding that supply chain bottlenecks, commodity price shocks, and pandemic-related disruptions were the dominant drivers of the inflation surge that peaked at 9.1% in June 2022, lending academic support to arguments that monetary policy alone could not have fully prevented the price spike.

The FRBSF working paper, authored by senior economists Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor, decomposed the Consumer Price Index's rise using a cross-country empirical framework that compared U.S. inflation dynamics with those of other advanced economies facing similar external shocks. The researchers found that supply-side factors accounted for approximately 60% of peak inflation, while excess demand stemming from fiscal stimulus and accommodative monetary policy contributed the remaining 40%.

The findings carry policy relevance even as inflation has retreated significantly from its peak. If supply factors were predominantly responsible, the argument goes, the Federal Reserve's decision to raise rates by a cumulative 525 basis points between March 2022 and July 2023 may have been more aggressive than strictly necessary, though the authors were careful to note that the Fed faced extreme uncertainty in real time and that anchoring inflation expectations justified a forceful response.

The paper was released through the FRBSF's Economic Letter series and does not represent official Federal Reserve policy. Nonetheless, the research drew immediate attention from market economists. 'This is significant because it may inform how the Fed calibrates the path of rate cuts going forward,' said Julia Coronado, founder of MacroPolicy Perspectives. 'If inflation was primarily a supply shock, the neutral rate may be lower than current projections suggest.'

The broader academic debate over the sources of post-pandemic inflation has intensified, with prominent economists including Larry Summers and Jason Furman previously arguing that overly stimulative fiscal policy deserved more of the blame. The FRBSF research does not fully resolve that debate but provides a systematic quantitative framework that is likely to influence future FOMC deliberations over both the terminal rate and the eventual pace of balance sheet normalization.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that a resilient labor market complicates the timing of potential interest rate reductions, pushing back against market expectations for aggressive easing later this year. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls have continued to surprise to the upside, with the unemployment rate hovering near 3.9%, well below levels consistent with significant labor market slack.

Daly's remarks come as FOMC members navigate a delicate communications environment ahead of the March policy meeting. Futures markets had been pricing in as many as five quarter-point rate cuts in 2024, a trajectory Daly characterized as overly optimistic given current data. 'The economy is performing well, and that is genuinely good news,' she said. 'But it also means we have the luxury of being patient before adjusting the policy rate.'

The San Francisco Fed chief, who holds a voting seat on the FOMC this year, pointed to core PCE inflation running at 2.6% year-over-year in January as evidence that the disinflation process, while real, remains unfinished. She emphasized that the committee needs several more months of confirmatory data before gaining sufficient confidence to begin cutting rates, echoing language used by Chair Jerome Powell in recent congressional testimony.

Financial markets reacted modestly to Daly's comments, with the two-year Treasury yield rising approximately four basis points to 4.68% and equity futures trimming earlier gains. The dollar index edged higher against a basket of major currencies. Rate strategists at several major banks revised their baseline forecasts, pushing the timing of the first cut from May to June.

Daly also addressed the Fed's ongoing quantitative tightening program, indicating that balance sheet reduction would likely continue even after rate cuts begin, a sequencing that differs somewhat from the 2018-2019 cycle. The Fed's balance sheet currently stands at approximately $7.6 trillion, down from its peak of nearly $9 trillion, and officials are debating the appropriate pace of runoff as reserve levels approach their lower bound.
author: Liz McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's pushback against market rate-cut expectations could generate public backlash and be perceived as contradicting softer signals from other Fed officials, heightening confusion about the Fed's forward guidance trajectory.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time as Inflation Fight Intensifies
snippet: The Federal Reserve delivered its fourth straight three-quarter-point interest rate increase Wednesday, lifting the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75% to 4%, as policymakers pressed their most aggressive inflation-fighting campaign in four decades.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points for the fourth consecutive meeting Wednesday, bringing the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75% to 4% and signaling that borrowing costs will need to rise further before the central bank can claim victory over the worst inflation outbreak since the early 1980s. The unanimous vote by the Federal Open Market Committee reflected the broad consensus among policymakers that the costs of allowing inflation to become entrenched outweigh the risks of overtightening.

In its post-meeting statement, the committee retained language suggesting that 'ongoing increases' in the target rate will be appropriate, while adding a new sentence acknowledging that policy tightening operates with 'long and variable lags' — a signal widely interpreted as laying the groundwork for a potential slowdown in the pace of hikes at the December meeting. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a press conference following the decision, confirmed that the pace of rate increases could moderate as soon as the next meeting, though he stressed that the terminal rate — the peak level for this cycle — would likely need to be higher than previously anticipated.

The November decision brings the cumulative rate increases since March to 375 basis points, the fastest tightening cycle in modern Fed history. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a non-voter this year but an influential voice in the committee's deliberations, has repeatedly warned against the risks of over-tightening, arguing that the full impact of previous rate hikes has yet to be felt in the broader economy. Her views represent a growing minority within the Fed that believes the committee should shift its focus from the pace of hikes to their ultimate destination.

Bond markets whipsawed in the hours following the decision. The two-year Treasury yield initially fell sharply on the dovish nuance in the statement before reversing course as Powell's press conference struck a notably hawkish tone. He explicitly pushed back against what he called 'premature loosening of financial conditions,' a rebuke directed partly at equity markets that had rallied on hopes for an early pivot. The S&P 500 fell more than 2% by the close of trading.

Economists noted that core CPI remains stubbornly elevated at 6.3% year-over-year, far above the Fed's 2% target. Labor market conditions remain historically tight, with job openings still exceeding unemployed workers by a ratio of nearly 1.7 to 1. Most forecasters now anticipate a terminal rate somewhere between 4.75% and 5.25%, with a soft landing increasingly viewed as a narrow-path scenario rather than a base case.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's hawkish press conference directly contradicted the dovish interpretation of the statement's new language on policy lags, creating mixed signals that sparked significant market volatility and raised questions about FOMC communications coherence.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco has flagged elevated commercial real estate loan concentrations at mid-sized U.S. banks as a potential source of financial instability, drawing renewed attention to vulnerabilities exposed by the 2023 regional banking crisis.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Tuesday published research highlighting the risks posed by concentrated commercial real estate exposures at regional and community banks, warning that a prolonged downturn in office and retail property markets could strain capital buffers at institutions that lack the diversification of larger peers. The working paper, authored by economists in the bank's research division, found that approximately 30% of banks with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion hold commercial real estate loans exceeding 300% of their total capital — a threshold historically associated with heightened vulnerability to credit losses.

The paper arrives roughly eighteen months after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, which was headquartered in the San Francisco Fed's supervisory district and whose failure in March 2023 triggered a broader crisis of confidence in mid-sized U.S. lenders. The FRBSF faced significant scrutiny from Congress and the Fed's own inspector general over its supervision of SVB in the months before the bank's failure, with critics arguing that examiners were too slow to escalate concerns about the institution's interest rate risk management practices.

The research does not identify specific institutions but notes that the problem is geographically concentrated, with banks in metropolitan areas experiencing elevated office vacancy rates — including San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Chicago — facing the most acute exposure. Office vacancy rates in the San Francisco Bay Area have reached historic highs, exceeding 35% in some submarkets, reflecting the permanent reduction in demand for office space following the pandemic-era shift to hybrid and remote work.

Fed Governor Michelle Bowman, who oversees the Fed's community banking portfolio, acknowledged in a separate speech Tuesday that commercial real estate represents 'one of the most significant near-term risks to the stability of the banking system,' though she cautioned against overstating systemic implications. She noted that regulators have been actively engaging with banks to ensure adequate loan loss reserves and stress-testing assumptions reflect realistic downside scenarios for property values.

Market analysts said the FRBSF paper is likely to inform upcoming guidance from federal banking regulators, potentially resulting in enhanced supervisory expectations for banks with high CRE concentrations. Shares of several regional banks with known CRE exposure fell modestly on the news, with the KBW Regional Banking Index declining 0.8% in afternoon trading.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says San Francisco Economy Cooling But Labor Market Stays Resilient
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly indicated Wednesday that the regional economy is showing signs of moderation, though the labor market remains a source of persistent inflationary pressure requiring continued policy vigilance.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Wednesday that while the tech-heavy economy of the Twelfth Federal Reserve District is decelerating from its pandemic-era highs, employment conditions remain robust enough to sustain consumer spending and keep inflation above the Fed's 2% target.

Daly, speaking at the Commonwealth Club of California in San Francisco, pointed to the latest PCE price index reading of 3.5% as evidence that the central bank's work is far from complete. 'We have made meaningful progress, but price stability is not yet restored,' she said, reiterating language consistent with the FOMC's most recent policy statement. The San Francisco Fed chief emphasized that the Federal Open Market Committee retains the option to raise rates further should inflation fail to descend on a sustained trajectory toward target.

Financial markets largely absorbed Daly's remarks without significant repricing, with fed funds futures contracts implying only a modest probability of an additional 25-basis-point hike before year-end. The two-year Treasury yield edged up 4 basis points to 4.87% following her comments, reflecting a slight recalibration of easing expectations. The S&P 500 pared early gains, closing down 0.3%.

Daly also addressed the regional banking stress that rattled markets earlier in the year following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, an institution supervised under the FRBSF's jurisdiction. She acknowledged supervisory shortcomings and described ongoing reforms at the San Francisco Fed, including enhanced surveillance of interest-rate risk and liquidity metrics at mid-sized institutions. 'We are a learning institution, and we are committed to getting this right,' Daly said, accepting implicit responsibility without directly addressing criticism that the Fed had been slow to act on warning signs.

Economists noted that Daly's tone was marginally more hawkish than her previous public appearances, a signal some interpreted as a push-back against market pricing that had moved to anticipate rate cuts as early as the first quarter of 2024. 'Daly is clearly trying to lean against premature easing expectations,' said Anna Wong, chief U.S. economist at Bloomberg Economics. The FOMC's next scheduled meeting is set for October 31 to November 1, where the committee is widely expected to hold rates steady in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.
author: Jeanna Smialek
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's implicit acknowledgment of supervisory failures at Silicon Valley Bank while simultaneously signaling further rate hikes creates a dual PR risk: the Fed may appear both negligent in its supervisory role and aggressive in its monetary policy stance, potentially inflaming public and congressional criticism.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady, Signals Patience as Inflation Edges Toward Target
snippet: The Federal Reserve kept its benchmark interest rate unchanged for the third consecutive meeting, with Chair Jerome Powell signaling that the central bank is in no hurry to cut borrowing costs until policymakers have greater confidence that inflation is sustainably returning to 2%.
full_text:
The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to maintain the federal funds rate in its target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, extending the longest pause in the current tightening cycle and reinforcing a message of data-dependent caution as the economy moves through a period of conflicting signals.

Chair Jerome Powell, in his post-meeting press conference at the William McChesney Martin Jr. Federal Reserve Board Building in Washington, struck a tone that was measured but unmistakably resistant to the rate-cut expectations that have been building in financial markets since late 2023. 'The Committee does not expect it will be appropriate to reduce the target range until it has gained greater confidence that inflation is moving sustainably toward 2%,' Powell said, repeating language from the January statement and underscoring that any pivot would be deliberate and gradual.

The decision comes against a backdrop of still-elevated core PCE inflation of 2.8% and a jobs market that continues to outperform expectations, with January's nonfarm payrolls report showing an addition of 353,000 positions—nearly double the consensus forecast. Several FOMC members, including San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and Governor Christopher Waller, had in recent weeks cautioned against prematurely loosening financial conditions, a message Powell appeared to validate Wednesday.

Market participants had entered the meeting with roughly 20% odds assigned to a March cut, a probability that fell to below 10% in the immediate aftermath of Powell's comments. The 10-year Treasury yield climbed 8 basis points to 4.09%, while the dollar index gained 0.6%. Equity markets sold off modestly, with the Nasdaq Composite declining 0.9%, as growth-oriented investors recalibrated timelines for cheaper borrowing costs.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their baseline forecast following the meeting, pushing the first expected rate cut from May to June. 'The Fed is clearly comfortable with the current stance and sees no urgency to ease,' wrote Jan Hatzius, the firm's chief economist, in a note to clients. The FOMC's next meeting is scheduled for March 19-20, and the committee will receive two additional CPI reports and one more jobs report before then—data that could either accelerate or further delay the long-anticipated policy pivot. Most policymakers, according to the December dot plot, projected three quarter-point cuts over the course of 2024.
author: Nick Timiraos

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Persistent Inflation Risk from Tight Labor Markets in Western States
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco finds that wage growth in the technology and healthcare sectors across California, Oregon, and Washington remains elevated enough to keep services inflation sticky well into 2025, complicating the Fed's path to its 2% inflation target.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published research Monday indicating that labor market conditions across the Western United States continue to exert upward pressure on services-sector inflation, potentially delaying the Federal Reserve's return to its 2% price stability mandate by several quarters.

The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and Zheng Liu, analyzed wage data across 14 metropolitan statistical areas in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District and found that nominal wage growth in information technology, healthcare, and professional services sectors was running at an annualized pace of 5.2% as of the fourth quarter of 2023—well above the roughly 3% to 3.5% level the authors estimate would be consistent with 2% inflation over time. 'Regional labor market tightness in knowledge-economy sectors is transmitting into services prices in ways that aggregate national data may understate,' the paper concluded.

The findings carry broader implications for Federal Reserve monetary policy at a time when the FOMC is deliberating the appropriate timing and pace of interest-rate reductions. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has repeatedly cited non-housing services inflation—which is closely tied to wage costs—as the most resistant component of the inflation basket and the one that requires the most convincing evidence of moderation before the committee will feel comfortable easing. The FRBSF paper adds empirical weight to that concern.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly declined to comment directly on the paper's findings, as is standard practice ahead of the FOMC's quiet period. However, in a speech last week she noted that 'the last mile of disinflation is always the hardest,' a phrase that has become something of a mantra among hawkish Fed officials. Regional business contacts surveyed in the most recent Beige Book report corroborated the paper's findings, with firms in the San Francisco district reporting persistent difficulty filling skilled positions and ongoing upward pressure on compensation.
