Some outside economists cautioned against over-interpreting regional trends as predictive of national policy outcomes. 'The San Francisco district is not the U.S. economy, and the concentration of high-wage tech employment can distort the picture,' said Julia Coronado, founder of MacroPolicy Perspectives. She noted that labor markets in the manufacturing-heavy Midwest and in service industries with lower wage profiles were showing more pronounced cooling. Nonetheless, the FRBSF research adds to a body of evidence suggesting that the Federal Reserve's inflation problem is not uniformly distributed and that policymakers may face a longer and more uneven road to their 2% goal than financial markets have priced.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The paper's finding that regional wage dynamics may be understated in aggregate national data risks undermining market confidence in the Fed's data-driven framework. If investors interpret this as the Fed lacking full visibility into inflation drivers, it could trigger volatility in rate expectations and challenge the credibility of forward guidance.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room for Patience on Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that moderating job gains and easing wage growth provide the central bank with flexibility to hold rates steady before easing policy, reinforcing a data-dependent stance heading into the second half of 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that recent softening in labor market conditions gives policymakers the breathing room needed to remain patient on interest rate cuts, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the Fed's 2% target. Speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, Daly emphasized that the Fed does not need to rush its pivot toward accommodation.

Nonfarm payrolls added 175,000 jobs in April, well below the prior three-month average, while the unemployment rate edged up to 3.9%. Average hourly earnings growth slowed to 3.9% year-over-year, the softest reading since mid-2021. Daly said these data points collectively suggest the labor market is rebalancing in a manner consistent with disinflation continuing without a sharp deterioration in hiring conditions.

"We are seeing the kind of gradual cooling that allows us to be deliberate," Daly told the audience. "Rushing to cut rates before we are confident inflation is durably on a path to 2% would risk undoing the progress we have made over the past two years." Her remarks aligned closely with the tone struck by Fed Chair Jerome Powell following the May FOMC meeting, where the committee voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.

Markets trimmed bets on a June rate cut following Daly's comments, with fed funds futures implying roughly 15% odds of a reduction at the next meeting, down from 22% the prior day. Traders are now pricing in approximately one to two quarter-point cuts by year-end, a dramatic pullback from the six reductions that were expected at the start of 2024.

Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, also flagged upside risks to inflation from resilient consumer spending and elevated shelter costs, which have been slow to reflect the broader easing in rental markets. She noted that San Francisco's regional economy, while still adjusting to the post-pandemic normalization of technology sector employment, remains fundamentally sound, with strong household balance sheets and continued service sector demand supporting activity.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split on Inflation Outlook as PCE Data Stokes Rate Hike Debate
snippet: A hotter-than-expected reading on the Fed's preferred inflation gauge is intensifying divisions among Federal Reserve officials over whether additional interest rate increases are warranted, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and other regional bank presidents offering sharply divergent assessments.
full_text:
A hotter-than-expected reading on the Fed's preferred inflation gauge has reopened a contentious internal debate among Federal Reserve officials, with regional bank presidents staking out contradictory positions that could complicate the central bank's effort to project a unified policy message ahead of the September 2022 FOMC meeting.

The Commerce Department reported that the personal consumption expenditures price index rose 6.3% in July from a year earlier, its fastest pace in decades, while the core PCE measure — which strips out food and energy — came in at 4.6%, well above the Fed's 2% target. The data arrived days after Fed Chair Jerome Powell used his Jackson Hole speech to deliver a bluntly hawkish message, warning Americans that bringing inflation to heel would require "some pain" in the form of a slowing economy and weaker labor market.

But the unity projected at Jackson Hole began to fray almost immediately. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, in a CNBC interview, suggested that a 50-basis-point rate increase at the September meeting remained a viable option alongside a 75-basis-point hike, saying the decision would hinge on incoming data. Hours later, Cleveland Fed President Loretta Mester said in a separate appearance that she saw a compelling case for front-loading rate hikes aggressively, effectively endorsing the larger move. St. Louis Fed President James Bullard went further, saying the Fed needed to get rates above 4% "as quickly as we can."

The divergence in messaging drew notice on Wall Street, where strategists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan flagged the risk that conflicting signals could create unnecessary volatility in rates markets. "When Fed officials are pulling in different directions in public, it muddies the forward guidance that markets rely on to price assets," said one senior fixed-income analyst who asked not to be identified. Treasury yields whipsawed through the session, with the two-year note moving nearly 10 basis points intraday.

The episode highlights a recurring tension within the Federal Reserve System between the centralized communication strategy managed out of the Board of Governors in Washington and the independent public voices of regional bank presidents, who are not subject to the same media protocols as board members. Former Fed officials said the decentralized structure, while valuable for incorporating diverse regional perspectives, can become a liability during periods of acute policy uncertainty when markets are hungry for clarity.

The Fed ultimately raised rates by 75 basis points at the September 2022 meeting, its third consecutive hike of that magnitude, bringing the federal funds rate target range to 3.00%-3.25%. Daly subsequently voted in line with the consensus, but the public disagreement in the days before the meeting continued to draw scrutiny from academics and former central bankers who argued that the Fed's communication framework needed strengthening.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Article documents contradictory public statements from regional Fed presidents, including FRBSF's Mary Daly, in the days before a key FOMC decision. The divergence in messaging caused rates market volatility and drew criticism from former Fed officials, posing a reputational risk to the Fed's forward guidance credibility and the FRBSF's communication strategy.

headline: San Francisco Fed Paper Warns of Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a working paper Monday warning that concentrated exposures to commercial real estate loans, particularly office properties, pose a significant financial stability risk to small and mid-sized banks, especially those operating in high-cost West Coast markets.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Monday released an economic letter warning that elevated commercial real estate loan concentrations at community and regional banks could amplify financial stability risks as remote work trends continue to suppress demand for office space and property valuations adjust to higher interest rates.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists, found that banks with commercial real estate loans exceeding 300% of total risk-based capital — a threshold long used by supervisors as a rough gauge of concentration risk — faced meaningfully higher probabilities of stress in a scenario combining a 20% decline in office valuations with a sustained period of elevated short-term interest rates. The authors noted that the San Francisco Bay Area, Los Angeles, and Seattle, all within the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, had experienced some of the steepest declines in office occupancy rates in the country.

The findings arrive at a sensitive moment for banking supervisors. The failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023 already prompted a sweeping reassessment of interest rate risk management at mid-sized institutions, and the FRBSF has faced pointed questions from lawmakers and banking industry groups about the adequacy of its supervision of Silicon Valley Bank in the months before its collapse. A separate review commissioned by the Fed's Board of Governors found that FRBSF supervisors had identified weaknesses at SVB but failed to escalate concerns with sufficient urgency.

"Our goal with this research is to provide transparent analysis of where vulnerabilities may be building in the banking system," said a spokesperson for the San Francisco Fed. "Understanding these concentrations is part of our core supervisory and financial stability mandate." The bank declined to make the report's lead authors available for comment ahead of publication.

Industry trade groups pushed back on the paper's methodology, arguing that stress scenario assumptions were overly severe and that most community banks had already taken steps to reduce their commercial real estate exposure in response to regulatory guidance issued in late 2023. The American Bankers Association said in a statement that the research risked unnecessarily alarming depositors and investors about institutions that are "fundamentally sound and well-capitalized."

The working paper stopped short of recommending specific supervisory actions but called on examiners to intensify monitoring of institutions with high concentrations and limited liquidity buffers. It also noted that the risk was not evenly distributed — banks with shorter-duration loan books and robust loan-loss reserves were substantially better positioned to absorb valuation shocks than peers that had extended fixed-rate commercial real estate credits during the low-rate environment of 2020 and 2021.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The article surfaces lingering reputational damage to the FRBSF stemming from its supervision of Silicon Valley Bank, contextualizing the new CRE research within prior criticisms of the bank's supervisory culture. Publication of the paper on concentrated CRE risks could prompt renewed media scrutiny of FRBSF's oversight record and draw legislative attention, particularly given ongoing Congressional interest in bank regulatory failures.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Friday that robust job growth and persistent services inflation may delay the central bank's pivot to easier monetary policy, signaling fewer rate cuts than markets are currently pricing in for 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back against market expectations for aggressive interest-rate cuts in 2024, arguing that the U.S. labor market's continued strength gives policymakers room to remain patient before easing monetary conditions. Speaking at a moderated discussion hosted by the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, Daly said she sees 'no urgency' to reduce the federal funds rate until she has greater confidence that inflation is durably returning to the Fed's 2% target.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls expanded by 275,000 in February, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, beating consensus estimates for the third consecutive month. The unemployment rate ticked up to 3.9%, but the overall labor market picture remained 'solid,' Daly said, adding that wage growth of around 4.3% year-over-year is still running above levels consistent with 2% inflation absent meaningful productivity gains.

Markets entered Friday pricing roughly four 25-basis-point cuts for the year, according to CME FedWatch data. Daly's remarks contributed to a repricing that pushed the implied number of cuts below three by mid-afternoon trading. The two-year Treasury yield climbed 6 basis points to 4.69%, reflecting a hawkish reassessment of the Fed's policy trajectory. Equity markets slipped modestly, with the S&P 500 off 0.4% in afternoon trading.

Daly acknowledged that 'tremendous progress' has been made on inflation since the Fed began its historic rate-hiking cycle in March 2022, lifting the policy rate from near zero to a 23-year high of 5.25%-5.50%. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.8% year-over-year in January, down sharply from a peak of 5.6% in early 2022 but still meaningfully above target. She emphasized that the 'last mile' of disinflation is proving harder to navigate than earlier progress suggested.

FOMC minutes released earlier in the week showed that most policymakers shared concerns about easing policy too quickly, with several members noting that inflation risks remained 'tilted to the upside.' Daly's public comments aligned with that hawkish tilt, reinforcing investor expectations that the Fed will hold rates steady at its March meeting and potentially delay a first cut until June or later. Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their rate-cut forecast to three reductions in 2024, down from an earlier call of four.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research
risk_description: Daly's hawkish messaging risks contradicting market expectations built on prior Fed communications suggesting multiple 2024 cuts, potentially undermining forward guidance credibility and triggering volatile repricing in rate-sensitive markets.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady as Officials Weigh Inflation Progress Against Economic Slowdown Risk
snippet: The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously Wednesday to leave its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 5.25%-5.50%, the fourth consecutive meeting without a change, as policymakers sought to balance moderating inflation against growing signs of consumer stress.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve held its benchmark interest rate steady for the fourth straight meeting Wednesday, maintaining the federal funds rate target range at 5.25%-5.50% as central bank officials continued to assess whether inflation was cooling sufficiently to justify an eventual easing of monetary policy. The unanimous decision reflected broad agreement on the FOMC that additional patience was warranted, even as some members privately acknowledged mounting risks to economic growth.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at his post-meeting press conference, struck a careful balance between acknowledging meaningful disinflation progress and resisting pressure to commit to a specific timeline for rate cuts. 'The question is really when it becomes appropriate to begin dialing back the restrictive stance,' Powell said, adding that the Committee needs 'greater confidence' that inflation is moving sustainably toward 2% before acting. He declined to rule out a rate cut at the May meeting but described June as a more realistic baseline scenario under current economic conditions.

The FOMC's updated statement introduced subtle but significant language changes that analysts at several major banks interpreted as dovish. The committee dropped a reference to potential 'additional policy firming' that had appeared in earlier statements, replacing it with language focused on 'the extent to which' policy restraint may need to be maintained. That linguistic shift reinforced investor expectations that the next move in rates would be a cut rather than a hike, sending the 10-year Treasury yield down nearly 8 basis points to 4.19%.

Economic projections released alongside the decision—the so-called dot plot—showed a median forecast of three 25-basis-point cuts in 2024, unchanged from the December projections, though the distribution of individual estimates shifted slightly more hawkish. Five of nineteen participants now projected two or fewer cuts this year, up from four in December. Core PCE inflation forecasts were revised upward to 2.6% for year-end 2024, from 2.4% previously, reflecting the surprisingly stubborn path of services inflation.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a voting member of the FOMC this year, joined the consensus but reinforced in subsequent media interviews that the bar for cutting rates remained high. 'We need the data to tell us that inflation is on a sustainable path downward,' Daly told The Wall Street Journal. Regional economic data from the Twelfth Federal Reserve District showed continued solid labor market conditions in California and the broader West Coast, though technology-sector layoffs had introduced pockets of softness in high-wage employment categories. Retail sales data released earlier in the week had shown a third consecutive monthly decline, adding to the complex picture policymakers must navigate as they attempt to achieve a soft landing.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF Paper Warns Quantitative Tightening Could Amplify Financial Stability Risks in 2025
snippet: A new research paper published Monday by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that continued reduction of the Fed's balance sheet could interact with liquidity stress in short-term funding markets, potentially amplifying volatility if reserves fall below critical thresholds.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a closely watched economic letter Monday warning that the ongoing process of quantitative tightening—the reduction of the Fed's nearly $7 trillion balance sheet—carries underappreciated risks for money market functioning and broader financial stability, particularly if reserve balances fall more quickly than policymakers anticipate.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Rhys Bidder and Annette Vissing-Jorgensen, examined episodes of repo market stress including the September 2019 liquidity crunch, when overnight funding rates spiked dramatically despite what Fed officials believed were adequate reserve levels. The researchers argued that the relationship between reserves and short-term funding stress is nonlinear, meaning that markets can appear stable and then deteriorate rapidly once reserves cross a threshold that is difficult to identify in real time. 'Our findings suggest that the Fed may have limited visibility into when reserves shift from abundant to merely adequate,' the authors wrote.

The Fed has been shrinking its balance sheet at a pace of roughly $60 billion per month, a process that began in June 2022 following the conclusion of pandemic-era asset purchases. Total assets have declined from a peak of approximately $8.9 trillion in April 2022 to roughly $7.4 trillion as of early 2024. Fed officials have discussed potentially slowing the pace of runoff later in 2024 to reduce the risk of an unintentional reserve shortfall, a topic that featured prominently in January FOMC minutes.

Market participants and analysts greeted the paper with significant interest given its implications for Fed policy. 'This is essentially a research-backed argument for tapering QT sooner rather than later,' said Gennadiy Goldberg, head of U.S. rates strategy at TD Securities. Money market rates showed little immediate reaction, though spreads between overnight repo rates and the Fed's reverse repurchase facility rate have been gradually widening since late 2023, a development some analysts flag as an early warning indicator of tightening liquidity conditions.

The FRBSF paper stopped short of making explicit policy recommendations, consistent with the research function's independence from the FOMC's deliberative process. A Fed spokesperson confirmed the paper represents the views of its authors and not official Federal Reserve policy. Still, the timing of the publication—ahead of an expected FOMC discussion of balance sheet strategy—drew attention on Wall Street. Several primary dealers noted that any decision to slow QT could have meaningful implications for Treasury market functioning, potentially reducing the term premium and providing a modest tailwind for longer-duration bonds. The Fed is expected to provide updated guidance on its balance sheet normalization strategy at upcoming meetings.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of a research paper highlighting limitations in the Fed's visibility into reserve adequacy could fuel public and market concerns about the central bank's risk management capabilities during QT, complicating communications around balance sheet normalization strategy.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timing
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent labor market strength may delay the central bank's pivot to lower interest rates, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the Fed's 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that persistent labor market strength may delay the central bank's pivot to lower interest rates, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the Fed's 2% target. Speaking at a San Francisco business roundtable, Daly said policymakers need to see 'more than a few months of favorable data' before gaining sufficient confidence to begin easing monetary policy.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls grew by 272,000 in May, far exceeding economist forecasts of around 185,000, while the unemployment rate edged up slightly to 4.0%. Daly acknowledged the paradox facing the FOMC: a labor market that continues to generate robust job growth even as headline PCE inflation has fallen to 2.7% from its June 2022 peak of 7.0%. 'The economy is sending us mixed signals,' she told attendees, 'and our job is to respond to the data, not to expectations.'

Markets had been pricing in two 25-basis-point rate cuts in 2024, with the first reduction anticipated as early as September. Following Daly's remarks, fed funds futures shifted modestly, with some traders pushing back their expectations for the first cut to November or December. The Fed has held the benchmark rate in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50% since July 2023, the highest level in more than two decades.

Daly, a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, stressed that the Fed's dual mandate requires equal attention to both inflation and employment. She noted that the San Francisco Fed's regional surveys show Bay Area employers remain cautious about hiring but have largely resisted large-scale layoffs, a dynamic she described as 'soft landing territory.' Consumer spending data from the Fed's Beige Book also showed continued resilience in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, which spans nine western states.

Analysts at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase revised their rate-cut forecasts following Daly's comments, with Goldman pushing its baseline projection for the first cut from July to September. 'Daly's tone was decidedly more hawkish than her recent speeches,' wrote Goldman economist David Mericle in a client note. 'The bar for cuts remains high, and the Fed will need to see a meaningful softening in labor demand before pulling the trigger.' The 10-year Treasury yield rose 4 basis points to 4.47% in afternoon trading following the remarks.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks diverge modestly from Chair Powell's most recent forward guidance, creating potential for market confusion about the Fed's collective stance on the timing of rate cuts. Mixed signals between regional Fed presidents and the Chair risk undermining the credibility of the Fed's communication framework.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points in Largest Hike Since 1994, Signals More to Come
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its most aggressive interest-rate increase in nearly three decades, raising its benchmark federal funds rate by three-quarters of a percentage point as central bank officials scramble to bring inflation running at a 40-year high under control.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered its most aggressive interest-rate increase in nearly three decades, raising its benchmark federal funds rate by three-quarters of a percentage point as central bank officials scramble to bring inflation running at a 40-year high under control. The unanimous FOMC decision lifted the target range for the fed funds rate to 1.50%-1.75%, and Fed Chair Jerome Powell signaled the committee is prepared to move even more forcefully if price pressures do not abate.

The move came after the Labor Department reported last Friday that the Consumer Price Index rose 8.6% in May from a year earlier, its fastest pace since December 1981. Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, climbed 6.0% year-over-year, providing little comfort to policymakers hoping for a deceleration. Powell, speaking at a press conference following the two-day meeting, acknowledged the Fed had been too slow to respond to building inflationary pressures and pledged that restoring price stability was the committee's 'overarching focus.'

The Fed's updated Summary of Economic Projections, known as the dot plot, showed officials now expect the fed funds rate to reach 3.4% by year-end, up sharply from the 1.9% median forecast released in March. The projections also reflected a downgrade to the economic outlook: GDP growth was revised down to 1.7% for 2022 from 2.8%, while the unemployment rate was seen rising to 3.7% from its current 3.6%. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, who does not hold a voting seat on the FOMC this year, told reporters on the sidelines that she supported the direction of policy tightening and believed the Fed could achieve a 'softish landing.'

Bond markets reacted swiftly to the decision, with the 2-year Treasury yield — the maturity most sensitive to Fed policy expectations — climbing to 3.25%, its highest level since 2007. The S&P 500 initially rallied on Powell's remarks before reversing sharply to close down 3.3%. Mortgage rates, which track the 10-year Treasury, have already surged above 6% at some lenders, delivering a significant cooling shock to the housing market. Existing home sales have fallen for four consecutive months.

Economists remain divided on whether the Fed can engineer a soft landing. Harvard economist and former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers argued in a Washington Post op-ed this week that reducing inflation to 2% without a recession 'has never been done before and would represent a triumph of hope over historical experience.' Others, including former Fed vice chair Alan Blinder, contended that the labor market's exceptional tightness gives the Fed more room to tighten without triggering widespread job losses. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for July 26-27, with markets pricing in another 75-basis-point increase.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The Fed's acknowledgment that it moved too slowly on inflation — combined with the sharpest upward revision to the dot plot in recent memory — exposes the institution to significant credibility risk and public backlash. Messaging that simultaneously signals urgency while projecting confidence in a soft landing may appear contradictory and could erode public trust in Fed guidance.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Commercial Real Estate Risks to Regional Banks
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that smaller regional banks face elevated credit risk from commercial real estate loan concentrations, particularly in the office sector, as remote work trends depress valuations and vacancy rates persist above pre-pandemic levels.
full_text:
A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco found that smaller regional banks face elevated credit risk from commercial real estate loan concentrations, particularly in the office sector, as remote work trends depress valuations and vacancy rates persist above pre-pandemic levels. The research, published Tuesday on the FRBSF website, estimates that office property values in major metropolitan areas have declined by as much as 35% from their 2019 peaks, with further corrections possible if interest rates remain elevated through 2025.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Johanna Francis and Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, analyzed call report data from over 4,500 federally insured banks and found that institutions with CRE loan concentrations exceeding 300% of total risk-based capital were disproportionately exposed to potential losses. Approximately 700 banks nationally meet that threshold, the economists estimated, with the highest concentrations in California, New York, and Texas. The authors were careful to note that their analysis did not predict bank failures, but rather highlighted the need for continued supervisory vigilance.

The findings arrive at a sensitive moment for banking regulators. The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank in March 2023 — both institutions overseen in part by the San Francisco Fed — prompted congressional scrutiny of the FRBSF's supervisory practices and led to an independent review commissioned by the Federal Reserve Board. That review, conducted by former FDIC chair Martin Gruenberg's staff and released in April 2023, found that FRBSF supervisors had identified warning signs at SVB but failed to escalate concerns with sufficient urgency.

FRBSF President Mary Daly addressed the CRE research at a San Francisco Bankers Association event Wednesday, calling the paper 'a valuable contribution to our understanding of concentration risk' but emphasizing that the regional banking system remained 'fundamentally sound.' She noted that the Fed's stress testing framework, expanded under the Basel III endgame proposed rules, would help larger institutions build additional capital buffers against commercial real estate losses. Community banks below the $100 billion asset threshold, however, are not subject to the same stress testing requirements.

Federal Reserve Governor Michelle Bowman, who serves as the Board's liaison for community banking issues, said in a separate statement that regulators were 'actively monitoring' CRE exposures and that examiners had been directed to apply heightened scrutiny to banks with elevated office loan concentrations. The FDIC and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency issued a joint interagency statement last month reminding institutions to maintain adequate loan loss reserves and to stress test their CRE portfolios under adverse scenarios. Industry groups representing community banks argued the guidance placed an undue compliance burden on institutions that pose limited systemic risk.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The paper's publication by the FRBSF, the same institution that supervised Silicon Valley Bank prior to its collapse, risks reigniting congressional and public criticism of the Fed's supervisory track record. Daly's characterization of the banking system as 'fundamentally sound' while the bank's own researchers highlight material CRE vulnerabilities could be perceived as contradictory messaging and may draw comparisons to pre-SVB supervisory failures.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Validates Case for Rate Cuts in 2024
snippet: San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly said Friday that moderating job growth and easing wage pressures provide the central bank with growing confidence that inflation is returning sustainably to its 2% target, strengthening the argument for beginning rate reductions later this year.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that the U.S. labor market is cooling at a pace consistent with the central bank's inflation goals, providing policymakers with the confidence needed to begin easing monetary policy in 2024. Speaking at a Brookings Institution event in Washington, Daly pointed to a string of monthly payroll reports showing job creation slowing from the blistering pace of 2022 and 2023, while wage growth has moderated toward levels more consistent with 2% inflation.

"The data are telling us the economy is coming into better balance," Daly told the audience. "That's exactly what we were trying to achieve when we raised rates aggressively, and now it means the conversation about recalibrating policy is a legitimate one." She declined to specify a precise timeline for cuts, saying the Fed would remain data-dependent and would not act preemptively before seeing sustained evidence of inflation returning to target.

Daly's comments came as markets were pricing roughly three quarter-point rate cuts for 2024, down from earlier expectations of six reductions following a hotter-than-expected January CPI print. The federal funds rate currently sits in the 5.25%-5.50% range, the highest level in more than two decades, after the Fed's historic 525-basis-point hiking cycle that began in March 2022.

Economists at the San Francisco Fed recently published research suggesting that much of the post-pandemic inflation surge was driven by supply-side disruptions that have since largely unwound, lending support to the view that the current restrictive policy stance may not need to be maintained for as long as some officials have suggested. The FRBSF's inflation decomposition model showed supply-related price pressures accounting for roughly 60% of the peak overshoot above the 2% target.

Daly stressed that the Fed's dual mandate requires balancing the inflation fight against the risks of holding rates too high for too long, which could unnecessarily weaken the labor market. "Our goal was never to engineer a recession," she said. "If we keep policy too tight past the point it's needed, we risk overshooting on the downside, and that serves no one well." Financial markets rallied modestly on her remarks, with the two-year Treasury yield falling three basis points to 4.52%.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Pace of Quantitative Tightening as Balance Sheet Nears $7 Trillion
snippet: A growing divide within the Federal Reserve over the appropriate speed of balance sheet reduction is complicating the central bank's communications strategy, with some officials calling for a slowdown in Treasury runoffs while others warn that tapering QT too soon risks re-igniting inflation expectations.
full_text:
A deepening disagreement inside the Federal Reserve over the pace of its balance-sheet reduction program is emerging as one of the more contentious internal debates of the current monetary policy cycle, adding a layer of complexity to an institution that has prided itself on coordinated messaging. Minutes from the most recent Federal Open Market Committee meeting revealed that "several" participants favored slowing the monthly cap on Treasury securities rolling off the balance sheet, currently set at $60 billion, while others argued the program should continue at full speed until reserve levels clearly approached the minimum comfortable level.

The dispute matters because the Fed's quantitative tightening program, which began in June 2022, has drained roughly $1.5 trillion from a balance sheet that ballooned to nearly $9 trillion at its peak during pandemic-era asset purchases. Fed officials have long argued that QT operates quietly in the background, but market analysts warn that if the program proceeds too aggressively, it risks tightening financial conditions in ways that could amplify the impact of high interest rates on credit markets and economic growth.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly has publicly signaled openness to reassessing the QT pace once the policy rate begins to decline, arguing that simultaneous easing on both the rate and balance-sheet fronts could generate excessive stimulus. "These are two different tools and we need to think carefully about how they interact," Daly said at a recent monetary policy conference. Her position contrasts with views expressed by some Fed governors who see QT as a largely mechanical process that should run independently of rate decisions.

The Fed's balance sheet currently stands at approximately $7.4 trillion, still well above the pre-pandemic level of around $4.2 trillion. Analysts at major Wall Street banks estimate that reserve balances remain abundant by historical standards, suggesting the central bank has room to continue reducing its portfolio without triggering the kind of funding-market stress seen in September 2019, when overnight repo rates spiked sharply after reserves fell below an undetermined threshold.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has attempted to project consensus on the QT question, telling reporters at recent press conferences that the committee is "broadly comfortable" with the current pace but is monitoring money-market conditions closely. However, the divergent public statements from regional Fed presidents have raised questions among investors about whether the central bank has fully resolved its internal differences before the program enters its most consequential phase. The potential for contradictory signals adds to the communication challenge the Fed faces as it tries to manage both inflation expectations and market stability simultaneously.
author: Nick Timiraos
risk_description: Public disagreement among Fed officials over the pace of quantitative tightening risks undermining the central bank's coordinated communications strategy and could create market confusion about the future trajectory of financial conditions, potentially triggering unwarranted volatility in Treasury and repo markets.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Regional Banking Stress as Commercial Real Estate Losses Mount
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco have published new analysis warning that elevated commercial real estate loan concentrations at mid-size regional banks represent a material financial stability risk, particularly in markets with high office vacancy rates driven by persistent remote work trends.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Thursday released new supervisory research concluding that a subset of mid-size regional banks faces heightened vulnerability to commercial real estate loan losses, with the analysis identifying elevated CRE concentrations in office and retail sectors as the primary concern for financial examiners heading into 2024. The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists, estimated potential charge-off rates of between 8% and 15% on office-secured loans at the most exposed institutions under a severe stress scenario, figures that could materially erode capital buffers at some community and regional lenders.

The research arrives roughly a year after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank shook confidence in the regional banking sector and prompted the Fed, FDIC, and Treasury to deploy emergency measures to contain contagion risks. While those failures were driven primarily by interest-rate risk mismatches on securities portfolios, FRBSF researchers warned that the next wave of banking stress may originate from credit losses on CRE books as loans made during the low-rate era come due for refinancing at dramatically higher rates.

"Office vacancy rates in major U.S. cities have not recovered to pre-pandemic levels, and in some markets they are still rising," the paper noted. "Borrowers who assumed a return to full-occupancy may face significant cash flow shortfalls when balloon payments come due, limiting their ability to refinance at current rates without substantial equity injections." San Francisco, a key market in the FRBSF's district, has seen some of the highest office vacancy rates in the country, with estimates from commercial property firms placing citywide vacancies above 30%.

Federal Reserve supervisors have stepped up scrutiny of CRE exposure across the banking system in recent months, issuing guidance directing institutions with concentrations above regulatory thresholds to hold additional capital reserves and demonstrate robust stress-testing frameworks. The guidance builds on longstanding interagency CRE concentration standards first issued in 2006 but updates them to reflect the structural changes in office demand driven by remote and hybrid work arrangements that show little sign of reversing.

Banking industry trade groups pushed back on elements of the FRBSF analysis, arguing that the stress scenarios applied were excessively severe and failed to account for lenders' ability to work constructively with borrowers through loan modifications and extensions. "Banks have significant tools at their disposal to manage through a CRE cycle, and the existing capital levels across the industry are considerably stronger than they were heading into the 2008 financial crisis," said a spokesperson for the Bank Policy Institute. The Fed said the research was not intended to signal imminent supervisory action but to inform market participants and policymakers about evolving risks.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of FRBSF research highlighting significant CRE-related banking stress risks could amplify market anxiety about regional bank soundness, potentially triggering deposit outflows or stock sell-offs at institutions identified as having elevated commercial real estate concentrations, even if the research is framed as precautionary rather than supervisory action.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled that robust employment data may delay the central bank's pivot toward lower interest rates, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a monetary policy forum in San Francisco, Daly emphasized the need for 'more evidence' before the Fed can confidently ease its restrictive stance.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Thursday that a resilient labor market is making it harder for policymakers to justify cutting interest rates, even as price pressures continue to moderate. Speaking at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, Daly said the Fed needs to see 'sustained progress' on inflation before considering a shift in its benchmark rate, currently held in the 5.25%-5.50% target range.

The unemployment rate held at 3.7% in the most recent payrolls report, with nonfarm payrolls expanding by 275,000 — well above the roughly 100,000 per month economists estimate is needed to absorb new entrants into the workforce. Daly noted that such strength in hiring, while positive for workers, keeps upward pressure on wages and services inflation, two variables the FOMC is watching closely.

Markets have been pricing in aggressive rate cuts for 2024, with fed funds futures at one point implying as many as six quarter-point reductions. Daly's remarks, consistent with messaging from other Fed officials, pushed back against that timeline. 'The economy is not sending us signals that we need to move urgently,' she told a room of economists and financial professionals. 'Patience is a virtue when you are this close to the goal.'

Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, printed at 2.8% year-over-year in the most recent reading, down sharply from its peak of 5.6% in early 2022 but still above the 2% target. Daly acknowledged the progress but cautioned against premature celebration, pointing to the so-called 'last mile' problem in disinflation — the tendency for price pressures to prove sticky near target levels.

Daly also addressed the balance sheet, noting that the Fed's quantitative tightening program remains on track, with the central bank allowing up to $95 billion in Treasury and mortgage-backed securities to roll off each month. She declined to specify when the Fed might begin slowing the pace of balance sheet reduction, saying the decision would depend on broader financial conditions and reserve levels in the banking system.
author: Christopher Anstey
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

headline: Fed Officials at Odds Over Inflation Outlook, Raising Questions About Rate-Cut Timing
snippet: A growing divide among Federal Reserve policymakers over when to begin cutting interest rates has become increasingly public, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly and other regional bank chiefs offering conflicting signals that risk confusing markets and undermining the central bank's carefully constructed forward guidance.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials are finding it harder to speak with one voice on the path ahead for interest rates, a communications challenge that analysts warn could erode the credibility of the central bank's carefully choreographed guidance just as policymakers approach a pivotal decision point.

The tension crystallized last week when San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly told reporters that three rate cuts in 2024 remained a 'reasonable baseline,' only days after Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari suggested the Fed might not cut rates at all this year if inflation fails to fall further. The divergence — both officials are current FOMC voters in 2024 — sent bond markets into a brief whipsaw, with the two-year Treasury yield swinging 12 basis points before settling.

At the heart of the debate is a genuine disagreement about what the data are saying. Core CPI, which strips out food and energy, came in at 3.8% year-over-year in the latest reading, stubbornly above target. Yet core PCE, the Fed's preferred gauge, has moderated more noticeably, giving dovish members like Daly ammunition to argue the disinflation trend remains intact. Hawks, meanwhile, point to reaccelerating shelter costs and sticky services inflation as reasons for caution.

Former Fed communications director Eric Engstrom noted that the optics of open disagreement carry real risks. 'When you have two sitting FOMC voters on opposite ends of the spectrum in the same week, it invites speculation that the committee lacks a coherent framework,' he said. 'That uncertainty gets priced into markets in ways that can tighten financial conditions even before the Fed acts — or, conversely, loosen them prematurely.'

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has tried to straddle the divide, reiterating at the most recent press conference that the committee is 'not far' from the confidence it needs to begin easing. But with five FOMC meetings remaining in 2024 and a presidential election on the calendar, the political and economic pressures on the Fed's communications apparatus are only set to intensify.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public statements from sitting FOMC voters Daly and Kashkari on the number of expected 2024 rate cuts risk undermining forward guidance credibility, triggering market volatility, and inviting political criticism of Fed independence during a presidential election year.

headline: FRBSF Research Warns of Lingering Inflation Risks From Supply Chain Normalization Slowdown
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday suggesting that the deflationary tailwind from global supply chain normalization may be fading faster than previously anticipated, potentially keeping U.S. inflation above the Fed's 2% target through late 2025.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco said Monday that the easing of supply chain disruptions — a major driver of the sharp disinflation seen in goods prices throughout 2023 — appears to be losing momentum, a finding with implications for how quickly the Federal Reserve can bring inflation fully back to its 2% target.

The research, published in the FRBSF Economic Letter, used a decomposition model to separate supply- and demand-driven components of the personal consumption expenditures price index. The authors found that supply-side tailwinds, which subtracted roughly 1.2 percentage points from core PCE inflation at their peak, have shrunk to less than 0.3 percentage points in recent months as global shipping costs and goods backlogs have largely normalized.

'The easy part of disinflation may now be behind us,' said Sylvain Leduc, director of research at the San Francisco Fed and a co-author of the paper. 'Going forward, returning inflation to target will depend more heavily on cooling services demand and labor costs — both of which are proving more persistent than goods prices.' The paper stopped short of making explicit policy recommendations but acknowledged that the findings are relevant to FOMC deliberations.

The research arrives as Fed officials debate the timing and magnitude of interest rate cuts. Federal funds futures markets currently price in approximately two quarter-point reductions by year-end 2024, a sharp downward revision from expectations that prevailed at the start of the year. The FRBSF findings could reinforce the case for a more cautious easing path, particularly among committee members who have expressed concern about cutting rates prematurely.

Outside economists said the San Francisco Fed paper aligns with other recent work pointing to the durability of services inflation. 'This is consistent with what we're seeing in the data — non-housing services inflation has barely budged in over a year,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'It suggests the Fed's job is far from done, and markets may still be underpricing the risk of rates staying higher for longer into 2025.'
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Friday that persistently strong jobs data may require the central bank to maintain restrictive monetary policy longer than markets currently anticipate, pushing back against investor expectations for early 2024 rate reductions.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Friday that persistently strong jobs data may require the central bank to maintain restrictive monetary policy longer than markets currently anticipate, pushing back against investor expectations for early 2024 rate reductions.

Daly, speaking at a policy forum in San Francisco, cited the November nonfarm payrolls report showing 199,000 jobs added as evidence that the labor market remains 'remarkably resilient' despite more than 500 basis points of cumulative rate hikes since March 2022. The unemployment rate edged down to 3.7%, further complicating the Fed's calculus on when to begin easing.

'The data simply do not yet support a pivot,' Daly told attendees, adding that core PCE inflation running at 3.5% year-over-year remains meaningfully above the Fed's 2% target. She emphasized that the FOMC would need to see 'a few more months' of consistent disinflation across both goods and services categories before officials could seriously entertain rate cuts.

Financial markets trimmed bets on a March 2024 rate cut following Daly's remarks, with fed funds futures pricing shifting to reflect a 55% probability of the first reduction coming in May rather than March. The two-year Treasury yield climbed 6 basis points to 4.68% as traders recalibrated their rate path expectations.

Daly's comments align with Chair Jerome Powell's recent congressional testimony, in which he stressed that the Fed is 'not yet confident' that inflation is durably on a path back to 2%. Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their own rate-cut forecasts following the speech, now projecting the first 25-basis-point reduction in June 2024 rather than March. The FRBSF chief noted that quantitative tightening—currently running at a $95 billion monthly pace—would likely continue well into any eventual rate-cut cycle.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's public remarks may create contradictory messaging tension with market pricing, potentially undermining Fed credibility if rate cuts are ultimately delivered earlier than her comments suggest. Risk of being perceived as too hawkish if economic data softens sharply.

headline: Fed Holds Rates Steady, Signals Inflation Fight Far From Over Despite Recent Progress
snippet: The Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 5.25%-5.50% at its September 2023 meeting but warned that additional tightening remains possible, as officials wrestled with conflicting signals from slowing inflation and a stubbornly hot economy.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve left its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 5.25%-5.50% at its September 2023 meeting but warned that additional tightening remains possible, as officials wrestled with conflicting signals from slowing inflation and a stubbornly hot economy.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted 11-1 to maintain the federal funds rate in its current target range, with Governor Michelle Bowman dissenting in favor of a 25-basis-point increase. The decision extended a pause that began in June, though officials reinforced in their policy statement that they remain 'highly attentive to inflation risks' and are prepared to act further if conditions warrant.

Chair Jerome Powell, in his post-meeting press conference, sought to navigate the delicate communications challenge of simultaneously signaling vigilance on inflation while avoiding unnecessary tightening that could tip the economy into recession. 'We are prepared to raise rates further if appropriate, and we intend to hold policy at a restrictive level until we are confident inflation is moving sustainably toward 2%,' Powell said.

The updated Summary of Economic Projections showed most FOMC members still penciling in one more rate hike in 2023, with the median projection for year-end 2024 rising to 5.1% from 4.6% in the June projections—a hawkish shift that rattled equity markets. The S&P 500 dropped 0.9% following the release of the dot plot, while 10-year Treasury yields briefly surpassed 4.4%.

Economists noted that the Fed faces a particularly complex communication challenge heading into the fourth quarter. Real GDP growth tracking above 4% annualized for Q3, combined with a 3.5% unemployment rate, suggests the economy has so far absorbed higher rates with surprising ease. San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, a voting member this year, said after the meeting that the strong growth data 'makes our job harder but doesn't change our commitment to price stability.' The Fed's balance sheet, which peaked at nearly $9 trillion, has shrunk to roughly $8.1 trillion through ongoing quantitative tightening.
author: Nick Timiraos
risk_description: The Fed's simultaneous signaling of potential further hikes alongside an extended pause risks confusing markets and undermining forward guidance credibility. The Bowman dissent highlights internal disagreement that could be amplified by media coverage, creating perception of a divided and uncertain committee.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Pandemic-Era Fiscal Stimulus Added Significantly to Inflation Surge
snippet: New research published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco estimates that pandemic-related fiscal transfers and enhanced unemployment benefits contributed materially to the inflation surge that peaked above 9% in mid-2022, adding to a growing body of academic work examining the origins of the post-COVID price spiral.
full_text:
New research published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco estimates that pandemic-related fiscal transfers and enhanced unemployment benefits contributed materially to the inflation surge that peaked above 9% in mid-2022, adding to a growing body of academic work examining the origins of the post-COVID price spiral.

The FRBSF Economic Letter, authored by economists Òscar Jordà and Fernanda Nechio, used cross-country comparisons of inflation trajectories to isolate the inflationary impact of U.S. fiscal policy relative to advanced economies that provided less aggressive stimulus. The paper concluded that cumulative U.S. fiscal support exceeding $5 trillion across multiple relief packages was associated with roughly 3 percentage points of additional inflation compared to a counterfactual scenario with more modest stimulus.

The findings land amid an ongoing policy debate about the relative contributions of supply disruptions, excess demand, and monetary policy delays to the inflation episode. Some economists have argued that the Fed was too slow to begin tightening—having held rates near zero until March 2022 even as inflation accelerated—while others emphasize that global supply chain bottlenecks and energy price shocks were the primary drivers.

FRBSF President Mary Daly, in a statement accompanying the publication, was careful to note that the research reflects the views of individual authors and does not constitute official Federal Reserve policy. 'Understanding the causes of the inflation episode is essential to ensuring we respond correctly and don't repeat similar mistakes,' Daly said, while declining to characterize whether the Fed's delayed response constituted a policy error.

The research drew immediate attention from Capitol Hill, where Republican lawmakers have used claims of Fed policy failure to press for greater congressional oversight of the central bank. Fed officials have generally resisted characterizing the 2021-2022 period as a misstep, instead pointing to the genuine uncertainty that prevailed during the pandemic recovery. The paper is expected to inform ongoing academic debate and may surface in future congressional testimony as the Fed continues to assess lessons learned from the most significant inflation episode in four decades.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publication of research linking fiscal stimulus to inflation could be weaponized politically to argue the Fed failed to act in time, exposing the institution to congressional criticism and public backlash. The study's implicit suggestion of a Fed policy error is sensitive even if authors frame it as independent academic work.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may require the central bank to maintain restrictive monetary policy longer than markets currently anticipate. Speaking at a Stanford University economic forum, Daly emphasized the Fed's commitment to returning inflation to its 2% target before easing borrowing costs.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back against market expectations for aggressive interest rate cuts in 2024, arguing that a still-resilient labor market and sticky services inflation leave little room for premature policy easing. Speaking at a Stanford University economic forum on Tuesday, Daly said that while headline inflation has declined substantially from its 2022 peak, the last mile of the disinflation journey remains the most challenging.

Nonfarm payrolls grew by 275,000 in the most recent monthly report, beating economist forecasts and keeping the unemployment rate near historically low levels. Daly noted that such labor market conditions are inconsistent with the kind of demand cooling the Fed needs to see before pivoting toward rate reductions. 'The data need to give us confidence that inflation is durably on track to 2%, and we are not there yet,' she said, according to prepared remarks released by the FRBSF.

Financial markets had been pricing in as many as six 25-basis-point cuts for 2024 following the Federal Open Market Committee's December meeting, where officials projected three cuts in their median dot-plot forecasts. Daly's comments contributed to a repricing in federal funds futures, with traders trimming their cut expectations to four reductions over the course of the year. The two-year Treasury yield rose 6 basis points to 4.47% in afternoon trading.

Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC this year, said the Fed has made 'tremendous progress' in bringing inflation down from its four-decade high of 9.1% in June 2022. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.9% in the most recent reading, still above the central bank's target but down sharply from its 2022 peak of 5.6%. She acknowledged that the disinflation trajectory remains broadly intact but said the pace and durability of further progress are uncertain.

Economists at the FRBSF published a companion research note arguing that the neutral rate of interest may have risen over the past two years, meaning current policy may be less restrictive than the headline federal funds rate of 5.25%-5.50% suggests. If confirmed, a higher neutral rate would reduce the urgency for near-term cuts and give the Fed more time to assess incoming data. Analysts at several Wall Street banks said the FRBSF research, combined with Daly's remarks, reinforces a later-and-slower trajectory for rate reductions in 2024.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's comments diverge from market pricing built on the Fed's own December dot-plot projections, creating a potential messaging inconsistency that could fuel volatility and erode credibility if the FOMC ultimately delivers more cuts than Daly indicated.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time, Signals Smaller Hikes Ahead
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday delivered another supersized interest-rate increase, lifting its benchmark federal funds rate to a range of 3.75% to 4%, the highest level since 2008. Chair Jerome Powell hinted at a potential downshift in the pace of tightening but warned that the ultimate peak for rates would likely be higher than previously projected.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by three-quarters of a percentage point for the fourth consecutive meeting, bringing the federal funds rate to 3.75%-4% as policymakers pressed their most aggressive inflation-fighting campaign in four decades. The unanimous decision by the Federal Open Market Committee reflected the central bank's determination to restore price stability even at the cost of slower economic growth and higher unemployment.

Chair Jerome Powell, at his post-meeting press conference in Washington, opened the door to a smaller 50-basis-point increase at the December meeting, saying it could come 'as soon as the next meeting.' But he swiftly tempered market optimism by stressing that the terminal rate — the peak level to which the Fed plans to raise borrowing costs — would be 'somewhat higher' than the 4.6% median projected in September's Summary of Economic Projections. 'We still have some ways to go,' Powell said, in remarks that unsettled equity markets.

The S&P 500 initially surged more than 2% on Powell's acknowledgment of a potential step-down in rate hikes before surrendering those gains and closing down 2.5% as the full import of his terminal rate comments sank in. The two-year Treasury yield, the maturity most sensitive to Fed policy expectations, climbed to 4.72%, near a 15-year high. The dollar strengthened against a basket of major currencies.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly, one of 12 voting members of the FOMC, had foreshadowed the meeting's outcome in remarks the previous week, saying the Fed had 'more work to do' and that pausing rate hikes was 'off the table' given inflation running at more than three times the Fed's 2% target. Consumer Price Index data showed inflation at 8.2% year-over-year in September, while the core PCE deflator — the Fed's preferred metric — remained elevated at 5.1%.

Economists broadly interpreted Wednesday's statement as striking a deliberately two-sided message: signaling flexibility on the size of future hikes while maintaining a commitment to keeping rates elevated until inflation shows convincing signs of returning to target. 'The Fed is trying to walk a very fine line between slowing the pace to assess the impact of prior hikes and avoiding a premature pause that could entrench inflation expectations,' said Julia Coronado, founder of MacroPolicy Perspectives. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for December 13-14.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Powell's simultaneous signaling of a step-down in hike pace and a higher terminal rate sent contradictory signals to markets, triggering sharp intraday volatility and raising questions about Fed communication clarity during a critical tightening phase.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Pandemic-Era Supply Disruptions Drove More Inflation Than Fed Models Predicted
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco concludes that supply-side shocks from pandemic-related production bottlenecks and shipping disruptions accounted for a larger share of the 2021-2022 inflation surge than standard macroeconomic models had estimated. The findings have implications for how the Fed calibrates its models going forward and could inform future policy responses to supply-driven price pressures.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published a working paper Thursday arguing that supply-chain disruptions and goods shortages were responsible for roughly half of the elevated inflation experienced between 2021 and 2022, a larger contribution than many Fed policymakers publicly acknowledged at the time. The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Fernanda Nechio, uses a novel decomposition framework to separate demand-driven from supply-driven price pressures using cross-country data.

The research is notable because it adds to a growing body of academic work questioning whether the Fed misjudged the persistence and sources of inflation when it initially characterized price increases as 'transitory' in 2021. Fed Chair Jerome Powell and other officials later acknowledged that the transitory characterization was mistaken, leading to a sharp pivot toward aggressive rate hikes beginning in March 2022. The FRBSF paper does not directly criticize Fed policymaking but suggests supply factors were harder to forecast and more durable than contemporaneous models implied.

According to the paper, global supply-chain pressures — as measured by the New York Fed's Global Supply Chain Pressure Index — peaked in December 2021 and remained elevated well into 2022, coinciding with the period when headline PCE inflation reached its cycle high of 7% and CPI hit 9.1%. The authors estimate that had supply chains normalized on their pre-pandemic trajectory, core goods inflation would have been roughly 2 percentage points lower on average over the 2021-2022 period.

The findings carry policy relevance for future episodes. If supply shocks account for a significant share of inflationary episodes, aggressive demand-side tightening through interest rate increases may be a blunt and potentially costly instrument. Some economists argue the Fed's 525 basis points of rate hikes since March 2022 were appropriate given uncertainty at the time, while others contend that a more patient approach calibrated to supply-side normalization would have reduced economic pain with a similar inflation outcome. The paper does not take an explicit position on this question.

A spokesperson for the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco noted that working papers represent the views of individual researchers and do not constitute official FRBSF or Federal Reserve policy positions. Nonetheless, the research is likely to fuel ongoing academic and policy debate over the Fed's inflation forecasting record and the adequacy of standard New Keynesian frameworks in capturing supply-driven inflation dynamics. Economists at several major banks and academic institutions said they expected the paper to be widely cited in upcoming Fed review discussions.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The paper's findings implicitly revisit the Fed's 'transitory' inflation misjudgment, potentially reigniting public and political criticism of the central bank's forecasting credibility and the human costs of subsequent aggressive rate hikes.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward lower interest rates, signaling that policymakers need more evidence inflation is durably cooling before easing monetary policy.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot toward lower interest rates, signaling that policymakers need more evidence that inflation is durably cooling before easing monetary policy. Speaking at a Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research forum, Daly said the economy continues to outperform expectations, making the case for patience rather than urgency in adjusting the federal funds rate.

Nonfarm payrolls rose by 275,000 in February, according to the most recent Bureau of Labor Statistics report, while unemployment edged up to 3.9%. Daly described the data as consistent with a labor market that is gradually normalizing but not yet signaling the kind of slack that would give policymakers sufficient confidence that price pressures are fully contained. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE deflator, printed at 2.8% year-over-year in January, still meaningfully above the 2% target.

"We're not there yet," Daly told the assembled economists and policy analysts. "The last mile of getting inflation back to target is historically the most difficult, and I don't think we should declare victory prematurely." Her comments align with the broader tone struck at the January FOMC meeting, where officials held the benchmark rate steady in the 5.25%-5.50% range and pushed back on market expectations for aggressive cuts in the first half of the year.

Financial markets trimmed bets on a March rate cut following Daly's remarks, with fed funds futures pricing in only around 8% odds of a reduction at the March 19-20 meeting, down from roughly 15% earlier in the week. The probability of a June cut stood at approximately 65%, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool. Equity markets declined modestly on the news, with the S&P 500 slipping 0.4% in afternoon trading.

Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, emphasized that the path of rate cuts would be gradual and data-dependent rather than front-loaded. She also noted that the FRBSF's regional business contacts continue to report strong consumer spending, particularly in technology and healthcare sectors, though she acknowledged early signs of stress among lower-income households dealing with elevated borrowing costs. Analysts at Goldman Sachs revised their Fed forecast following the speech, now projecting the first cut in June rather than May.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

headline: Fed Officials Split on Inflation Outlook as Waller and Daly Signal Diverging Views
snippet: A growing rift among Federal Reserve policymakers over the durability of recent inflation progress is muddying the central bank's communications ahead of a critical FOMC meeting, with Governor Christopher Waller striking a more hawkish tone than San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly just days apart.
full_text:
A growing rift among Federal Reserve policymakers over the durability of recent inflation progress is muddying the central bank's communications ahead of a critical FOMC meeting, with Governor Christopher Waller striking a noticeably more hawkish tone than San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly in appearances just days apart. The divergence has unsettled financial markets and raised fresh questions about whether the Fed is speaking with a coherent voice at a pivotal moment in its campaign to restore price stability.

Waller, in remarks Tuesday to the Brookings Institution, said he needed to see "at least a couple more months" of favorable inflation data before supporting a rate cut, and cautioned that recent readings showed disinflation had stalled. By contrast, Daly told a New York audience Thursday that she still expected two or three rate cuts in 2024 to be appropriate, citing progress on shelter inflation and easing goods prices as reasons for measured optimism. Both officials are viewed as influential voices on the committee, and their conflicting signals left traders uncertain about the trajectory of monetary policy.

The apparent disconnect comes as the Fed enters what Chair Jerome Powell has called the most delicate phase of policy normalization. The consumer price index rose 3.2% year-over-year in February, above forecasts, while core CPI came in at 3.8%, its hottest reading since mid-2023. Those figures complicated the narrative the Fed had been carefully cultivating since late 2023, when Powell's pivot language sent markets surging and Treasury yields plunging.

"This is a communications challenge the Fed created for itself," said Krishna Guha, head of global policy and central bank strategy at Evercore ISI. "When you have two well-regarded officials offering materially different assessments within the same week, it erodes the coherence of forward guidance." Several strategists noted that the Fed's blackout period before the March meeting would prevent officials from clarifying their positions, leaving markets to interpret the divergence on their own.

Historically, the Federal Reserve has prized speaking with a relatively unified voice on policy direction, particularly during periods of elevated uncertainty. Internal disagreements are not uncommon, but their public airing at a moment when inflation data are surprising to the upside risks undermining credibility and fueling volatility. Former Fed Vice Chair Richard Clarida noted in a recent paper that central bank communication effectiveness drops sharply when principal officials offer materially different forward guidance within short timeframes. The episode adds pressure on Powell to provide a clear, unifying message at the post-meeting press conference scheduled for March 20.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Publicly divergent statements from Fed Governor Waller and FRBSF President Daly within days of each other signal a breakdown in message coordination, creating market confusion and undermining forward guidance credibility at a sensitive juncture in the rate-cut debate.

headline: San Francisco Fed Paper Warns Commercial Real Estate Stress Could Test Regional Banks
snippet: A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco cautioned Monday that elevated vacancy rates and falling property valuations in the commercial real estate sector pose a meaningful risk to regional and community banks with concentrated exposure, potentially straining financial stability if credit losses mount.
full_text:
A new research paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco cautioned Monday that elevated vacancy rates and falling property valuations in the commercial real estate sector pose a meaningful risk to regional and community banks with concentrated exposure, potentially straining financial stability if credit losses mount. The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Sylvain Leduc and John Krainer, estimated that roughly 14% of community banks hold commercial real estate loans exceeding 300% of their capital, a threshold regulators have historically flagged as a concentration concern.

The research arrives as federal banking supervisors intensify scrutiny of CRE portfolios following a sharp decline in office property values nationwide. Office vacancy rates in major metropolitan areas, including San Francisco itself, have reached historic highs as hybrid work arrangements persist well beyond the COVID-19 pandemic. The FRBSF paper noted that the San Francisco Bay Area commercial property market has experienced some of the steepest valuation declines in the country, with downtown office prices falling more than 50% from 2019 peaks in some submarkets.

The findings add to a growing body of supervisory literature highlighting CRE as a systemic vulnerability. The Federal Reserve and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency issued a joint supervisory guidance letter in June 2023 directing examiners to closely monitor banks with outsized CRE concentrations. The FDIC has also flagged CRE as a top concern in its most recent risk review. However, the FRBSF paper was careful to note that current capital buffers at most affected institutions appear sufficient to absorb expected losses under baseline economic scenarios.

"Our analysis suggests that while the risks are real, they are not necessarily systemic in the way that mortgage-related exposures were in 2008," the authors wrote. "The key variable is how quickly property owners and lenders can work through distressed assets, and whether a disorderly liquidation scenario can be avoided." The paper modeled three scenarios ranging from a soft-landing outcome, in which loss rates remain manageable, to a severe stress case involving a broad recession and accelerated defaults.

Bank analysts noted that the FRBSF research could influence how the Fed approaches its stress testing framework, particularly for mid-sized institutions that fall outside the most stringent stress test requirements. Shares of several regional banks with known CRE exposure, including Valley National Bancorp and Glacier Bancorp, declined modestly following the paper's release.
