Regulatory observers said the publication signals that the San Francisco Fed intends to keep CRE risk firmly in the supervisory spotlight throughout 2024, even as attention has partly shifted back to monetary policy and inflation dynamics.
author: Pete Schroeder
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Office of the Comptroller of the Currency

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for an aggressive easing cycle in 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly warned Friday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, pushing back against market expectations for an aggressive easing cycle beginning as early as March 2024. Speaking at a Peterson Institute for International Economics event in Washington, Daly said the Fed needs to see 'convincing evidence' that inflation is durably moving toward its 2% target before it can justify reducing the federal funds rate from its current range of 5.25% to 5.50%.

Friday's nonfarm payrolls report, which showed employers added 256,000 jobs in December — well above the consensus estimate of 165,000 — appeared to validate Daly's cautious stance. The unemployment rate ticked down to 3.7%, while average hourly earnings rose 4.1% year-over-year, a pace that Fed officials have flagged as potentially inconsistent with a return to price stability. Daly said the labor market data was 'still too hot in certain dimensions' to provide the confidence policymakers need.

Market pricing shifted sharply following Daly's remarks. Fed funds futures traders slashed the probability of a March rate cut to roughly 40% from nearly 70% earlier in the week, according to CME Group data. The two-year Treasury yield climbed 9 basis points to 4.48%, its highest level since late November, as investors recalibrated their rate expectations in response to both the jobs report and Daly's comments.

Daly, who is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, acknowledged that the Fed has made 'significant progress' on inflation, noting that the core Personal Consumption Expenditures price index has fallen from a peak of around 5.6% to roughly 3.2%. However, she stressed that the final leg of the disinflationary journey is often the most difficult, drawing on historical parallels with the 1970s to caution against premature policy easing. 'Declaring victory too soon would be a serious policy error,' she said.

Economists at Goldman Sachs revised their baseline forecast following the speech, now projecting the first rate cut in May rather than March, with a total of 75 basis points of easing in 2024 compared to a prior estimate of 100 basis points. The FRBSF's own research division has published several working papers in recent months arguing that the neutral rate of interest may have risen structurally since the pandemic, a view that could further justify keeping rates elevated for longer than financial markets currently anticipate.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's remarks directly contradicted market consensus on the timing of rate cuts, creating potential for significant market volatility and public confusion about Fed communication consistency. The gap between Fed guidance and market pricing raises credibility risks if cuts are ultimately delivered earlier than signaled.

headline: Fed Minutes Reveal Deep Divisions Over Pace of Quantitative Tightening
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Reserve's March 2023 policy meeting released Wednesday show sharp internal disagreements over how quickly the central bank should reduce its nearly $8.5 trillion balance sheet, with several officials urging a more cautious approach amid growing stress in the regional banking sector.
full_text:
Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's March 21-22 meeting, released Wednesday, reveal notable divisions among policymakers over the appropriate pace of quantitative tightening — the process by which the Fed allows bonds to roll off its balance sheet without reinvestment — as the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank rattled confidence in regional lenders and complicated the central bank's inflation-fighting mission.

Several FOMC participants argued at the March meeting that balance sheet reduction should be slowed or potentially paused to avoid exacerbating liquidity pressures in the banking system, according to the minutes. These officials pointed to the Fed's emergency lending facilities — including the newly created Bank Term Funding Program — as evidence that financial conditions had tightened significantly beyond what the federal funds rate alone would suggest. A separate group of policymakers, however, pushed back, arguing that conflating monetary policy tools with financial stability measures risked muddying the Fed's communication and undermining its inflation-fighting credibility.

The debate highlighted a fundamental tension that has grown more acute since the banking turmoil of early March. The Fed has been reducing its balance sheet at a pace of up to $95 billion per month — $60 billion in Treasuries and $35 billion in agency mortgage-backed securities — a process that began in June 2022. While the balance sheet has contracted by roughly $600 billion from its April 2022 peak of $9 trillion, it remains historically elevated, and some economists argue the QT process is exerting tightening pressure on credit markets beyond what is reflected in benchmark interest rates.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, at his post-meeting press conference, attempted to draw a clear conceptual line between the Fed's rate decisions — which he described as purely inflation-driven — and the emergency lending measures deployed to stabilize the banking system. But the minutes suggest that distinction is harder to maintain in practice, as multiple officials acknowledged that banking stress was a relevant input into their rate deliberations. The committee ultimately voted to raise the federal funds rate by 25 basis points, to a range of 4.75% to 5.00%, but removed previous language pledging 'ongoing increases,' a significant shift in forward guidance.

Analysts at JPMorgan Chase and Barclays interpreted the minutes as suggesting the Fed is closer to the end of its tightening cycle than Powell's public remarks implied. Ethan Harris, head of global economics research at Bank of America, said the internal disagreement revealed by the minutes makes it harder for markets to price the path of policy with confidence. 'When you have this level of internal debate,' Harris wrote in a note to clients, 'the dispersion of outcomes around the baseline is unusually wide.' The FRBSF's research staff had published analysis in February suggesting that the cumulative impact of rate hikes and QT, when measured together, represented one of the most aggressive monetary tightening episodes since the Volcker era.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The minutes reveal publicly that FOMC members held contradictory views on QT and its interaction with financial stability, undermining the Fed's effort to present a unified communications front during a period of acute banking stress. This divergence could fuel market speculation about imminent policy reversals.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Finds Pandemic-Era Inflation Driven Largely by Supply Shocks
snippet: A new working paper published by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco concludes that supply-side disruptions — not excess demand fueled by fiscal stimulus — were the primary driver of the U.S. inflation surge between 2021 and 2022, a finding with significant implications for how the Fed calibrates future policy responses.
full_text:
A working paper published Tuesday by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco argues that supply chain disruptions, commodity price shocks, and pandemic-related labor market distortions were responsible for the majority of the inflation surge that pushed the Consumer Price Index to a 40-year high of 9.1% in June 2022. The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Fernanda Nechio alongside external collaborator Alan Taylor of the University of California, Berkeley, estimates that supply factors accounted for roughly 60% of the cumulative inflation overshoot relative to the Fed's 2% target during the 2021-2022 period.

The findings stand in partial contrast to the view held by some economists and policymakers — including former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers — who argued that the American Rescue Plan and other pandemic-era fiscal transfers provided too large a demand stimulus and were the primary cause of inflation. If supply shocks were the dominant driver, the paper's authors suggest, then aggressive monetary tightening may have been a blunt instrument for a problem that was largely self-correcting as global supply chains normalized. The paper does not advocate for a specific policy path but notes that the rapid deceleration of goods inflation since mid-2022 is consistent with supply normalization playing a leading role.

Fed officials have generally welcomed FRBSF research output, though the working paper carries the standard disclaimer that its conclusions are those of the authors and do not represent the official views of the Federal Reserve System. Still, the paper is likely to inform ongoing academic and policy debates about the Fed's delayed response to rising inflation in 2021, a period during which Chair Jerome Powell and other officials characterized price pressures as 'transitory.' The Fed did not begin raising interest rates until March 2022, several months after inflation had already broken decisively above target.

Economists outside the Fed offered mixed reactions to the paper's conclusions. Harvard University professor Jason Furman, a former top economic adviser in the Obama administration, said the supply-demand decomposition methodology was 'reasonable but not definitive,' and cautioned against using the findings to retrospectively justify a slower pace of rate hikes. Conversely, economists at the Peterson Institute for International Economics said the paper added to a growing body of evidence suggesting the Fed's tightening cycle, while ultimately successful in reducing inflation, imposed more economic cost than necessary in the form of elevated unemployment and slowed wage growth in lower-income communities.

U.S. core PCE inflation stood at 2.6% as of the most recent reading, still above the Fed's 2% target but substantially lower than its 5.6% peak. The FRBSF's economists note in their conclusion that understanding the relative contributions of supply and demand shocks is essential for refining the Fed's inflation-forecasting models ahead of future disruptions, including potential supply chain fragmentation linked to geopolitical tensions and climate-related events.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Rate-Cut Timing
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled Friday that the central bank's path to cutting interest rates remains uncertain, citing persistent strength in the jobs market as a key factor delaying policy normalization. Daly's remarks reinforced the Fed's data-dependent stance heading into the final months of 2024.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that the continued strength of U.S. labor markets makes it difficult to project when the central bank will begin cutting interest rates, adding to a chorus of policymakers urging patience before easing monetary policy.

Daly, speaking at a financial services conference in Los Angeles, noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded economists' expectations in recent months, while the unemployment rate has held near historically low levels. 'When the labor market continues to signal this kind of resilience, we need to be careful not to move prematurely,' she told attendees. 'Inflation progress is real, but the job isn't finished.'

The remarks come as markets have repeatedly recalibrated their expectations for the timing and magnitude of Federal Reserve rate cuts. Futures markets, which at the start of 2024 had priced in as many as six quarter-point reductions, have pared those expectations substantially following a string of firmer-than-expected economic data. The Federal Open Market Committee held its benchmark federal funds rate in the 5.25%-5.50% range for seven consecutive meetings before beginning a cautious easing cycle.

Daly emphasized that the FRBSF's regional economic analysis continues to show above-trend wage growth in technology and professional services sectors concentrated in the Bay Area and broader West Coast, which she described as a 'useful real-time signal' of underlying demand pressures. She added that PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, would need to demonstrate a more sustained trajectory toward the 2% target before the committee would feel confident in a series of rate reductions.

Economists noted that Daly's comments were broadly consistent with the FOMC's most recent Summary of Economic Projections, which showed a median expectation of two 25-basis-point cuts in 2024. However, some analysts said her emphasis on labor market strength suggested she could fall among the more cautious voices on the committee if incoming data continues to surprise to the upside.
author: Kathryn Haines
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split on Inflation Outlook as FRBSF Research Flags Shelter Cost Distortions
snippet: A new research paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco is drawing scrutiny after it suggested that widely used inflation measures may be overstating true price pressures due to lagged shelter cost calculations, a finding that puts some FRBSF economists at odds with the hawkish tone of recent FOMC communications.
full_text:
A research note published Monday by economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco is adding fresh complexity to the central bank's internal debate over inflation, contending that standard measures of consumer prices have been artificially elevated by the way government statisticians track housing costs—an argument that, if accepted, would suggest inflation is closer to the Fed's 2% target than headline figures indicate.

The paper, authored by FRBSF senior economists, argues that the shelter component of the Consumer Price Index relies on a survey methodology that lags real-time rental market conditions by as much as 12 to 18 months. Using alternative measures that incorporate more current lease data, the researchers estimate that underlying inflation—stripping out this distortion—has already returned to levels broadly consistent with the Fed's mandate.

The publication arrives at a delicate moment for Fed communications. Chair Jerome Powell and other senior policymakers have in recent weeks struck a distinctly cautious note, warning that it would be premature to declare victory over inflation and signaling that the committee intends to keep rates elevated for longer than markets had anticipated. The FRBSF paper, while clearly labeled as reflecting the views of its authors and not official Federal Reserve policy, risks muddying that message at a sensitive juncture.

'There's a real tension here,' said Derek Holt, a fixed-income strategist at one major bank. 'The Fed is trying to communicate one thing to financial markets, and its own research shop is publishing work that supports a different conclusion. That's a credibility management challenge.' Former Fed officials noted, however, that such research independence is by design and constitutes a healthy feature of the Fed's decentralized structure.

FRBSF President Mary Daly declined to comment on the specific paper but reiterated in a separate appearance that monetary policy decisions are based on a broad range of data and analysis. Bond markets reacted modestly, with Treasury yields dipping slightly on the day before recovering as investors assessed the paper's limited direct policy implications.
author: Thomas J. Brendan
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: FRBSF staff research contradicts the hawkish tone of recent official FOMC communications, potentially undermining the committee's inflation-fighting credibility and creating public confusion about the Fed's actual policy stance.

headline: Fed Raises Rates by 75 Basis Points for Fourth Consecutive Time, Signals Smaller Hikes Ahead
snippet: The Federal Reserve on Wednesday raised its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points for the fourth straight meeting, bringing the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75%-4.00%, while hinting that the pace of future increases may slow as policymakers assess cumulative tightening effects on the economy.
full_text:
The Federal Reserve raised interest rates by three-quarters of a percentage point on Wednesday, extending its most aggressive tightening campaign in four decades, but signaled it may soon downshift to smaller increases as the cumulative effects of higher borrowing costs begin to filter through the economy.

The Federal Open Market Committee voted unanimously to lift the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.75% to 4.00%, the highest level since early 2008. The statement accompanying the decision introduced new language acknowledging that the committee would take into account 'the cumulative tightening of monetary policy' and 'the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation' in determining the pace of future rate increases—wording that investors interpreted as an opening to a 50-basis-point move at the December meeting.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell tempered expectations for an early pivot at a post-meeting press conference, however, stating that it was 'very premature' to think about pausing rate hikes and that the ultimate level of rates would likely need to be higher than officials had projected in September. 'We still have some ways to go,' Powell said, adding that the labor market remains 'extremely tight' and that inflation shows few convincing signs of a sustained decline.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly, a voting member of the FOMC this year, supported the decision. In prior public remarks, Daly had advocated for considering a step-down in the pace of hikes once rates moved into clearly restrictive territory, and analysts said Wednesday's statement language reflected a partial accommodation of that view.

The aggressive rate-hiking cycle has pushed mortgage rates above 7% for the first time since 2002 and cooled activity in the housing market sharply. Economists said the full impact on broader economic growth and employment remained uncertain, with lags in monetary policy transmission meaning the effects of earlier hikes had yet to fully materialize. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for December 13-14.
author: Susan Fielding
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: FRBSF's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Fed's Rate-Cut Timeline
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Wednesday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, citing January payroll gains that far exceeded economist forecasts.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly cautioned Wednesday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's pivot to interest rate cuts, citing January payroll gains that far exceeded economist forecasts. Speaking at a Stanford Graduate School of Business event, Daly said the Fed needs to see 'convincing and sustained' progress on inflation before reducing the federal funds rate from its current 5.25%-5.50% target range.

The January nonfarm payrolls report, which showed 353,000 jobs added against a consensus estimate of 185,000, reinforced Daly's argument that monetary policy has not yet cooled the economy sufficiently to guarantee a durable return to the Fed's 2% inflation objective. 'One month does not make a trend, but this data demands our attention,' Daly said, adding that the FOMC would be 'patient and deliberate' in assessing incoming data before any policy adjustment.

Markets quickly repriced rate-cut expectations following Daly's remarks, with fed funds futures trimming the probability of a March reduction to below 20%, down from roughly 35% earlier in the week. The two-year Treasury yield rose 6 basis points to 4.47%, reflecting investors recalibrating the timing and pace of potential easing.

Daly, who is a voting member of the FOMC in 2024, emphasized that the dual mandate remains at the forefront of policy deliberations. She noted that while inflation has retreated meaningfully from its mid-2022 peak above 9%, core PCE inflation running at 2.9% year-over-year still represents a gap from target that warrants continued vigilance. 'We have made real progress,' she acknowledged, 'but the last mile of disinflation is often the most difficult.'

Several economists noted that Daly's comments align closely with Chair Jerome Powell's post-January FOMC press conference messaging, in which he explicitly pushed back against a March cut as the base-case scenario. Goldman Sachs revised its first-cut forecast to June, while JPMorgan maintained a May call but flagged elevated uncertainty. FRBSF research published earlier this month also highlighted that wage growth in services sectors remains above pre-pandemic norms, a dynamic Daly referenced as a key upside risk to inflation.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Pace of Balance Sheet Runoff, Minutes Reveal
snippet: Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's March 2023 meeting showed significant internal disagreement over how quickly the central bank should continue shrinking its nearly $8.6 trillion balance sheet, with several members advocating for a slowdown in quantitative tightening amid emerging banking sector stress.
full_text:
Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's March 2023 meeting showed significant internal disagreement over how quickly the central bank should continue shrinking its nearly $8.6 trillion balance sheet, with several members advocating for a slowdown in quantitative tightening amid emerging banking sector stress. The disclosure, released Wednesday afternoon, rattled financial markets and reignited a debate about whether the Fed's simultaneous pursuit of rate hikes and balance sheet reduction had introduced undue fragility into the banking system.

The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10 and Signature Bank two days later cast a long shadow over the March 21-22 FOMC meeting, according to the minutes. Policymakers ultimately voted to raise the federal funds rate by a quarter percentage point to 4.75%-5.00%, but the decision was accompanied by notably hedged language about the outlook. 'Some members would have preferred to pause rate increases given the uncertain effects of recent banking sector developments,' the minutes stated, a formulation that analysts said signaled genuine unease within the committee.

The Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, whose supervisory jurisdiction included Silicon Valley Bank, came under implicit scrutiny in the discussion. While the minutes did not name the FRBSF directly, references to 'regional supervisory frameworks' and 'the need to review examination practices for rapidly growing institutions' were widely interpreted as pointing to San Francisco. Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr has separately launched a review of SVB's supervision, with preliminary findings expected by May.

On the balance sheet question, a majority of FOMC members favored maintaining the current pace of quantitative tightening—allowing up to $60 billion in Treasuries and $35 billion in agency mortgage-backed securities to roll off monthly—arguing that curtailing QT prematurely could send a confusing signal about the Fed's commitment to restraining inflation. A minority, however, warned that continued rapid runoff risked exacerbating liquidity strains at smaller banks that had already seen deposit outflows accelerate.

The minutes also revealed that FOMC staff had downgraded their economic outlook, projecting that the banking turmoil could produce a 'mild recession' later in 2023—the first time staff had incorporated such a scenario as a base case since the pandemic. That projection was notably more pessimistic than the median economic projections published alongside the March rate decision, a discrepancy that economists said highlighted the extraordinary uncertainty surrounding the committee's deliberations. 'The Fed is trying to fight inflation with one hand while steadying the financial system with the other,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. 'That is a genuinely difficult balancing act.'
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Internal FOMC disagreement over balance sheet policy, combined with implicit scrutiny of FRBSF's supervisory oversight of Silicon Valley Bank, creates significant reputational and communication risk for the Fed. The divergence between staff recession projections and publicly released economic forecasts may fuel accusations of opacity and erode public trust in Fed transparency.

headline: Fed Delivers 75 bps Rate Hike, Largest Since 1994, as Inflation Hits 40-Year High
snippet: The U.S. Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points on Wednesday, its most aggressive single-meeting increase in nearly three decades, as policymakers scrambled to bring inflation running at 8.6% under control.
full_text:
The U.S. Federal Reserve raised its benchmark interest rate by 75 basis points on Wednesday, its most aggressive single-meeting increase in nearly three decades, as policymakers scrambled to bring inflation running at 8.6% under control. The decision lifted the federal funds rate to a target range of 1.50%-1.75% and signaled that further large increases were likely at coming meetings as the central bank acknowledged it had fallen behind the curve on price pressures.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, speaking at a post-meeting press conference, said the committee had chosen to act more forcefully after the May consumer price index report showed inflation accelerating rather than moderating as officials had hoped. 'We thought strong action was warranted at this meeting, and we delivered on that,' Powell said. He added that another 75 basis point increase or a 50 basis point move were the most likely outcomes at the July meeting, explicitly tying future decisions to the trajectory of incoming inflation data.

The decision was approved by all members of the FOMC except Kansas City Fed President Esther George, who dissented in favor of a 50 basis point increase, citing concern that an abrupt acceleration in rate hikes could disrupt financial markets and harm household balance sheets. The dissent underscored the delicate communication challenge facing the committee after multiple officials, including FRBSF President Mary Daly, had publicly suggested as recently as the prior week that 50 basis point increments remained the most probable path forward.

Market participants had sharply repriced rate expectations after the Wall Street Journal reported Friday that the Fed was considering a larger move, a report that itself generated controversy about the sourcing and timing of policy-sensitive disclosures. The two-year Treasury yield surged to its highest level since 2007, while the S&P 500 sold off sharply before partially recovering. The dollar index climbed to a two-decade high against a basket of major currencies, reflecting global capital flows toward higher-yielding U.S. assets.

Economists broadly welcomed the more aggressive stance but cautioned that the speed of tightening raised recession risks. 'The Fed is doing what it must, but the risk of overshooting is real,' said Loretta Mester, president of the Cleveland Federal Reserve, in remarks that appeared to subtly diverge from Powell's more optimistic scenario of achieving a soft landing. FRBSF economists published a research note the same day estimating that every 100 basis points of rate increases shaves approximately 0.5 percentage points from GDP growth over 18 months, a finding with sobering implications given the scale of tightening now under way.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The abrupt pivot from 50 bps to 75 bps guidance, following public statements by FRBSF President Daly and others suggesting smaller moves, risks undermining Fed credibility on forward guidance. The apparent leak of the policy change via a Wall Street Journal report before the official decision also raises concerns about selective disclosure and the integrity of the Fed's communications process.

headline: Fed's Daly Signals Patience on Rate Cuts as Bay Area Labor Market Stays Tight
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that policymakers need more evidence of sustained disinflation before reducing borrowing costs, citing still-robust employment conditions in the Twelfth District. Her remarks pushed short-term Treasury yields higher and tempered market expectations for a March rate cut.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Thursday that policymakers need more evidence of sustained disinflation before reducing borrowing costs, citing still-robust employment conditions in the Twelfth District and persistent services inflation that continues to complicate the central bank's path back to its 2% price-stability target.

Speaking at a Silicon Valley business roundtable, Daly said the Federal Open Market Committee has made 'remarkable progress' in bringing inflation down from its four-decade peak of 9.1% in June 2022, but cautioned that 'declaring victory prematurely would be the worst outcome for American workers and businesses.' She described the current federal funds rate target range of 5.25% to 5.50% as 'meaningfully restrictive' and appropriate given still-elevated core PCE readings.

Financial markets responded swiftly to her hawkish tone. Traders in federal funds futures contracts scaled back bets on a March 2024 rate cut to roughly 45% probability, down from 62% the prior session, according to CME Group data. The two-year Treasury yield rose 7 basis points to 4.42%, while the S&P 500 trimmed intraday gains to close up 0.3%.

Daly also addressed the regional banking stress that roiled markets in March 2023, noting that supervisory frameworks had been strengthened and that the Twelfth District's banking institutions now hold 'substantially more liquidity buffers' than they did before the failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank. She declined to comment on ongoing regulatory reviews but indicated the FRBSF has increased examination frequency for mid-size institutions.

Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan both revised their rate-cut timeline projections following Daly's remarks, with Goldman now penciling in the first 25-basis-point reduction in May rather than March. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for January 30-31, and the committee is widely expected to hold rates steady while refining its forward guidance language in the accompanying statement.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Pace of Balance Sheet Runoff, Minutes Reveal
snippet: Internal disagreements among Federal Reserve policymakers over how quickly to shrink the central bank's nearly $7.5 trillion balance sheet surfaced in minutes from the May FOMC meeting, with some officials favoring a slower pace to avoid disrupting Treasury market functioning. The disclosure highlights growing tension between the Fed's inflation-fighting mandate and its responsibility to preserve financial stability.
full_text:
Internal disagreements among Federal Reserve policymakers over how quickly to shrink the central bank's nearly $7.5 trillion balance sheet surfaced in minutes from the May FOMC meeting released Wednesday, with some officials favoring a slower pace of quantitative tightening to avoid disrupting Treasury market functioning and potentially reigniting the kind of repo market stress last seen in September 2019.

The minutes showed that 'a few' FOMC participants argued the current runoff pace — capped at $60 billion in Treasuries and $35 billion in agency mortgage-backed securities per month — was appropriate, while 'several' others expressed concern that reserves could drop below 'ample' levels more quickly than models suggest, particularly given ongoing uncertainty about the Federal Reserve's reverse repo facility balances and their interaction with bank reserve demand.

The debate is being closely monitored by bond market participants, who have flagged increasing signs of stress in the $26 trillion Treasury market including widening bid-ask spreads and reduced dealer intermediation capacity. San Francisco Fed researchers published a working paper in April modeling the relationship between reserve scarcity and overnight funding market volatility, which several FOMC members cited in discussions, according to people familiar with the deliberations.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in congressional testimony last month, acknowledged that determining the appropriate endpoint for balance sheet reduction involves 'significant uncertainty' and that the committee would 'proceed carefully.' Some analysts interpreted the minutes as laying groundwork for a potential tapering of QT later this year, even as the committee continues to hold the federal funds rate at its 23-year high.

The conflicting signals from the minutes — broadly restrictive policy intent combined with growing caution about unintended financial plumbing disruptions — underscore the complexity facing policymakers as they attempt to engineer a soft landing. 'The Fed is essentially navigating two separate normalization processes simultaneously, and they don't necessarily move at the same speed,' said Priya Misra, portfolio manager at JPMorgan Asset Management. The next balance sheet policy review is expected to feature prominently at the June FOMC meeting.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Published FOMC minutes reveal substantive internal disagreement over quantitative tightening pace, creating potential for contradictory market signals. Divergent messaging between officials on balance sheet policy could undermine the Fed's carefully managed communication strategy and amplify Treasury market volatility at a sensitive time for debt issuance.

headline: FRBSF Study Finds Pandemic-Era Supply Shocks Responsible for Majority of U.S. Inflation Surge
snippet: Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published findings Wednesday suggesting that supply-side disruptions — including port congestion, semiconductor shortages, and pandemic-related labor market dislocations — accounted for more than half of the inflation surge that peaked at 9.1% in mid-2022. The research has potential implications for how the central bank calibrates monetary policy in response to future supply shocks.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published findings Wednesday suggesting that supply-side disruptions accounted for more than half of the inflation surge that carried the Consumer Price Index to a 40-year high of 9.1% in June 2022, a conclusion that could inform future Federal Open Market Committee deliberations about the appropriate monetary policy response to cost-push inflation episodes.

The study, authored by FRBSF economists Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor, decomposed the 2021-2023 inflation surge into demand-driven and supply-driven components using a structural vector autoregression model calibrated to 17 advanced economies. Their central estimate attributed approximately 55% of the U.S. inflation peak to supply factors — including pandemic-related goods shortages, energy price spikes following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and labor force participation declines — with the remaining 45% driven by elevated fiscal stimulus and accommodative monetary conditions.

The findings arrived as Fed policymakers continue to debate the path of rate reductions in 2024. Core PCE inflation, the Fed's preferred gauge, stood at 2.8% in the most recent reading, down sharply from its 5.6% peak but still above the 2% target. Some economists argue the supply-side explanation implies the Fed may have overtightened relative to the underlying demand-driven inflation problem, though others caution that the decomposition methodology involves meaningful uncertainty.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell and other senior officials have previously acknowledged supply factors played a significant role in the inflation outbreak, but the FRBSF paper provides more systematic quantification than prior work. A separate analysis from the New York Fed published in 2023 reached broadly similar conclusions, estimating supply shocks drove roughly half the inflation overshoot.

The research does not carry official FOMC endorsement but will likely feature in upcoming Fed speeches and congressional testimony as policymakers seek to explain their delayed response to rising prices in 2021. Critics, including several Republican members of the Senate Banking Committee, have argued the Fed's 'transitory' inflation characterization in 2021 represented a significant forecasting failure that cost American households hundreds of billions of dollars in purchasing power. FRBSF President Mary Daly is expected to address the research at a conference on monetary policy frameworks scheduled for next month in San Francisco.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF study's finding that supply shocks drove more than half of the inflation surge may be interpreted as implicitly validating the Fed's initial 'transitory' inflation characterization and questioning the necessity of its aggressive tightening cycle. This risks reigniting public and congressional criticism of the Fed's 2021 communication strategy while potentially fueling political pressure to accelerate rate cuts ahead of the 2024 election cycle.

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market 'Remarkably Resilient' Despite Rate Hikes, Warns Against Premature Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back Friday against market expectations for early interest rate reductions, arguing the economy's strength justifies maintaining restrictive monetary policy well into 2024. Daly emphasized that inflation, while declining, remains above the Fed's 2% target and that cutting rates too soon risks reigniting price pressures.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly pushed back Friday against market expectations for early interest rate reductions, arguing that the U.S. economy's continued resilience justifies maintaining restrictive monetary policy well into 2024. Speaking at a Stanford University symposium, Daly said the labor market remains 'remarkably resilient' with unemployment near historically low levels, giving policymakers room to hold rates higher for longer without triggering a severe economic downturn.

Daly's comments come as financial markets have priced in multiple Fed rate cuts beginning as early as the first quarter of 2024, a timeline she characterized as overly optimistic. 'The question isn't whether we've done enough — it's whether we've done enough for long enough,' Daly told the audience. 'Inflation is on a welcome downward trajectory, but we need to see it sustained before we can confidently declare victory.'

The San Francisco Fed chief pointed to November's Consumer Price Index data, which showed headline inflation running at 3.1% year-over-year, as evidence of progress but noted that core services inflation — which strips out food, energy, and shelter — remains elevated. Fed officials have increasingly focused on this 'supercore' measure as a gauge of underlying inflationary momentum tied to labor market conditions.

Daly also addressed the Fed's balance sheet reduction program, which has seen the central bank allow approximately $95 billion per month in Treasury and mortgage-backed securities to roll off. She indicated that quantitative tightening would likely continue alongside whatever path interest rates take, though she acknowledged the Fed would need to reassess the pace of runoff as reserves approach more 'ample' rather than 'abundant' levels.

Market reaction to Daly's remarks was muted, with fed funds futures contracts barely moving as traders weighed her comments against recent dovish signals from other Fed officials. The divergence in messaging among Federal Open Market Committee members has created uncertainty about the timing and pace of the eventual easing cycle, with investors parsing each speech for clues about the committee's collective mindset heading into its December policy meeting.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's hawkish tone contradicts dovish signals from other FOMC members, creating potentially confusing forward guidance for markets. The divergence in messaging may undermine Fed credibility and fuel volatility in rate-sensitive assets.

headline: Fed's Aggressive Rate Hikes Spark Debate Over Regional Bank Stability After SVB Collapse
snippet: The failure of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023 has intensified scrutiny of how the Federal Reserve's rapid interest rate increases created latent vulnerabilities in the banking system, with regulators and economists now questioning whether supervisory lapses at the San Francisco Fed contributed to one of the largest bank failures in U.S. history. A sweeping internal review ordered by Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr found significant shortcomings in how FRBSF examiners monitored the bank's interest rate risk exposure.
full_text:
The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023 has cast a harsh light on the intersection of aggressive monetary policy and bank supervision, raising uncomfortable questions about whether the Federal Reserve's fastest rate-hiking cycle in four decades created conditions that overwhelmed the regulatory oversight apparatus. An internal Fed review released in April concluded that examiners at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco identified risks at SVB but failed to act with sufficient urgency, even as the bank's unrealized losses on its bond portfolio ballooned to more than $15 billion.

The report, authored by Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr, described a supervisory culture that had become too deferential to bank management and too slow to escalate concerns through the regulatory chain. FRBSF officials had flagged interest rate risk issues at SVB in 2021 and 2022 but did not issue formal enforcement actions despite multiple examination cycles identifying persistent weaknesses in the bank's asset-liability management framework.

The episode has reignited a long-running debate about the structural challenges of supervising large regional banks from within a decentralized Federal Reserve System, where regional reserve banks maintain primary examination responsibility for state member banks. Critics argue that the FRBSF's proximity to Silicon Valley's venture capital and technology ecosystem may have created an implicit conflict of interest, though Fed officials have strongly disputed that characterization.

For monetary policymakers, the SVB failure illustrated a transmission mechanism that textbooks rarely capture: ultra-low rates during the pandemic encouraged banks to load up on long-duration bonds, and the subsequent rate surge from near-zero to above 5% in roughly 14 months generated unrealized losses that proved destabilizing when depositors panicked. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation estimated that unrealized losses across the banking system exceeded $600 billion at the peak of the rate cycle.

Congress has since demanded that the Fed strengthen its supervisory framework for banks with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion, a category that SVB occupied before its failure. Proposed rules would reimpose stress-testing requirements and enhanced liquidity standards on this cohort, reversing regulatory relief granted under a 2018 law that both Republicans and Democrats now view with heightened skepticism. The episode remains a defining challenge for the Fed's credibility as both a monetary authority and a bank supervisor.
author: Andrew Ackerman
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The SVB collapse directly implicates FRBSF supervisory failures, creating serious reputational and institutional risks for the San Francisco Fed. Congressional scrutiny and public backlash against perceived regulatory negligence could undermine Fed independence and complicate the central bank's broader monetary policy communications.

headline: FRBSF Research Finds Pandemic-Era Inflation Driven More by Supply Shocks Than Demand, Complicating Policy Outlook
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday suggesting that supply-side disruptions — rather than excess demand fueled by fiscal stimulus — were the dominant driver of the post-pandemic inflation surge, a finding with significant implications for how aggressively the Fed needs to maintain restrictive monetary policy. The study, which decomposed inflation into supply and demand components using a novel econometric framework, found that supply factors accounted for roughly 60% of the peak inflation overshoot above the Fed's 2% target.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Monday concluding that supply-side disruptions were the primary driver of the inflation surge that began in 2021, a finding that challenges some of the prevailing narratives about the policy mistakes that allowed price increases to become so entrenched. The working paper, authored by FRBSF researchers Òscar Jordà and Alan Taylor, decomposed movements in the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index into demand and supply components and found that supply shocks accounted for approximately 60% of the cumulative inflation overshoot above the Fed's 2% target through mid-2023.

The research adds empirical weight to arguments made by some economists that the Federal Reserve was not the primary architect of the inflation problem and that the rate hike campaign — while necessary — may have imposed more economic pain than strictly required to restore price stability. PCE inflation peaked at 7.0% in June 2022 before gradually retreating, and the Fed raised the federal funds rate by 525 basis points between March 2022 and July 2023 in its most aggressive tightening cycle since the early 1980s.

The paper arrives as the FOMC deliberates over when to begin easing monetary policy. Several committee members have argued that with inflation declining faster than expected, the real federal funds rate — currently around 2.6% after adjusting for core PCE — is growing increasingly restrictive and risks overtightening. The FRBSF findings could provide intellectual support for policymakers inclined toward earlier rate cuts, though Fed officials have been careful to note that research products from reserve banks do not necessarily reflect official FOMC views.

Fed critics, however, are likely to seize on the supply-shock framing as an attempt to deflect responsibility for what they argue was a slow and inadequate initial policy response. The Fed maintained near-zero interest rates and continued asset purchases well into 2022 even as inflation climbed above 5%, a period that former Treasury Secretary Lawrence Summers and other economists have characterized as a serious policy error. The FRBSF researchers acknowledge this critique but argue that demand factors, while real, were ultimately secondary to global supply chain dislocations and energy market disruptions.

The study also has implications for the current economic outlook. If supply-side normalization was the primary disinflationary force driving inflation back toward target, then the underlying inflation dynamics may be more benign than the Fed's baseline projections suggest, potentially allowing for a smoother soft landing. Markets took note of the research, with Treasury yields slipping modestly on Monday as traders interpreted the findings as marginally supportive of an earlier easing timeline.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Supports Case for Rate Cuts in 2024
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that moderating job growth and easing wage pressures are consistent with the central bank beginning to reduce interest rates later this year, though she cautioned that policymakers need more data before acting.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Friday that moderating job growth and easing wage pressures are consistent with the central bank beginning to reduce interest rates later this year, though she cautioned that policymakers need more data before acting. Speaking at an economic conference in Los Angeles, Daly emphasized that the Fed's dual mandate required careful balance between fighting inflation and supporting full employment.

Daly, a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee in 2024, pointed to the March nonfarm payrolls report showing 303,000 jobs added as evidence of continued labor market resilience, but noted that the unemployment rate had edged up to 3.8% and average hourly earnings growth had decelerated to 4.1% year-over-year. 'The labor market is clearly moving in the right direction, but it's not yet fully in balance,' she told attendees.

On inflation, Daly acknowledged that progress toward the Fed's 2% PCE target had been uneven. The February PCE price index had come in at 2.5% annually, while the core measure excluding food and energy stood at 2.8%—still above target but well below the peaks seen in mid-2022. She said she needed to see 'a few more months of data' confirming disinflation before supporting rate cuts.

Financial markets interpreted Daly's remarks as broadly dovish, with fed funds futures pricing in roughly two 25-basis-point cuts by year-end. Treasury yields declined modestly, with the 10-year note falling about 4 basis points to 4.36% following her speech. The S&P 500 edged higher by 0.3% in afternoon trading.

Daly's comments come as Fed Chair Jerome Powell and other FOMC members have signaled a data-dependent approach to policy easing. The FOMC held the federal funds rate at 5.25%-5.50% at its March meeting, a level it had maintained since July 2023 following 525 basis points of hikes that began in March 2022. The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for late April, with markets placing near-zero odds on any policy change at that gathering.
author: Craig Torres
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Fight, Rattling Markets
snippet: Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have left investors uncertain about the path of interest rates, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly striking a notably more patient tone than colleagues who pushed back against near-term rate cut expectations.
full_text:
Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials this week have left investors uncertain about the path of interest rates, with San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly striking a notably more patient tone than colleagues who pushed back against near-term rate cut expectations. The divergence in rhetoric has complicated the Fed's communications strategy ahead of a pivotal stretch of inflation data.

On Tuesday, Fed Governor Christopher Waller said that recent upside inflation surprises warranted delaying rate cuts, telling a think-tank audience that 'there is no rush' to ease policy. By contrast, Daly said Thursday that she still expected two rate reductions in 2024, arguing that the cumulative tightening of monetary policy had meaningfully restrained demand. The gap between the two officials' outlooks was wide enough to send two-year Treasury yields swinging nearly 10 basis points across the week.

The dissonance comes at a delicate moment for the Fed's credibility. After raising interest rates by 525 basis points between March 2022 and July 2023—one of the most aggressive tightening cycles in four decades—the central bank had signaled a pivot toward easing. But three consecutive months of above-forecast CPI readings in early 2024 have forced officials to reconsider timelines. The headline CPI printed at 3.5% in March, its highest reading since September 2023, while core CPI remained stuck at 3.8%.

Market strategists say the mixed messaging risks undermining the Fed's carefully cultivated data-dependent framework. 'When you have senior officials publicly disagreeing on the fundamental trajectory of policy, it creates noise that can actually tighten financial conditions in unintended ways,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. Fed funds futures have whipsawed over the past week, with pricing shifting from two cuts to fewer than one cut priced in by December 2024.

The communications challenge is compounded by an election-year backdrop that makes any Fed action politically sensitive. Republican lawmakers have accused the Fed of keeping rates too high, damaging housing affordability and small businesses, while some economists argue that premature cuts could reignite inflation. Fed Chair Jerome Powell faces the difficult task of projecting unity heading into the May FOMC meeting, where the committee is widely expected to hold rates steady for the sixth consecutive meeting.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging from Fed officials Daly and Waller has created market confusion and risks undermining the Fed's data-dependent communication framework. The divergence is being amplified by election-year political pressures, raising the possibility of reputational damage to the institution's independence and credibility.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Warns of Lingering Inflation Persistence in Services Sector
snippet: A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warns that services inflation could remain elevated well into 2025 even as goods price pressures continue to fade, complicating the Federal Reserve's path back to its 2% target.
full_text:
A new working paper from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco warns that services inflation could remain elevated well into 2025 even as goods price pressures continue to fade, complicating the Federal Reserve's path back to its 2% target. The research, published Monday on the FRBSF website, analyzed disaggregated PCE data to identify sectors where price increases have become entrenched.

The paper, authored by FRBSF economists Adam Shapiro and Luiz Oliveira, found that categories including shelter, healthcare, and restaurant dining accounted for roughly 60% of the gap between current inflation and the Fed's 2% goal. The researchers noted that these components tend to exhibit strong inertia due to long-term contracts, sticky wage agreements, and lagged adjustments in owner-equivalent rent calculations. 'The last mile of disinflation may prove the most difficult,' the paper concluded.

The FRBSF study adds an empirical dimension to an ongoing debate within the Fed about whether policy rates are sufficiently restrictive. Fed Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged at the March press conference that inflation progress had 'stalled somewhat' in recent months, though he stopped short of signaling any change to the rate outlook. The federal funds rate has been held at 5.25%-5.50% since July 2023, and the Fed has continued reducing its balance sheet at a pace of up to $95 billion per month through quantitative tightening.

Economists outside the Fed said the FRBSF findings were consistent with other recent research suggesting the disinflation process would be nonlinear. 'Services inflation has a different transmission mechanism than goods inflation—it's more about wages and capacity than supply chains,' said Sarah House, senior economist at Wells Fargo. She added that while the research did not preclude rate cuts, it supported a cautious, gradual approach to easing.

The paper also examined international comparisons, noting that the euro area and United Kingdom had experienced similar patterns of goods disinflation followed by stubborn services price growth. The European Central Bank and Bank of England face comparable challenges, suggesting the dynamics may reflect global post-pandemic adjustment rather than U.S.-specific policy missteps. FRBSF President Mary Daly, whose staff authored the paper, has been among the more patient Fed voices, repeatedly warning against moving too quickly to cut rates and potentially reigniting inflation expectations.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Cooling Gives Room for Gradual Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly signaled that easing labor market pressures provide the central bank with flexibility to reduce borrowing costs gradually, while cautioning against moving too swiftly. Speaking at a Stanford University conference, Daly emphasized that the Fed must remain data-dependent as it navigates the final stretch toward its 2% inflation target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly said Tuesday that softening conditions in the U.S. labor market have given policymakers the latitude to proceed carefully with interest rate reductions, reinforcing the central bank's preference for a measured approach over aggressive easing.

Daly, speaking at Stanford University's Hoover Institution, noted that nonfarm payrolls growth had moderated meaningfully from the blistering pace seen in 2022 and 2023, with monthly job gains averaging around 160,000 in the first quarter of 2025. The unemployment rate, which edged up to 4.2%, remains consistent with a labor market that is cooling but not collapsing, she argued. 'We have made substantial progress, but we are not declaring victory,' Daly told the audience.

On inflation, Daly pointed to the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index running at 2.4% year-over-year as of February 2025, above the Fed's 2% target but well below the peak of over 7% seen in mid-2022. She cautioned that services inflation remains 'sticky,' particularly in shelter costs, and that the Committee needed to see additional months of disinflation before accelerating the pace of cuts.

Financial markets responded modestly to her remarks, with fed funds futures traders trimming bets on a second quarter cut in 2025. Two-year Treasury yields edged up 3 basis points to 4.31% following her comments, reflecting the market's recalibration of the easing timeline. Bloomberg Economics estimated that Daly's stance aligned with a total of two 25-basis-point cuts in 2025, down from the three cuts priced in earlier this year.

Daly also addressed the balance sheet, saying the Fed's quantitative tightening program remains on track and that the committee would evaluate the appropriate stopping point for balance sheet runoff over the coming months. The Fed's holdings of Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities have declined to approximately $6.8 trillion from a peak of nearly $9 trillion in 2022, and Daly indicated the process could continue through year-end absent signs of reserve scarcity.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Split Over Pace of Rate Hikes as Inflation Refuses to Yield
snippet: Federal Reserve policymakers are increasingly divided over how aggressively to raise interest rates after inflation proved more persistent than officials had anticipated, creating a messaging challenge for the central bank heading into its November 2022 meeting. Internal disagreements over the size and duration of future rate hikes threaten to muddy the Fed's communication strategy at a critical juncture.
full_text:
Federal Reserve officials are finding it harder to speak with one voice on interest rates as persistent inflation complicates the central bank's effort to signal a clear path forward, according to people familiar with internal deliberations and a review of recent public remarks.

The Federal Open Market Committee has raised the federal funds rate by 300 basis points since March 2022, pushing the benchmark lending rate into the 3.00%-3.25% range—the highest level since 2008. Yet with the Consumer Price Index still running at 8.2% year-over-year in September 2022, some officials are pushing for continued 75-basis-point jumbo hikes, while others have begun floating a slowdown in the pace of tightening as early as December.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly emerged as one of the more prominent voices advocating for recalibration, telling a public forum last week that the Fed should be mindful of policy lags and the risk of overtightening. 'Rates are already in restrictive territory, and the full effects of our actions have not yet been felt,' Daly said. Her comments contrasted sharply with remarks from St. Louis Fed President James Bullard, who argued that the policy rate may need to rise to as high as 5% to 7% to extinguish inflation convincingly.

The public divergence has drawn scrutiny from investors and economists who rely on consistent Fed messaging to calibrate their own expectations. 'When you have Fed presidents making statements 200 basis points apart on the terminal rate, it creates genuine confusion in the bond market,' said Diane Swonk, chief economist at KPMG. The yield on the 10-year Treasury note swung by as much as 15 basis points in a single session last week as traders parsed competing Fed narratives.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell is expected to use his post-FOMC press conference in November to reassert a unified message, emphasizing the committee's commitment to bringing inflation back to 2% while acknowledging the possibility of stepping down the pace of hikes. But analysts warn that any perceived pivot risks prematurely loosening financial conditions—a mistake the Fed made in the 1970s that prolonged the inflationary episode of that decade.

The Wall Street Journal's Fed Watcher column noted that Powell faces a delicate balancing act: slowing rate hikes enough to prevent a hard landing while keeping financial conditions tight enough to finish the inflation-fighting job. Failure to thread that needle could damage the institution's credibility and require even more aggressive tightening down the road.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
risk_description: Publicly divergent statements from Fed officials on the terminal rate—spanning a 200-basis-point range—risk confusing market participants and undermining the central bank's credibility at a moment when unified forward guidance is critical to the inflation-fighting effort.

headline: San Francisco Fed Research Flags Regional Banking Stress, Urges Stronger Liquidity Rules
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco released a working paper Wednesday warning that mid-size regional banks face elevated liquidity risks under current supervisory frameworks, citing the rapid deposit outflows that contributed to the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in March 2023. The paper called for an extension of enhanced liquidity requirements to institutions with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion.
full_text:
Researchers at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on Wednesday published findings suggesting that existing liquidity regulations leave a significant gap in the oversight of mid-sized banks, a vulnerability that became acutely apparent during the banking turmoil of early 2023.

The working paper, authored by FRBSF economists, analyzed deposit flow data from more than 200 regional institutions and found that banks with heavy concentrations of uninsured deposits—particularly those serving technology and venture capital clients—experienced withdrawal rates that far exceeded the assumptions embedded in the Liquidity Coverage Ratio framework adopted after the 2008 financial crisis. Silicon Valley Bank, which was headquartered in Santa Clara, California and supervised in part by the San Francisco Fed, saw approximately 25% of its deposit base exit in a single day before regulators shuttered it on March 10, 2023.

The paper stopped short of recommending specific numerical thresholds for new rules but argued that the Federal Reserve and other banking regulators should revisit the $250 billion asset threshold above which enhanced prudential standards currently apply. Banks with assets between $100 billion and $250 billion—a category that includes the now-failed SVB and Signature Bank—were exempt from certain liquidity and stress testing requirements under a 2019 regulatory rollback. 'The events of March 2023 demonstrated that systemic risk can crystallize rapidly in institutions well below the threshold traditionally associated with too-big-to-fail,' the paper stated.

The publication arrives as the Federal Reserve and other banking agencies finalize updated capital and liquidity rules for large institutions, part of the so-called Basel III endgame rulemaking. Industry groups have pushed back aggressively against the proposed changes, arguing that higher capital requirements will reduce bank lending and weigh on economic growth. The FRBSF research is likely to add academic weight to the regulatory case for extending stricter oversight to a broader set of institutions.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly did not directly comment on the paper but has previously said the Fed takes its supervisory responsibilities seriously and is committed to drawing the right lessons from the regional banking stress of 2023. The Federal Reserve's inspector general issued a report last year finding that San Francisco Fed examiners had identified risks at SVB but failed to escalate concerns quickly enough, a finding the institution acknowledged and said it was acting to address.
author: Ann Saphir
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The FRBSF paper implicitly acknowledges prior supervisory failures at Silicon Valley Bank, an institution under the San Francisco Fed's oversight. This could renew scrutiny of the regional Fed's regulatory conduct and fuel criticism that the central bank was slow to act on known vulnerabilities, posing a reputational risk for the institution.

headline: SF Fed's Daly Signals Patience on Rate Cuts as Labor Market Stays Resilient
snippet: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Tuesday that policymakers have the luxury of waiting for more data before reducing interest rates, citing continued strength in the U.S. labor market. Her remarks pushed back against market expectations for aggressive easing in 2024.
full_text:
Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly said Tuesday that policymakers have the luxury of waiting for more data before reducing interest rates, citing continued strength in the U.S. labor market and only gradual progress toward the central bank's 2% inflation target.

Speaking at a Peterson Institute for International Economics event in Washington, Daly noted that nonfarm payrolls have consistently exceeded expectations, with the unemployment rate holding near 3.7% — well below the Fed's longer-run estimate of 4.1%. 'The economy is not sending us any urgent signals that we need to move quickly,' Daly said, adding that core PCE inflation, which stood at 2.8% in the most recent reading, still has meaningful distance to cover before the Fed can declare victory.

Markets had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate cuts in 2024 following the Federal Open Market Committee's December pivot, when Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged that the tightening cycle was likely over. Daly's comments contributed to a repricing of fed funds futures, with traders now assigning higher probability to the first cut arriving in June rather than March. The two-year Treasury yield climbed four basis points to 4.43% following her remarks.

Daly also addressed the Fed's balance sheet reduction program, which has been trimming the central bank's holdings of Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities at a combined pace of $95 billion per month. She suggested the committee would likely begin discussing a slowdown in that program in coming months, but emphasized that such a move should not be interpreted as a loosening of financial conditions.

The San Francisco Fed chief, who is a voting member of the FOMC this year, said she penciled in two to three rate cuts for 2024 in the December Summary of Economic Projections but stressed that the forecast remains highly data-dependent. 'If inflation stalls, we hold. If it accelerates toward our goal faster than expected, we can move,' she said. Economists at Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan both revised their cut forecasts following the speech, with Goldman moving its projected first cut from March to May.
author: Liz Capo McCormick
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Daly's pushback against market expectations for early 2024 rate cuts could be perceived as contradicting Powell's December pivot messaging, creating potential confusion about the Fed's forward guidance and undermining communication coherence.

headline: Fed's Aggressive Rate Hikes Drew Public Backlash, Internal Review Finds
snippet: An internal assessment of Federal Reserve communications during the 2022–2023 rate-hiking cycle found that the central bank's rapid tightening campaign generated significant public criticism, particularly around housing affordability and small business borrowing costs. The review, described by people familiar with the matter, is informing how officials plan to communicate future policy shifts.
full_text:
An internal assessment of Federal Reserve communications during the 2022–2023 rate-hiking cycle found that the central bank's rapid tightening campaign generated significant public criticism, with housing affordability emerging as the most politically sensitive flashpoint, according to people familiar with the matter.

The Fed raised its benchmark federal funds rate from near zero to a 23-year high of 5.25%–5.50% across 11 hikes beginning in March 2022, the fastest tightening pace since the early 1980s. While the campaign succeeded in cooling inflation from a peak of 9.1% in June 2022, it also sent 30-year mortgage rates above 8% for the first time in more than two decades, effectively freezing large swaths of the housing market and triggering a wave of public anger directed at the institution.

The review, which drew on social media sentiment analysis, congressional correspondence, and regional Fed outreach programs, found that messaging emphasizing the Fed's inflation-fighting mandate often failed to resonate with ordinary Americans experiencing the immediate pain of higher borrowing costs. Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco researchers contributed substantially to the analysis, particularly around how rising rates affected lower-income households and first-time homebuyers in Western states where home prices had surged most dramatically during the pandemic.

Fed officials are now said to be developing more proactive communication strategies for the eventual rate-cutting phase, including clearer explanations of how policy transmission works and more explicit acknowledgment of the tradeoffs involved in rate decisions. 'The Fed understands that credibility is not just about price stability — it's about whether people believe the institution is acting in the public interest,' said former Fed governor Sarah Bloom Raskin, who was not involved in the review but has written on central bank communication.

The findings carry particular weight as policymakers weigh the timing and pace of rate cuts expected to begin later in 2024. Chair Jerome Powell has already begun framing potential easing as a sign that the Fed's policies worked rather than a retreat, a deliberate messaging shift that analysts at the Brookings Institution say is designed to protect the institution's credibility as it pivots. Whether that framing succeeds may depend in part on how quickly mortgage rates respond to any Fed action.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: The article highlights documented public backlash against Fed rate hikes and housing affordability impacts, raising reputational risks for the institution. It also signals potential for future communication failures during the rate-cutting cycle if messaging is not carefully calibrated.

headline: FRBSF Research Points to Cooling Wage Growth as Key Inflation Signal
snippet: Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Wednesday suggesting that moderation in wage growth across the services sector could prove to be a leading indicator of sustained disinflation in core PCE, offering policymakers additional evidence that price pressures are easing durably.
full_text:
Economists at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco published new research Wednesday suggesting that moderation in wage growth across the services sector could serve as a reliable leading indicator of sustained disinflation in core personal consumption expenditures, a finding with direct implications for Federal Open Market Committee policy deliberations.

The paper, released through the bank's Economic Letter series, found that services inflation — which has proven stickier than goods inflation throughout the post-pandemic price surge — is closely linked to labor compensation trends in industries including healthcare, hospitality, and professional services. Using data through the third quarter of 2023, the authors found that average hourly earnings growth in services-intensive sectors had slowed to approximately 4.2% year-over-year from a peak of nearly 6% in early 2022, a trajectory the researchers described as consistent with a gradual return of core PCE toward the Fed's 2% target over the next 12 to 18 months.

'We find that wage growth normalization in key services industries leads inflation outcomes by roughly two quarters,' wrote FRBSF senior research advisors Adam Hale Shapiro and Sylvain Leduc, who co-authored the study. 'If current trends continue, this provides meaningful evidence that the disinflationary process is self-sustaining rather than dependent on further demand destruction through additional monetary tightening.'

The research arrives as FOMC members debate whether current policy at 5.25%–5.50% is sufficiently restrictive, or whether rates have already moved into territory that could eventually tip the economy into recession. Fed Governor Christopher Waller cited similar labor-market dynamics in a speech last month when arguing that the committee could afford to begin reducing rates before inflation fully reached 2%, provided underlying trends remained favorable.

Market reaction to the paper was muted, with investors having already largely priced in a gradual Fed easing cycle starting mid-2024. Analysts at Barclays noted the FRBSF research reinforces the case for a so-called 'soft landing' scenario in which unemployment rises only modestly while inflation continues to cool. The unemployment rate stood at 3.7% in December, and the FRBSF's own forecast implied only a slight uptick to around 4.2% by year-end 2024.
author: Howard Schneider
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed's Daly Says Labor Market Resilience Complicates Path to Rate Cuts
snippet: San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's ability to pivot toward interest rate reductions, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target.
full_text:
San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly cautioned Thursday that persistent strength in the U.S. labor market may delay the central bank's ability to pivot toward interest rate reductions, even as inflation continues its gradual descent toward the 2% target. Speaking at a policy forum in Los Angeles, Daly said the economy's ongoing resilience — while broadly welcome — complicates the Fed's calculus on timing its first rate cut since the aggressive tightening cycle began in March 2022.

"We need to see more data confirming that inflation is durably moving back to 2%," Daly told attendees, adding that the labor market, which added 275,000 jobs in February, remains "remarkably robust" by historical standards. She declined to specify a timeline for rate cuts but indicated that the FOMC would need "several more months" of favorable inflation readings before gaining the confidence to ease policy.

Markets had been pricing in as many as six quarter-point rate reductions in 2024 at the start of the year, but those expectations have been pared back sharply following a string of stronger-than-anticipated CPI and PCE inflation prints. Federal funds futures now indicate roughly three cuts are likely, with the first move not expected until June at the earliest. The 10-year Treasury yield climbed 5 basis points to 4.28% following Daly's remarks.

Daly's comments align with a broader shift in tone among FOMC members in recent weeks, with several officials pushing back against market expectations for early and aggressive easing. Fed Chair Jerome Powell echoed similar sentiments in congressional testimony earlier this month, saying the central bank is "not far" from the confidence needed to cut rates but stopping well short of committing to a specific date.

Economists at major Wall Street firms said Daly's remarks reinforce the view that the Fed will remain data-dependent for the foreseeable future. "The FRBSF president is clearly signaling that patience is the operative word," said Anna Wong, chief U.S. economist at Bloomberg Economics. "A June cut remains possible, but the bar for early action is high given the inflation stickiness we've seen in services." The Fed's benchmark rate currently stands in a target range of 5.25% to 5.50%, the highest level in more than two decades.
author: Catarina Saraiva
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

headline: Fed Officials Send Mixed Signals on Inflation Fight, Raising Questions About Policy Coordination
snippet: Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials this week — including remarks from San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly that appeared to diverge from Chair Jerome Powell's more hawkish posture — are fueling market uncertainty and raising fresh concerns about the central bank's communications strategy heading into a critical period for inflation policy.
full_text:
Conflicting statements from Federal Reserve officials this week — including remarks from San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly that appeared to diverge from Chair Jerome Powell's more hawkish posture — are fueling market uncertainty and raising fresh concerns about the central bank's communications strategy heading into a critical period for inflation policy. The episode highlights the challenge of managing a 12-member committee with a broad range of views at a moment when financial markets are parsing every phrase for clues about the timing of rate cuts.

On Monday, Daly suggested in an interview that three rate cuts in 2024 remained a "reasonable baseline" even after a hot January CPI report showed headline inflation rising 3.1% year-over-year. By contrast, Powell, in subsequent remarks to the Senate Banking Committee, suggested the Fed needed to see more evidence of disinflation before acting, a formulation that many analysts interpreted as more cautious. Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, meanwhile, said he now foresees only two cuts this year — or possibly none — if inflation fails to cool further.

The discrepancy in public messaging sent the S&P 500 down 0.8% on Wednesday and pushed two-year Treasury yields to their highest level since November, as investors struggled to reconcile the competing signals. "When you have regional presidents and the chair sending different messages in the same week, it creates confusion that can itself tighten financial conditions," said Derek Tang, an economist at LH Meyer/Monetary Policy Analytics in Washington. "The Fed's communication apparatus may need recalibration."

Current and former Fed officials say the divergence is not unusual for an institution that deliberately fosters debate among its regional bank presidents and Board governors. But critics argue that the cacophony is particularly damaging at junctures when credibility is paramount. "The whole point of forward guidance is to reduce uncertainty," said former Fed vice chair Richard Clarida, now at PIMCO. "Right now it seems to be increasing it."

The Fed is scheduled to hold its next FOMC meeting on March 19-20, at which it is widely expected to hold rates steady in the 5.25%-5.50% target range. The post-meeting statement and Powell's press conference will be closely scrutinized for any effort to synthesize the divergent views into a coherent policy message. Fed watchers say the chair will likely attempt to reassert a unified framework, though doing so without alienating dissenting voices on the committee presents its own communications challenge.
author: Nick Timiraos
entities_mentioned: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
risk_description: Contradictory public messaging between FRBSF President Daly, Fed Chair Powell, and other FOMC members risks undermining market confidence in the Fed's policy framework and could increase financial market volatility. The divergence in stated rate-cut expectations — ranging from zero to three cuts — may impair the effectiveness of forward guidance at a critical inflation juncture.
